## 3 The History of the Bauhaus

From Henry van de Velde's Arts and Crafts School to the Founding of the Bauhaus in .1919

In 1917, three years after the outbreak of the war, the Belgian Henry van de Velde left Weimar, where he had been active for many years, under the pressure of political events, in particular the hostilities to which he was subjected as a foreigner. In 1901he hadbeen called to Weimar as the artistic adviser of the Grand Duke of Saxony, and there he founded the Großherzoglich sächsische Kunstgewerbeschule (Grand ducal Saxon arts and crafts school)in1906. In contrast to the traditional academic education for artists, as it existed at the neighboring Großherzoglich sächsische Hochschule für bildende Kunst (Grand ducal

Saxon academy of arts), the teaching and instructional activity at the Kunstgewerbeschule was, in keeping with the efforts at a renewal of the arts and crafts that were sketched out in the previous chaptér, directed toward the solution of practical design tasks that were relevant to everyday life rather than the production of autonomous works of art.1

In 1915 van de Velde proposed as his successor, alongside the prominent Jugendstilartists Hermann Obrist andAugust Endell,Wa/ter Gropius (1883–1969), who hadsidedwith him against Muthesius in the so-called Werkbund Debate of 1914 in Cologne (see the sections “Henry van de Velde" and “Hermann Muthesius and the German Werkbund“ in chapter 2).²

13 Walter Gropius (with Adolf Meyer), Fagus shoe last factory in Alfeld an der Leine, 1911

![](images/9c36febf57d1da11f8e71c1e4d2bb7ce83d85fa318a1146bd7ce10bbcb169e99.jpg)

![](images/ef59b680340aaefd537bd9904dbdc5802123b2134dcc166a1073f931800f08f9.jpg)  
14 Walter Gropius, Model (reproduction) of the office building and machine hall at the Werkbund exhibition in Cologne, 1914

Gropius, who learned a new conception o architecture while working as an assistant to Peter Behrens (AEG Turbine Hall, 1909), had made his name even before the war with a series of buildings that showed him to be a progressive architect whodidnot deny technology but rather consciously exploited its constructional and aesthetic possibilities (Fagus factory, Alfeld an der Leine, 1911; office building and machine shop at the Werkbund exhibition in Cologne, 1914).³ Already in 1910 he had formulated his forward-looking architectonic goals in a program proposal as follows:

Instead of good proportions and practical simplicity, pomposity and a false romanticism have become the trend of the time. . . .

Enterprises whose production is based on craftsmanship (to a certain extent still including the building trade) can no longer bear the competition of industry.... The fundamental principle of all industry is the division of labor. The inventor concentrates all his mental energies on the viability of the idea, the invention; the manufacturer on cheap and durable production; and the merchant on organized marketing of the finished product. It is only with the aid of such specialists that it becomes possible to make the essential, i.e., the spiritual creation, economically feasible and at the same time provide the public withproducts of esthetically and technically good quality.4

In his program Gropius called for the “founding of a general housing-construction company following artistically uniform principles" that should function on this same principle of the division of labor that was common elsewhere in industry. Only in this way would it be possible for

the projects of this company and the designs for their individual components [to] be .. . prepared down to the minutest detail by responsible designers before they are ready to be executed. . . Thus, art and technology would be happily united and the public at large would be able to acquire truly mature, good art and solid, genuine goods.. ..

To implement the concept of the industrialization of house construction, the company will repeat individual components in all of its designs and hence facilitate mass production, promising low costs and easy rentability.⁵

This program, which discusses, among other things, “functionalism and solidity" (Sachlichkeit und Solidität) and which is an early outline of the basic features of functional building (one senses the proximity to Muthesius and the Werkbund), became reality when Gropius put it into practice some fifteen years later at the Dessau Bauhaus. That it seems valid as an intellectual blueprint, as a utopia in Ernst Bloch's sense, even in the intervening period—though admittedly strangely watered down and obscured by visions of the past— can be seen from his “Recommendations for the Founding of an Educational Institution as an Artistic Counseling Service for Industry, the Trades, and the Crafts," which Gropius submitted to the State Ministry in Weimar in January 1916.

In its first section these "Recommendations" consist of a repetition of well-known ideas from the Werkbund and thus continue the line of the program proposal of 1910. Gropius called on the artist to “sensibly [come] to terms. . . with the machine . . . and [press] it into his service"6 (cf. Ashbee's idea of the domesticatedmachine in “Ruskin, Morris, and the Arts and Crafts Movement" in chapter 2). In his view, the quality of machine production can be ensuredif the artist participates from "the moment the form which will be mass-produced is invented": “For the artist possesses the ability to breathe soul into the lifeless product of the machine. ... His collaboration is not a luxury, not a pleasing adjunct; it must become an indispensable component in the total output of modern industry." Only a"working community between the artist, the businessman and the technician"7 could, according to Gropius, guarantee that the present and future tasks of industrial society would be adequately fulfilled.

In the second section of the “Recommendations," which is concerned with“an educational institution established by the state as an artistic counseling service," Gropius seems, by contrast, to have completely changed his attitude with respect to his position in 1910. This applies first to his attempt to accommodate himself to the craftsmen and smallmanufacturers active in Weimar by seeking "the school's dependence on existing practical firms" in that the students would bring their “work material from the workshop into school."

One might suspect that this is a tactical maneuver "more likely [to] win the confidence of the manufacturer," but in that case it is especially surprising to find the idea that a

similarly happy partnership might re-emerge as that practicedin the medieval “lodges," where numerous related artist-craftsmen—architects, sculptors, and craftsmen of all grades—came together ina homogeneous spirit and humbly contributed their independent work to the common tasks resting upon them. This was done out of respect for the unity of a mutual idea which inspired them and the meaning of which they understood.8

These emotional sentences no longer speak of a synthesis of art and industry, and it is interesting to see that it is precisely this backwardlooking utopia, which aimed at a thorough social andcultural renewal, that three years later formed the heart of the Bauhaus founding manifesto. When, at the end of the war, the question of van de Velde's successor was decided in his favor, Gropius, following the idea of a unified academy of free and applied art, founded State Bauhaus in Weimar as a merger of the former academy of art and the school of arts and crafts. It is clear that the medieval masons' lodge (Bauhütte) actedas the godparent at the christening. The founding manifesto, with a cuboexpressionist title page by Lyonel Feininger, reads in part:

The ultimate aim of all visual arts is the complete building!. . . Architects, painters and sculptors must recognize anew and learn to grasp the composite character of a building both as an entity and in its separate parts. Only then will their work be embedded with the architectonic spirit which it has lost as "salon art."

The old schools of art were unable to produce this unity; how could they, since art cannot be taught. They must be merged once more with the workshop. ... Architects, painters, sculptors, we all must return to crafts! For art is not a “profession." There is no essentialdifference between the artist and the craftsman. The artist is an exalted craftsman. . ..

Let us . . . create a new guild of craftsmen without the class-distinctions that raise an arrogant barrier between craftsman and artist! Together let us desire, conceive and create the new structure of the future, which willembrace architecture and sculpture and painting in one unity. . . .9

![](images/6dfb0239ea7c5db85f1e1abb52281ea6b09aec5a8ece11a3985883e2e0cc5976.jpg)

In the Bauhaus program that was appended to this manifesto, Gropius stated that a "thorough training in the crafts acquired in workshops and in experimental and practical sites, is required of all students as the indispensable basis for all artistic production" and that "every student must learn a craft." The return to the medieval crafts ideal—to the ideas of community in the guild and the idea of the Gesamtkunstwerk (total work of art)—that is evident from theseprogrammatic sentences stands in remarkable contrast to Gropius's progressive prewar conceptions (for more detail on this, see "An Overview of the Goals and Organizational Structure of the Teachings at the Bauhaus" in chapter 4).

15 Lyonel Feininger, Cathedral, 1919

Study for the title-page woodcut for the manifesto and program of the Staatliches Bauhaus in Weimar; the final version in mirror reverse

This change in orientation canonly be explained against the background of the specific circumstances of the time. The events of the war and the disruptions of the immediate postwar period gave Gropius strong misgivings about the machine and the doctrine of progress associated with it. Hence, affected by a far-reaching crisis of meaning and existence, he clung to the hope that his own activities could be qiven purpose and direction by evoking the——no doubt idealized—Middle Ages. There is abundant evidence for such an interpretation. For example, Gropius remarked in his speech for the first exhibition of student works from the Bauhaus in June 1919:

We find ourselves in a colossal catastrophe of world history, in a transformation of the whole of life. .. . Before the war we put the cart before the horse and wanted to drag art backward into the public sphere by means of organization. We designed artistic ashtrays and beer mugs, andin that way hoped to work up to the great building.... That was an incredible presumption on which we were shipwrecked, and now things will be reversed. No large spiritualorganizations, but small, secret, self-contained societies, lodges. . . .10

And in a letter to Tomás Maldonado, then rector of the Hochschule für Gestaltung (College for Design) in Ulm, Gropius wrote retrospectively in 1963 that the manifesto had been written out of a

mixture of profound defeat as a result of the lost war and of the breakdown of intellectual and economic life and an ardent hope that something new could be built up from these ruins, without the state, which had been felt so oppressively before, becoming mixed up in it.

A more sober call for sober work would have missed the point at the time, which was to offer to young people, filled with new ideas, a broad basis upon which these ideas could be elucidated and tested in practice. The success of the manifesto speaks for itself; young people arrived from Germany and abroad, not in order to build “workable" lamps but to be part of a community that wanted to build a new humanity in a new environment and to release creative spontaneity in everything. That kind of beginning always has something romantic and utopian. . . .11

The idea of a "new, gothic"12 age of the future was also circulating at the time within the Arbeitsrat für Kunst (Work council for the arts), which was founded in Berlin following the November Revolution of 1918 from a group of artists and intellectuals inclined to social revolution, and whose ideas substantially influenced the early

## 16 Bauhaus chronology

Based on 50 Jahre Bauhaus (Stuttgart: Württembergischer Kunstverein, 1968); the divisions into the phases proposed by Friedhelm Kröll have been added (translation)

Bauhaus. This circle, which included Bruno and Max Taut, Adolf Behne, and Walter Gropius (who was chairman for a time), among others, was concerned with anchoring art to the people once again or, as it was expressed in its manifesto: “Art and the people must form a unity! Art should no longer be the enjoyment of a few but the joy and life of the masses."13 This is one of the fundamental social goals that also became part of the Bauhaus. In the jargon of the German Democratic Republic in which he wrote, Karl-Heinz Hüter wrote in his groundbreaking and still important study on the Weimar Bauhaus: “The recognition of necessary social processes led Gropius so far beyond the limits of his own class that he saw the growing class consciousness of the class that had been oppressed until that time as a necessity for the art of the future. The oppressed wouldhave to become aware of their conflict with the ruling class, throw off the traditional ethicalandaesthetic norms, and achieve their own meaning for their intellectuallives."14 Thereare many reasons, which we cannot discuss here, that the Bauhaus never became an educational institute that developed an aesthetic specifically for the working class.

![](images/a456c383e4b19e3daf19a9355d547039d094e3450038afc668e5671d83e764f4.jpg)

In presenting the historical development of the Bauhaus, there are several options for dividing it into periods. One would be basedon the sequenceof directors: the Walter Gropius era (1919–28), the Hannes Meyer era (1928–30), and the Ludwig Mies van der Rohe era (1930–33). Another results from the three locations of the Bauhaus: the Weimar Bauhaus (1919–25), the Dessau Bauhaus (1925–32), and the Berlin Bauhaus (1932– 33). Whereas these periods are determined more by external events, Wulf Herzogenrath has proposed a division into five phases, basedprimarily on stylistic aspects:“1. expressionist, individual, focusedon crafts (Itten, Schreyer, and so on), 1919–21; 2. formal, emphasizing basic forms and colors (the influence of Kandinsky, Theo van Doesburg, and so on), 1922–24; 3. 'functional,' the first phase of collaboration with industry (Moholy-Nagy), 1924–28;4. analytical, Marxist under Hannes Meyer, 1928-30; 5. emphasizing materials and aesthetics under Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, 1930–33."15

The presentation that follows is oriented around the analysis in Friedhelm Kröll's Bauhaus, 1919–1933,16 which, though it keeps to the notion of three phases, focuses more on social psychology (and the history of ideas) in selecting the dates to divide them than is the case of the tripartite divisions based on the directors or the locations, which seem much less persuasive and indeed rather arbitrary. Kröll distinguishes between the founding phase (1919–23), the phase of consolidation (1923–28), and the phase of disintegration (1928–33). Although most art historians have been rather reserved toward this division, it proves to be especially well suited when the focus is on the question of the extent to which, in these individual phases, the Bauhaus as a collective, as a social system, was not just in conformity with the true idea of the Bauhaus but actively took part in its realization.

• In the historical sketch that follows, questions of pedagogy will be discussed only on the margins, since they will be treated in greater depth later.

## The Founding Phase (1919–1923)

After Gropius had taken over the direction of the Bauhaus on 1 April 1919, and the work of the school was begun without an official opening, the new director called the painters Lyonel Feininger and Johannes Itten and the sculptor Gerhard Marcks to Weimar and thus expanded the academic teaching staff of the former Hochschule für bildende Kunst (Academy of the fine arts)—-Max Thedy, Walter Klemm, Otto Fröhlich, and Richard Engeimann—with three artists who could be considered representatives of a modern perspective on art. By 1922 they had been joined by Georg Muche, Oskar Schlemmer, Paul Klee, Lothar Schreyer, and Wassily Kandinsky. In the spring of 1923 Itten left the Bauhaus, and László Moholy-

17 Henry van de Velde, Kunsthochschule, Weimar, 1904–11; from 1919 to 1925 main building of the Staatliches Bauhaus

![](images/88757aab9bd19f78c1b745b820304bd7b3c0e29f959f5a1e1e7cea5b9dffb779.jpg)

Nagy took his place. This stakes out the outer ranges of the first years of the Bauhaus, what Kröll has called the founding phase. One of the main problems of the Bauhaus during these four years was its “structural instability,"17 for which there were a wide variety of reasons.

The situation was unstable, first of all, because the teaching staff was extremely heterogeneous at first. It soon proved to be an illusion that the professors retained from the former Academy of the Fine Arts could commit to the Bauhaus program, which they found far too progressive. “Even after the secession of the old professors and the foundation of their own art academy (1921), which reaffirmed the academic tradition, these problems did not come to an énd. The Bauhaus was now faced with the vital problem of which of the two rival institutions would become the legal successor to the former Grand-Ducal school, with all the rights deriving from such succession."18 This question was ultimately decided in favor of the Bauhaus.

In addition to the tensions between the old, academic teaching staff and the new Bauhaus teachers recruited from the artistic avant-garde of the day, there was in the early phase of the school another “structurally conditioned locus of con: flicts"19 that resulted from the position or hierarchy of the institute as a result of the dual directorships of the workshops. Every workshop was led by two directors, an artist and an artisan, or— to use the Bauhaus terminology—a “master of form" and a “master of craft." Thus, in the early phase, in which the responsibilities were frequently not clearly defined and individual teachers were responsible for more than one workshop at a time, the following artists acted as masters of form:20

Feininger:Printing workshop

Marcks: Ceramics workshop (located outside of the main Bauhaus in the town of Dornburg an der Saale)

Itten: Stone sculpture workshop until 1922 (1921–22, together with Schlemmer) Metal workshop until 1923 (from 1921 on, together with Schlemmer)

Wall painting workshop until 1922 (1921–22, alternating with Schlemmer) Glass painting workshop until 1922 Cabinetmaking workshop until 1921 Weaving workshop 1921

Schreyer: Stage workshop 1921–23

Schlemmer: Stone sculpture workshop 1921–23 (1921–22, shared with Itten) Wood carving workshop 1922–23 Metal workshop 1921–23 (together with Itten) Wall painting workshop 1921–22 (alternating with Itten) Stage workshop 1923–29

Muche: Weaving workshop 1921–27 (from 1926 on, shared with Gunta Stölzl) Wood carving workshop 1920–21

![](images/16715bf32410414cc1d999ea8f75c00fff3858e19b6b5fddc54bed7e2d8a7b7b.jpg)  
18 Gerhard Marcks, Woman with Infant, 1919; wood 19 Lothar Schreyer, Figure of the Loved One from Crucifixion, 1920; woodcut with watercolor

ueliebte

![](images/e5d09340a45bb346292f58f3e4091820ffe8b07d85f6a7b2742fb87bbeafa9a8.jpg)

![](images/900e77cc992827b15f23c28c80626e376e47ffa38fe6fbf2290648ccb0bc1cb0.jpg)  
20 Georg Muche, The Table, 1923; oil on canvas

Klee: Bookbinding workshop 1921–22   
Glass painting workshop 1922–23   
Kandinsky: Wall painting workshop 1922–25   
Gropius: Cabinetmaking workshop 1922–25

The pairing of a master of form with a master of craft was intended in the early years to "help make up for the lack of individuals who combined artistic abilities with experience in the crafts."21 Nevertheless, this sensible arrangement provedin practice to be extremely problematic in terms of group psychology. Although Gropius's programmatic statements in the founding document of the Bauhaus amounted to a Songof Songs to the crafts, the masters of craft were, according to the principles, granted no decisionmaking powers in the social hierarchy of the Bauhaus. The final power lay with the director, who was the chairman of the so-called council of masters, an "advisory and decision-making body in all didactic and organizational questions relating to the institute."22 In the council of masters, only the masters of form held the right to vote; the masters of craft were given only an advisory function. This subordination of the masters of craft to the masters of form, which necessarily led to tensions, clearly shows that in the early phase—despite all the praise of the crafts that the rhetoric evoked, despite all of the programs aimed at demystifying the traditional image of the artist—priority was given to autonomously creative artists who were primarily focused on the province of the arts. Without a doubt, one factor in this was that near-Iy all of the masters of form active at the Bauhaus were artists of high standing and distinctive personality, whose artistic authority, as it were, created a distance between them and the masters of craft.

Despite all of the internal controversies at the Bauhaus during the founding phase, Johannes Itten contributed decisively to its stabilization by introducing elementary instruction, the so-called preliminary course, as a fixed element within the teachings of the Bauhaus, leading to some degree of formalization of the teaching system at the Bauhaus, which still remained somewhat indeterminate. The goal of this preliminary course, which was obligatory for everyone entering the Bauhaus and took on something of the character of a rite of initiation (one had to pass it to be accepted into the Bauhaus community), was not just to remove the slag of academic ideas about art and design and to develop free artistic personalities but also to pass on basic qualifications for design in the form of a “supraindividual design language"23 that could serve as the basis for understanding and communicating the self. (That in the end this "design language" differed significantly from one teacher to another and thus in no way equalized individual conceptions will be discussed in more detail in chapters 5 through 7.)

Despite the potential for conflict that was inherent in the structure of dual leadership of the workshops, the Bauhaus derived increasing stability from these very workshops, which Kröll has interpreted as the very “loci of stability."24 Building on the preliminary course, they provided training aimed at the acquisition of specific technical/artisanal and artistic/design abilities in the form of practical work with concrete tasks, some of which had the explicit character of projects.25 Here the private language of the masters of form with which they expressed themselves as painters or sculptors in their own artistic production, was transformed “into a language shared by the institution," a result of the increased "technological orientation of problem-solving attempts."26 Consequently, one contribution of the masters of craft, with their knowledge of materials and practical experience, was to keep the Bauhaus from lapsing back in its goal of facilitation between art and handicrafts and twisting the Bauhaus idea back into an elite avant-garde academy. Nevertheless, the Bauhaus in its early phase was far from being a training institute that would realize the idea of a Gesamtkunstwerk under the “aegis of architecture." Paradoxically, not only was a department of architecture lacking in the first years, which in keeping with the original idea ought to have formed the axis and hinge of the Bauhaus, but also there was no coordination among the individual workshops, so that “one can observe grave differences in the structure of productivity and conformity to the goal."27

The Bauhaus was subjected to one especially difficult test of strength in the conflict between Gropius and Itten, which lasted for some time and was only resolved with Itten's departure from the Bauhaus in 1923. Structurally, the conflict resulted from two fundamentally different, even diametrically opposed, philosophies of life, and—though it was certainly representative of the overall situation at the early Bauhaus, with its preponderance of fine artists—it found a very personal culmination in its two most distinguished figures, Gropius and Itten. It was the conflict between autonomous artistry and design intended to meet social obligations. Although Itten had contributed much to the stability of the institute by establishing the preliminary course, the subcultural/bohemian attitudes and the quasi-religious activities of the Itten group (Mazdaism) found little sympathy with Gropius. He viewedthese tendencies as a threat of sectarian return to the ivory tower of art, which meant a turn from the guiding principles of the Bauhaus. For Gropius's fundamental concern was to find a new place in society for the artists who had become homeless during the nineteenth century, a place that would enable them to participate in a socially constructive way in the design of reality. Moreover, for Gropius it was essential that the Bauhaus "be kept open to productive contacts with the industrial sphere."28

In 1922 Gropius formulated his standpoint in the conflict with Itten as follows:

Recently, Master Itten demanded from us a decision either to produce individual pieces of work in complete contrast to the economically oriented outside world or to seek contact with industry. It is here, in this method of formulating the question i believe, that the big unknown that needs to be solved is hidden. Let me at once clarify this: I seek unity in the fusion, not in the separation of these ways of life. . . .

The Bauhaus could become a haven for eccentrics if it were to lose contact with the work and working methods of the outside world. . . . I see a danger to our youth in the indications of a wild romanticism that derives from an understandable reaction against the predominating state of mind—numbers and force—and from the breakdown of countries. Some of our Bauhaus members subscribe to a kind of misunderstood “return to nature" doctrine of Rousseau's. . . .

How the broad gulf between the activity we practice in our workshops and the present level of crafts and industry outside will someday be closed, that is the unknown quantity. . .. Contact with industry and with the practical work of the world can only be established gradually. It is possible that the work in the Bauhaus workshops will lead more and more to the production of single prototypes [which will serve as guides to the craftsman and industry]. Students who have gone through the Bauhaus will be in a position, with the knowledge they have acquired there, to exert a decisive influence on existing craft [enterprises] and industrial works.29

Hans M. Wingler has noted that the emphasis on industrial production in this text is symptomatic of the first great change within the Bauhaus.3º It marks the return of the Bauhaus director to his original ideas from the prewar years, which were close to those of the Werkbund, and it is summarized in his formula “Art and technology, a new unity! Technology does not require art, but art very much needs technology."31 It is surely no coincidence that this phrase was contemporaneous with the return of the expressionist influence and increased entrée of constructivist ideas at the Bauhaus (and of course with the economic recov: ery of Germany with the end of inflation in 1923).

In 1922, with the appointment of Kandinsky and El Lissitzky's participation in the Dada-Constructivist Congress in Weimar, the Bauhaus came in contact with Russian constructivism. Moreover, in 1921–22, Theo van Doesburg, the theoretician of the Dutch constructivist group de Stijl, was in Weimar several times, where he gave private seminars on the "fundamental concepts of the new art of design" (to quote the title of volume six in the Bauhausbücher series). These seminars were attended above all by those members of the Bauhaus who were opposed to the circle around Itten and its expressionistic and spiritually informed orientation. Walter Dexel emphasized that “the fundamental change that took place there [at the Bauhaus] during 1922–23... would havé been inconceivable without Doesburg's influence."32 By sharply criticizing the expressionist tendencies and the production of individual works of art at the Bauhaus and by recalling the original intentions of an artistic and social synthesis, of which he could find no trace, van Doesburg's contribution to the clarification of the school's self-perception and the determination of a new course was by no means trivial. As Theo van Doesburg's mouthpiece, the de Stijl member Vilmos Huszár summarized this criticism as follows in 1922:

What the masters do, they teach. What do they achieve? Everyone does what whim proposes, far from any binding discipline. Where are there at least attempts at cooperative work . .. ; where is there a consolidation of several disciplines; where are there even attempts at a unified work of art, at unified design of space, form, color? Paintings, nothing but paintings and little sketches, graphic work, and individual sculptures. What Feininger is exhibiting was done better in France ten years ago (cubism of 1912). His latest watercolors are a diluted rehash of Klee! Klee himself scribbles morbid dreams of the sort that Prinzhorn's paintings by the mentally ill provide just as beautifully, or even more so. Kandinsky constantly repeats hisdaubed decorations.... Arbitrary and whimsical! The works of G. Muche are still more arbitrary and whimsical, intellectual, without any intuitive power, angst-filleddottings. Itten's vacantly pompous smears aim only at superficial effects. Schlemmer's works are experiments of the sort we already know from other sculptors.... Director Gropius places an expressionist memorial at the cemetery in Weimar that, the result of a cheap literary inspiration, cannot even compete with Schlemmer's sculptures. To achieve the goals the Bauhaus has set in its program would require other masters, masters who know what it means to design a unified work of art and whose works have demonstrated their ability to achieve it.33

As unmeasured in judgment and exaggerated in diction as this critique was, it seems nonetheless to have had an effect on Gropius and the council of masters, as can be seen from the development at the Weimar Bauhaus from 1923 on. Admittedly, Doesburg's hope of being appointed to the Bauhaus by Gropius remained unanswered, because Gropius considered him too aggressive and dogmatic as a teacher.34 At the time, Feininger assessed the question of Doesburg's appointment to the Bauhaus as follows:

For most of them the unsentimental—if also completely uninspired—Doesburg is something of a pillar among all the heated and conflicting points of view, something definite and clear, which they can hold on to. . . . If Doesburg were a master at the Bauhaus, it would not harm the work in general, it could even be useful, for he would be a counterattraction to that rampant romanticism that is haunting us. OnlyIdon't suppose he would be capable of keeping within his own bounds but would, like Itten, soon want to assume command over the whole works.35

## The Phase of Consolidation (1923-1928)

The Gropius-Itten conflict ended, as mentioned, in 1923 with Itten's departure from the Bauhaus36 and the appointment of Moholy-Nagy. Since Gropius had already appointed Kandinsky in 1922, one can observe with Moholy's arrival at the Bauhaus in 1923 a "strengthening of loyalties"37 within the teaching staff and the end of the sectarian divisions of the early years. The institute was also stabilized internally in 1925 with the acceptance into the teaching staff of several junior masters who had been trained at the Bauhaus. Through this process of recruiting its own, by placing former Bauhaus students and apprentices in leading positions, the conformity and continuity of the work goals at the Bauhaus could be assured. The double qualification of the junior masters as artists and artisans also made it possible to abandon the—conflict-ridden—system of two directors in the workshops. The workshops were now filled with several junior masters:

Herbert Bayer: Printing workshop (focus on typography and advertisements, formerly Feininger)

Marcel Breuer: Furniture workshop (formerly cabinetmaking under Gropius)

Hinnerk Scheper: Wall painting workshop (formeriy Kandinsky)

Joost Schmidt: Plastic arts workshop (formerly sculpture under Itten or Itten and Schlemmer)

Gunta Stölzl: Textile department (formerly weaving; 1925–26 together with Muche; 1926–31 on her own)

This process of reorganization—the new names, which were intended to eliminate connotations of art, should also be seen in this context—was completed in 1927 with the establishment of a department of architecture, under the direction of Hannes Meyer.

It is obvious that the Bauhaus emerged stronger from this process of organizational revitalization, so that the years from 1923until the departure of Walter Gropius, László Moholy-Nagy, Herbert Bayer, and Marcel Breuer in 1928 are quite accurately characterized as a "phase of consolidation." The replacement of the masters of form with junior masters as directors in the workshops created a new problem, however: the connection between the art courses and the workshop activity. The older masters of form—overwhelmingly fine artists—now concentrated more on "elementary questions of the production of individual artworks," whereas "for the junior masters the basic artistic and technical problems of mass production occupied the foreground." Because of a lack of "institutionally provided links," both areas led increasingly independent lives, a development that would soon lead to the "marginalization of the painters" and ultimately to their “loss of function"38 in a production group operating under the guiding principle of the integration of art and technology. In 1926 GeorgMuche spoke out pointedly against the escalating industrialization of the Bauhaus, and in 1927 he left the institute. He was suspicious of the increasing tendency to “emphasize efficiency in an instrumentalized way"39 and to tailor the individual, artistic sphere to a general industrial orientation, because, as Muche put it, “Art and technology are not a new unity; they remain essentially different in their creative worth."40 It can be shown that the Bauhaus from 1923 on did indeed increasingly become a teaching institute and especially a production center focused on the design and production of prototypes for industry. (One reason behind this, by the way, was the director's idea of possibly making the institute independent of public funding or at least to improve the perpetually tight budgets.) The notion ofmechanical reproduction now became a universal guiding principle for action, with the result that the production of individual works of art was by and large banished to the sphere of the private studio. With the gradual elimination of the medievalizing romanticism of crafts and the expressionist cult of the unique object, a rigorous, sober functionalism began to develop, the beginnings of which can be seen in the legendary Bauhaus exhibition of 1923, the school's first large public presentation.

![](images/e82579ddcde6b6c66c67044173af6bb75d98defa40aacde45c5e1e7185895705.jpg)  
21 Walter Gropius (with Adolf Meyer), Plan for the Chicago Tribune Building, 1922

The outstanding contributions to this exhibition were—in addition to designs from the courses—an overview of international architecture, the "model house" (the House “Am Horn") by Georg Muche, and the wall designs by Joost Schmidt (ill. 215), Herbert Bayer (ill. 151, and especially Oskar Schlemmer (ill. 196) in the rooms of the Bauhaus.

In the architecture exhibition concepts were presented that were distinguished by a shared “tendency to achieve the highest form of homo: geneity, relevance, and clarity" that came "much closer to the spirit of our age than all 'Utopian' and 'Expressionistic' architecture."41 The sketches and models from the international avant-garde that were exhibited (Oud, Le Corbusier, Mies van der Rohe, and Gropius, among others) were early examples of the Neues Bauen42 (New Building) movement that was just forming at the time, which would soon lead to the so-called International Style. At the same time, they were symptomatic of the changes taking place at the Bauhaus at the time in moving from the expressionistic early phase to a clear functionalist orientation.

Although there was no architecture class during the Weimar period or even during the early years in Dessau, and despite the financial difficulties (inflation) of the postwar period, great efforts were made in the Bauhaus exhibition not to allow the principles of a new functional architecture to remain a mere theoretical postulate but to turn them into a reality, if only a modest one. The only contribution to a planned large Bauhaus housing colony was the“model" or “experimental" house (Haus am Horn), which was not unproblematic in its distribution of space, designed by the painter Georg Muche4³ and furnished by the Bauhaus workshops. Although the discrepancy between the rhetorical pathos of the founding manifesto and the single-family home realized here is unmistakable, one can nevertheless see the effort to achieve an architecturalperspective that aims at practicality (Sachlichkeit)andfunctionality. Equally evident is the effort (also in keeping with the founding program) to bring the individual genres of art, or sectors of the crafts, together in the construction, if not in an ideal unity then at least in a division of labor that contributes to the overall task. This could be seen in the earlier expressionist SommerfeldHouse that Gropius designed in Berlin-Dahlem (1920–21; ill. 213), the favorite pedagogical model at the Bauhaus for project methodology.

To complete this discussion of the Bauhaus exhibition, we should mention the wall reliefs and paintings produced for the occasion by Joost Schmidt and Herbert Bayer in the main building and by Oskar Schlemmer in the workshop building. They were impressive demonstrations of the search for unity called for in the program, which sought to abolish the autonomy of the individual work of art by synthesizing the artistic genres of architecture, sculpture, and painting44 (see "Stages of Artistic Development" in chapter 10 and “Biographical Notes" in chapter 11).

The Bauhaus exhibition of 1923, which contributed decisively toward clarifying the school's self-image and defining its goals, received generally favorable press in Germany and abroad. This fact and the declaration of solidarity with the Bauhaus made by the Werkbund, which was meeting in Weimar at the time, no doubt represented an intellectual reinforcement of its position.45 Financially and politically, however, the situation was extremely precarious. The constant criticism from right-wing parties and the negative attitude of the Social Democratic minister of finance Hartmann, who considered the school "a superfluous and unpromising institution"46 and was not prepared to support the Bauhaus by granting it credit, dramatically limited its ability to function.

![](images/5269401d1994a09ab04b01f7d3a1ce268d5297874812b63d8ab5df47ad7b4962.jpg)  
22 Georg Muche, Model (reproduction) of the Haus am Horn, Weimar, 1923  
23 Oskar Schlemmer, Overall plan for the wall decorations in the workshop building of the Staatliches Bauhaus in Weimar, 1923; watercolor over pencil

![](images/e40a99960fb3fd3ad710257be0e1ff517939af65394876956fedd670b379a5e0.jpg)

![](images/6602cfcf67666783b8b8cf5aa223ddf24424e255782db8e694cb67c29b386992.jpg)  
24 Walter Gropius (with Carl Fieger and Ernst Neufert), Bauhaus building in Dessau, 1925–26, view from the southwest

The endof the Weimar Bauhaus soon followed when the elections for the state parliament in February 1924—after the army of the Reich had marchedinto Saxony andThuringia in 1923—ended with the right-wing parties victorious. The funds for the Bauhaus were cut so drastically that the Bauhaus masters declared the dissolutionof the institution on 31 March 1925.47

It was thanks to the initiative of the Social Democratic mayor of Dessau, Fritz Hesse, that the Bauhaus was able to find a new home in this expanding industrial city in 1925. This change of location, which could have jeopardized its existence and led to disintegration, proved on the contrary to contribute extraordinarily to stability and consolidation. Under the aegis of Hesse,48 who protected the Bauhaus "with diplomatic skill and exemplary courage"49 until 1932, when the National Socialists took power in Dessau, and now with considerable financial means, the Bauhaus was able to develop more steadily and systematically than had ever been possible in Weimar. Other factors in this were that the entire teaching staff, with the exception of Gerhard Marcks, moved to Dessau intact; that the staff was, as already mentioned, supplemented by a series of junior masters; and that the construction of a new school building, as well as the masters' houses, could be built according to plans by Gropius under optimal circumstances.

The shift away from the expressionist founding phase, already visible in Weimar, was in abundant evidence in Dessau and can be seen most clearly in the workshops, the main sphere of activity for the junior masters.50 Cabinetmaking—renamed the furniture workshop in 1925—was one of the most successful workshops, along with the metal workshop. During the Weimar period it had produced, under Gropius's direction, furniture that was characterized by a severely cubic tectonics and revealed in part the influence of the de Stijl group, especially that of Rietveld (though without Rietveld's constructivist playfulness, which sometimes neglected the function and made the furniture look like autonomous art). Marcel Breuer (1902–81), who had come to the Bauhaus as a student in 1920 and had already focused on problems of the construction of seating furniture during his studies, took over the furniture workshop in 1925 as a junior master. By in-

25Bauhaus student with a Schlemmer mask on the steel-tube chair Wassily by Marcel Breuer, designed 1925   
Photo: Erich Consemüller

troducing steel-tube armchairs and chairs—Breuer Metal Furniture, as it was called—he achieved a breakthrough in functional furniture design that was suited to the possibilities of industrial mass production. The most striking feature of this furniture is the“reduction of the furniture's volume";51 its weight is minimal, its“cradle form increases mobility," and they are "without style"52 in the sense that they can be used in the most varied rooms, so they are multifunctional. In the first volume of the magazine bauhaus (1926), beneath the title“A Bauhaus Film: Five Years Long" there is a photo montage that demonstrates the stages of dematerialization of furniture using a series of chairs that Breuer designedbetween1921 and 1925. The final image in the series defines the utopian goal of sitting on an “elastic column of air."53 Under the direction of Josef Albers (1928–29) and Alfred Arndt (1929–32), the furniture workshop was made to conform more to the programmatic goals of the second Bauhaus director, Hannes Meyer, in that it gave particular attention to the development andproduction of practical furniture that was affordable to a broader section of the public.

The metalworkshop—which in1929 was combined with the furniture workshop to form the workshop for interior furnishings (as part of an increased emphasis on architecture)—was led by László Moholy-Nagy (1895–1946) as master of form from 1923 to 1928. Under his direction (until 1925 with the assistance of Christian Dell as the master of craft), the workshop made the step from aestheticizinghandicrafts to"design that answered the new call for functionality. The forms

![](images/5a7a8bfae9b3dab11065385797ae72f2ba1f9376211f23a0c57058d2bb317959.jpg)

26 Marcel Breuer, A Bauhaus Film: Five Years Long, circa 1926, from: bauhaus, no. 1 (1926)   
27 Carl Jakob Jucker and Wilhelm Wagenfeld, Electric table lamp, 1923–24   
28 Bauhaus utilitarian fabric (in collaboration with the company Polytex)

of objects used in everyday life . . . were rethought with their use in mind. The simple ball lamps, of the sort used in kitchens, for example, and the office desk lamps in common use today have their origin in the metal workshop of the Bauhaus."54

![](images/a0107c6ac5487121bde7cb5e6ee90243a5d87ecfa33c93618cdf4869dfc7d638.jpg)

![](images/764e79cb0e6753f310acb6e134f5fe0c6a7b18ee5e0fbc530d700133f6a7c04b.jpg)

The textile department, led by Gunta Stölzl (1897–1983)also recognizedduring the Weimar period that design should conform to the demands of automatedmassproduction. Whereas in the weaving workshop in Weimar the focus had been unique objects produced according to craft methods, often folk-inspired but also in a strictly constructive language of form, the workshop in Dessau began to explore new materials (e.g., synthetic fibers) and production methods. A desire to meet the needs of the masses while keeping to quality materials and high aesthetic standards played a significant role in this change. Gunta Stölzl remarked on this process of transformation in bauhaus in 1931:

![](images/0c7b823cc6ef0293192cc48063b6657c2395111a48bb81aca87a7fc55cc3e279.jpg)

![](images/1df63edd2da7f7ade4fd3f8126adf2fde4a20a1e6363b0de11b41b48a0c0a10c.jpg)

We had a very different idea about the home in 1922–1923 than we have today. Our textiles were still permitted then to be poems heavy with ideas, flowery embellishment, and individual experience. . . . Gradually there was a shift. We noticed how pretentious these independent, single pieces were. ... The richness of color and form began to look much too autocratic to us, it did not integrate, it did not subordinate itself to the home. We made an effort to become simpler, to discipline our means and to achieve a greater unity between material and function. This way we came to produce textiles sold by the yard, which were clearly capable of serving the needs of a room and the problems of the home. The slogan of this new era: "prototypes for the industry!"55

The workshop for wall painting, directed by Kandinsky from 1922 to 1925, was taken over by Hinnerk Scheper (1897–1957) in Dessau. In contrast to Kandinsky's interest in monumental abstract painting—which could hardly be realized within the context of the Neues Bauen of the twenties and thirties, with its tendencies toward dematerialization (glass and steel)--Scheper's focus was on problems of architectural and spatial design with color, and thus he became a representative of what would later be called "color dynamics." The most outstanding examples of this search for new possibilities of architectural design with color are the staircase for the school building of the Bauhaus, opened in 1926, and the masters' houses in Dessau. For example, in the masters' house that Kandinsky occupied the ceilings of several rooms were painted black—an unusually bold approach for the time—and the masters' houses for Feininger and Klee, which were reconstructed in the 1990s, reveal a palette that includes intense red and blue hues. Another result of the efforts to design space using color was the development of Bauhaus wallpapers (1930), usually monochrome with discreet patterns, which continued to be produced by Rasch & Co. even after World War Ill. Alongside experiments with the relationship between space and color, the work program of the workshop for wall painting also included practical experiments with materials and techniques in order to assure a technological means for architectural and spatial design with color.56

![](images/54c151b2ec7b89820441e41945cf170568ee06d9a4dd42d988ebc86c7716aa7d.jpg)  
29 Alfred Arndt, Proposals for the colors of the master houses in Dessau, 1925; shown from below in parallel perspective; undersides blue and red, balconies and window reveals red From: Offset 7 (1926)

The turn at the Bauhaus toward the solution of applied design problems, which resulted in a decline in fine arts activities and the marginalization of the painters, is especially evident in the workshop for the typography and advertising (formerly printing). It was led by Herbert Bayer (1900–1985) in Dessau until 1928 and then by Joost Schmidt (1893–1948). Experiments with typographical elements had already been made in Itten's circle, but in the early years at the Bauhaus the emphasis was on individual style and expressive form, which often led to significantly decreased legibility. As early as 1923, Moholy-Nagy reacted against this: "Typography is a tool of communication. It must be communication in its most intense form.... Legibility—communication must never be impaired by an a priori esthetic. Letters may never be forcedinto a preconceivedframework. .. ."57 Herbert Bayer logically put this thesis into practice, as the sans serif typefaces he designed show (Universal; no-outline shadow typeface). Similar attempts to rationalize typefaces and scripts are the basis for Josef Albers's stencil type and the typographic works of Joost Schmidt. Herbert Bayer set an example in the combination of typeand images. He considered himself a painter but in the late twenties he successfully applied the compositional principles of surrealism to appliedgraphics in a way that was both impressive and useful.58

The phase of consolidation found its end in 1927, when the architectural teachings found an institutional form in thelong-overdue architecture department, which Hannes Meyer (1889- 1954) took over as director.59 Until then budgetary difficulties had prevented the establishment of an architecture department (the site for experimental buildings, for which Gropius had applied in 1923, was not approved) so that the only option available to members of the Bauhaus was sporadic participation in Gropius's building projects, which gave few opportunities to address problems of architecture. Now, however, architectural theory was given the substance within the Bauhaus teaching canon that it deserved. Meyer's contribution was to create a systematic course of architecture on a scientific basis, which he stripped of all aestheticism that contradicted his socially determined conception of function. In retrospect, Meyer summarized his pedagogical effect in the architectural class as follows:

In our architecturalcourse we developeda "functional building," which, unlike the vulgar interpretation of the term, went beyond the "purely technical." By analyzing the state of society and carefully studying all biological factors, we hoped to deepen and enrich architecture, with special attention to psychological factors and the organization of life. We explored the living spaces of individual blue- and white-collar families in order better to standardize their living cells. Finally, we made an overall analysis of urban construction in the city of Dessau, which clearly showed the inadequacies and class nature of this "exemplary city." The workers' quarters lay, without exception, in the harmful industrialzones, andthe cultural establishments were concentrated in theresidential zones of the wealthy population.60

![](images/076dec5c9f169eaa420a66e138adc1b9a8c104e445c26affa517d2b754a374af.jpg)  
30 Herbert Bayer, Title page of bauhaus, no. 1 (1928)

Elsewhere he states the foliowing conception of architecture: "Architecture is no longer building art. Building has become a science. Architecture is building science. Building is not a matter of emotion but of knowledge. Building is thus not a matter of composition determined by emotion. Building is an act of superior organization."61

If we borrow from Kröll the designation "phase of consolidation" for the period between 1923 and 1928, we should add that another sign of the increasing stability of the Bauhaus during the early years at Dessau was the publication of the journal bauhaus (1926–31) and the book series

Bauhausbücher (14 volumes between 1925 and 1931).

To summarize: in the years 1923 to 1928 the school's focus on "goal-oriented task[s] that were (in part) determined by commissions from industry"62 grew stronger and became reality. "Standards, norms, serial production, mass production"—that is, everything that Muthesius and other representatives of the Werkbund had been calling for almost twenty years earlier—became the guiding principle. Under the slogan “Art and technology, a new unity," an "instrumentalist terminology"63 rolls through all of the formulations of goals for the Bauhaus. Both the artistic and the social synthesis remained the true goal as before. It consisted of a comprehensive

plan for transforming society that Gropius would later term “optical culture.". . . Optical culture is presented as a process and the results of a goal-oriented practice that always makes use of the most advanced technologies and technical means. Optical culture is supposed to penetrate into the design vacuum of a secularized, "conflicted community" that has lost the designs that give it unity.64

The Gropius era ended in the spring of 1928. Gropius, who had worn himself out with organizational activities and who remained the focus of conservative criticism even in Dessau, chose to work as a freelance architect in Berlin. With the departure of the man who had given meaning and direction to the Bauhaus for nine years, and the simultaneous departure of Herbert Bayer, Marcel Breuer, and László Moholy-Nagy (Georg Muche had, as noted, already left in 1927), the Bauhaus suffered a substantial loss that, from the perspective of group psychology, doubtless marks the beginning of a gradual process of disintegration.

## The Phase of Disintegration (1928-1933)

On 1 April 1928 the Swiss architect Hannes Meyer (1889–1954) became Gropius's successor. A committed socialist adverse to any form of aestheticism, he presented his views on the theme "Bauhaus and society" with all possible vigor:

Building and designing are one, and they are a social activity. As a "School for Design" the Dessau Bauhaus is not an artistic phenomenon but a social one. As designers, our activity is determined by society, and the circle of our tasks is fixed by society. Does not society in Germany today require thousands of schools, gardens, and houses for the people? Hundreds of thousands of apartments for the people? Millions of pieces of furniture for the people? . . . As designers we are the servants of this community of people. Our activity is to serve the people. . . . We do not see a Bauhaus style or a Bauhaus fashion. No fashionably flat surface ornament divided horizontally and vertically and filled up with neoplasticism. . . . We despise every form that prostitutes itself as a formula. Thus the ultimate goal of all work at the Bauhaus is to gather together all the forces that contribute to life in a harmonious design for our society. . . . Today through our art we long only for knowledge of a new objective order, fit for all, the manifesto and mediator of a collective society. . . . The new theory of the Bauhaus is an epistemological theory of existence. As a theory of design, it is the Song of Songs of harmony. As a social theory, it is a strategy to balance cooperative forces and individual forces within the living community of a people. . . .65

These programmatic sentences make it clear that under Meyer's direction the Bauhaus had broken once and for all with the original idea of a unified art school and that the idea of a production site for satisfying social needs was now dominant. The architecture department now became the heart of the Bauhaus: not in the integrating sense intended in the founding manifesto but as a largely autonomous special department that was separated from the other departments (with the exception of the workshop for interior furnishings). This resulted in yet another decrease in the influence of the painters and, as bourgeois critics have emphasized, a lost of spiritual quality. The establishment of "free painting classes," where no attempt was made to integrate them with other work at the Bauhaus, can surely be seen as a step backward with regard to Gropius's guiding principle of a new unity of art and technology. If we rub this history against the grain, however, and climb down from the high horse of idealistic fantasies, it is impossible to escape the conclusion that this "new unity" ultimately had to remain unfulfilled. Although the artists in the early phase of the Bauhaus did indeed have lasting effects on the sphere of applied design, even under Gropius these influences had begun to decline with the institute's increasingfocus on industry and the American spirit. By transforming the Bauhaus into a modern architectural academy with several associated design departments—one can hardly speak of an“art school of modernism" any longer—Hannes Meyer was merely following the logical consequencesof this development, in which the earlier connections between fine arts and applied design ceased to exist as institutions as well. Under these circumstances, the symptoms of disintegration necessarily followed. Schlemmer left the Bauhaus in1929,Klee in 1931, and Kandinsky became Hannes Meyer's main opponent. Nevertheless, it must be said that between the years 1928 and 1930 the Bauhaus worked with unsurpassed efficiency from the point of view of production and economy.

![](images/83dcdf43dc5873a1b9249040e04c7904bd8d17d1d843b7c9eaf76c1cd2212f7d.jpg)  
31 Walter Peterhans, Untitled (Still Life with Eggs), circa 1930

32 Telephone factory in Berlin-Steglitz, site of the Bauhaus from 1932 to 1933 Photo: Kurt Kranz  
![](images/40b5b61cfaa8e208a98047133ae74fe7de51bd0f3d1f74e098f23eb6f02c64ce.jpg)

In 1930, for political reasons, Hannes Meyer was replacedby Ludwig Mies van der Rohe (1886–1969) as director of the Bauhaus. Mies had already proven himself an outstanding architect of the time. Like Gropius, he had worked as an assistant to Peter Behrens, from 1908 to 1911. After the war he designedglass architecture in Berlin that pointed the way to the future, though they could not be realized (office building on Friedrichstraße, 1921; glass skyscraper, 1922). He became famous for directing the planning and construction of the Werkbund housing colony in Stuttgart, the famous Weißenhofsiedlung, which was built in 1927 with the participation of an international group of architects, and for the German Pavilion at the 1929 World's Fair in Barcelona, a prototype of modern architecture for exhibitions. Mies van der Rohe vigorously limited “Meyer's call for social efficiency with his unconditional concept of quality."66 Nevertheless, Mies remained true to the course set by Hannes Meyer in that, even under his leadership, the Bauhaus retained the features of an architectural academy with several courses for design, two classes for fine arts painting, and one class for photography. The last-named was established in 1929 and was led by Walter Peterhans (1897–1960).67 In contrast to the Meyer era, however, the "production by the workshops for industry was drastically reduced in favor of the teaching program"68 during the Mies van der Rohe era.

With the defeat of the Social Democrats in the local elections in Dessau in 1932, the Bauhaus had to find a new location yet again. Berlin-Steglitz was chosen, and there the work was continued as a private institute in an old factory building, with more limited space and materials and a dramatically reduced teaching staff. The following year, however, the National Socialists put an end once and for all to the Bauhaus, which it had defamed as cultural bolshevism and Communist: repression from the police, the SA, and the Gestapo forced the Bauhaus to close its doors on 20 July 1933.69

# 4 The Fundamentals of Bauhaùs Pedagogy: Premises, Parallels, Trends

The previous chapter demonstrated that the core of the program for the Bauhaus concentrated on two goals above all: aesthetic synthesis (the integration of all genres of art and branches of the crafts under the primacy of architecture) and social synthesis (the orientation of aesthetic production around the needs of a broad segment of the population and not exclusively around the demand of a tiny stratum of the socially andeconomically privileged).

The realization of both goals required modifications to education for artists and designers and thus new pedagogical conceptions. Before we go into the details, we need a brief digression to sketch the stations in the development of artists' training from the Middle Ages to the nineteenth century, in order to locate the Bauhaus and its pedagogy in the larger historical context. After that, one section will show that this "art school of modernism" should not be understood as a singular phenomenon, but rather as it is embedded within the overarching context of art school reform from 1900 to 1933. The reform of the art schools in postrevolutionary Russia, especially in the Higher Artistic-Technical Workshops (Vkhutemas) in Moscow, reveals strong parallels with the Bauhaus.

## Notes on the History of Artists' Training up to the End of the Nineteenth Century

In view of the theme of this book, it would go too far afield to trace the history of artists' training back to antiquity. In light of the deliberate allusion intended inGropius's choiceof the term "Bauhaus" and of the program of the Bauhaus we have sketched, it seems appropriate to start our brief retrospective with the masons' lodges (Bauhütten) of the Middle Ages.

The masons' lodge of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries was a cooperative organization of the artists and artisans engaged upon the building of a large church or cathedral under the artistic and administrative direction of persons appointed or approved by the body which had commissioned the building.'

The masons' lodges represented a tightly organized social system with a clearly defined hierarchy (master builder-master of other crafts—apprentices—assistants) and a precisely delineated distribution of tasks. It was typical of the lodges that

restrictions [were] laid upon the members' rights of intellectual property in their own work, and its unconditional subordination of the individual to the artistic requirements of the common task. The aim was to achieve a frictionless division and integration of the labor available.... This aim could only be attained where a real unity of spirit possessed those taking part, for only a willing subordination of personal wishes to the will of the architect and continuous and intimate contact between the artistic director and each of his fellow workers could enable the required leveling of individual differences to be achieved without impairing the artistic quality of the particular products.²

It is self-evident that a concept of collective aesthetic production of the sort characteristic of the medieval masons' lodges—and evoked anew in the manifesto for the founding of the Bauhaus—is an anachronism in the early twentieth century. Gropius recognized this relatively early, and from 1921–22 on he advocated discarding all the romanticism of salvation inspired by the Middle Ages.

The artists or artisans active in the masons' lodges gained the necessary qualifications during an apprenticeship with a master—specifically, by practicing on the spot, without any set canon of instruction, according to the age-old principle of imitatio (demonstrate and imitate).³ Thisprinciple of training continued to be legitimate even as, with the rise of the urban middle class, which from the fourteenth century on represented, alongside the clergy and nobility, a “regular market for works of art," painters and sculptors "emancipated themselves from the lodge and went into business on their own account."4 Like other artisans, painters and carvers began to organize in guilds. The guild orders, which acted as a sensitive social regulator, determined, among other things, the length of apprenticeship, the requirements for acceptance into the guild, and the total number of workers, but they still did not have a fixed canon of instruction. The training continued to be based entirely on actual practice and focused on the particular tasks to be fulfilled. Despite the rigid statutes of the guild orders, the artistic freedom in individual medieval workshops was significantly greater than in the masons' lodges, in which individuality was entirely subordinate to the collective. The guild rules strictly limited the masters' freedom of movement, and tradition-bound thinking permitted only limited room for aesthetic innovations, as the concept of the “artist as individual personality" did not yet apply. Nevertheless, the autonomous master of the late Middle Ages, accountable only to himself, already prefigured the modern type of the independent artist.⁵

With the emancipation of the individual from the intellectual and social obligations of the Middle Ages—in Italy at least from the period around 1400, in other European countries about a century later—began a development that led to the dissolution of the medieval unity of practice and teaching in artists' training. This process, which happened gradually and required that the social corset of the guild be relaxed (even in progressive Florence the workshop apprenticeship dominated throughout the fifteenth century), took the form of a gradual separation of artists' training from the sphere of the workshop and its transfer to teaching facilities, the academies, established specifically for this purpose.

The Platonic background of the idea of the academy cannot occupy us here. It is enough to recall that the academy was originally a school for philosophers founded by Plato in 385 B.c. and closed by Justinian in A.D. 529. Its true goal was to transmit a comprehensive intellectual education to prepare the youth for service to the (ideal)

state; the later academies—apart from certain exceptions like the Platonic Academy in Florence— were considerably more limited in their goals, focusing on subsections of society (e.g., art, music, mining, or architectural academies) and abandoning the ambition for more general training and education in response to pressures from professional specialization and increasing differentiation within society.

The history of the art academies can be traced back to the famous Medici Garden in Florence at the end of the fifteenth century. This garden, under the supervision of the bronze sculptor Giovanni di Bertoldo, not only held the antiquities collection of the Medici but was also a meeting place for the intellectual and cultural elite of the city. It was also a place for artists' training, where Bertoldo gave instruction to young talent (including Michelangelo). Around the middle of the sixteenth century, Giorgio Vasari, the biographer of Renaissance artists and to some degree the father of modern art history) spoke "of this instruction as a 'Scuola' or 'Accademia.' He must therefore have had the feeling that the teaching was done differently than in the workshops associated with the guilds."6

The true history of the art academy, however, begins only in the second half of the sixteenth century, when Vasari founded the Accademia del Disegno in 1561 or 1563.7 There, "theoretical auxiliary subjects, such as geometry, perspective and anatomy were to be taught in regular academic courses" to supplement the workshop apprenticeship, which continued to exist.8 With this came a priority on reality, which the Parisian master builder Jean Mignot had called for as early as 1398 in a report for the masons' lodge for the Milan Cathedral, “Ars sine scienta nihil est"9(Art without science is nothing), and which Leonardo had formulated as follows: “Those who enjoy practice without science are like sailors who travel without rudder or compass; they are never certain where the journey is headed. Practice must always be based on good theory."10

In the age of mannerism, the art academies were already “organized from top to bottom"11 with systematic teaching plans. This was especially true of the Accademia di San Luca, founded by Federigo Zuccari in Rome in 1593, whose teaching program, in contrast to the Accademia del Disegno, was supplemented by life drawing and courses in art theory. Both Vasari's and Zuccari's academies were, however, only short-term programs. The focus of artists' training was still the apprenticeship to a master, and artists continued to belong to a guild.

This changed only with the founding of the Parisian Académie de peintre et de sculpture in 1648.12 This academy, originally a free association of members of equal rights, began to be transformed in 1664, under the supervision of Colbert, the French minister of finance and trade, and Le Brun, the “premier peintre du roi," into a statecontrolled,official teachinginstrument that servedabsolutist politics as an “instrument of stategovernment with the special function of raising theprestige of the monarchs."13 That meant that the Académie predominately produced art that enhanced the monarch's status or otherwise conformed to the intentions of the court. At the same time, the Académie—which was, as it were, a state monopoly in the area of artists' training—became the normative authority in all aesthetic questions and thus an important social regulator, the seat of centralized control over an artist's future artistic or social success or failure, in that the Académie alone decided on admissions to competitions, the awarding of prizes, and so on. The teaching program of the Paris Académie was precisely specified. The student sketched first from drawings, then from plaster casts, and finally from a live model. Antiquity, Raphael, and Poussin were considered the models and requisites in all aesthetic matters. (We can mention only in passing that these norms were relaxed during the later controversy between the respective advocates of Poussin and Rubens.) The themes that could be addressed had a clear hierarchy: at the lowest level were still lifes and landscapes, followed by depictions of animals and people, and at the top the depiction ofhistorical, mythological and allegorical themes.14

In addition to its task of aiding the apotheosis of the ruler through art, the academies, both in France and in other absolutist states, were also subject to the economic (i.e., mercantilistic) goals of absolutism. Thus the Academy in Vienna was reorganized in 1725 “in recognition of art and to support trade," and in Dresden in 1763 it was said, "It does honor to the country to produce excellent artists, but it is no less useful to increase the demand that the overseas trade has for products." In Copenhagen, "around the same time, the all-powerful minister Struensee believed that those 'qui n’arrivent point à l’excellence' could be directed into the craft industry. Here, for the first time, we encounter the distinction between high and low art."15 This distinction, despite all of the reform efforts of the Bauhaus and more recent efforts to break down hierarchies, has survived into the present.16

In the wake of the French Revolution, the Académie in Paris was closed, initially, but it soon survived as the defender of a conservative and classicistic conception of art, a rigid institute in support of traditions and an enemy of progress against which modernizers like Eugène Delacroix, Gustave Courbet, the impressionists, and others would struggle for decades. In Germany, under the influence of romanticism, there were strong antiacademic currents, especially amongthe Nazarenes. The artists branded the academy as an "infirmary" or a "chicken coop." Johann Friedrich Overbeck, one of the outstanding Nazarenes, said that at the academies "every genuine feeling, every worthwhile thought... was suppressed."17 Despite the reform efforts in the early decades of the nineteenth century—in Düsseldorf Peter von Cornelius and especially Wilhelm von Schadow led the way—the academies remained, in general, organizations characterized by a remoteness from life and a hostility toward progress; they found themselves increasingly in the firing lines of criticism. The German impressionist Max Liebermann, for example, found that “In and of itself, 'academic' is not an insult. . . . But gradually . . . it has come to be the case that no self-respecting artist wants to be called an academic. .. . Now academic has come to mean 'antiquated.'"18

In the second half of the nineteenth century, decisive impetus for renewal came from the arts and crafts schools. They had originated from a recognition of the need to "improve the taste of artisans and thus bridge the gap between the trades and art [that had resulted from mechanization—R.W.]."19 Thus, the hoped-for aesthetic education for producers²º was based on a mercantilistic motive: namely, to elevate the crafts, hard hit economically by competition with machine production, to the level of arts and crafts, in order to regain a portion of the market with tasteful, high-quality work. Arnold Hauser has shown that this conception of the "ennobling of the craft to art" was born of a typically romantic way of thinking; and the political economist and sociologist Werner Sombart had, in Hauser's view, rightly raised,the objection to this "idealism" that the "mass of the artisans never attained a respectable level of artistic quality" and that craft and artisanship had always been distinct.²1

Worried by the rapid decline in the quality of artisanship and by the rise in badly designed, machine-produced mass articles, the German architect Gottfried Semper (1803–1879), in his essay "Wissenschaft, Industrie und Kunst" (Science, industry, and art) on the 1851 World's Fair in London, called expressly for a "general development of the people's taste."22 He proposed the establishment of collections (what would later become arts and crafts museums), whose exemplary exhibits promised, in his view, to make a decisive contribution toward educating the tastes of both producers and consumers. In keeping with Semper's conception of education, the rooms of these collections would have supplementary educational offerings on “art and industry" that would cover the areas of ceramics, textiles, wood, and stone as well as comparative architectural theory. In light of the program at the Bauhaus, it is instructive to note that Semper—like Gropius later— sought a "working together" of the first four of these educational topics "under the hegemony of architecture, "23 and that, in addition, he advocated “the promotion of workshop instruction [as] the only instruction beneficial to our future artistic conditions," while harshly criticizing traditional drawing schools. Only in this way would it be possible "to rejoin what a false theory earlier separated."24 According to Semper, the "separation of ideal and applied art"—as manifested in the institutional juxtaposition, or more precisely, superposition, of art academies and industrial schools—was "altogether inappropriate." Practical education in a workshop offered, through the "fraternal relationship of the master to his fellows and apprentices," an opportunity to "bring about the disappearance of the academies and industrial schools, at least in their present form."25 This anticipated the idea of the integrated art school that Gropius sought to realize through the Bauhaus.

Semper's recommendations led—following the establishment of the South Kensington Museum (today's Victoria & Albert Museum) in London—to the founding of a number of museums and schools for the arts and crafts on the continent during the 1860s and '70s. One pacesetter in this development was the Österreichische Museum für Kunst und Industrie (Austrian museum for art and industry), founded in Vienna in 1864, to which an arts and crafts school was added three years later. Both held—in some respects as an ensemble—an "authoritative position"26 throughout Europe for many years to come. The 1868 statutes for the founding of the Kunstgewerbeschule (Arts and crafts school) divided the faculty into three specialized schools for architecture, sculpture, and painting and drawing and a socalled preparatory school of two to three years. The latter was intended to provide qualifications for “more mundane forces" and thus was clearly quite different from,the preliminary instruction that Itten would introduce at the Bauhaus some fifty years later.27 Despite Ferstel's new building, it was not possible for the Vienna Arts and Crafts School “to run the teaching in the manner of a workshop [and] model the school according to the practical aspect," that is, "to develop further the aspects of the training that applied to art in general to a more specific sort that addresses the art industries."28 Later, this would begin to take place around the turn of the century, when the Secessionists Josef Hoffmann and Kolo Moser began to take over the Arts and Crafts School. In their double function as teachers at the school and artistic directors for the Wiener Werkstätte (Viennese workshops) founded in 1903, Hoffmann and Moser gave priority in their teachings to the principle of design work oriented around actual practice "rather than copying inherited customs,"29 That the Vienna Arts and Crafts School (like other reform schools of the period) was in many respects a “Bauhaus before the Bauhaus"30 can now be taken for granted. However, it is not just parallels in its program and conception that speak for such a claim. Gropius was very well informed about the situation in Vienna owing to first wife, Alma Mahler; and, of course, Johannes Itten should be mentioned as another informant, as he lived in the Austrian capital from 1916 to 1919, where he too had contact to the circle aroundAlma Mahler, before being called to the Bauhaus.

During the second half of the nineteenth century in Germany, southern Germany initially led the way in promoting education in the arts and crafts. Here we will mention only the founding of the Kunst- und Industrieschule (School of art and industry) in Offenbach in 1868, because it reveals especially saliently the changes in objectives with respect to traditional academies. In a speech at the opening, the architect was given priority as a teacher over the painter and sculptor, because the architect has "above all the right to work to elevate and support the arts and crafts," since “in superior periods of art architecture has always been the teacher of the other fine arts and of the crafts" (a view that is taken for granted in design education in Italy to this day). The instruction at this school should achieve “an interpenetration of the fine arts and applied utility in producing an object that satisfies a particular need," that is to say, a "fusion of the beautiful with the necessary."31 This view, which clearly has its intellectual origins in Semper, was formative throughout the history of the Bauhaus as well— despite otherwise different positions among the Bauhaus members.

## Bauhaus Pedagogy in the Context of Art School Reform
