Subjective aspects declined in significance in the teachings, and the ability to solve objective design problems—which, according to Gropius, should range "from the simplest household appliances to the finished dwelling" and be based on "research into the nature of objects"142 became the focus.

## Hannes Meyer

This development, which had already begun under Gropius, was cortinued, with more radical ideology, when Hannes Meyer took over the directorship of the Bauhaus in 1928. One external sign of this process of dissociation, having to do with the organization of the school, was the establishment, discussed above, of so-called free painting classes—-separated from the actually coursework and on the basis of the voluntary participation of interested students——in which it was still possible to cultivate individuality as a designer. Everything else, by contrast, in the form instruction and especially in the areas of architecture and design, was dominated by teaching"objective" solutions to form. Thus the dream of the "homo totus" was all but over, less than a decade after the founding of the Bauhaus. Only Oskar Schlemmer made another attempt, in his ambitious, wide-ranging class “The Human Being" (see “The Human Being as an Object of Study" in chapter 10) in 1928–29, to work toward the totality that had been evoked often enough in Bauhaus programs. But it was in vain, for functionalist thinking and instrumentalist goals increasingly gained the upper hand under Hannes Meyer.

In comparison with Walter Gropius's more complex pedagogical thinking, which built on the maxims of the Werkbund, categories from utopian socialism, ideas from the debate over art school reform, Grunow's theory of harmonization, andneohumanist ideals of education, Hannes Meyer's ideas on questions of education were rather modest. In any case, they were not permeated with reflections on pedagogy in general as Gropius's were; even the educational euphoria borne by the spirit of utopia represented by Gropius during the founding phase of the Bauhaus is not found in Meyer. Admittedly, the historical situation of 1928 provided no opportunity for this. Meyer's pedagogical thoughts and deeds were, however, imbedded in an all-embracing, socially motivated general concept that sought to improve the living conditions of the wider public, especially the working classes.

40 Hannes Meyer, circa 1930

![](images/4b8375c15ee3da84d3c02027910b6c7dad0ad8782d1e577c07cdff370fa3ee56.jpg)

Hannes Meyer is a tragic figure to the extent that bourgeois critics of the Bauhaus have long tended either to neglect him or make him the victim of a very one-sided history. Walter Gropius himself laid the cornerstone for this sort of treatment with the New York exhibition Bauhaus, 1919–1928 that he initiated after emigrating to the United States in 1938, by choosing the ending date of 1928, the year he left the Bauhaus, which gave the impression, deliberately or not, that Gropius and the Bauhaus were synonymous and there was never a Bauhaus after him. And in his groundbreakingcollection of source material, Hans Maria Wingler never included anything that could have corrected the clichés about Meyer that were current at the time—that is to say, he never tried to refine the common image of Hannes Meyer as a supposedly aggressive, dogmatic person, an “irrational rationalist," nor did he try to correct to view that Meyer's Marxist convictions and his commitment to society and politics made him the "gravedigger" of the Bauhaus.

To counteract such negative judgments, it seems appropriate to show that Hannes Meyers in no way severed the continuity of development at the Bauhaus, as is often claimed, but rather he continued the line that Gropius himselfhad begun since the midtwenties: a gradual increase in emphasis on the sciences in the design process together with a focus on social needs in the work done at the Bauhaus. Admittedly, he did not do so without different emphases on a number of points related to education at the Bauhaus that in some sense can be seen as a problematic reduction of the original Bauhaus idea or as a betrayal of Gropius's original idea of unity. However, the discussion above should have made clear that, as circumstances changed with time, Gropius himself would have found it difficult to uphold this conception of unity.

An outline, however brief, of several stations from Meyer's life143 may help provide a background against which his pedagogical convictions can be better evaluated. After completing his education as an architect in Basel between 1905 and 1909, Hannes Meyer worked from 1909 to 1912 in two architectural offices in Berlin and was an auditor at the School of Arts and Crafts and at the Landwirtschaftliche Hochschule (School of agriculture) in Berlin. At the time, he was already an advocate of the land reform and free land movement led by Damasch, among whose goals were a more socially just distribution of land and property and the reduction of land speculation. One of the pillars of his later architectural and pedagogical practices is thus already visible here, namely, his engagement on behalf of the socially disadvantaged.

This was followed in 1912-13 with trips through England, where Meyer studied problems of English urban development and especially the garden cities that were then still new. In his 1898 book Tomorrow Ebenezer Howard had advocated the garden city, surrounded by a wide greenbelt to be used for agriculture, as a reform model to counteract rural exodus and urban overpopulation. Like many of his contemporaries, Meyer saw this as a decisive means for overcoming the existential problems of the lower classes that had been provoked by industrialization. From 1916 to

1918 Meyer was active as a department head in the building offices of Krupp's in Essen, where he took part in the planning of the Margarethenhöhe housing colony.

From 1919 on he was involved in the design and realization of the first large-scale building project of his own: the Freidorf housing colony near Basel, a model housing colony for a workers' cooperative, which can be considered one expression of the architect's social engagement. Two reform concepts combined in this project: on the one hand, the idea of the garden city and, on the other, the idea of a cooperative, egalitarian social order. The formal design corresponded to the egalitarian lifestyle of the inhabitants: "The unified form of the apartment buildings, the similarity of form and color of the housing blocks and the harmony of the building elements.... Also an attempt at symbolism? More than that: a struggle for the truth."144 Though other aspects of these buildings were rather traditional, after establishing contacts with Le Corbusier and the Dutch de Stijl group in the midtwenties, Meyer joined the so-called Neues Bauen movement. His 1926 essay "Die neue Welt" (The new world) is a “hymn of praise to modernism"145 and reveals him to be an uncompromising functionalist, for whom architecture is not primarily an aesthetic process but rather a technical one, as the following brief citation shows: "Architecture as a continuation of tradition and as a search for effects has ceased to exist. The individual form and the building body, the color of the material and the structure of the surfaces arise automatically, and this functional view of building . .. leads to pure construction. Pure construction is the characteristic of the new world of forms."146 This somewhat mistaken and misleading view—that form results as a logical consequence of the function to be fulfilled and that technical construction is ultimately the formal equivalent of these functions—was one that Meyer repeated forcefully, although a glance at his competition entries for the Peters School in Basel (1926) and the Palace of Nations in Geneva (1927) as well as the Federal Union School in Bernau near Berlin (1928-30) and the summer homes in Dessau-Törten (1928–30)147 shows that these plans and realized buildings can in no way be reduced to functionally determined forms of construction but reveal a specific "will to design" (to paraphrase Riegl's term "will to art"). Nevertheless, Meyer clung fast to his favorite notion of constructive form, as can be seen from his commentary on his design for the Palace of Nations building: "This building is not beautiful and not ugly. It wants to be evaluated as a constructive invention"148 [emphasis in original]. And even before Meyer was appointed to the Bauhaus, he let Gropius know that the basic direction he would see for instruction in the department of architecture would be “absolutely a functional, collective, constructive" one149 (see “An Overview of the Goals and Organizational Structure of the Teachings at the Bauhaus" in the present chapter).

Gropius had taken the occasion of the opening of the new Bauhaus building in Dessau in December 1926 to ask Hannes Meyer whether he would be interested in taking over the direction of the department of architecture that was going to be established, which, according to the programs of the Bauhaus from the beginning, would be the goal and focus of this new school for design (see “The Phase of Consolidation" in chapter 3). After considerable hesitation, Meyer accepted the position in the spring of 1927, not least because, by his own admission, as a decided “collectivist" he was attracted not only by the prospect of “dealing with the vocational education of students of architecture" but also by "collaboration within a working collective"150—the latter was, one could say, a common idea at the Bauhaus.

Hannes Meyer agreed with Gropius that a “proper education in architectural design [could] only be done in direct association with the practice of architecture itself." Otherwise, as Meyer put it, “nothingwould distinguish this new department of architecture from that of some technical university—i.e., it would be superfluous."151 As has been shown in the previous chapter and the present one, the clear orientation toward practice in the architecture program and—after Meyer took over the directorship in 1928—teaching at the Bauhaus in general in no way represented the rejection of theory; on the contrary, there was a definite increase of theory in the Bauhaus curriculum. For Meyer, it was neither aesthetic considerations nor Gropius's somewhat ominous-sounding “research into essence" that would form the basis for the process of planning and designing, but rather analyses that were as scientifically precise as possible. Consequently, disciplines like psychology, sociology, engineering, and political economy were given greater space on the teaching plan. We should recall the lectures of the Leipzig psychologist Count Karlfried von Dürckheim and the Marxist sociologist Hermann Duncker, as well as the classes by the civil engineer A/car Rude/t, which were intended to contribute to the scientific basis of education at the Bauhaus and in the architecture department in particular.

In all this, Meyer's overarching cultural and pedagogical perspective was “the needs of the people not the needs of luxury." In his view, then, the main task of the Bauhaus was not the reform of bourgeois design culture—which was, in the end, true of Gropius, who was still operating in the Werkbund tradition—but the service of the people, whose mass needs would become “the starting point andgoalof every design activity,"152 though by “the people" Meyer meant the large class of workers andlow-level employees who were dependent on wages. In the workers' movement in particular, in light of increasing class conflicts, Meyer saw a political and cultural alternative to the petit bourgeoisie and its focus on rights; the working class was, in his view——following the model of socialist Russia—the bearer of hope for a new, broad-based democratic culture of the future.

Initially, however, the goal was to improve the actual living circumstances of these people, to which endMeyer established a program at the Bauhaus that aimed to develop low-cost and functional "people's apartments" (Volkswohnungen). With this, the Bauhaus director and his institute were following a trend of the time, as parallel activities in other places make clear. It was certainly no coincidence that, in light of the approaching world economic crisis, the problem of the general housing shortage became the subject of the Second International Congress for Modern Architecture (Congrès international d'architecture moderne, CIAM II), held in Frankfurt am Main in 1929 under the motto "Die Wohnung für das Existenzminimum" (The dwelling for the subsistence minimum). Nor is it a coincidence that new large housing colonies were being built from the midtwenties on—for example, by Bruno Taut in Berlin and under the direction of Ernst May in Frankfurt (“The New Frankfurt")—to address mass poverty. Against this background, Hannes Meyer's concerns for instruction at the Bauhaus were no longer aesthetic experiment but the fulfillment of a social mission:

[not]individuallyproducedfurniture for some snob with “modern" tastes. Rather, standardized furniture for the use of the people, the product of modern mass production, the product of a study of the people's habits, social standardization,physiologicalandpsychological functions, the result of norms in the production process and the most careful economic calculation. .. . Whereas the metal workshop was previously occupied with silver jewelry and formalist lamps, now it is contractedto sendevery month, or even every week, one coworker to Körting& Mathiesen in Leipzig, Germany's largest manufacturer of lamps, where he inspects the entire production of this manufacturer for export and brings new models of lamps from the workshop to be mass-produced. Inven\~ tive students have caused the static forms of the metal chair to fold, turn, and fan out, in order to achieve greater biological utility. As a result, we designedwork stools for the kitchen, work chairs for laborers, folding chairs for public halls—all in forms suited to mass production.

In the cabinetmaking workshop it was easier to realize this new approach, because that workshophadalwaysrespectedstandards and norms. There, the challenge was to move from the standard of the bourgeoisie to that of the masses. “Plug furniture" wasdeveloped, in which the individual elements could be easily plugged together. The life of the masses had become more "mobile," the struggle for existence greater, so that it was necessary to move more often. Consequently, weconstructed“lightweight furniture" of the thinnest types of wood, so that it did not pay to transport them, and they could be abandoned as worthless. Relatively large deliveries of furniture, for entire apartment units or even buildings, were produced according to the idea of being able to combine all the individual pieces of furniture by using standard dimensions. This gives the user the ability to arrange (alter) the entire ensemble of furniture to suit any purpose.

Finally, in order to provide for mass production of the models designed in the cabinetmaking workshop, we contracted to cooperate with thecabinetmakers' union in Bremen, and its products have gone on the market as “Bauhaus Furniture."153

Both in the department of architecture and in the closely related workshop for interior furnishings (the result of the combination in 1929 of the metal workshop, the cabinetmaking workshop, and the workshop for mural painting; direction: AlfredArndt), the pedagogical maxim under Hannes Meyer was "Study through practical work."154 This meant that ideally it should not be a question of solving invented design tasks but rather of collaborating in real projects, not about dry exercises in the pedagogical laboratory but about serious examples. This was also true of the two remaining divisions of the Bauhaus (after the stage workshop was closed in 1929): namely,

41 Folding chair, collaborative project at the Dessau Bauhaus under Hannes Meyer, circa 1929

![](images/80efd485383fd03d990f30462286a7a1f30ca69c70ff4832ec4c3c9dbcf454e8.jpg)

the textile division (formerly weaving) and the advertising department, which was consolidated with the photographic department that had been established in 1929 and was led by Walter Peterhans. (The new organizational structure of the Bauhaus, including the school's external relationships, is shown in diagram below) It is clear that the nature of the school changed dramatically under Hannes Meyer. Whereas the workshops under Gropius were primarily teaching workshops, from 1928 to 1930 they were increasingly transformedinto productive workshops. In this context, Meyer himself coined the term “productive pedagogy"; the director of the textile department, Gunta Stölzl, spoke of “pedagogy built on production."155

Already with the establishment of the architecture department in 1927, the original structure of teaching at the Bauhaus underwent a decisive change, by putting the teachings on architecture on the same level as other Bauhaus workshops. This meant that the hierarchical division into preliminary instruction—main instruction (with instruction in. form and practical instruction)—architectural instruction was abandoned, and the students could begin studying architecture immediately on completing the preliminary course, without first having completed the crafts instruction that was formerly called for in the program. They did, however, have to complete two workshop semesters in a Bauhaus workshop of their choice—as a kind of abbreviated version of the former practical instruction—in order to acquire the manual skills that were still considered essential.156 At the same time, the artistic components at the Bauhaus were increasingly reduced, so that in a letter of 26 March 1930 Josef Albers complained not only of the expansion of theoretical teachings of a materialist bent but also of the “elimination of the painters."157

In summary: Under the directorship of Hannes Meyer, the social goals of work at the Bauhaus clearly gained the upper hand; ideologies of individualism and educational philoso-

42 Hannes Meyer, Organizational chart of the Bauhaus and its relations to the outside, 1930

![](images/2bc975f6ea88ebc2d275b28c3ec6e9a1ef23bcf23c4d67b15611bc056af0eacd.jpg)

phies aimed at the whole human being lost their authority, and the creative potential of art as a way of encouraging innovation in design and architecture was cast into doubt. The result, from the point of view of group psychology, was a process ofdisintegration that was announced with Muche's departure from the Bauhaus, became quite clear with the marginalization of Klee andKandinsky in "free painting classes," and continued with Schlemmer's move to the Breslau Academy in 1929. (Although it took place only in 1931—i.e., after Meyer's dismissal from the Bauhaus—Klee's acceptance of a position at the academy in Düsseldorf shouldalso be viewed in this context, as Klee had already informed Meyer in May 1930 of his intention to leave the Bauhaus.)158 With that, the Bauhaus ceased to be a model for the "art school of modernism" that it had been in the beginning in 1919.

Nevertheless, the services that Hannes Meyer did for the Bauhaus and Bauhaus pedagogy are undeniable. Claude Schnaidt, the author of the first groundbreaking monograph on Meyer, praised him somewhat apologetically as follows:

The boldest innovation... that Meyer introduced at the Bauhaus was to place the methodology on a scientific basis. Numerous scientific and technical fields were included in the study plan, and Meyer succeeded in attracting German and foreign scholars of international reputation for the lectures. Meyer not only recognized the efficiency that came with concrete knowledge but also saw the educational consequences of a method that was intended to develop a reasoning ability that was both correct and modest. These were qualities that he contrasted to the indeterminacy of utopianism, the arbitrariness of aestheticism, and the arrogance of pseudorationalism. Whether pedagogical, organizational, or political, Meyer's actions corresponded fully and completely to the idea of the Bauhaus. In no way did they represent destruction but rather construction that was typical of its time and yet pointed toward the future.159

Despite the succesful work as an architect and educator that Hannes Meyer accomplished at the Bauhaus, he was dismissed as director without notice in the summer of 1930—for having tolerated, or even actively supported, a leftist politicization of the Bauhaus and for being untrustworthy in any case because he was a Marxist—by, ironically, Dessau's Social Democratic mayor, Fritz Hesse.

## Ludwig Mies van der Rohe

Meyer's successor, Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, had already been recommended by Gropius in 1928 to be the director of the Bauhaus but at that time he was rejected. Although Mies had been a member of the Novembergruppe (November group)—an organization founded in 1918 that was inclined to social revolution and called for a radical, new cultural beginning—and although the monument for Karl Liebknecht and Rosa Luxemburg he designed was not dedicated until 1926, he was no longer considered a leftist. This was because he was known as a designer of elegant steel tube furniture as well as of villas and country homes for the bourgeoisie (Wolf House, Guben, 1925- 27; Lange House and Esters House, Krefeld, 1927–30; Tugendhat Villa, Brno, 1929–30), the elegant Pavilion of the German Reich at the World's Fair in Barcelona (1929), and a block of apartment houses designed for bourgeois living at the Weißenhofsiedlung in Stuttgart (1927). In appointing Mies to the Bauhaus, "Hesse's strategy was apparent. Mies was a man neither of the right nor the left, nor even of the center; he was an artist. Thus he could be expcted to keep the political dogs at bay without compromising the progressive standards of the school."160

Whereas Hannes Meyer had placed great value on promoting democracy at the Bauhaus, Mies, as an “elitist jail warden,"161 made every effort to seize control of the school in authoritarian fashion. One of his first acts in office as newly appointed director was compulsory removal of students from the enrollment lists in order to readmit them to studies if they passed a test of attitudes in the form of “in-depth personal interviews"162 in which they were prepared to give up all political (meaning leftist) activities—all in the name of keeping the Bauhaus out of the crossfire of the political parties.

In a 1931 brochure—the "brochure with capital letters" (so called because, after years of writing without capital letters on principle, they reappeared here for the first time)—the explicitly apolitical character of the Bauhaus is emphasized: "The administration and teaching staff direct the institute in keeping with the statutes in full awareness of their pedagogical and cultural responsibility. They declare explicitly that the Bauhaus is run completely apolitically and purely from an objective point of view."163 The statutes of 1930 referred to here, and even more so the "addendum to the statutes"·that “contains the precise ’provisions for the students,'"164 represent an enormous “concentration of the director's powers of decision"165 with a corresponding decrease in the participation of the students and a rigorous tightening of disciplinary rules. The Bauhaus curriculum was also tightened up, both in terms of content (see “An Overview of the Goals and Organizational Structure of the Teachings at the Bauhaus" in the present chapter) and in terms of length of study. For example, the maximum length of study of nine semesters (in the architectural department) was reduced to six semesters. This action cannot be explained entirely with reference to the economic difficulties of the time but implies a pedagogical decision, namely, against the "generalized study"166 in Gropius's sense and in favor of specialized study. The preliminary course was no longer a required course. Given adequate prior training, it was possible to be accepted directly into a higher year.167 Last but not least, Meyer's productive workshops were transformed back into teaching workshops,

![](images/ed5144a6ce66398bdc2a3264ad6083904028121af4c659e809065cd238c0d29c.jpg)  
43 Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, 1933 Photo: Werner Rohde

44 Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, Steel-tube chair, 1927  
![](images/94e32679dff87467907462006d70f7ca5c274873f23cdc323c416be1f862354c.jpg)

![](images/b55ddd8d9078f752d2e7e95b4db08992d283ee4694d19493ff78a558dbaaf224.jpg)  
45 Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, Barcelona Pavilion; perspective drawing of the view from the street (preliminary design), 1928

which is evidence of the enormous trend toward schooling, as is the rule requiring proof of achievement in the form of independent practical work in order to advance to the next semester.

Unlike Gropius and Hannes Meyer, Mies did not formulate a pedagogical program, much less something like a philosophy of education. He was dismissive of the socially based functionalism of someone like Hannes Meyer and the educational maxims that derived from it, and he even made nobonesabout his“negative judgment of Gropius as an architect."168Mies can, without qualification, be classifiedas a classicist within the modern movement (his borrowings from Schinkel are evident), and for him reflections on questions of aesthetic education in the sense of a unified education were at least marginal if not totally irrelevant. In his view, the educational task of the Bauhaus consisted entirely in solid vocational training of future architects and designers of high quality. That meant that the Bauhaus under his direction became “something approaching a school of architecture,"169 which was expressed in, among other things, the increasing influence of the architect and city planner Ludwig Hilberseimer170 and the interior designer, exhibition designer, and new director of the textile department, Lilly Reich.171 Questions of form and their most aesthetically satisfying solution now stood at the center of teaching at the Bauhaus, as can be seen, for example, in the famous "courtyard house project" (design for a singlefamily dwelling with a walled interior courtyard) that Mies had his students work on as an assignment in extenso again and again. By unanimous agreement of contemporary witnesses, Mies, who was certainly impressive as a personality and enjoyed great respect as an architect, was anything but a good educator. Though the American Bauhaus student Howard Dearstyne remarked in 1931 that one could“learn a great deal" from him ("If he doesn't make good architects of us, he'll at least teach us to judge what good architecture is"),172 in his teaching methods he was "more inclined to advise a student to try again, rather than to help him with detailed critique or encouragement."173 Looking back, the Bauhaus member Hubert Hoffmann came to the following critical assessment:

Mies van der Rohe was the worst educator one could imagine, because—unlike, say, Gropius— he was not interested in what possibilities existed or could be awakened in the student but only whether and to what extent the student was in a position to think “Miesish" and to design within the limits of such a narrow framework! Mies could not express (in words) what he wanted. He would sketch on a sheet, shove it at the student, and say, "Try doing it like this." Mies's method was an extreme case of "academy." An academy built around a single master— that is, precisely the thing the Bauhaus was originally founded to overcome.174

It is only possible to form an image of the Bauhaus with a sufficiently differentiated methodological and didactic perspective by abandoning the level of the global analysis of overarching circumstances, parallel tendencies, and lines of development internal to the institute in order to focus on the level of detaiíed analysis— that is, by providing·depictions of the pedagogicalpractices of individual teachers at the Bauhaus. That will show, more clearly than has been possible so far, that Bauhaus pedagogy was anything but a massive, monolithic structure, a sel.fcontained and coherent system; it took as many forms as there were teachers at the Bauhaus. This in no way refers merely to nuances in the choice of methods and decisions about content but also to differences on points of principle such as conformity to the fundamental goals of the Bauhaus (from the synthesis of aesthetics and society to the education of the new human being for a new society) and the ability and the readiness to apply these things pedagogically.

The goal of the following seven chapters is not to provide an encyclopedic list of the pedagogical activities of all of the teachers at the Bauhaus—the primary masters of form and craft and the secondary teaching staff and guest lecturers—but rather to examine the course offerings that were central to the institute's strategy and that were fundamental for the way the members of the Bauhaus understood themselves and made themselves understood. This refers to the courses that were required of all students, either because they were prerequisites to special training in a workshop (the preliminary course) or because they were offered in parallel to the practical instruction as an instruction in form intended to provide greater depth. This precludes a systematic study of the training in the workshops,

46 Howard Dearstyne, Model of the Hofhaus project,   
1932–33; Mies van der Rohe's course

![](images/f06dcec0c963657a64acf1d81354dcb3c628c36b6072d548ec47166eaa3c28ab.jpg)

which were famously drawn increasingly into the vortex of commissions from industry (except during Mies van der Rohe's tenure as director), as it does of the architecturalinstruction or the content of the supplementary theoretical courses or the offerings of quest lecturers. Although a more precise analysis of the teachings in the workshops would be an interesting topic of research, aspects of the practical instruction are addressed in what follows only to the extent that the artist-teachers who offeredrequiredcourses also had teaching duties in the workshops.

As was stated, the obligatory coursework at the Bauhaus is the focus of the individual depictions that follow. We will begin with the preliminary course under Johannes Itten (1919–23), LászlóMoholy-Nagy (1923-28), and Josef Albers (1923–33). This is followed by an in-depth look at the more theoretically oriented required courses in design theory, intended as supplements or extensionsof thepreliminary course, taught by WassilyKandinsky(1922-33)andPaulKlee (1921–31)and the obligatory courses offered by Oskar Schlemmer (figure drawing and the course "The Human Being," 1927-29) and Joost Schmidt (lettering, basics of design, 1925–32). A systematic presentation of the metaphysical theory of harmonization based on harmonies of tone, color, and form taught by GertrudGrunow from 1921 to 1925 is not offered here, because its significance for pedagogy at the Bauhaus and the achievement of the goals of the Bauhaus as a school are difficult to assess (see the discussion in this chapter).

"What the masters do, they teach," Vilmos Huszár wrote in 1922 in his critique of the Bauhaus (see p. 38). As exaggerated as the overall critique was, this sentence nonetheless seems apt. For what we are subsuming here under the topic of Bauhaus pedagogy derived only in part from the general pedagogical debates of the day (from reform pedagogy, for example) but depended decisively on the relevant art theory or design practice of the particular artist-teacher. As a result, it will prove indispensable to discuss in comparative depth their ideas about art and their artistic activity itself in order to make an adequate assessment of their teachings at the Bauhaus.

The problem with this essay is that it was written (or at least published) retrospectively, ten years after Meyer was dismissed from the Bauhaus. Many details are certainly incorrect or misleading. For example, the assertion that the metal workshop was concerned with silver jewelry is not false, but in the Weimar phase the design of jewelry played a tiny role and in the Dessau period none at all. Although this essay clearly has the stamp of self-justification, it nevertheless helps to make clear the new emphases that Meyer had.

# 5 Johannes Itten (1888–1967)

"My Preliminary Course at the Bauhaus"

The preliminary course at the Bauhaus, in some sense the backbone of Bauhaus pedagogy, was introduced in October 1919—that is, one year after the institute had been founded——as an elective course at first and then, by resolution of the board on 20 September and 13 October 1920, as a required course. This action was made at the instigation of Johannes Itten, whom Gropius hadmet in Vi-

![](images/a6f3c81c59eef3fe74d42397fb52fea7d5b52ab2207640ae1ed76cb271dedcfa.jpg)  
47 Johannes Itten, 1920

enna through his wife, Alma Mahler-Gropius, and whom he invited to the Bauhaus soon thereafter.

the service of nonartistic goals (i.e., design), as will be seen in the section "Between Art and Design" in this chapter—establishing thepreliminary course at the Bauhaus was and is this artist's greatest pedagogical achievement. Reyner Banham comments that the preliminary course Itten founded achieved suchfame that “it has come to be regarded as the essence, even the entirety, of the Bauhaus Method"²a view that is, of course, untenable in those terms. Otto Stelzer characterized the

In his 1963 textbook on design and form theory, Mein Vorkurs am Bauhaus: Gestaltungs- und Formenlehre (My preliminary course at the Bauhaus: Theory of design andform; translated as Design andForm: The Basic Course at the Bauhaus in 1964), Johannes Itten wrote that the primary goal of the preliminary course had been to “provisionally accept for one semester all students who showed an interest in art. ... The name indicated neither a special syllabus nor a new teaching method."1 Without a doubt, Itten was, along with Gropius, the outstanding, dominant personality during the founding period of the Bauhaus. Because he not only led the preliminary course but was also responsible, as master of form, for several workshops, he had a great deal of power for a time, which enabled him to become the informal, secret director of the Bauhaus; particularly because Gropius was absorbed with organizational work and was also occupied with outside architectural commissions. Although Itten in no way concurred with Gropius's social and aesthetic goals—the statutes of January 1921, which were probably decisively influenced by Itten, nowhere mention Gropius's original conception of the unified work of art; Itten was also rather dismissive of the idea of placing art in importance of the preliminary course—and thus of Itten himself—for the Bauhaus as follows: "The entire Bauhaus teaching method is based on the idea of this course. Doubts about the importance of this institution within the Bauhaus itself coincided with the fall of the institute. But the idea of the course survived. It penetrated into art instruction at the secondary schools, art academies, and even the technical colleges."³ It should be added, moreover, that its effects continued to be felt in particular at the former appliedart schools (the Werkkunstschulen, today's institutes for design), as well as in the manual arts training programs at general education schools and in the design training at vocational schools (especially at technical schools and colleges). It is interesting not only to observe the tenacity of the preliminary course as an institution (reflected in names like preliminary class, preliminary teachings, preliminary semester, basic teachings, and so on) but also to register the fact that it is above all elements of the subject matter of Itten's preliminary course that appear—refracted to a greater or lesser degree—in the official curricula of general education or vocational schools and continue to survive in the so-called secret curricula, whereas the school teachings on art and design in Germany take into account the didactic principle of Albers only rarely and that of Moholy-Nagy scarcely at all. (One exception is Alfred Ehrhardt's

Gestaltungslehre [Design theory], published in 1932, which represents a virtual transplanting of Albers's "teachings of applied form," as well as elements of other Bauhaus teachings, such as those of Kandinsky, into the artists' training at general education schools; see the "Weimar Republic" section of chapter 12.)

The institution of the preliminary course as a step in artists' training was not an invention of the Bauhaus or of Johannes Itten in particular, but rather goes back, as we have seen, to the nineteenth century, and it was expressly encouraged in the early twentieth century by numerous art school reformers (see chapter 4, "Bauhaus Pedagogy in the Context of Art School Reform"). The reason that the preliminary course at the Bauhaus, in contrast to similar courses at other art or arts and crafts schools, achieved the legendary fame that it has today is, however, closely linked with the personality of Johannes Itten, who, thanks to his dual qualifications both as a trained teacher with progressive views on education and as an avant-garde artist, succeeded in giving the pedagogy at the Bauhaus an unmistakable profile during the founding years of the institution.

## Formative Influences

Born in 1888 in the Bernese Oberland in Switzerland, Johannes Itten attended the teachers' seminar in Bern from 1904 to 1908 to become a primary school teacher. Looking back, he would write:

When I began my career as aprimary school teacher in a Bernese village in 1908, I tried to avoid anything that coulddisturb the naive unself-consciousness of the children. Almost instinctively I recognized that any criticism or correction has an offensive and destructive effect on self-confidence, that praise and appreciation of work well done encourages personal growth.⁴

Another feature of his teaching method, which was highly unusual for its time, was that Itten never corrected the mistakes in his students workbooks, because he considered corrections to be an inappropriate incursion into their personal development, so instead he would discuss errors generally with the entire class. This principle of unconditional respect for the individuality of the student—a principle of reform pedagogy that he encountered at the teachers' seminar in Bern (on the influence of reform pedagogy, see "The Background of Itten's Pedagogy" in this chapter)—is one to which Itten remained true when later teaching at the Bauhaus and other art schools.

In October 1909 he began to study art at the Academy of Art in Geneva, but he was so disappointed by the academic teaching method, which was diametrically opposed to his own conceptions of pedagogy, that he returned to Bern one semester later for training as a secondary schoolteacher in the subjects of mathematics, physics, and chemistry from 1910 to 1912. After completing his teaching exams in 1912, under the influence of the art of the Blue Rider and cubism, which he had encountered on extended journeys through Germany, Holland, and France (including the Sonderbund exhibition in Cologne and a Kandinsky exhibition in Munich) he decided to

give up teaching and become a painter. I now believed I knew what I wanted to learn, and went back to Geneva. In a course run by Prof. Gilliard I learned something very important: he introduced me to the geometrical elements of form and their contrasts.⁵

Elsewhere Itten states more specifically that these "studies in contrasts using the elementary forms of the square, the circle, and the triangle represented an introduction into [his] nonobjective designs of the years 1915 to 1919."⁶ In light of his position on the question of whether art could be taught, which would later be discussed with some degree of controversy at the Bauhaus, it is interesting to sée a letter from Itten to Gilliard from 1914 in which he writes that he considers the Geneva professor's teaching method outstanding because it "offers something fundamental that every artist should know, because you [Gilliard] present the elements, the basis, for all artistic work."7

As Willy Rotzler has shown persuasively, the prehistory of Itten's preliminary course can be traced back one important step through Gilliard. Rotzler writes:

His teacher Eugène Gilliardset Itten on a trail that would later become important for the art teacher: the study of the means for design. From Gilliard, who was himself working to revitalize the teaching of drawing, Itten learned about the textbook on the fundamentals of design written by Eugène Grasset—which even today is not sufficiently appreciated. When this methodologically innovative textbook for composition in drawing is stripped of its obligatory flourishes, with its debt to the Art Nouveau design sensibility, it represents the first cornerstone of the theoretical framework on which Itten would later build his basic teachings on design, the preliminary course.8

This 1905 textbook by Eugène Grasset (1845–1917), Méthode de composition ornementale,9 represents a significant moment within the history of the French tradition of academic training in drawing in the late nineteenth and early twentieth century in that—in keeping with a trend of the time—it entirely abolished the "dessin géométrical" that was then part of the standard training for artists. From his fundamental thesis that "the laws of matter are those of geometry,"Grasset deduces that it is necessary "to study the rules that are dependent on geometry, because they contain the essence of all the others." He continues: “The return to the primitive sources of simple geometry ... is a certain guarantee of the soundness of our method. Every crystal, every flower, every leaf is a well-founded geometrical construction, and the law that forms these things is the same one on which we ourselves depend. Geometry is assuredly an absolute truth for our world."1º In Grasset's view, it is not necessary (in ornamentation) to invent new forms, but rather the new, according to his thesis, would emerge from the possibility of infinite variation of known reserves of forms: “To innovate is to preserve by modifying"11—a principle that was widely accepted at the Bauhaus as well.1² We cannot cite Grasset at length here; we need only mention that he constructed his theory of ornament on, among other things, the following basic geometrical forms: point, line, triangle, square, circle, arc, S-curve, spiral—that is, on elementary forms that would later become important in Itten's design theory (as well as in Klee's and Kandinsky's). The examples that Grasset gives in his book reveal a highly rationally controlled, deterministic design procedure, and a glance at Itten's early diaries shows studies in ornament that are very similar to Grasset's in their reduction to elements.

It is surely not wrong to view Grasset's book as the expression of a highly sculptural rationalism; Itten encountered another sculptural rationalist when he went to Stuttgart in 1913 to study at the Academy there with Adolf Hölzel (1853-1934). Itten was not, however, immediately accepted by the admissions committee, and so—because Hölzel, as a professor at the Academy, was not permitted to accept private students—he had to be content with studying with one of Hölzel's students, Ida Kerkovius, from whom he learned the basics of Hölzel's theory. Soon, however, the teacher-student relationship would be reversed when Ida Kerkovius, at the age of forty, took part in Itten's preliminary instruction at the Bauhaus. There is no question that Hölzel's theory had a decisive impact on Itten, both artistically as well as pedagogically. Itten took from Hölzel, this "moderate avantgardist"13whose abstract compositions made him a pioneer in twentieth-century art (who is still insufficiently appreciated), fundamental insights into the process of artistic creation, which he understood as the application of objective pictorial rules. We cannot treat Hölzel's theory in depth here, so we will have to be satisfied with a few hints with an eye toward Itten.

In the book Farben und Formen als lebendige Kräfte (Colors and forms as living forces), whose third part contains "impressions derived from Hölzel's teachings," Carry van Biema writes: "It was . . . Goethe who said that 'painting lacks a figured bass like that of music' and 'the way Kant wrote in The Critique of Pure Reason is how a capable person would have . . . to write a critique of the senses and human reason now. Hölzel's theory presents a kind of figured bass of painting and especially a critique of the sense of sight."14 In this view, the laws of design can be derived from the laws of vision. Even so, the application of these laws is no guarantee of high art, and it is never an end in itself but rather at most a preliminary stage for the freedom of artistic creativity. "The highest artworks, emotion and law combine and interpenetrate. The tolerance in Hölzel's attitude toward the laws of art can be seen in remarks like this one: 'The laws are there merely to work deliberately with them or deliberately against them; all that is necessary is to know them.' Goingeven further, he remarks, 'every work is in fact a deviation from the law.'"15 Of course, this is not acknowledged in his theory, where Hölzel asserts that the first task is to become familiar with the “eternal laws of art,"16 to appropriate them rationally, and consciously apply them, because “anyone who does not know the ABC's of art will always have to fake it in a painting."17 Thus it is only logical that Hölzel should attribute extraordinary significance to a knowledge of the elementary means of design: "I can turn it this way or that, however I wish, it is always the same: 'Art is in the means.' All we have to express something artistically are these means. And the spiritual in the work of art can only be produced with these means."18In Hölzel, Itten found confirmation for what he had already experienced .in Geneva; namely, that "simple forms"—by which he meant the triangle, the square, and the circle—are “the best and most certain basis for artistic exploration"19; moreover, that “the strength of great artistic personalities lies in the invention of new combinations and possibilities that can be traced back to the elements."20 The proximity of these ideas to Grasset—whose book Hölzel knew just as well as the theoretical writings of Leonardo, Dürer, John Burnet, Owen Jones, Delacroix, andothers—is obvious.

![](images/5e69a1b10fd237f453010ee9264d3957ed28b23b9a77dff10500a8b30e85b6f6.jpg)  
48 Johannes Itten, Light-dark divisions of the picture plane according to Hölzel, Diary, vol. 1 (1913–14)

The object of Hölzel's theory of design, which does not exist as a self-contained theoretical edifice but has been handed down only in aphoristic form and in the notes of individual students (including Itten), is to experiment with the picture plane and frame; with the elementary pictorial means of the point and the line (also and especially in relation to the picture plane); with the geometrical forms of the triangle, the square, and the circle; with the rules for proportion (especially the Golden Section); with fundamental principles of composition (horizontal, vertical, diagonal, circular); and with contrasts. The contrast of light and dark held the highest significance for Hölzel, and it was considered central by Itten as well. It is certainly not wrong to view Hölzel's theory—and consequently Itten's theory of design— as essentially a theory of contrasts. Just as in music “in counterpoint one sets 'punctus contra punctum'—that is, note against note—so it is in composing a painting with line and counterline, form and counterform, light and dark, color and countercolor, number and measure."21 The goal of artistic design, according to Adolf Hölzel, is harmony, understood as a balance of contrasts. This idea is based on, among other things, a polar way of thinking inspired by Goethe but also founded in the psychology of perception: "The more, and the more strongly, that opposites are concentrated in one place, the more the viewer's glance will be directed there."22 Again and again, we find allusions in Hölzel to laws of vision from which the laws of design and even harmony are derived. "In painting the eye is our dictator. What it takes to be good and pleasant becomes our law. For this reason alone, Hölzel's laws of harmony are not arbitrarily defined." The hand "must execute what the eye demands. The latter obeys the laws of vision. It is far more objective and impersonal than the hand."23 This overlooks, of course, the fact that vision (and thus design) is always a historical process that is determined by a series of cultural andsocial factors, so that Hölzel's—and consequently Itten's—efforts to formulate laws of vision and design of timeless andcrosscultural validity should be approached with caution.

Hölzel's significance for Itten can be stated concretely as follows:

(1) Itten's study of color theory "by Goethe, Bezold, Chevreul, and Hölzel as well as the color circles they developed";24 Itten concerned himself most especially with Hölzel's theory of color contrast, which in turn goes back to Goethe.

(2)The great influence of Hölzel's explorations of the problem of chiaroscuro as well as his analyses of old masters, which were intended to give students insight into the constructions and laws on which older works of art were based.

(3) The period between 1913 and 1916—that is, the very period Itten spent in Stuttgart—was for Hölzel himself a phase of intense experimentation. He made abstract collages of torn paper and shreds of fabric,25 which in turn motivated Itten to try similar experiments. He remarked on this in his diary in 1914: "On gluing. Using paper I paint (glue) a reality on a surface. In such a way that the distribution of masses corresponds to our sensibility. . . . To reproduce this glued object using the means available to the painter—i.e., paint—gives a higher reality, because it is something harmonious. Prof. Hölzel copies all of the details of the glued painting and achieves a vividness that is astonishing."26 As we will see, these sorts of montages of materials, as well as exercises in transforming them into drawings and/or paintings, would later play a large role in Itten's teachings.

(4) The gymnastic exercises that Hölzel regularly performed²7 seem to have been equally influential, and Itten introduced them at the Bauhaus in order to relax the students, develop their motor skills, and put them in the mood for the rhythmic studies that would follow. We should also mote that Hölzel encouraged automatic writing. Itten reports that once during a life-drawing session Hölzel had the students "'draw a nude with a single stroke the way Rodin did it.' That a professor at the academy at the time had his students draw based on a rhythmic automatism was unheard of, and his colleagues were outraged."28

This covers several of the important premises—though by no means all of them—of Itten's later preliminary training course at the Bauhaus.

At Hölzel's suggestion, Itten began giving private lessons while still in Stuttgart, then in 1916 he was invited by one of his female students to move to Vienna, where he opened a private school. There he had an opportunity to take what he had learned from Gilliard and Hölzel about the interplay of the teacher's own artistic activity and pedagogical practice and put it to the test, modify it, and expand on it:

Geometric and rhythmical forms, problems of proportion and of expressive pictorial composition were studied in depth. Tasks involving textures and the evolution of subjective forms were new. Besides the theory and practice of polar contrast the exercises in relaxation and concentration required of the students proved amazingly successful. I recognized creative automatism as one of the most important factors of artistic activity. My own work focused on geometrical-abstract subjects based on careful pictorial composition.29

The Vienna years represented a time of maturation for Itten as both an artist and a teacher; at the end of this period his pedagogical concept was developed and fixed to such an extent that he was able to transfer it seamlessly to the Bauhaus in 1919. Information about his time in Vienna is sparse. Itten himself remarked that he was "completely isolated" in Vienna, and he had no contact even with "other painters with similar aims," though he did have contact with the composers Arnold Schoenberg, Alban Berg, and Josef Matthias Hauer—allof whom were writing twelve-tone compositions—as well as with the architect Adolf Loos.30

His contact with Josef Matthias Hauer (1883– 1959) was especially important, as both Itten and Hauer were interested in exploring the relationships between music and color (a problem that also occupied Kandinsky extensively), and in doing so Hauer alluded to Itten's twelve-part “circle of expression."31 (Itten's own color theory, which was first published in 1961 under the title Kunst der Farbe: Subjektives Erleben und objektives Erkennen als Wege zur Kunst [translated that year as The Art of Color: The Subjective Experience and Objective Rationale of Color], is in turn full of musical analogies and allusions.)

Adolf Loos (1870–1933), the author of the famous essay “Ornament und Verbrechen" (Ornament and crime)—whose influence on the Bauhaus still needs to be examined more closely—not only organized an Itten exhibition in 1919 (which Itten claimed was the first exhibition of nonobjective art to be shown in the Austrian capital)³2 but also mentioned Itten by name in his “Richtlinien für ein Kunstamt" (Guidelines for a department of art), which was concerned with the reform of the arts in the wake of the collapse of the Austrian-Hungarian Empire. In the latter he calls for a new form of arts education: “training in drawing must serve merely as a means to an end. The end is the sharpening of the senses. The type established by the painter Itten shouldbe the basis."33 This is an indication of the high esteem Itten already enjoyed as an art teacher, at least within informed circles.

It is interesting that Itten's name appears in these guidelines but not that of Franz Cizek (1865–1946). The art course for children that Cizek had established in Vienna in 1897 as a private school (integrated in 1904 into the School for Arts and Crafts) had met with great response. “Do not teach, do not learn!Allow things to grow from their own roots!"34 and “deschooling of the school"35were the sorts of pedagogical maxims with which he attracted attention and gained a reputation in London in 1908, at Arts Education Day in Dresden in 1912, in Cologne in 1914, and elsewhere later. Cizek's teaching methods seemed new and revolutionary to many educators at the time, because he did not have students draw from dead objects in an academic and sterile manner but rather encouraged children to “paint spontaneously,"36 to make paper collages, and to experiment with a wide range of materials, but especially because he never intervened in the process, or even in the finished product, to make corrections. In addition to his art courses for children, Cizek also gave courses in “ornamental form theory"37 at the School of Arts and Crafts, in which he transferred these pedagogical principles to his work with “a more mature group of students."38 In addition to applied works like wallpaper designs, architectural models, andornamental relief sculptures, they also did free studies in rhythm and expression—all of this was an interesting amalgam of trends in cubist, expressionist, futurist, and constructivist forms. A review from 1921 remarked:

The formal and craft aspects are developed not from appearance but from inner experience; the student learns to design independently abstract feelings and indefinable sensations like those of colors, noises, and so on. What is at first—once it is roused—freely and unrestrictedly pursued as a, personal expression is then purified according to the pictorial laws of painting or sculpture and elevated to a planar or cubic form. In addition, the school's true concern is the painterly or sculptural formation of movements that have been experienced(kinetism)" (emphasis added).39

The reviewer believed the educational significance of Cizek's class to be that “it encourages self-sufficient experience and creativity; craft and form are not seen as something ossified, something transferable, but rather as something that has to be conquered anew each time and that recognizes that something new is valid for every age and for every individual."40

The question arises, What does all this have to do with Itten and his preliminary course at the Bauhaus? In the relevant literature it is frequently asserted that Itten's preliminary course was decisively based on what happened in Cizek's course in Vienna. Wingler, for example, says that "the nucleus of [ltten's] basic course, inspired by Cizek, consisted of studies on materials,"41 and Sibylle Zrost even goes as far as to say that Itten carried on “the ideas for arts pedagogy of his former teacher Professor Cizek."42 In fact, Itten was never Cizek's student, and“Itten himself expressly deniedhe hadever come in close contact with Cizek."43Itten's remark that Cizek's former students felt “at home"44in his ownprivate art school at most points to shared principles of educationbasedon reform pedagogy; however, to imagine on the basis of formal similarities between the tasks both teachers gave their students that Itten must have been dependent on Cizek seems extraordinarily audacious. Because all the works we know from Cizek's course that show certain formal correspondences to student works from Itten's teachings in Vienna and Weimar (rhythmic studies, montages of materials, and so on) appear to have begun in the years between 1920 and192445—that is to say, after Itten had left Vienna. For his part, Itten had exhibited samples of his students' work in Vienna in 1919, so hypothetically it is even possible that Cizek was somewhat dependent on Itten (which would explain why Loos mentions Itten but not Cizek in his guidelines). Without being able to answer these difficult questions definitively, we can nevertheless sum things up, against the usual reading, that understanding the principles of the preliminary course at the Bauhaus does not require that we see Cizek as a connecting link or transitional figure, especially if we take into account the defining experiences Itten took away from Hölzel and from his own independent artistic development—which went beyond Hölzel and was completely independent of Cizek—inthe years before he was called to the Bauhaus.46

![](images/07d4a1ce2d0322c18661750d08def0efb7b6d7ab93f1cf7b108e78fa706cd402.jpg)  
49 Erika Giovanna Klien, Material Painting, from Cizek's course in Vienna, 1924  
50 Heinz Reichenfelser, Study Sheets with “Ornamental Rhythms of Movement," from Cizek's course in Vienna, before 1922

![](images/0848cf7328bc35bd08dca43486eddb137883d302a6db71988f59e2f100846fd2.jpg)

## Early Work

Before we examine Itten's preliminary course at the Bauhaus more closely, we should look at the artist's early work in order to mark the horizon against which his artistic theory developed. The point is not to provide a seamless chronological depiction of the development of his oeuvre or to provide analyses of individual works but rather to summarize several of the basic pictorial problems explored in Itten's early work. This work oscillates—as in fact does his entire artistic oeuvre between objective and constructivist, nonobjective tendencies.

The objective paintings of the period before 1921, which recall Hodler to some extent, can be disregarded here. In 1913, when Itten came to Hölzel in Stuttgart, he had“in his artistic baggage . . . already some essential items along: first, his overcoming of Ferdinand Hodler's symbolic and expressionist art, then the decisive experience with early cubism which became the real initiation into painting for him and his colleagues [especially for another Hölzel student who later became an instructor at the Bauhaus, Oskar Schlemmer—R.W.],"47 as, for example, the painting House with Staircase (1912, CW 21) shows.48

Toward the end of his student days in Stuttgart he created his first purely geometrical and abstract compositions, whichare distinguished by their vivid palette from the gray and brown hues derived from analytical cubism that had previously dominated his work and represent his immediate reaction to the occupation with color theory that Hölzel had inspired (e.g., Horizontal-Vertical [1915, CW 97]; The Encounter [1916, CW 101]). In his essay “Fragmentarisches" (Fragmentary remarks), written in 1916 for the small publication Hölzel und sein Kreis (Hölzel and his circle), Itten noted:

The form most easily grasped and defined is geometrical, andits basic elements are the circle, the square, and the triangle. These form elements contain the seed of every possible form. Visible to those who can see; invisible to those who cannot.

Form is also color. Without color no form, without form no color. Form and color are one. The colors of the spectrum are the most easily grasped. They contain the seed of every possible color. Visible to those who can see, invisible to those who cannot. . . .

The geometrical forms and the colors of the spectrum are the simplest and most sensitive and thus the most powerful and delicate means to represent an expressive example of form.49

This occupation with the pictorial possibilities of basic geometrical forms and the colors of the spectrum is found continuously through Itten's lifework. It is particularly characteristic of the artist's early and late work, whereas his middle period is more concerned with landscapes and problems of style relating to objective painting.50

![](images/e93dd7a0fce87b91a11f063085c22adfce1080cfbe8311e725ef65e91005cc7e.jpg)  
51 Johannes Itten, The Encounter, 1916; oil on canvas

In parallel with his first purely abstract, rhythmically subdivided compositions (which were exhibitedby Herwarth Walden in the gallery Der Sturm in Berlin in 1916), and to some extent even later, Itten created a series of works that remained objective. They reveal a cubism expressive in color and form (insofar as such schematic characterizations are capable of saying anything at all), such as the Bach Singer of 1915 (CW 65), the Red Tower of 1918 (CW 121), or the Last Watch also of 1918 (CW 137). Willy Rotzler has expressly noted that this development occurred independently of Hölzel. Of the Bach Singer he remarked: "The compositional principles are original—namely, a richly contrastive, dialectical use of rectangular and circular forms with an emphatic rhythm; original, too, is the use of contrasting color effects, like simultaneous vibrations."51 Summing up, Rotzler characterizes the phase between 1915 and 1918 as follows:

![](images/3d265a57fab589942b10b552fd23aa44355531683cbf6b598f8ebad4675f8295.jpg)  
52 Johannes Itten, The Bach Singer (Helge Lindberg), 1915; oil on canvas  
53 Johannes Itten, Composition of Two Formal Themes, 1919

"The common feature is a rhythmical pictorial structure in which diagonals and arcs produce a dynamic whole. The color is . .. richly orchestrated and produces strong three-dimensional effects. The expressive, vitalistic character of all these paintings leads them to be called 'futurocubist.'... In Itten's work a rhythmical vibration permeates the prismatic, circular, spiral forms. Opposing movements of form are often juxtaposed contrapuntally."52

Clearly inspired by Hölzel—and years before the surrealists placed "psychological automatism" on their agenda—Itten gained insight into the meaning of "creative automatism" as one of the most significant "factors of artistic activity."53 This can be seen above all in the artist's early drawings, which, in contrast to the relatively stringently constructed studies in composition, have something of the character of psychograms, of spontaneous "jottings of impressions."54 In view of Itten's procedure in the preliminary course at the Bauhaus, this aspect seems especially worthy of mention.

![](images/688cf8e3ac0ed8c66c54cf36651ec138798d7e9042b5517ff72e7ab1d20b20d4.jpg)

Nearly all of the artist's works from 1915 to 1919-20—paintings, drawings, sculptures—share a strong dynamic, a distinct emphasis on movement. The artist was not, however, concerned with the futurist model of depicting a course of superficial motion but rather—much like Klee (see “Movement" in chapter 10)—with the pictorial manifestation of a principle of life and of design that was central for him. Thus he wrote in 1921:

Everything living reveals itself to us by means of movement. Everything living reveals itself in forms. So all form is movement and all movement is revealed in form. The forms are vessels of movement; and movements are the essence of form. . . .

Every point, every line, every plane, every shadow, every light, and every color is a form born of movement that in turn gives birth to movement. . . . If 1 want to experience a line, then I have to move my hand in a way corresponding to the line, or I must follow the line with my senses, that is to say, be moved spiritually. Finally, if i can imagine a line mentally—see it—then I am moved mentally.

These are just three different levels of being moved. IfI draw a line with my hand, then I am moved physically—that is the first, the physical level of being moved. If I move my senses along a line, then Iam movedin the second, the spiritual levelof being moved. When I imagine the line mentally, that is the third, the mental level of being moved. . . .

Before me stands a thistle. My motoric nerves sense a conflicted, jumpy movement. My senses of touch and sight perceive the sharp pointedness of the movement of its form, and my mind sees its essence. . . .

It requires no explanation to understand that the drawn form of the thistle can only be correct if the movement of my hand, of my eye, of my mind corresponds precisely to the acutely pointed, prickly, painfulformof the thistle. That means:

Character of motion equals character of form. This is a basic proposition of our entire exploration."55

Thus we findourselves at the heart of Johannes Itten's artistic philosophy and pedagogy: the connectionbetween movement andform conceived as an identity; the overall view of body, spirit, and mind; the emphasis on the emotionalover the intellectual—allof these things are fundamentalideas for thepreliminary course that Itten offered.

## Fundamental Pedagogical Intentions

Itten began his activity at the Bauhaus at a time when the traditional moral and social norms had lost their validity following the war and the postwar events, a time “of terrible discord,"56 a time of a general loss of orientation, in which the students at the Bauhaus were more inclined to discuss and debate, even to be active politically, than to work as artists or artisans, in which, as Schlemmer remarked, “the students apparently ha[d] little enthusiasm for practicalcraftsmanship."57 In this time of extreme difficulties Itten managed by means of a great deal of personal involvement, pedagogical skill, and strict discipline to motivate the newly arrivedstudents at the Bauhaus (including more than twenty of his Viennese students, who followed him to Weimar) to confront in useful ways problems of form and color but especially to confront themselves. (In February 1921 Schlemmer wrote that “the pillar of calm amidst the student chaos" were“the teachers," and that Itten was working to overcome this chaos "with dictatorship, exhibiting . . . ruthlessness and school masterly pedantry”;58 by mid-1922, however, he acknowledges him as “pedagogically... more skilled than the rest" and as having a “decided talent for leadership."59

By his own account, Itten set himself three tasks for the preliminary course:

(1) To liberate the creative forces and thereby the artistic talents of the students. Their own experiences and perceptions were to result in genuine work. Gradually, the students were to rid themselves of all the dead wood of convention andacquire the courage to create their own work.

(2) To make the students' choice of career easier. Here, exercises with materials and textures were a valuable aid. Each student quickly found the material with which he felt the closest affinity; it might have been wood, metal, glass, stone, clay, or textiles that inspired him most to create work. . ..

(3) To present the principles of creative composition to the students for their future careers as artists. The laws of form and color opened up to them the world of objectivity. As the work progressed it became possible for the subjective and objective problems of form and color to interact in many different ways.

. Iconsidereditessential, in teachingthe means of artistic representation, to evoke an individual response in students of various temperaments and talents. This was the only way to generate the creative atmosphere conducive to original work. The work was to be “genuine."60

In the citations from 1930 that follow, Itten's pedagogicalintentions are more clear than in the citations above:

From the very beginning, my teaching was not directed toward any particular fixed, external goal. The human being itself, as a creature capable of improvement and development, seemed to me to be the task of my pedagogical efforts. Developing the senses, increasing the ability to think and experience spiritually, relaxing and developing the bodily organs and functions—these are the means and the paths available to the teacher concerned about education.61

These sentences clearly reveal that Itten's ambitions for arts education were comprehensive, integral, and went beyond simple training to qualify students for a career in the arts. Consequently, the preliminary course, too, was not to be understood as a way of transmitting basic drawing abilities like that of the traditional training at the academies, where drawing was acquired in three stages: first, by copying drawings, then drawing plaster casts, and finally working with a live model. Instead, it was to be seen as a program that viewed the "entire human being" as a physical, spiritual, and intellectual whole—that is, in the sense of a "theory of totality," to borrow a term from Till Neu's analysis of Itten's pedagogy.62

## The Course

A reliable reconstruction of Itten's course at the Bauhaus is made more difficult by a series of problems, just two of which will be mentioned here:

(1) Although the book Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923, which Walter Gropius edited for the Bauhaus exhibition in the summer of 1923, contains numerous illustrations from Itten's course, his name is shamelessly underrepresented in this publication. Johannes Itten, amember of the teaching staff for many years and acritical force during the founding years of the institute, is mentioned in only one place, as the master responsible for "the teaching of form."63 The captions in the chapter on the teaching of form are limited to the general remark "From the preliminary course"64 (it is telling that only illustrations from works not made at the Bauhaus are characterized by the additional remark "from Itten's course in Vienna"), and the works that were created in the workshops under Itten's direction are not marked as such but appear under the names of other masters of form who were assigned to succeed Itten in the direction of the workshops (e.g., Schlemmer or Moholy-Nagy). The background of this sort of practice is no doubt the conflict between Gropius and Itten mentioned earlier65 (see “The Founding Phase" in chapter 3), which obviously led to the director of the Bauhaus putting some distance between himself and Itten, even publicly, in the first substantial and elaborate presentation of his institute. This was made that much easier, as Itten hadannounced his resignation already in 1922 and had left the Bauhaus at Easter 1923. There can be no question that this book publication obscured for many years the crucial contribution Itten made to the pedagogy of the Bauhaus.66

(2) Itten himself, in his book Mein Vorkurs am Bauhaus (1963), did not really contribute to an authentic reconstruction of what had happened in Weimar. For a significant portion of this book, he illustrated and commented on student works that were done from 1926 on at his private art school in Berlin and later at the Higher Technical School for the Textile Arts in Krefeld and the School for Arts and Crafts and the Technical School for Textiles in Zurich. This means that Mein Vorkurs am Bauhaus contains an abundance of material that is not genuinely derived from the Bauhaus and is really closer to Kandinsky's sense of form and other constructivist design concepts from the twenties than to the distinctly expressive tendencies (with some traces of dadaism) that clearly predominatedin Itten's teachings at the Bauhaus.67 In what follows, then, we will limit ourselves to examining the period up to the end of 1922 and bracket out all later developments, however much they might continue the same practices.

![](images/480ad28a8ad86dfd14d079c15927669826c0a79455dd8a9e96b0dd53b57430f4.jpg)  
54 Writing or drawing with both hands; course at the private Itten School in Berlin, 1930 55 Werner Graeff, Rhythm Study, 1920

As a rule, Itten began his course with gymnastics, in order to “enable the body to express itself, to experience things, to awaken these things in it. First it has to experience. For that reason, I needed gymnastic exercises, to experience, to feel, to unleash chaotic movements, to shake up the body. Only then did the harmonization exercises follow."68 These preliminary exercises, no doubt inspired by Hölzel, includedexercises for rhythmic form, which Paul Klee described, somewhat ironically, in 1921 as follows:

After he has made several rounds, he heads toward an easel that holds a drawing board with a layer of jotting paper. He grabs a piece of charcoal; his body collects itself, as if he were charging himself with energy, and suddenly breaks loose twice in succession. One sees the form of two energetic strokes, perpendicular and parallel // on the uppermost sheet of jotting paper, and the students are asked to copy it. . . . Then he gives orders in time, then he has them do the same exercise while standing. It seems to be intended as a kind of body massage, in order to school the machine in quasi-emotional functions. In the same way, new elementary forms, like and others are presented and then imitated. For example,  and, with repeated explanations of the why and of the form of expression.69

![](images/fa7d26e5a5ea85728f616f328e3c26156dc1e84b4ff9498ad54c2e15b046761b.jpg)

This seemingly restrictive method, which is reminiscent of the timed drawing and rapiddrawing practiced in the late nineteenth century (based on the American timed-writing method), should not be understood to mean that Itten was merely interestedin preparing the students in the sense of a giving them a skill (as was the case with the so-called dictation drawingtaught in schools). The intention was the reverse: the students were to act out with their motor skills in order to free themselves up, to relax, andalso to experience movement and rhythm, directly and physically, as theprimordialexistentialprincipleand as the fundamental principle ofpictorial organization. Oneusualfeatureof theseexerciseswasthat Itten had them write rhythmic forms withboth hands in order to train the coordination of motor skills in a unified way and to reduce the deficit of dexterity in the weaker hand.

There were also free improvisations, which immediately call to mind East Asian calligraphy, but in fact differ fundamentally from it in that they are spontaneous jottings of psychological states and not artificially fixed, codified symbols. It is striking how closely many of these rhythm studies, in which the "line [is] freed of the obligation to represent a thing, and instead itself functions as a thing,"70 resemble the pictorial forms of abstract expressionism that would come two or three decades later.

The real task for the semester consisted of a sequence of varied exercises in improvisation and construction, beginningwith two-dimensional studies and moving to three-dimensional essays in composition using diverse materials. According to Itten, everything perceptible is perceived through difference, and so a general theory of contrast formed the basis of the entire course: "The chiaroscuro (brightness-darkness) contrast, the material and texture studies, the theory of forms and colors, the rhythm and the expressive forms were discussed and demonstrated in terms of their contrast effect."71 Consequently, Itten would have his students in the preliminary course work through a number of contrasts, for example, "large-small, long-short, broad-narrow, thick-thin, black-white, much-little, straightcurved, pointed-blunt, horizontal-vertical, diag-

![](images/aed00641fcaef6924ad77759672409f1c55cb190ec2f912057caeba0001e5b0b.jpg)

56 Friedl Dicker, Study of Light and Dark with the Character of the Circle and the Square, 1919; charcoal, collage 57 Johannes Itten, sketches from his course showing contrast of light and dark (transition between black and white; light-dark chords); Diary, vol. 12 (1919)

![](images/fc5fa33198e753e84ad9e070ef78d59f3836190d1bde387eb9c77456800c53d1.jpg)

![](images/37ca1e2e3f38bf7ca8553685a3c508a93187169ce2994f815d020c05509d20d0.jpg)  
58 Willi Dieckmann, "Material study, combination of the simplest materials. Practice task: developing the sense of touch and the observation of subjective material feeling," 1922 From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923 59 Johannes Itten, On Materials; Diary, vol. 4 (1917)

![](images/9c59375ed8e34366d321c386bb9d1ebb7516117f99ddc013b7b56b50e569dfb8.jpg)

onal-circular, high-low, plane-line, plane-body, line-body, rough-smooth, hard-soft, still-moving, light-heavy, transparent-opaque, continuous-intermittent, and others."72

To these were added the seven color contrasts inspired by Hölzel and taken over from him: the contrast of color as such, light-dark contrast, hot-cold contrast, complementary contrast, simultaneous contrast, quality contrast, quantity contrast.73

Exercises in the light-dark contrast, one of the fundamental design methods andin Itten's view the most expressive one, took up a great deal of the course. Before “exercises to be solved in an intellectually constructive manner [were] set," Itten had the students work on themes that could“be mastered on the basis of free perception and imagination."74 In general, charcoal was used for this, as it was sensitive and well suited to produce chiaroscuro. Because the students generally found it difficult at first to recognize differences in light and dark with sufficient differentiation and to realize them in their drawings, Itten had scales of shades made that moved in equal gradations over the range from the lightest gray to the darkest black. These sorts of exercises weresupplementedwith ribbons of “light-dark chords” on equal planes or with varied proportions—tasks that then led to studies in which“surfacesofvaryingsize anddifferent tonal values" were to be distributed in a“balanced composition" that was usually strictly orthogonal.75
