In order to heighten the student's senses, both optically and haptically, Itten carried out studies with materials and textures in his course that were taken over, in modified form, by his successor László Moholy-Nagy. Itten remarked on these:

At the Bauhaus, I had long chromatic series of material samples made for the tactile assessment of the various textures. The students had to feel these sequences of textures with their fingertips and their eyes closed. After a little while their sense of touch improved dramatically. I then asked them to make texture montages of contrasting materials. Fantastic structures were produced and their effects were completely novel at the time.76

These two- andthree-dimensional texture montages are an interesting example of avant-garde design principles that were then common in cubism and dadaism being introducedinto the teaching canon of artists' training. Already in his Stuttgart period, aswe have seen, Itten made glue paintings of paper, but the collages did not interest him as“autonomous designs" but rather as a “means to discover new compositions of color and form."77 Shortly after moving to the Austrian capital, he wrote in his diary on 20 October 1916 something that suggests this field of experimentation had been greatly expanded:

Experiment withmaterials. Attach pieces of straw, wood, iron, and cloth to a wooden board and then give all of them a coat of paint, go wild. ... I am looking for a formative principle based on the knowledge of the form that determines the connections."78

This note and a sheet of studies from 1916 entitled On Materials (CW 115) not only demonstrate Itten's great interest in researching the materiality of things, their sensory qualities, but also seem appropriate to further undercut the speculations discussedearlier (in“Formative Influences") that Cizek influencedItten. For it is clear that Itten was following a line of development that had already begun while studying with Hölzel, and that this was done independently of Cizek and his circle, about whom he may have known nothing at all at this early date. A diary entry from 1918 shows that Itten's “material studies"79 were imbeddedwithin the overarching context of his theory of contrasts:

The study of nature is above all a study of the purely material. Contrasts: loose-solid, softhard, fluffy-smooth, rough-smooth, pointedblunt, shiny-matte, fibrous-consistent, tightknit-holey, cloudy/airy-rigid.

Veil, wool, silk, knits, weaves, crocheted, hammered, filed, sawed, marbled, fur, glass, brush, metallic, leather, wood, flesh, grid, wheel hair, stone.

The study of material things exercises sharp vision."80

As early as 1917, Itten had introduced “material studies” into his teachings: “So I hope that in this way the stroke, the line, will also be felt as something material, that the love of the line will grow from the love of silk."81

These exercises withmaterials and textures were not an aesthetic end in themselves for Itten, but rather they occupied a precise place within his pedagogical process. Through a playful approach to various materials, they were intended to qive those starting their studies assistance in choosing from among the subsequent teachings offered in the Bauhaus workshops. At the same time, the haptic, optic, and even emotional experiences acquiredwhile putting together and “copying" the“material compositions,"82 along with theelementary light-dark exercises (scales and ribbons) would serve as a prerequisite for the study of nature, to which Itten devoted particular importance in his teachings, and which wouldremain at the center of his own work during the Bauhaus years and long after.

In 1918 Itten described the importance of studying nature for his course as follows:

In order to train their observational abilities to be sharp and exact, beginners must make precise,photographicallyaccuratedrawings—in color as well—from nature. I want to train the eye and the hand, and memory as well. That is to say, to learn to see by heart. [And indeed in the preliminary course students drew things from memory that captured the materialcharacter of the objects with deceptive accuracy.— R.W.] First I train the physical body, the hand, arm, shoulder, and senses. That is the training of the external existing human being. The education of the mind follows step by step: clear, simple, thoughtful observation of that which can be perceived by the senses.83

Thus the study of nature is for Itten not l’art pour l’art, mere training in dexterity, or a simple coordination exercise, but rather it serves to sharpen the ability of the senses to recognize and to expand“concrete thinking," a connection that Itten once expressed in the concise formula, which he did not further explain: “sharp senses = exact, real thinking."84

![](images/b14bf291eac9715dd7e0cc534f727bfa80223d572917e118e5ba3d611cf84a43.jpg)  
60 Student works depicting the characteristic properties of different materials From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

61 Lore Leudersdorff-Engstfeld, “Drawing depicting the characteristic properties of a material (wood). Practice task for the development of the eye as a receptive sense and the sense of tase as a productive sense" From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

![](images/5f442f8f10f7e4c6f4815359ad5ad23649c093c294e3698cb3d71550053ebeb1.jpg)

![](images/4f4bc6819b47921468aa9c68762177b6f71e0d8ab13486daef49972c4e8f939a.jpg)

If the nature studies aimed for the most objective specification of sense impressions possible by acquiring “an accurate idea of tonal values and characteristic forms,"85 in order to pursue “research into the essence of theworld of forms,"86 then the procedure used for life drawing was rather the reverse. Itten was not concerned with anatomically precise reproduction of an external reality but with finding the typical “expressive form" and “inner movement."87

Today I want to teach students to draw nudes rhythmically. The students must draw an entire nude using circular motions while I count. Draw in circles to the right or left, whichever way the nude moves. . . . Then, because the nude as a form character has straight lines as well, draw the same thing in straight lines. It is important that it be a hand movement of the same form, because everything is in motion."88

Many of these life drawings are directly related to the opening exercises for rhythmic form, reducing the body to the briefest stenograms of movement or to silhouette-like diagrams; others use an expressionistic manner to emphasize the inner vibrations of the model (or perhaps those of the artist) or accentuate the function of joints

62 Johannes Itten, Course note on rhythmic life drawing; Diary, vol. 12 (1919)   
63 Martin Jahn, Rhythmic Nude Study (emphasis of the circular movement)

![](images/7937d64043469965b0bc29fb9db6ae31f5ad2a49dfeff4627499464a52606af2.jpg)

![](images/28eb99129f03801bfb413eb1fa7590de938f14f5a0afae8555f4fe41c85e0d1e.jpg)  
64 Ima Breusing, Reclining Nude (Positive-Negative Form), 1921–22

and the main directions of the figure; still others aim to depict sculptural values and material qualities of the thing by means of subtle modulations of light and dark. Naturally the works of the students are a mirror of Itten's own aesthetic practice. The master's nudes at that time are distinguished——as Gisela Fiedler-Bender has rightly noted—"bya rotating and sculpturally accentuated rhythm of corporeal forms, by an emphasis on the subdivisionof form andatransformation of movements into widely swinging vibrations."89 Occasionally in Itten's course life drawing would be accompanied by music, in order to increase the sense of rhythmic expression of the moving model, a method that Oskar Schlemmer would also use later at the Dessau Bauhaus. In other respects, however, Schlemmer—who did life drawings for sculptors from 1921 on at theBauhaus90 and whose artistic ideal was very much based on creatinq a human type that was refined in the classical style—criticized the expressive tendencies of the life drawings that were done in Itten's course. He admonished others to “lay aside. . . expression," to work “toward objectivity," and“in front of the model. . . [in order to] draw as correctly as possible"91(see “The Human Being as Object of Instruction" in chapter 10).

A central place in Itten's course was occupied by the analysis of old masters, which was also inspired by Hölzel, and which Itten would teach in his courses in both Vienna and Weimar as well as later in Berlin, Krefeld, and Zurich. In his course at the Bauhaus in particular, he didnot perform these analyses as intellectualdissections—of the sort done in an art historical course—but rather by way of feeling, through empathy with the image. The purpose of the analyses of pictures that the students preparedwas to discover the essence of the painting through their own creative act. “The feelings thus aroused would then be committed to paper in terms of light and dark contrasts, distribution of mass, rhythms, lines of composition, or other factors suggestedby the picture."92In 1921Oskar Schlemmer remarked on the prominence placed on the affective in these analyses:

In Weimar Itten teaches “Analysis." He shows photographs and the students are supposed to draw various essential elements, usually the movement, the main contour, a curve. Then he shows them a Gothic figure. Next he displays the

![](images/ebe34e38db72c637cd68213b1be37496511ccf12fb9c0c67cf1848cf416651b5.jpg)

![](images/5d48e391bab60b39e7cee6463a24c2730dc3a769526a2cbabc4ca56e14ac14d2.jpg)

65 Johannes Itten, Geometrical analysis of an ancient Egyptian scribe; Diary, vol. 3 (1915)   
66 Johannes Itten, Rhythmic analysis of individual formal motifs following a medieval miniature The Creation of Eve; Diary, vol. 5 (1917 )

![](images/9ced3298b6e8782deee4f0549035d6420b1f585e8b228c243405795f2fb43834.jpg)

weeping Mary Magdalen from the Grünewald Altar: the students strugqle to extract essential feature from this complex picture. Itten glances at their efforts and then bursts out: if they had any artistic sensitivity, they would not attempt to draw this, the noblest portrayal of weeping, a symbol of the tears of the world; they would sit silent, themselves dissolved in tears. Thus he speaks, then departs, slamming the door!"93

As with life drawing, the emphasis in the analyses was on the emotional understanding of the formal structure and/or the primary meaning of a painting. A note from 1919 in Itten's diary states:

Now we want to try to draw the essential feeling out of the work of art. So that we might have some control over whether the feeling is correct—that is, whether it corresponds to the work—we have to depict the thing felt, using our hand, charcoal, and paper. This depiction of the feeling is the essential aspect of my course. To develop the participants' ability to feel and to liquefy, to solidify the feelings, these flaming ethereal forms, in visible, perceptible forms."94

Although the feeling plays the dominant role here, this does not preclude the possibility that in his own written or sketched analyses of old masters Itten had also worked out the rationally comprehensible pictorial structures and organizing principles. This certainly holds for his analysis of the geometry and structure of the seated figure of an ancient Egyptian scribe, which is found in Itten's Stuttgart diary from 1915, as well as for his Vienna lecture “"Über Komposition" (On composition; 1917), in which, for example, he traces Giotto's Prayer over the Rods back to diagonals, horizontals, and verticals, to circle and triangle.95

Feeling and thinking, intuition and intellect, expression and construction are the poles that define not just Itten's artistic oeuvre but also his practice as a teacher of art. (They also mark the domain within which Bauhaus pedagogy as a whole operates. This needs to be emphasized separately, because the Bauhaus has all too often been reduced to the merely functional and its pedagogy to the merely rational.)

This relationship of tensions also characterizes Itten's theories of form and of color. With respect to the Bauhaus period, both are documented only in fragments. As a result of the selfimposed burden of teaching obligations that resulted from his commitment to the task at hand, not only did his own artistic production receive short shrift in the period from 1919 to 1922–23, but Itten also found no time to conceive his theory of forms and of colors as the sort of self-contained whole that both Kandinsky and Klee managed to achieve at the Bauhaus. Only in 1930 was Itten's theory of form published, in a private printing entitled Tagebuch von Johannes Itten: Beiträge zu einem Kontrapunkt der bildenden Kunst (Diary of Johannes Itten: Essays on a counterpoint of the visual arts; a reprint was published in 1980 as Elemente der bildenden Kunst),96 and then another thirty odd years passed until his color theory was published (Kunst der Farbe, 1961). In order to present only what Itten taught at the Bauhaus and to avoid mixing this with traces of his later courses in Berlin, Krefeld, and Zurich, these two books will be excluded from the following discussion.

![](images/eaa4c61b8307cad916e6faa01e1895257c8111dc4fece781660c49f96801b979.jpg)  
67 Margit Téry-Adler, Analysis of proportion and lightdark in Giotto's Annunciation to St. Anne, 1918–19; Itten's course in Vienna 68 Margit Téry-Adler, Analysis of expression in El Greco's Grand Inquisitor, 1918–19; Itten's course in Vienna

Experiments with the three basic forms—the square, the triangle, and the circle—took up much of his theory of form. Under Eugène

69 Johannes Itten, Course note on the elementary physical forms; Diary, 1920

![](images/0438984c9620a1db9b681c24266aed90a58b03b86d9b71bc045aa5dee17115dc.jpg)

Gilliardin Geneva, he had already become acquainted with their fundamental significance for design and had had this confirmed under Hölzel in Stuttgart. Numerous diary entries show that Itten haddealt extensively with clarifying the “formcharacter" of these three elementary forms. In 1916, for example, one entry reads:

Square: calm-death-black-dark-red

Triangle:severity= life-white-bright-yellow Circle: zero to infinity = constant. . . calm. . . always blue.97

Or in 1917:

Emphasize the square as calm, the triangle as a severe contrast of directions the circle as movement.98

Also in 1917:

Square: horizontal-vertical, calm-rigidity, harmonious

Triangle: diagonal, mediation, unharmonious Circle: formal, movement, harmonious.99

We can be certain that such attempts at classificationwere inspiredby similar reflections that Kandinsky includedin his 1912 book On the Spiritual in Art, as there was a copy of it in Itten's library. Kandinsky was convinced that every form, “even if it is completely abstract [and resembles] ageometric form, has its own inner sound“ and “is a spiritual being possessing qualities that are identical with that form. . . . Here, it may easily be remarkedthat the value ofmany colors is reinforced by certain forms and weakened by others. At all events, sharp colors have a stronger sound in sharp forms (e.g., yellow in a triangle). The effect of deeper colors is emphasizedby rounded forms (e.g.,blue in acircle)."100 In an elaborate procedure to justify this, at the Bauhaus (in particular in his book Punkt und Linie zu Fläche, 1926, translatedas Point and Line to Plane in 1947) Kandinsky then assigned the basic forms andcolors to one another as follows: triangleyellow, square-red, circle-blue (on this, and criticisms of it, see “Theory of Form" in chapter 7).

This result corresponds exactly to what Itten noted in his diary in 1917:

On the local color of forms:

(1) Every form has a local color, even every geometrical form.

(2) To what extent is this color a consideration? Every pure form presumably corresponds fully to only one color: circle-blue, square-red, triangle-yellow.101

![](images/e58d1117d8234667d4c0dc6b084d10549522850cf3174cef095cd2d6a47165a3.jpg)  
70 Johannes Itten, Cube Composition, 1919; plaster 71 Gerhard Schunke, "Sculptural representation. Spatial horizontal-vertical composition. Practice task to develop a feeling for proportion and static sensitivity," early 1920s  
From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

![](images/520ba41b28da61dc92240a223eb723ef9def7d5d03f4de4662d67f7dee1c16ee.jpg)

After the students had first experienced the three elementary forms physically by means of body movements (relaxed swinging motions or tense angular ones)—a fundamental difference from Kandinsky's “exercises solely in the head"102\_—Itten would have them work through the design possibilities of square, triangle, and circle in a series of exercises and studies in composition—compositions on the nature of the square, triangle, or circle; combinations of two or three natures; dividing forms according to their natures as square, triangle, and circle—which led to intensive studies of proportion.103

We now move from the second to the third dimension. Within the framework of Itten's theo-ry of form, the study of plastic forms and their representations was highly important:

Representations of simple bodies: sphere (infinite central movement), cube (horizontal, vertical, diagonal), pyramid, cylinder, cone. . . . Then I assigned the task of developing a composition from each form character, then combinations of two bodies, three bodies, and so on, or even better: combinations of all the form elements of similar expressive nature, interpenetration of bodies, spatial rhythms."104

In addition to these more rationally organized, stereometric compositions (usually cube compositions),105 which are reminiscent of the cubo-constructivist sculptures of the de Stijl member George Vantongerloo, free montages of materials such as found objects from the junkyard were also made as part of the preliminary course. Thesemontages can besituated within the sphere of dadaism and they reveala distinctly playful character. Just like the cube compositions, which are not utopian architectural models but studies of the fundamentals of the relationship between body and space, they make it clear that the purpose of Itten's preliminary course was not primarily direct preparation for the subsequent teachings in one of the workshops but rather a chance for students to discover and develop their own creative abilities, free from any attempt to judge them by other than aesthetic criteria. As we have seen, it was characteristic of Itten's teaching, despite all the emphasis on the affective aspect, that intensive exercises in the geometry and construction of design caused design to be experienced as a fundamental dialectic of subjectivity andobjectivity, of spontaneous selfexpression and a rationally determined choice of form.

This is also true of Itten's theory of color, which has enjoyed a widespread reception in arts education since the publication of his book Kunst der Farbe (1961), whose principles Itten probably taught at the Bauhaus as well.106 Georg Muche, who taught the preliminary course twice during the founding years of the Bauhaus,107 emphasizes that he did not teach color as part of the preliminary course, because this was consideredItten's special privilege.108 It is instructive that the minutes of the Councilof Masters for 20 October 1922 contain an entry permitting Itten to establish his own course on color, though this plan was never carried out, as Itten had already announced that he was planning to leave the Bauhaus.109Instead, Ludwig Hirschfeld-Mack110gave a seminar on color as an unofficial course offering in the

72 Max Bronstein, "Material study. Composition from various materials, bound by rhythmic forms. Practice task: Observation of the complementary effect of various materials," early 1920s

From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

![](images/75c8e36c7aa0c8b7fc91808c51105de4713843f8a21088ef4684bba99ad229a2.jpg)

![](images/3757b9490827a46b502bba12530cd2587959cfefad766f4f0bd916a97dd454a3.jpg)  
73 Johannes Itten, Color sphere in seven levels of light and twelve hues  
From: supplement to Bruno Adler, ed., Utopia: Dokumente der Wirklichkeit, 1921

winter semester 1922-23, andPaul Klee also taught color as part of his “theory of pictorial form" (see "Theory of Color" in chapter 9). Nevertheless, there can be no doubt that Itten's intense occupation with the phenomenon of color—we need only point to his diary entries on the subject, to his collaboration with Hauer, to his Tower of Fire (1920, CW 215–17), and to his planimetric sketch of color spheres at seven light levels and with twelve shades (supplement to Utopia [1921, CW 228])—surely transferred to his teachings at the Bauhaus, even if the sources that could permit a confident historical reconstruction of this aspect of his teaching are considerably less numerous than those for the theory of form, and very few color studies by his students survive from this period.111 As we cannot give an account on Itten's color theory here, it should at least be emphasized that it—like the artist's pedagogical practice in general—aimed for an arts education based on "subjective experience and objective rationale" (to cite the subtitle of his book The Art of Color).

## The Background of Itten's Pedagogy

With his teachings at the Bauhaus, as well as those later in Berlin, Krefeld, and Zurich, Johannes Itten had a lasting influence on both the training of artists and arts education in the schools, and he is widely celebratedas the founder of a new form of arts pedagogy. Nevertheless, it should not be overlooked that his pedagogy was not a fundamentally new approach but had models and precursors that can be concretely defined. Itten's real contribution to arts pedagogy consisted in being one of the first to combine these trends into an effective and productive form for arts education.112 It is obvious that his pedagogy was not nourished on the spirit of the academic training for artists or on the sources of orthodox drawing but rather stood in the tradition of the liberal pedagogical reform movement of Rousseau, Pestalozzi, Fröbel, Montessori, and others.

In Itten's diary for July 1916 one finds the following remark: "Vision without an idea is blind. H. Pestalozzi—An idea without vision is dead. J.-J. Rousseau."113 It is quite evident that Itten is aligning with Pestalozzi's position that "vision is the absolute fundament of all knowledge, in other words, that every piece of knowledge must derive from vision and must be led back to it."114 Itten may have borrowed other things from Pestalozzi as well, in particular the idea of a uniform training of the head, heart, and hand—the credo of ail experiential educators. (Pestalozzi's pedagogy for drawing, which aimed to overcome the unproductive method based on copying by introducing a method, then still new, for reducing drawing to elements, must be criticized in one respect in that it ultimately ossified into a rigid schematism, from which arts education in the schools did not begin to free itself until very late, specifically, the late nineteenth century).

Generally speaking, the efforts of the teachers named above can be characterized by saying that they aimed to develop abilities that are latent or dormant in a child or young adult by means of a process of a playful acquisition of reality and independent learning. This means that education and training are not understood in the sense of the old school of rote learning and cramming, in which a stock of supposedly unchanging facts, abilities, and skills are transmitted according to the model of the Nuremberg funnel. Itten compares the "true teacher" to a gardener:

He prepares the ground and sows. The seed germinates out of sight in the dark bosom of the earth; after the appropriate amount of time, it pushes upward; and finally it appears as a young plant. . . . It gets everything it needs from the earth and atmosphere around it. Its own essence develops out of itself into a flower and fruit. . . . The clever gardener cautiously allocates the care he gives it. He knows that his help is limited, but the strength and power of nature is enormous.115

This quotation reveals Itten's pedagogical naturalism, which is reminiscent of Rousseau. Itten's pedagogical reflections begin with the child. The artist shares with Goethe the conviction that there would be nothing but geniuses “if human beings were to keep developing in the direction they point as children." In Itten's view, the child acquires for itself "in a complete totality of thinking, feeling, and acting . . . its world by playing in a creative way." Only on entering school, with the foreign influence of a teaching plan, the personality of the teacher, and the regulation of the school system, is the natural growth of the child threatened. With the help of new methods of education, it is possible to counteract these dangers in order to "preserve the growth period of child genius" and thus to give “a completely different expression to the face of our culture."116 In his pedagogical thinking, then, Itten is not simply fixed on the individual but considers the overall cultural context, though not in a way that the contours of this "other" face are recognizable— that is, in the sense of a utopian plan. In formulating his pedagogical goals, Itten goes back to Plato's division of education into three areas: gymnastic, artistic, and mathematical. For Itten, education is consequently a liberation, strengthening, and differentiation of the physical, spiritual, and intellectual functions. Because these three areas are developed only in very different ways in our industrial society—Itten criticizes the overemphasis “of an uncreative intellectualism"117 to the detriment of artistic education—he calls for people to be educated as artists:

We must, on the one hand, see to it that all young people are educated in a way that they can develop in an original, characteristic manner, so they remain creative; on the other hand, we must make them familiar with all the rules governing the means of artistic representation that they must master in order to be able to give shape to their new and original ideas.118

It is evident, then, that Itten viewed the knowledge of the means and rules of design as a conditio sine qua non of artists' training, but also that another aspect is at least as decisive: namely, the creative self-development of the active subject in the design. His respect for the individual and his or her creative abilities can be seen in that for him the highest principle was the effect of teaching on the individuality of each student, that on principle he would not correct the students'work, and that he was also suspicious of students' taking after the manner of their master. At the same time, one cannot overlook the fact that he influenced the students who followed him like disciples more strongly—because of his forceful per: sonality, his natural charisma, and his outstanding pedagogical principles—than the maxims of his emancipationist pedagogy would lead one to expect. It should be noted as an aside, however, that other members of the Bauhaus decidedly turned away from him and sought their salvation in the private courses of the de Stijl artist Theo van Doesburg, for example.

The idea of an education that points the students at the center of its concerns, that starts with their temperament, their talent, and their abilities, that builds up from the basis of an informal student-teacher relationship, that gives priority to action over purely cognitive learning is one that stands within a large context of tradition with a highly complex nature. I am referring to the tradition of reform pedagogy. Itten had encountered the progressive ideas of reform pedagogy as part of his training as a teacher at the teachers' seminar in Bern. In his autobiographical fragment, Aus meinem Leben (From my life), he writes:

The advanced seminar was led by the twentyeight-year-old revolutionary Dr.Ernst Schneider. . . . The problems of the latest school reform were discussed around1905-6, the Sonnenschule of Vienna, Scharrelmann's reform ideas from Hamburg, all of these efforts found ardent partisans in us. There was a revolutionary air in the house. . . .119

Ernst Schneider (1878-1957) studied pedagogy with Wilhelm Rein (1847–1929), among others, in Jena at the turn of the century and practiced at the training school of the pedagogical department of the university there; he had also encountered the Educational Residence at Castle Biberstein in the Röhn that had been founded by Hermann Lietz (1868–1919), a student of Rein, before—as a committed supporter of reform pedagogy—he was appointed director of the teachers' seminar in Bern in 1905.120 Once there, however, he had to defend himself from the beginning against the criticisms and attacks of his conserva tive enemies, who finally forced his dismissal in 1916. The young Schneider was fond of experiment and fervently admired by the students in his seminar, but his critics considered him eccentric and disconnected from actual practice. He was accused of siding too much with the students and too rarely with the teachers, of not communicating enough of the material that future teachers would need, and of placing too much weight on psychoanalysis in the teachers' seminar, which was out of place and even harmful.121 With the fervor of a missionary, Schneider tried to put his convictions about reform pedagogy into practice at the seminar in Bern. This can be seen most clearly in his concept-—common in progressive pedagogy of the day—of the "free composition." Thus he refused to consider writing when grading essays and advocated "liberation instead of the coercive norms, imagination instead of grammatical correctness, dialogue between teacher and student instead of testing spelling skills."122 As mentioned, Itten practiced these principles with conviction, not only as a primary school teacher but also later as an instructor in the arts:

How can I permit myself, as an educator and teacher, to cross out the ideas of the children, to show all their mistakes andweakness with red ink?. .. The teacher as a constant corrector becomes the gravedigger of the initial childlike thinking. ... Don't cripple the students inside by making corrections but overlook their mistakes with praise and approval. This increases self-confidence, their faith in themselves. And every time the results are astonishingly good.123

Schneider's maxims of reform pedagogy were not limited to rejecting the memorization drills of the old school, to favoring teaching by experience, or to advocating the Work School (Arbeitschule) that was being promoted by Georg Kerschensteiner at the time; rather, the director of the seminar in Bern recognized the extraordinary importance of art and arts education for school reform. Here, again, he came up against Kerschensteiner, whose wide-ranging empirical study Die Entwicklung der zeichnerischen Begabung des Kindes (The development of drawing talent in children; 1905) had impressed him just as much as the artists' training movement had in general (see below for more on Kerschensteiner).

In his lecture "Die Grundlagen der Schulreform" (The principles of school reform), Schneider argued as follows:

The whole movement demands an intensive education of emotion, which the traditional, purely intellectually oriented school allowed to atrophy. In solving the question of education for the arts through the arts, we must necessarily demand an education from the inside out, in contrast to mere training, to education from the outside in. The experience of art should become a factor in education in lieu of mere learning.

He formulates the general goal of education as follows: "Education of the entire person from the inside out by means of personal achievement (work) within the community and for the community."124

Given the background of these brief remarks, there can be no doubt that Itten owed his élan for reform pedagogy above all to the teachers' seminar in Bern, and that it was probably Schneider who introduced him to the goals and methods of the arts education movement.

The arts education movement125 originated at the end of the nineteenth century and reached its high point at the First Arts Education Day in Dresden in 1901. This movement for the reform of arts pedagogy was too heterogeneous to be explored in depth here, but it can be said that it was directed above all “against intellectualism andschematism in education"andwatched "with disconcertment the lack of artistic sense" as it was “articulate in the stifling of artistic creation, of its own creative design."126 The child was discovered andcelebrated as a creator, inventor, and artist, as Carl Götze's book Das Kind als Künstler (The child asartist; 1898) shows. Götze characterized "the goal of education as the ability to control life with one's own creative forces in order to achieve good andbeautiful things. The constructive principles of life can best emerge if the child can freely develop its organic abilities, in order to be turned from its gradually crystallizing talent toward its appropriate paths. By encouraging the child to act on its own, in its interactions with other people and with things, its creative forcesare awakenedandexercised."127

In the small book Das Malen und Zeichnen (Painting and drawing) by the reform educator Heinrich Scharrelmann (1871–1940), Itten found remarks that may have confirmed for him his own pedagogical views: “The straight line, the triangle, the rectangle, the circle, and at most the oval and ellipse—these are thebasic mathematical forms of all drawing."128And somewhat later: "As long as .. . training in drawing does not connect directly to the child's experience, it willalways remain unchildlike"(my emphasis— R.W.).129

Another strandwithin reform pedagogy should also be mentioned: namely, that of John Dewey (1859–1952) and the American pragmatism that he founded, whose pedagogical essence can be expressed in the concise formula "learning by doing" (for more on this, see “Learning by Doing" in chapter 7).

Dewey's pedagogical views found their way into the German Work School movement by way of Georg Kerschensteiner (1854–1932). As early as

1908, at the first Congress of the German Werkbund, Kerschensteiner called for an end to the constricting arts education in primary schools, to stop loadingchildren up with bits of knowledge—as was the practice—and instead to see to it that each child discovered its own soul and produced that which was hidden dormant within it. Like other educators of the time, Kerschensteiner was concerned about the education of the "complete human being," which definitely included developing creative abilities. For Kerschensteiner, however, the demand for creativity was not a universal principle of education but was confined to a specific period of development that ended around the age of ten. After that, in his view, children should begin to practice precise vision and manual dexterity.130

The opposition to the "decapitation" of the schools, and specially to the reduction of the artistic aspect to a cognitive one, was a common one at the time. For example, Hermann Muthesius (1861–1927), from whom we have already heard in another context (see“Hermann Muthesius and the German Werkbund" in chapter 2), also took a critical position with regard to arts education in the schools, in his capacity as adviser on arts and crafts schools in the State Department for Arts and Crafts in Berlin:

Our schools are positively teeming with scientif; ic approaches. The German schoolteacher still views art as a remote specialized subject that has nothing to do with him and for which the school has no time. . . .

We Germans have a strange tendency to allow our natural artistic abilities, which are by no means slight, to be pushed against the wall by our stronger characteristic: a scientific approach. Though we do introduce training in drawing into our schools, we organize it scientifically, so that it becomes mathematics or optics or physical exercise or who knows what. But we remain indifferent to the one thing it really should be: a means to artistic education. . . . This sort of drawing instruction is worse than none at all; for most people it destroys any pleasure in drawing for the rest of their lives. . . .

In the primary schools in London, training in drawing is done at a level that is astonishing to us; many schools devote four hours a week to drawing . . . and in most schools it is done on a genuinely artistic level. They do not laboriously copy models but follow nature. . . .“131

At the time, most of the suggestions for improving the state of arts education remained fixed at the primary school level. The area of professional training for artists was, as a rule, ignored. One exception was Joseph August Lux (1871–1947), who in his book Das neue Kunstgewerbe in Deutschland (The new arts and crafts in Germany; 1908) called for the ideas for reforming arts pedagogy to be carried out on all levels of artistic education, from the elementary level to the art academies. Lux's maxims on education can be characterized with the modern slogan "student-centered." For example, the teaching is supposed to start from the creative possibilities of the students and not from the teacher's notions of what they should be doing; that is to say, the student, not the teacher, is the main issue in the teaching and learning process. The teacher is merely the hand that should leadgently from a certain distance.132

Alongside the framework of reform pedagogy, which was one factor in the development of Johannes Itten's art pedagogy, his thoughts and actions and also his theory were heavily influenced by certain philosophical, religious, and esoteric writings. Though we cannot discuss these influences in detail here, it can be said that the bulk of the authors that the artist was reading during the Vienna years and the Bauhaus period in Weimar were those "who spoke of a ... unified view of the world that rejected positivist and materialistic thinking and in doing so pointed to ancient religious or esoteric traditions."133In this context, Itten's interest in theosophy played an important role, as is clear from extended excerpts from a popular book from the turn of the century, Die grossen Eingeweihten: Skizze einer Geheimlehre der Religionen (The great initiated: Sketches of a secret doctrine of religions) by Édouard Schuré (1841–1929), that appear in his diary aroundNovember 1916. In the subsequent period, too, the diary contains indications that he was reading other important theosophical treatises, such as Die uralte Weisheit: Eine kurzgefasste Darstellungder Lehren der Theosophie (The age-old wisdom: A brief portrayal of the teachings of theosophy) and Das Denkvermögen (The ability to think) by Annie Besant (1847–1933; Besant was world president of the Theosophical Society from 1907 on) and to the essay Das Christentum als mystische Tatsache (Christianity as mystical fact) and Theosophie by Rudolf Steiner (1861–1925). In Vienna, through the circle around Alma Mahler, whom Walter Gropius had married in 1915. Itten came into contact with theosophical and—its successor—anthroposophical ideas. This was a way of thinking that is also extremely important as a key to understanding Kandinsky's work, as Sixten Ringbom has emphatically demonstrated.134A diary entry from 6 July 1918 reads:

Already for some time, and now once again when I was with Frau Mahler, the characteristic forms and colors have come to mind again and again; often they are only perceptible around people. I was always comparing and wanted to arrive at a set of characteristics of forms and colors as the expression of human ability and desires. Yesterday, almost by coincidence, I passed the theosophicalbookstore, . . . and suddenly I asked for books with illustrations, and I was given Thought-Forms by C. W. Leadbeater and A. Besant and Man Visible and Invisible by C. W. Leadbeater. Both are perhaps exactly the books that I need at the moment. After some comparison with many paintings, I have confirmed that their colors accurately characterize the things depicted.135

This quotation shows that Itten's study of esoteric texts not only corresponded to his striving for religious and spiritual involvement but also stood in direct relation to his artistic practice, with his search for the "spiritual in art" (to use the now classic title of Kandinsky's book). In this view, every color, and every form, is the expression or equivalent of a hidden spiritual element.

It was precisely the knowledge of this "spiritual element" and its laws—that is, what lies behind what is manifestly perceptible—that interested the artist and educator Johannes Itten, who in this context usually spoke of the "essence of things" or of "essences." Walter Hess has pointed out that this is a way of thinking that is related to the main movements within modern art: "The hope was to discover the laws that were as much laws of the world, of its raison d’être, as laws of the work of art."136

This strivingfor insight into “essences," for universal knowledge of art and the world, also led Itten to take an interest in Indian mysticism, in the teachings of Buddha (ca. 540–ca. 480 B.c.), and in the works of Chinese philosophers. Particularly worthy of emphasis in this context are the aphorisms of Lao-tzu (604–520 B.c.) collected in the Tao-te ching, especially the eleventh aphorism, which the artist noted in his Vienna diary several times in slightly different forms and with which he opened a 1918 exhibition of works by his Viennese students:137

Thirty spokes meet at the hub, but the empty space between them establishes the essence of the wheel; pots are made from clay, but the emptiness in them establishes the essence of the pot; walls with windows and doors form the house, but the emptiness in them establishes the essence of the house; Fundamentally: The material conceals utility; The immaterial establishes essence.138

There is an entry in Itten's diary on 27 May 1918 along similar lines: "The essence of the material is its effect of space, the immaterial. Space is the soul of the material. Space is the material of the immaterial."139And in March 1919, half a year before he moved to the State Bauhaus in Weimar,

74 Johannes Itten, Mazda's Temple (triad mind—soul— body); Diary, 1920

![](images/cf0197035b1a7d4914df50098977011f2eecac9d2e34566368145d5936983124.jpg)

under the title “Course for Beginners" the artist wrote: “My goal is to awaken in people the feeling for the essence of things," specifically through observing nature and through a sensitivity to

the clear feelings that are formed in artworks. The relationship of feeling and form. . . . It seems to me all vitality, all existence, lies in movement. ... The question arises, How does form result from movement? If we cananswer this question, we can also form essences. For essence is essentially movement. All movement—light, wind, water, fire, earth, the human being, thought, feeling. In feeling the world reveals itself as movement at its purest. If I can form feeling, I can form movement, essence. . . . I transform movement as feeling into a movement as a body, and this happens most completely when my entire body becomes feeling, or when my entire body becomes capable of movement. . . . The meaning of my course is that I want to make the body capable of emotion, capable of movement. . ."140

In summary, we can underscore Till Neu's remark that Itten found in the writings mentioned above, in highly varied aspects, the very problems that

obviously occupied him most intensely. . .. (1) the desire for the totality of man and for the totality of the relation of man to world; (2) the dominant role of feeling or intuition; (3) the faith that the individual is relatively determined, and related to that: (4) the comparative insignificance of learning, especially the learning of knowledge.141

In particular, Itten saw numerous paralleis between the Taoist philosophy of Lao-tzu and the ideals of education of Itten's own day, which were based on the individual finding him- or herself and on trusting in intuitive processes. Marcel Franciscono notes that Itten's recognition that “every student [is] burdened with a huge number of things learned that must be thrown off before he can experience and find his own knowledge"142 has its pendant in the remark by the Taoist philosopher Chuang-tzu that one should tend to everything that is inside oneself and block out everything external, because too much knowledge is a curse.143

Also of great significance for Itten was the syncretistic Mazdaism that Otoman Z. Hanisch (1844-1936) founded on the basis of the ideas of the ancient Persian religion of Zoroaster (Zarathustra):

[One of] those exclusive substitute religions which sprang up after World War I to avert the despair of the Europeans that were faced with the irretrievably sundered order by promising them inner tranquillity by turning to Oriental forms of belief. In this case, it was a European reedification of the ancient Persian Mazdaism founded by Zarathustra to which fragments from other Asiatic religions had been added. . . . The belief is based, in short, on a duo-deism. The creation of light. . . is engagedin a constant struggle for the rule of the worldagainst the forces of darkness. . . . It is man's task to contribute towards the victory of the creation of light. This he achieves through his own uplifting as he liberates himself from the downfall of the influence of the counter-creation. He must refine his own being by expelling the forces of the material from his body, for instance, by fasting, through vegetarian food and a special diet, purges, meditation, and breathing exercises, which restore his inner peace and free him from the rat-race of civilized society.144

According to Mazdaism, the body is the "temple of the living god," and consequently the individual is obliged to bring intellectual, spiritual, and physical forces into harmonious balance. The relaxation exercises that Itten introducedat the Bauhaus, inspired by Hölzel but given an increased ideological significance by Mazdaism, should be seen against the backgroundof this quasi-religious philosophy of life.

## Between Art and Design

Naturally these were practices that sometimes divided the teachers and students into two camps (as mentioned, Itten's opponents among the students sometimes sought refuge with Theo van Doesburg), and they played a role in the conflict between Itten and the director that would weigh heavily on the Bauhaus for years, though they were certainly not the decisive factor. Two diametrically opposed conceptions were the cause behind that. Itten remarked in retrospect: "My conception for the Bauhaus was, in the end, that of a school for the arts based on new principles of education. Gropius, by contrast, wanted a program for a school of architecture, and he considered my plans—which were, admittedly, then still somewhat vague—a fantasy."145

![](images/7367f59e71c86e7ed3aeb55400d1d8add7092fe95233004bd8f2e80372ddd53c.jpg)  
75 Johannes Itten, Design for a chest with the character of the square and the triangle; Diary, vol. 9 (1918)

Although Gropius's plans certainly did not include reducing the idea of the Bauhaus to an academy for architecture—or he wouldnever have appointed so many visual artists—Itten was rather remote from the director's interest in addressing problems of applied design (that is, design solutions in the way that term is used today). Consequently, he never viewed the preliminary course as merely a preliminary stage to prepare the students for their future activities as architects and designers but rather as a platform for an integral education for "complete human beings."

In fact, there were narrow limits set on the transfer of what took place under Itten in the preliminary course to the area of applied design. Marcel Franciscono notes that most of the studies produced in the preliminary course hardly addressed formal and technical problems, especially of industrial production. These studies were at best suited to guard the students against conventional, run-of-the-mill solutions that had been used a thousand times; at worst, however, the represented a serious impediment to the students' willingness to subject themselves to the formal and technical discipline that is necessary for applied design. 146

Although Itten's diaries from the years in Vienna and Weimar frequently address problems of furniture design and architecture, and at the Bauhaus he even had furniture made from his own designs (see CW 313–15), in the end he seems to have considered visual art and applied design to be incompatible, as the following statement makes clear:

Wall painting—architecture. Only in one tiny aspect do the two come in harmonious contact; namely, where architecture becomes an end in itself, as pure art. Only then is the fundamental attitude a pure one. Everything else is a compromise. Architecture is made “useful." Functional architecture is an absolutely inartistic goal. There is only one art.147

In light of this view of the problem, it is easy to understand why Itten would necessarily have fallen into a dilemma about his role as a master of form in several of the Bauhaus workshops, where the point was to put aside the role of the artist or to mediate between artistry and goal-oriented design. That this was something of a tightrope walk for Itten is demonstrated by the student works from the artists' workshops that Itten ran in parallel with the preliminary course. (As a reminder, these included the following workshops: stone sculpture until 1922, in conjunction with Schlemmer for the year 1921–22; metal workshop until 1923, from 1921 on, together with Schlemmer; workshop for wall painting until 1922, in 1921-22 in alternation with Schlemmer; glass painting until 1922; cabinetmaking until 1921; weaving in 1921).

![](images/0674e1d8d6c3d8b242c67e55f8b56873924baba49615a41bcae74b34771ea413.jpg)  
76 Heinrich Busse, "Wood sculpture: First work study with carving tools for carving various structures in wood," early 1920s From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

Because we cannot analyze Itten's activity in the workshops in detail here,148 a few brief comments will have to suffice. Nearly all of the student works produced from the workshop are distinguishedby a clear, geometrical language of form that is based on the explorations of form carried out in the preliminary course on the nature of the circle, triangle, square, and cube. This is illustrated, for example, by a carved chest by Lili Gräf produced in the workshop on wood sculpture that displays the character of a square (1921–22) and the door with the character of a triangle designed by Joost Schmidt for the Sommerfeld House in Berlin-Dahlem (1920-21). Not only are these works based on the form studies of the preliminary course, they make use of the studies in texture and the "first work exercises with carving tools." In the stone sculpture workshop, Kurt Schwerdtfeger produced his Architectural Sculpture with the character of a square in

![](images/bf3c6cc9f5208cd77b5b3b7002b670d9eb9efa0261fd78d14198ebed0fa22675.jpg)

1921 (with several astonishing formal parallels to Josef Hoffmann's overdoor in the right-hand side hall of the Vienna Secession exhibition of 1902); in the weaving workshop, we should mention Max Peiffer Watenphul's wall hanging with a triangle-and-square character (ca. 1920–21) and an anonymous woven, colorful silk scarf with the character of a square (ca. 1920). The metal workshop produced a door fitting designed with the character of a square and a beaten conic container with bossing designed with the character of a circle, both by Naum Slutzky around1920–21, and a metal container made by Alfred Lipovec that is based on the contrasting form characters of the cube and the cone and is ornamented with small and large circles (1921).

Despite their geometrical vocabulary, these workshop pieces do not speak the language of the machine age in the sense evoked even before the war in Werkbund circles, for example. Pro-

77 Max Peiffer Watenphul, Tapestry with the character of the triangle and the square, 1920–21

ducedby hand and conceived as unique objects (that is to say, not as prototypes for industrial mass production), they are individual creations of a somewhat expressivemanner, occasionally mixed with elements that recall folk art. These are certainly not objects that could be considered harbingers of functionalist industrialdesign or that reveal the spirit of standardization that Muthesius and others had called for at the Werkbund Convention in Cologne in 1914.

Nevertheless, these workshopprojectsare quite typical of the early Bauhaus. They are the visual corollary of the serious struggle for a new beginning in design and design education that, in a historical situation that had been transformed completelysincethe prewar period, initially worked under the banner of a new synthesis of art and craftsmanship, before industry took the place of craftsmanship soon thereafter. They are the expression of an optimistic departure into a "new, gothic" age that—as Gropius expressed it effusively in December 1919"found its first symbols in expressionism."149 To that extent, Itten and Gropius were initially in agreement, but with time the break between them was inevitable. Already in 1922, under the motto "Art and technology: a new unity," very much in conformity with the rise of a new zeitgeist, Gropius undertook the first through change in direction for the still young Bauhaus, which marked the end of the utopian and expressionist phase of the founding years and the transition to the pragmatic and constructivist, industrially oriented phase of consolidation.

Johannes Itten was not prepared to take part in this change of course or share in the responsibility for it. His letter of resignation is dated 4 October 1922: "Dear Mr. Gropius: Because my view of how to solve the task before the Bauhaus differs in essential points from the way you, as director of the institute, have attempted to solve it, I find myself unable to take responsibility for contributing to a successful solution. This compels me to ask you to release me from the teaching staff of the Bauhaus at the end of the winter semester 1922–23."150

![](images/35fed3bedbf172381f9368bd062cebf176570ba378b5a86683c499d14e0fd69d.jpg)  
78 Naum Slutzky, Container with a texture of stippled lines, 1920; copper

In the spring of 1923 Itten left the Bauhaus. The new man was László Moholy-Nagy. The radical changes that would result for both the preliminary course and the workshops will be discussed in the following chapter. First, however, it seems worthwhile to give a brief sketch of Itten's reception after 1945 in arts pedagogy for schools.

## Itten's Reception in Arts Pedagogy for Schools after 1945

Although Itten, at the latest following the appearance of his books Kunst der Farbe (1961) and Mein Vorkurs am Bauhaus (1963), had a widespread influence on arts training in schools—even without the express approval of the makers of teaching plans and the school authorities—the reception of his theory in the professional literature on arts pedagogy in recent decades swings between devout adoration and sharp rejection. It follows a pattern that was already typical in evaluations of Itten's pedagogical practice at the Bauhaus itself, where Itten's disciples and opponents fought sectarian battles.

As we have seen, Itten's express goal was to develop the students' abilities for artistic design by means of “subjective experience and objective rationale." Only this could establish "wholeness" andachieve the goal of the "complete human being."151

We already see this goal unmistakably reversed for the first time among Itten's successors after 1945withKurt Schwerdtfeger (1897– 1966),152who studied with Itten, among others, at the Bauhaus from 1920 to 1924. On superficial examination, his book Bildende Kunst und Schule (Visual arts and school; 1957) is entirely in debt to the pedagogical maxims of his teacher.

Compelled by the necessity to translate teaching practices and materials that were actually developed for art students to arts education in schools, and thus take into account the vertical division according to grade level, Itten's call for totality is lost in Schwerdtfeger's didactic model, in which“subjective experience" is meant to dominate at the primary schoollevel whereas “objective rationale” should dominate for older children and youths. This means that “in gradeschool years the task is a ceaseless stream from the source of the arts."153 Expressive drawing, painting, and modeling as well as handicrafts are the focus of the training. "With puberty, this takes a turn. The lingering in the realm of dreams and fantasy comes to an end and with it creation from primal things."154 Without in principle abandoning the framework of the artistic (musisch), which was Schwerdtfeger's substitute for Itten's principle of totality (Itten himself later spoke more frequently of the artistic), with older children and youths he is concerned with “education to pictorial form,"155 with visual experience of pictorial means (borrowing much from the theory of design of Itten and other Bauhaus teachers). “Here lies, even if Schwerdtfeger is not aware of it, the beginning of a rational concept of art didacticism, . . . in which art too can . .. claim its due,"156 an approach that found broad acceptance in the seventies under the rubric “formal art instruction." Thus among Itten's immediate successors, Schwerdtfeger plays a curious double role. By embodying Itten's principle of totality within his concept of “artistic education", he fulfills, on the one hand, the “expectations of the irrational group"157 in aesthetic education, while, on the other hand, his didactic model contains a decisive impulse to founda rationally conceived arts training that aims at knowledge of pictorial laws.158

Gunter Otto rightly pointedout how problematic it is to dissociate in this way Itten's theory into a subjective and an objective side, because it would ossify either into a sermon on pure subjectivism or a training based on studies of form. "Isolating these two aspects from each other is precisely what exposes the lack of any social connection, the 'ahistorical' approach in the objective domain."159Nevertheless, Otto's criticism of Itten is balanced. On the one hand, he admits to uncertainty whether it would be possible to "integrate into pictorial constellations" the "material, structural, color, or other such stimuli" that are "practiced in isolation" for reasons of the training. He also criticizes Itten's analyses of old masters as overgrown with "subjective assumptions and attempts at symbolist interpretation,"160 though, on the other hand, he credits Itten for rightly pointing to the "importance of motor activity" in the artistic process and for having contributed"to the systematization of artistic forms." His summary: "It is beyond question that Itten's concept is important for the specialist, and much of his formulation of the problem can be applied to pedagogical situations [by which Otto clearly means arts training in theschools— R.W.]."161

Hans Giffhorn, whom we shall use as a representative of Itten's opponents, comes to an entirely contrary assessment. In his book Kritik der Kunstpädagogik (Critique of arts pedagogy) of 1979, he considers Itten entirely from the no doubt one-sided perspective of whether and to what extent his pedagogy is appropriate to help students "correctly recognize their own interests, and those of the society as a whole, and to fulfill them optimally."162 This concept of society, in which Giffhorn provides the measure for what is correct and what is not, has been diluted with vulgar Marxism. The focus of Giffhorn's criticism is that Itten in general left social reality out of consideration, that he understood aesthetic practice as “self-expression," and that his conception of talent was “static” (because it insisted on innate ifferences among individuals).163 In his view, Itten's principle of knowledge is a form of “art pedagogical irrationalism,"164 which manifests itself, among other places, in that it “operates" (emphasis in the original) with a false claim of objectivity “as if there were objective good and bad pictorial means and structures, correct or false principles of artistic design."165 Giffhorn sees the danger for the students in that they are placed in the "ghetto situation of the artistic," rather than being taught political responsibility and the ability to communicate.166

Given the introduction of politics into arts pedagogy that leftist prophets advocated in the seventies, such objections were quite popular. They did nothing, however, to assess Itten's teachings in their proper context—namely, that of Bauhaus pedagogy. It is beyond question, moreover, that any attempt to set the current debates among specialists as the benchmark for a historical phenomenon raises the suspicion that it is ahistorical and inappropriate to the matter at hand.

105 These cube compositions are, by the way, also reminiscent of Malevitch's "architectons." In fact, however, it is rather implausible that these constructivist and suprematist tendencies could have influenced Itten at the Bauhaus (despite the presence of Doesburg in Weimar in 1921–22). It is more plausible—if we wish to search for "influences"—to point to the 1905 design theory of Eugène Grasset (discussed earlier in "Formative Influences" in this chapter), where the chapter "Groupement des volumes" contains drawings that can be seen as precursors of the three-dimensional composition with elements that Itten taught at the Bauhaus (Grasset, Méthode [note 9], pp. 85 f.).

106 In his 1962 essay "Der Vorkurs" in Rotzler, ed., Johannes Itten (note 6), p. 274, Itten indicates that "even the complicated problem of colors in their seven contrasts was considered" in the preliminary course.

107 In an undated manuscript note (Berlin, Bauhaus Archive) Georg Muche says the following about his participation in the preliminary course: "1919 to March 1921: Itten; summer semester 1921: Muche; winter semester 1921-22: Itten; summer semester 1922: Muche; winter semester 1922–23: Itten; then Moholy; first semester 1926: Muche." To the question to what extent Muche's work in the preliminary course was distinct from Itten's work, to what extent his pedagogical views and the works of his students in the preliminary course differed from Itten's, Muche replied in a letter to the author dated 28 April 1981 as follows: "You ask about my style of pedagogy. It cannot be described. I have no system and no method. I have pedagogical talent and a great deal of experience. That is all. I have never collected the works the students produced in my preliminary courses at the Bauhaus. I consider them the concern of the students themselves. Nevertheless, here and there they were reproduced and attributed to Itten's class. I have nothing against that."

108 This is also confirmed by the fact that Itten signed a letter to the art historian Hans Hildebrandt dated 1 July 1920 with the addition "Master of Color." Muche was of the view that color theory could not be taught, "because colors are a sensory phenomenon that should not be systematized." From Muche's speech on receiving an honorary doctorate from the Hochschule für Architektur und Bauwesen in Weimar on 28 June 1979, WZHABW 4–5 (1979): 294.

Bauhäusler und Visionär, exh. cat. (Bozen: MUSEION, Vienna: Jüdisches Museum, Frankfurt: Jüdisches Museum, Ostfildern-Ruit: Hatje Cantz, 2000).

# 6 László Moholy-Nagy (1895-1946)

## A Born Educator

In the previous chapter, we saw that Johannes Itten remained distant from—not to say critical of— the goal of an integration of art and technology Gropius had postulated and that his view of art ultimately remained indebted to tradition. Despite the modernity of his pictorial language, he seldom ventured beyond the boundaries of easelpainting. Moholy-Nagy, by contrast, was progressive and avant-garde in that his interests as an artist were entirely focused on exploring new artistic media, on bringing“art's conception of it-

self" in line "with the media of mechanical reproduction,"1 and on providing a new place for art in an advanced industrial society. For Moholy-Nagy the question of the social relevance of aesthetic production was one of central importance; for Itten, by contrast, artistic activity was primarily an individual problem.

In the spring of 1923, László Moholy-Nagy was called by Walter Gropius to the Staatliches Bauhaus in Weimar. Although at the time he had only been active as an artist for a few years, his success as an experimental designer was remarkable; and although he had had no systematic experience as a teacher, Gropius must have recognized intuitively that he was dealing with a natural pedagogical talent, a "born educator."² There is sufficient evidence to suggest that the question of pedagogical qualification played a particular role in Gropius's appointments. For example, we know that he consideredTheo van Doesburg too dogmatic to be a teacher and that he would not consider appointing Archipenko “because he assumed he lacked pedagogical interests."3 Although in putting together the teaching staff Gropius had at first frequently sought out advice—his wife, Alma Mahler-Gropius suggested Johannes Itten; Itten, in turn, promoted the appointments of Klee, Schlemmer, and Muche4—Moholy seems to have been Gropius's personal discovery. Gropius was clearly not disappointed, as can be seen from the fact that Moholy's teaching career at the Bauhaus lasted five years, and Gropius later wrote that Moholy's teaching decisively influenced developments at the school.⁵

![](images/508e5291c7351f2e29a41d68037b652b07a7539b6e02373a4adc459c0d90427d.jpg)  
79 László Moholy-Nagy, 1926 Photo: Lucia Moholy

It is not possible here to even begin to do justice to Moholy-Nagy's universal personality. The

theme of this book compels us to limit ourselves to Moholy's Bauhaus years and specifically to his activity as a teacher. As a consequence, we can neither treat his work in the years between 1928 and his death in 1946, including his activity in Chicago at the New Bauhaus, the School of Design, and the Institute of Design (see chapter 13) nor explore in detail. his oeuvre as a painter, typographer, sculptor, photographer, stage designer, industrial designer, and theoretician of design.6 Picking out particular aspects—in this case, the pedagogical one—from a complex overall context may seem especially irresponsible in Moholy's case, as he himself always insisted on totality and polemicized against the "segmented human being."7 The fact that Moholy-Nagy as pedagogue is the focus here should not be allowed to obscure the artist, designer, and thinker, especially as there is no question that Moholy's activity as a teacher was deeply affected by his own aesthetic practice. In what follows, then, we must first provide a limited overview of this aesthetic practice.

## Aspects of His Artistic Work

László Moholy-Nagy, born in Hungary in 1895, was self-taught as a teacher and artist. It seems it was precisely this lack of formal training that enabled him to go beyond artistic boundaries and expand the traditional concept of art. Free of any academic fixations that might have hindered innovation, the specific features of his constitution—which his first wife, Lucia Moholy, identified as confidence in his intuitions and an ability to transform quickly—enabled him in an astonishingly brief period to find his way into the ranks of the postwar avant-garde.

Moholy's artistic development began with expressive watercolor and chalk drawings that were made in 1917 in response to horrible experiences in the war, employing an impenetrable thicket of lines to depict the barbed-wire landscapes of the battlefields and dying soldiers. The use of the line as the basic artistic element seems to be highly emotionally charged in these works, that is to say, it does not serve primarily to represent an object but rather "communicates agitation andvibration."8Moholy's decision after thè war to give up the legal studies he had begun earlier9 was accompanied by profound internal conflicts. His propensity for social concerns led him to ask self-critically in 1919 whether, in a time of great social revolutions and difficult problems for everyday life, he was at all justified to "claim the privilege of art for myself"10 and become a painter. In the end, his decision in favor of art was firm: “I know now that if I unfold my best talents in the way suited best to them—if I try to grasp the meaning of this, my life, sincerely and thoroughly—then I'm doing right in becoming a painter. It is my gift to project my vitality, my building power, through light, color, form. I can give life as a painter"11 (my emphasis—R.W.). In 1917, suffering from delirium caused by fever while recovering from a war injury, Moholy wrote his poem "Light Visions"12the earliest evidence of his view of light as the principle of existence and a means for design—and in the passage just cited the phenomenon of light is once again the theme, a theme that would stay with Moholy-Nagy throughout his life.

![](images/80e84d8dc0d60d0e60daf0e1d6eb233872d5e8b5f8aca333a3c390307d7afdda.jpg)  
80 László Moholy-Nagy, Landscape, 1917–18; chalk on paper  
81 László Moholy-Nagy, Bridges (Sketch), 1920–21; watercolor and gouache on paper

![](images/d56b0593108a9983f650d53de1e12567f1dab3a77c5d61f629f30f5f2dd63d95.jpg)

After the war, Moholy-Nagy established contact with the artists of the Ma group in Budapest (ma is Hungarian for "today"), a group of artists inclined to the avant-garde and to social revolution with cubist and expressionist leanings. The collapse of Béla Kun's brief soviet republic in Hungary made Moholy a political émigré in 1919. With Vienna as a way station, he arrived in Berlin in 1920, then the center of the latest artistic movements. Moholy-Nagy had already confronted cubism in his work in Hungary, and his painting in Berlin found strong stimulus for its maturation in his encounter with Dada and with works by artists from the Eastern European avant-garde, like Malevitch, Rodchenko, El Lissitzky, Tatlin, Kandinsky, and others. After a brief period in which he produced paintings, drawings, and graphic works that are essentially objective (despite the strong influence of constructive and abstract design principles) and that reveal the enthusiasm for technology common at the time (bridge construction, railway signals, gear mechanisms, and so on), Moholy was quickly able to learn the “language of geometry”13 and to develop a form of constructivism with a very personal element. In 1922 he justified his decision in favor of constructivism as his basic conception as follows:

Reality is the measure of human thinking. It is the means by which we orient ourselves in the Universe. . .. And this reality of our century is technology: the invention, construction, and maintenance of machines. To be a user of machines is to be of the spirit of this century. It has replaced the transcendental spiritualism of past eras. Everyone is equal before the machine. . . There is no tradition in technology, no class-consciousness. Everybody can be the machine's master, or its slave. This is the root of Socialism. . . This is our century: technology, machine, Socialism. ... Art crystallizes the emotions of an age: art is mirror and voice. The art of our time has to be fundamental, precise, all-inclusive. It is the art of Constructivism. Constructivism is neither proletarian nor capitalistic. . . . It expresses the pure form of nature—the direct color, the spatial rhythm, the equilibrium of form. It is not confined to picture-frame andpedestal. It expands into industry andarchitecture, into objects and relationships. Constructivism is the socialism of vision.14

The contrast to Itten's metaphysical and individualistic tendencies could not be more sharply expressed than it is here. Moholy sought to resolve his affirmation of the constructive aspect, of the machine, and of new technology not only in formal terms but also by experimenting with new techniques and materials.

Among the most spectacular experiments in which Moholy made very early use of the possibilities of modern technology are the so-called

Telephone Pictures. As the artist himself expressed it, they sought to

demonstrate that a complete mechanization of techniques poses no threat to the essential creative power. . . . Painting by hand may retain historical significance; sooner or later, however, it will lose its exclusivity. . . . In 1922 I ordered by telephone fivepictures ofporcelain enamel from a sign company. I had the company's color scheme in front of me andI sketched my pictures on graph paper. On the other end of the line the director of the company had the same squared paper before him. One of the pictures was delivered in three different sizes, so that I could study the fine differences in color relations that result from enlargement and reduction.15

It is difficult to judge whether this description corresponds with reality or whether it belongs among the innumerable legends of artists. Lucia

82 László Moholy-Nagy, Telephone Picture, Em 2 (enamel picture), 1922; enamel, steel

![](images/60f1927b820ad31da87119176eae017a664b5db5d720617c372339d698ecde60.jpg)

Moholy, the artist's first wife and his closest collaborator during the early twenties,16 has attempted to strip away the embellishment of legends that has surroundedthe creation of these Telephone Pictures; in her book Marginal Notes she indicates that Moholy, satisfied with the result of industrially produced enamel pictures, cried out: “I could even have ordered them by phone!" She also notes that in a commentary on these pictures the artist merely made the hypothetical suggestion that “Imight even have done it over the telephone."17 In light of this, it would not seem quite so certain that, as is generally asserted in art historical literature, Moholy actually used the telephone to communicate aesthetic instructions coded in units of millimeters to the company manufacturing the pictures. In any case, the idea of ordering pictures by telephone can be found several years earlier in an essay in the Dada-Almanach that Richard Huelsenbeck published in 1920: "The good painter can be recognized by the fact that he orders his pictures from the carpenter by phone."18 Whether or not the telephone actually played a role in ordering enamel pictures, it must be considered a pioneering act that Moholy proved by example that art in the industrial age could consist of an anonymous machine process of high precision and exist independently of the personal intervention of the artist's hand; thus the act of artistic creation should be seen in the intellectual aspect and not in the manual one.

One important field of experimentation for Moholy-Nagy was photography; his wife, Lucia, a trained photographer, put him on the track of its unsuspected aesthetic possibilities. Apparently without any knowledge of the experiments of Christian Schad (schadograms) and Man Ray (rayograms), Moholy began in 1922 to explore photography without a camera.19 The result was the invention of the photogram.2º The early photograms correspond closely in form to the artist's constructivist approach to design, and they come across as translations of his pictorialproblems from painting into photography. By contrast, the later photograms, which are much freer in form, show a genuiné interest in actively modulating space by means of nonobjective light forms.²1 The separation of conceptual work and its material execution that was typical for the Telephone Pictures is also, in somewhat modified form, relevant to the photograms. In his subtle analysis of Moholy-Nagy's photographic oeuvre, Andreas Haus explicitly alluded to this aspect: “He [Moholy] sought to eliminate completely all material work . . . and transposed the process of 'design' into the intellectual disposition of 'preparation. In doing so he was performing as an artist the ,equivalent of the separation of manual and intellectual labor found in modern technological production. Whereas until then the artist was still the bearer of the ideology of ’identical' and thus unalienated work, Moholy enthusiastically turned over to mechanical means the process of actually shapingthematerial and insteadmerely designed its 'application.' In the photogram, finally, it is light that does the work."22 In this respect Moholy adopted an attitude that is modern in the sense that it is clearly distinct from the back ward-looking utopias of the arts and crafts of the nineteenth century and, in their wake, of the founding manifesto of the Bauhaus. Moholy saw an opportunity to overcome alienatedwork not by reconstructing a lost unity of the art and the crafts but rather by “implementing means of production using artistically 'productive' design and thus relieving it of its heteronomy."23 This was very compatible with Gropius'spostulate of a new unity of art and technology (1923), so that Otto Stelzer was right to remark that the Gropius era did not really begin with the founding of the Bauhausin 1919 but with Moholy's appointment in 192324 (which might suggest that the early years of the Bauhaus could be called the Itten era).

![](images/b0e730ed6b6a7cc74615e9189985a6d9275c7d0348e5bca12571a563fb68eb39.jpg)

We return from this briefdigression to Moholy-Nagy's use of photography. In addition to his experiments in the area of abstract photography without a camera (photograms with objective forms like heads, hands, and so on, are rare), we should mention a second variation: the camera photographs,2⁵ which stood out by their unusual framing and camera angles (close-ups, bird's-eye andworm's-eye views, diagonal compositions) and became models for the development of the so-called Neues Sehen (New vision)26 in the photography of the twenties. They are not realistic in the sense of striving for an objective portrayal of reality but rather are an example of "using and testing Constructivist theories of perception and composition as applied to reality. This results . .. in quite novel aesthetic insights which could not have been attained either through photography or through painting by themselves."27 Many of Moholy's photographs are characterized by the sort of strange states of floating and weightlessness that are also found—though in different form—in the photographic works of other members of the Bauhaus (Walter Peterhans, for example, but also some of the Bauhaus students who did photography) and can be interpreted as the expression of a desire typical of the time to escape “beyond the order of gravitational forces" and “perspectival spatial structure"28 of the certitude of earth. Jeannot Simmen has describedfloatingas the definitive sign of modernism. There is sufficient proof of this: Lissitzky's dream for the future of overcoming the foundations, Malevitch's "separation from earthly points of orientation in an omnidimensional, cosmic perspective,"29 and Kandinsky's liberation from the material and the substitution of the pictorial center with the homelessness of the “anywhere."
