# 7 Josef Albers (1888-1976)

## A New Concept?

Any representation of the preliminary course at the Bauhaus would beincomplete without an examination ofJosef Albers's contribution. Under his leadership the fundamental tasks of this propaedeutic course remained intact: removing the shellac of theacademic view of art from the Bauhaus initiates, releasing creative energies, confrontation not with art but with the most elementary design problems. With respect to didactics

![](images/c079f74b3d78de7b37d2aa05aac90a9fc96578fb8aae5d324e771cf2928233a1.jpg)  
108 Josef Albers, 1928

and methodology, however, in the view of Lux Feininger, sonof LyonelFeininger,Albers changed “the concept of the course itself . . . so drastically... that nothingbut the name remained."1

This assessment is surely accurate to the extent that Albers gave the preliminary course a new, unmistakable profile. It should not be overlooked, however, that he was working in a very specific context of conditions and tradition at the Bauhaus, since he himself hadtaken part in Itten's preliminary course as a student and from 1923 to 1928 he was active in a teaching capacity in the course, alongside Moholy-Nagy, before he wasgivencomplete responsibility for it from 1928on.²Bothexperiences—being taught by Itten and collaborating with Moholy-Nagy—had, as we will see, consequences for Albers's teaching, so that Lux Feininger's statement, which gives the impression of a radical new beginning or a fundamental change of course, would seem to be in need of qualification.

Werner Spies has outlined what—despite the continuity asserted here, which will be demonstrated below—clearly distinguishes the preliminary course as Albers taught it from that of his predecessors:

[N]o one ever conducted a more comprehensive, systematic preliminary course than he. His teachingwas probably the most universal—it was not, like that of Itten, geared only to the creative individual, and was also not directedprincipally toward the constructivist, like, for instance, that of Moholy-Nagy. BothIttenand Moholy-Nagy had as their ideal the individual achievement, the individual artistry. Albers' teaching encompassed a much wider sphere—a comprehensive visual training. The works done by the students of Albers'preliminary course which are still valuable show

that he did not want them to think and compose primarily in a formal manner but that the important thing was the intensive use and study of a given working material.³

Chapters 5 and 6 will have made clear that these remarks about Itten and Moholy are untenable as stated. Nevertheless, we can agree with Spies in one respect: the concern with materials became the focalpoint of the preliminary course under Albers.

During the Bauhaus years in particular, an interest in exploring the technological and aesthetic possibilities of materials was one of the central aspects of Albers's own artistic work. Because with Albers——as with Itten, Moholy-Nagy, Kandinsky, Klee, and Schlemmer—pedagogical and artistic aspects were directly interconnected, and to some extent even determined each other, it will aid our understanding if we first examine the key aspects of the artist's aesthetic practice-both his artistic oeuvre and his activity as a designer—before movingon to examine the preliminary course.

## The Artist

Albers's development as an artist proceeded relatively slowly. Comparedto the great masters of his generation, hè was a “latecomer,"4 as his international breakthrough came only in the sixties. Together with Victor Vasarely, he has since become known as the father of “op art" or, more accurately, of “perceptual art." To a broad section of the art public he is the painter of squares, the creator of the famous series Homage to the Square, which began in the fifties and continued into the seventies. The Homage to the Square paintings consist of staggered juxtapositions of three or four squares, always in such a way that the inner squares tend toward the bottom (i.e., they are not arrangedaround the geometrical center) so that the lower portion has narrow stripes while the upper zone has larger fields. With this scheme, in which the principles are constant but the dimensions can be varied, the artist found his way “to a brilliant way of demonstrating color phenomenona."5 The square, “a form codified by Malevitch, an intellectual form,"⁶ was used by Albers to depict the all but inexhaustible richness of color relationships and the cross-influence of colors and to sharpen perception of these mutual interactions of colors. Consequently, it is not surprising that his theoretical magnum opus on color theory, written in the United States, is called Interaction of Color.

We cannot explore here the design problems of the Homage to the Square series. What is interesting for our purposes is the following: (1) Albers worked in series “because there is not just one solution to an aesthetic problem,"7 a principle that was decisive for his work in the preliminary course as well; (2) Albers sought “the least possible compositionalexpenditure, the reduction to the geometrical element,"8 in order to optimize the relation of “effort" and “effect," a principle that the artist communicated to his students at the Bauhaus with the slogan "economy of means."

The paintings of squares serve to characterize Albers's oeuvre only to an extremely limited extent. It is not possible to go into the details to show that it is richer in many respects. Because of their importance, however, we must at least mention two groups of works, Structural Constellations and Double Centric Paintings, which were made in the United States from the forties on in various series such as Biconjugate, Vice Versa, and Variation. The theme of this chapter is not, however, Albers in America, neither as an artist nor as a teacher, but rather Albers at the Bauhaus. Consequently, his teaching activity in America from 1933 on (Black Mountain College, North Carolina; Harvard University; Yale University; and guest lectureships at numerous universities) must be excluded from what follows just as much as his artistic activity after 19339 (on this, see chapter 13).

![](images/39b16b0f3ee40db5af9940dfe360850e0ceec2e0c9a7fe6312f50c20a947395e.jpg)  
109 Josef Albers, Self-Portrait, circa 1918; transfer lithography

Born in Bottrop in 1888, he began—like Johannes Itten—as a primary school teacher before deciding to study art. In 1913 he registered at the Königliche Kunstschule (Royal Art School) in Berlin, where he receiveda certificate as an arts educator in 1915. Berlin was significant for him in that he came into contact there with the progressive artistic developments of the day through reqular visits to Paul Cassirer's gallery and the Galerie Gurlitt. In 1916 and 1919 he was active as a teacher in his native city, Bottrop. At the same time he took courses at the School of Arts and Crafts in Essen. There he was a student of the symbolist artist Jan Thorn Prikker, who was known for his stained glass windows (this biographical detail is interesting, as Albers would later, at the Bauhaus, become especially interested in glass as a means for design). Albers's first lithographs and wood- and linocuts were made around this time; they reveal the influence of expressionism and cubism. He kept his expressionism in check, however, and it never had the exalted aspects of, say, the artists of Die Brücke (The Bridge). This restraint is best demonstratedby his self-portraits, which "rather than a strong, soulful aspect [reveal] a more critical glance from out of a safe, introverted sphere."10 The tendency to abstraction is especially marked in the lithographs from this period, which were inspired by cubism in their form and whose rich facets and diaphanous pictorialstructure are reminiscent of Feininger. In 1919 Albers went to the Academy of Art in Munich, where he joined the painting class of the prince of painting at the time, Franz von Stuck, with whom Kandinsky and Klee had studied almost twenty years earlier. It is not surprising that the thirty-year-old remained dissatisfiedwith the instruction in Stuck's course. “Though at the turn of the century it may still have been important to study with this imaginative artist, it seems inappropriate for an artist already thirty, who had seen important cubist paintings in Berlin and who had decided on a simplifying technique: black and white and sober themes."11 So Albers left Munich in the spring, to begin again at the Bauhaus, which had opened just one year before:

All I knew of the Bauhaus was derived from a single sheet, the first manifesto, with one side giving Gropius' new program for reuniting art and the crafts and theother side showing Feininger's woodcut, “The Cathedral." This program gave me the impulse to try out the new idea. After destroying most of my academic studies I began over again in Weimar, beginning with the "Preparatory Course," the introductory course required of all students entering the school.12

1n the preliminary course taught by Johannes Itten—who was Albers's age—Albers acquired a new view of things, the view of “materials not notjced before; the assemblages of discarded materials interested him; the teaching method of allowing students to develop on their own inspired him."13 We will see later that Albers would later introduce elements of Itten's course into his own instruction, though admittedly in modified form.

After completing the preliminary course, it was Albers's intention to transfer to the glass workshop. The council of masters, however, initially placed him in the workshop for wall painting, which was led by Itten and Schlemmer in alternation, a decision to which Albers did not submit:

Since I could not agree, so far as I was concerned that painting walls was a necessary preparation for making glass pictures, I worked without being a member of any workshop.

Equipped with a knapsack and a hammer, I scouted the town dump; the bottles I found there provided me with all the kinds of glass I needed for making my glass paintings.

At the end of my second semester Gropius, much concerned, reminded me several times, as was his duty, that I could not stay on at the Bauhaus if I persisted in ignoring the advice of my teachers to engage first of all in the wall-painting class.

.. . In the required exhibition at the end of my second semester I showed various of my glass-painting studies. They were combinations of bottle shards, arranged in an unusual (unprofessional) way on flattened tin cans and wire screens, etc. I felt that my show would be my swan song at the Bauhaus. . . .

But soon thereafter I received a letter from the Masters' Council informing me first, thatI could continue my studies at the Bauhaus and, secondly, asking me to set up a new glass workshop for them. Thus suddenly I got my own workshop and it was not long before I started to get orders for glass windows.14

Albers himself referred to his earliest glass compositions as Shard Paintings. They were inspired by Itten's preliminary course in that they applied the principle of assemblage taught in the course to the area of glass design. It is significant that in these works Albers had already “severed his relationship with the world of objective painting. Besides a new and formalistic occupation with the pieces of glass he had found, there is for the first time the inner debate with the material which was to become so typical of his later teaching activity."15 These early glass works, which, precisely because they were "unprofessional" (Albers) in their craftsmanship, opened up new dimensions of the optic as well as the haptic (glass shards mounted in sheet metal, e.g., Figure; Rhenish Legend; both 1921), were followed around 1922–23 by a series of glass windows that reveal a strictly geometric, formal construction as well as a return to a traditional technique of craftsmanship (and are thus "professional"), namely, lead glazing. Examples include the glass windows in the Sommerfeld House in Berlin-Dahlem that was furnished by the Bauhaus workshops; in the staircase of the Ullstein publishing house in Berlin; and in the entry hall of Dr. Otte's house in Berlin-Zehlendorf.

![](images/27751e00acc6f178fdbd8186d0728cfea244d089a8ceeaec7347db617d4e4391.jpg)  
110 Josef Albers, Rhenish Legend, 1921; glass assemblage

![](images/5c5dd9f86dba1d97f40be9049310d6ba3eaf8f89109f7602aa4802d8a6cac6b6.jpg)  
111 Josef Albers, Stained-glass window in the entry hall of Dr. Otte's house in Berlin-Zehlendorf, circa 1923 From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

In comparison to these glass works, which were quite new in their formal severity but nonetheless did not go beyond the usual in terms of their technical production, the so-called singledisk glass pictures (from about 1925 on) repre: sented a definitive innovation. They are glass pictures of relatively small format in which the glass is not a translucent medium but rather an opaque one. These glass pictures were produced from pieces of milky glass flashed with color, usually dark red, dark yellow, or dark blue. "The forms were constructed by means of cutting out stencils for sandblasting,"16 that is to say, the sandblasting removes some of the flashed color and exposes the white underlayer. These single-disk glass pictures evince a tectonically grounded, geometrically simple formal language and a technical precision that leaves nothing of an individual signature. Horizontal and vertical stripes of color rhythmically articulate the surface and evoke associations with architecture (City, 1928) and music (Fugue, 1925). It is clear that the principles evident in these glass pictures—anonymity of the formal language, exclusion of the fortuitous, "activation of the negative elements"17—apply to the student works created in Albers's courses as well, which emphasizes once again the extent to which the aesthetic maxims of the Bauhaus teachers determined their pedagogical practices.

The transition from Albers's earlier glass works to the precise geometrical organization of the single-disk glass pictures is generally attributed to the influence of Theo van Doesburg's de Stiil courses in Weimar.18It is surely attributing too much to a single cause, however, to explain, solely on the basis of Doesburg's presence, a transition that was typical of the whole of the Bauhaus after 1923as it movedfrom thecraftsbased, romantic early phase to the constructive, engineering-oriented phase. It seems more likely that the transition was a complex process determined by a number of factors.

Even at Weimar, the glass workshop—initially under the direction of Johannes Itten, then under Paul Klee in 1922–23—had difficulty asserting its right to exist (Albers was hired as a technical director after completing his apprenticeship in 1923; in 1924 it was subsumed under the workshop for wall painting). After the exodus from Weimar, it was not thought necessary to continue the glass workshop at the Dessau Bauhaus. This was not only because the craft methods typical of glass pictures were geared toward unique objects and thus incompatible with the new ideology of mechanical reproduction, but also because the progressive architecture of Neues Bauen produced no commissions for glass painting. It is interesting to see that Albers created his single-disk glass pictures, with their technical and formal innovations, withoutanyinstitutionalsupport, somewhat against the official trend, and without any connection to architectural tasks. The strictly rectangular constructions, which seem to paraphrase the principles of Neues Bauen, seen in glass pictures like Interior I and Window (both 1929) were followed by a phase of relaxation of form with the introduction of rounded and diagonal parts, for example, in the sandblasted single-disk glass pictures Glove Lasts or Steps (both 1931). In Steps Albers took as his theme a specific perceptual problem: the confusion of the eye by forms that can be read in many ways, a theme that he would later vary in numerous series in America.

Before discussing Albers as a designer, it is worth looking at the artist's photography, not least because it documents the proverbial versatility that was typical of artists at the Bauhaus.

![](images/153f43c8f2c1c78e13f52f81f7796c95625db3f233a9e2a47d37bf149209fb6e.jpg)  
112 Josef Albers, Fugue, 1925; milky glass, red flashing, partially sanded and partially painted with oil paint 113 Josef Albers, Interior I, 1929; opaque glas, sandblasted

![](images/e7c447a24286551de0e4ba835f379d451dc847516d2097b1c91610d139f3293b.jpg)

Like most other members of the Bauhaus, Albers was not a professional photographer (in contrast to Walter Peterhans) but rather he photographed in addition to other activities—usually as a mere sideline and without professional ambitions. The fact that he approached photography as an amateur was no doubt a decided advantage that enabied him to use the medium in a naive or—to use his word—"unprofessional" and yet innovative way. It goes without saying that his photographic work did not stand outside the historical context but rather bore the signature of the age, more precisely, of the so-called Neues Sehen (New vision): boldperspectives, angled horizons, extreme top or close-up views, diagonal compositions, hard shadows, unexpected detail views, and unusual structures. This interest in structures and the effects of surfaces was doubtless awakened in Itten's preliminary course. Naturally one other influence on these photographs leaps out: that of Itten's colleague in the preliminary course, Moholy-Nagy, who revolutionized photography of his day as an experimental designer par excellence and was a lasting influence (see “Aspects of His Artistic Work" in chapter 6).

Albers's photographic works are, in a sense, private photographs, because they were not intended for publication, and also because they often depict peoplefrom theartist'spersonal sphere (e.g., colleagues at the Bauhaus). Nevertheless, it is remarkable to see the artist's approach to the material, which was very unconventional at the time, of arranging individual photographs of the same size, or even different sizes, by subject in ensembles. “The grouping of pictures in this way manifests his interest in sequence, in the repetition of a motif for emphasis. [They] are his most original and unusual contribution to photography. When he combines [several] photographs..., he is always conscious that the effect is made stronger by the aggregation—either through the similarity of the images or through contrast. It results in sequences that are reminiscent of film sequences."19 The modernity of Albers's approach is evident both in the rejection of the individual image and in the serial procedure. It is certainly no coincidence that Robert Rauschenberg, a student of Albers in the United States at Black Mountain College, used precisely this method in his combines.

![](images/52f5d79e5b76a71974cb1e9ff9a36040858884f2225a310e5647426348f022f7.jpg)  
114 Josef Albers, Oskar Schlemmer, Tut Schlemmer, Ernst Kállai, and Hans Wittwer, 1928–29; Collage of eleven photographs

## The Designer

After this necessarily hurried overview of Albers's work as a fine artist during his years at the Bauhaus, it is worth examining his contribution to applied design. One of Albers's most remarkable qualities was his versatility, which was expressed in the variety of objects he designed.

Alongside his artistic experiments with glass Albers also came into contact with problems of industrial glass production, for example, in designing a fruit bowl and an industrially produced tea glass. Both designs were done around 1924 in the metal workshop led by Moholy-Nagy. Their design was based on a formal severity that is remarkable for the time. For example, the fruit bowl is constructed from simple, basic forms: on three ball feet of wood rests a circular band (nickel silver), and "the round glass plate is also inserted into the wooden balls . . . ; the tea glass is also economical and charming in its construction: the concave center of the glass is surrounded by a metal band that wraps just three quarters of the way around and to which two wood handles are attached. Here a'nonprofessional' inventeda minimal form that is functional, economical, and aesthetically satisfying."20

Another focus within Albers's activity as a designer was woodworking. The earliest examples we should mention are the furnishings for the waiting room in the director's office, which were created in the cabinetmaking workshop led by Gropius as master of form in 1923: a newspaper rack, a glass cabinet, a meeting table, and a matching folding chair.21 These pieces of furniture, freed from decorative constructivism à la

![](images/921066bc07cf27f679ea1a91095ad356983f32fcee0ac50072278504bc00776d.jpg)  
115 Josef Albers, Fruit bowl, 1923; chrome-plated brass, painted wood, mirror glass 116 Josef Albers, Tea glass, circa 1924 Photo: Erich Consemüller

Rietveld, reveal Albers to have been searching for simple, practical, unpretentious functional forms. This can also be seen in the stackable side tables and the woman's writing desk, both of which were designed around 1926 and feature a work surface of colored glass.

The experience with wood that Albers was able to gain during the Weimar period in the cabinetmaking workshop made him seempredestined to take over the leadership of the furniture workshop in Dessau in 1928 when Marcel Breuer stepped down (Albers remained until 1929, to be followed by AlfredArndt). There Albers created the bentwood armchair "Ti 244," designed to be easily disassembledand industriallyproduced, which was particularly well suited to meet Hannes Meyer's emphatic call for practical, inexpensive furniture.

Albers's above-mentioned versatility is also demonstrated by his experimental works in the area of type design. The stencil lettering (1925) he designed was based on the basic geometrical forms of the square, the triangle, and the circle (three-quarter circle)—in keeping with the formal reductionism that was popular at the Bauhaus. Albers conceived it as a typeface for advertising and posters with the intention of improving legibility, especially seen from great distances.22 That a typeface does not automatically gain in legibility from being drastically simplified to elementary signs is demonstrated by Albers's combination typeface “3," which, according to its designer, was based"not primarily on considerations of form but of economy."23 In fact, however, it fails to withstand critical examination from either perspective.

![](images/aacd70e7ee9b83b00416bb0f70134857abfecc2ccc3898b7533fd8a62cfe8d08.jpg)

![](images/aaa36733076a3c7d8c23853fb4e6d27a491ac6f97c54b055e872d8fbdcd5b490.jpg)

It was a central pedagogicalgoal ofJosef Albers, especially within the preliminary course, to pass along to his students something of the spirit of this versatility that was so characteristic of the Bauhaus as a whole. The ideas that guided Albers in this will now be discussed briefly.

## Criticism of the School

One of these ideas was a critique of the traditional school—a criticism that began early and remained a central theme for decades. Albers expressed it with acuity in 1924 in his essay “Historisch oder jetzig" (Historical or contemporary).

117 Josef Albers, Armchair Ti 244, circa 1928; bentwood, with flexible backrest; knocks down 118 Josef Albers, Stencil lettering, 1925 From: Offset 7 (1926)

# abedefqhijkimn opqrfsffuyw xqz Tlddkkz ABCDEFCHHJEL mnOPORTSTU

At its heart it has a double nature: it first addresses the fruitless process of the school's circular reproduction of historical teaching matter; and second, the ideology of individualism the school was propagating. In his view, the school had lost

its original goal; it is a teaching institute, and it revolves around a center, its highest figure, the professor. He passes on so-called “established" facts: knowledge, methods, rules, to enable historical thinking. . . . The old school seeks, in addition to its main goal of popular education, to pass on abilities but only a few essential ones: language, writing, arithmetic. Today people want knowledge and so they want scientific de partments. There, people are teaching, writing things up and writing things down, reading things aloud and looking them up, finding snacks everywhere but never eating their fill The highest students are called auditors, they take many books and turn them into a single one, after which they are called doctor and they call up their own auditors: the teaching moves in circles. Today, passing something along without increasing its value is called wangling. So the school produces wanglers rather than creators. Rather than having the students design, it has them take notes. . . . That is a way to make managers, not designers. . .. Today's youth notes the wrong direction: that ... historical knowledge hinders production. And that hearing teaching without being allowed to forget is like taking a meal without a stool to follow, and that the substitute for the latter—regurgitating in exams— is unhealthy. . . . A lot of history leaves little room for work. The reverse—little history and much work—is our task.24

What Albers is articulating here in terms of critical discontent matches point for point the criticism expressed by the reform pedagogues²⁵ of the old tutorial and book schooling, a criticism that came to the fore at the end of the nineteenth and the first decade of the twentieth centuries, with vehemence and a determined will to change it. In the course of his own training as a teacher and subsequent practical experience in schools, Albers would naturally have encountered this critique. It is a critique of the teaching material, of dead book knowledge, of false scientization and superficial knowledge of everything ("finding snacks everywhere but never eating their fill“), of the figure of the dogmatic, authoritarian teacher, but especially of the excess of historically oriented instruction, which rejects the present and any productive approach to it—since Nietzsche, this has been a common theme in the critique of education.26

A second aspect of Albers's critique of the school concerns the "cultivation of the individual." The task of the school as a public institution is, in Albers's view, to “integrate" the individual into the community or society.27 The development of personality is an “extracurricular task," for “the development of personal goals can only be achieved by the individual alone."28 And elsewhere:

Individualism is not primarily a concern of the school, because individualism emphasizes separateness; the school is concerned with integrating into the events of time, into society (state, career, economy). The cultivation of individuality is the individual's own task and not that of collective enterprises like the school. The school should cultivate individuals passively, i.e., by not interfering with personal development. . . . Sociological economy must reject the cult of personality of existing pedagogy: productive individuality asserts itself without and despite education.29

This put Albers, who continued to take a position against "forced," “exalted," or even "lawless" individualism³0 (and whose own artistic practice was anything but free, individual self-expression), in sharp contrast to the reform pedagogues (e.g., Berthold Otto), who sought to redesign the school explicitly to promote the individuality of the child or youth. Albers should rather be seen in the context of approaches to reform pedagogy that aimed to connect the individual more closely to society, which were promoted under slogans like “living community schools" or—with an eye to the overall social and political context—“education for the citizen of the state" (Foerster, Kerschensteiner). It is clear that Albers's dismissal of "the arrogance of the individual" and the “cult of personality" (which naturally flowers most fully in the cult of genius in the arts) was part and parcel with the ideals of community formulated in the Bauhaus program and went together with the guiding social ideas that were influential there. It is equally clear that this view found its most visible expression in the anonymity of the student works (and admittedly also in their interchangeability) produced in the preliminary course.

## Learning by Doing

Albers countered this negative idea—criticism of the existing school—with his own conception of education, which can be expressed in the succinct formula "learning by doing."

We need not examine in detail the assertion that the old tutorial and book schooling barely offeredan opportunity to develop and encourage the creative potential of the students. The appearance ofthearts education movement within the reform pedagogy movement resulted from the realization of the necessity of creative education as a corrective to one-sided cognitive schooling andmechanical acquisitionof traditional content and methods.

In light of the goal at the Bauhaus to educate students to be “human beings who design,"³1 Albers also focused on the creative aspect in his pedagogical approach, which he characterized in the pithy slogan “Experimenting takes priority over studying."32 He objected that the teaching of knowledge and skills based on tradition (both in the old learning school and in an apprenticeship in the crafts based on the principle of imitatio) “hinders creation and invention":

But invention—and re-invention too—is the essence of creativity. . . .

Learned working methods and their application develop insight and skill but not creative energies.

The ability to invent through construction and to discover through observation is developed—at least at first—by undisturbed, uninfluenced, and unprejudiced experiment that is (at first) a playful tinkering with concrete goal, that is to say unprofessional (i.e., not burdened with instruction) experimental work.

It often happens that what were thought to be innovations in the application or treatment turn out afterward to have existed before.

But the result is experienced and thus a possession because it is learned and not taught.

Learning is better, because it is more intensive, than teaching: the more that is taught, the less can be learned.

Sometimes the results of these experiments represent innovations in the application or treatment of material. But even when we evolve methods which are already in use, we have arrived at them independently, through direct experience and they are our own because they have been re-discovered rather than taught. Learning is better, because it is more intensive, than teaching: the more that is taught, the less can be learned.

We know that this instruction by learning takes longer paths, even detours and false paths. But no beginningis straightforward. Andmistakes that are recognized encourage progress. Conscious detours and controlled false paths willsharpen criticism, will make those once burnt twice shy, and will produce a desire to find the right paths.33

"Learning and not teaching," the principle of trial and error as the condition for the creative process, “learning by discovering" as a necessary element of teaching creativity—with these maxims Albers demonstrated that he was an educator who took the progressive educational ideals of the pedagogical reform movement of the period around and after 1900 and productively applied them to the field of artists' training. The pedagogical ideas of the American John Dewey (1859–1952) seem to have had an especially lasting influence on Albers. Dewey's writings on pedagogy—for example, The School and Society and Democracy and Education—were published during the first two decades of the twentieth century in German translation and were extraordinarily stimulating for the reform pedagogy movement, especially for Kerschensteiner. Dewey's pedagogical pragmatism, which he himself characterized as "instrumentalism," can be summarized very briefly as follows: The school should prepare students for practical life; consequently, dead book knowledge (ancient languages, to some extent mathematics as well) should be abandoned. "Knowledge is obtained not through abstract insight but through action itself. ... The experiences gained through action benefit further action."34 "Learning by doing" is thus the fundamental pedagogical principle. As a result, among the reforms that Dewey called for, "conscious development and exercise of manual dexterity. . . is of primary importance"35 (see “The Background of Itten's Pedagogy" in chapter 5).

In Germany this goal of training in manual dexterity was addressed by Georg Kerschensteiner (1854–1932) in his conception of the work school. He encouraged the student's own activity; the acquisition of knowledge through experience, an emphasis on objectivity (meaning the ability to let the ego withdraw and subsume one's own subjectivity to the task), training in fundamental qualities for work—these are some of the main goals of the work school that also played a central role in Albers's concept of education. Albers saw his own view of practical instruction in deliberate contrast to the practical instruction of the work school, which was focused only on craft skills: "Where there is a bit of cabinetmaker's work, a little bookbinding, a bit of tailoring. Then sawing and planing (the most difficult of the cabinetmaker's tasks), filing and driving, pasting and gluing all remain unproductive. Because they satisfy only the need to remain occupied, not the creative urge."36 Although Albers appealed to the handicrafts, he tried to expand the dimension of the creative, which he considered essential. In order to activate the creative energies in the students, in his "Werklicher Formunterricht" (Instruction in form through work), which was anything but a theoretical course in form and design, he rigorously limited the use of tools at first in order to encourage “thinking and fiddling about on one's own"37 and to counteract a tendency to fall into actions guidedby tradition. Part of his pedagogical strategy forbade everything that is known and familiar, and thus does not need to be invented:

For example, out there (in the handicrafts and industry) paper is usually used lying down, flat, and glued, which means that one side of the paper usually loses its expression, and the edge is almost never used. That gives us an opportunity to use paper standing, uneven, sculpturally vivid, with both sides and an emphasis on the edges. ... Thus the material is intentionally treated differently than it is outside but not on principle. Not in order to do it differently (that usually means considering the norm) but in order not to make it like the others do (whereby the emphasis is on the method). That means not to imitate but to search on one's own and to learn to find on one's own—constructive thinking.38

Albers himself characterized his method of instruction as inductive.39 That means that he did not confront his students with an elaborately worked-out theory that was abstract and often remote from the student's range of experience (at the Bauhaus this contrasting approach was especially true of Kandinsky's design theory), but rather he let the students—freed from all theoretical ballast—accumulate primary experiences with the simplest materials of the sort that had traditionally been considered unworthy of art: paper, cardboard, wire, glass, straw, rubber, cellophane, matchboxes, razor blades, phonograph needles, and so on. Uninhibited experimentation with these materials, with an eye to their technical and aesthetic possibilities, led directly to experience with elementary forms that—in the classic manner of reform pedagogy—were reflected on in the process of self-control and collective discussion of the results (for, according to Kerschensteiner, there is no purely manual activity, it always implies an intellectual activity as well). In this way, the students inductively achieve through "precise observation and new vision"40not only a basic technological understanding but also knowledge of universal formal principles like harmony, rhythm, scale, proportion, and symmetry.

In contrast to the official opinion at the Bauhaus (as stated in the founding manifesto) that art cannot be taught, Albers took a somewhat modified standpoint on the issue. In his view, although art “cannot be taught directly," it can be “learned."41 This linguistic nuance contains that which distinguishes the principles of Albers's concept of education from the practical training of the old school, as manifested in the activities of the art academies. The question that remain are: According to what principles does this learning happen concretely? To which of the premises that the teacher establishes is it related? To which fields of sensory knowledge does it relate?

## Exercises with Matière and Materials

A minute reconstruction of Josef Albers's teachings between 1923 and 1933 willnot be undertaken here. It is possible, however, to distinguish three phases clearly. A first phase from1923, after Itten's departure, to the end of the Weimar Bauhaus in 1925, during which Albers, an apprentice at the Bauhaus, was responsible for the socalled work studies that formed part of the preliminary course, then still just one semester, that was officially led by Moholy-Nagy. In 1925 he became a regular member of the teaching faculty as a junior master and until 1928 took over responsibility for teaching the first semester of the now two-semester preliminary course; Moholy taught the second semester. That marked the second phase; and the third began in 1928 with Moholy's departure from the Bauhaus and ended with the final closing of the school in 1933. During this last phase Albers had sole responsibility for the entire preliminary course.42

Substantial knowledge of Albers's pedagogical activities in Weimar is as sketchy now as it ever was. Without going into the Gropius-Itten conflict again, we need only recall that Johannes Itten's teachings were increasingly in the cross fire of the Bauhaus director's criticism, as can be seen clearly from a passage in a circular letter of 13 February 1923 to the council of masters:

The failure of the students . .. seems to me to have been partially caused by the way the preliminary instruction was handled. I have the impression that the goals set for the preliminary instruction—to educate individuals to creative work—would certainly be affirmed by all of us but that the way that it is carried out is not right and that it produces in many of the students the exact opposite of what was sought. The fog of the artist that we wanted to clear grew even more dense. The free speculative work of the first semester and the many intellectual offerings clearly overfed their brains and led some of the young and dependent students to misjudge themselves and become vain.43

In order to establish contact with the workshops as early as possible—the question of the organic continuation of the work of the preliminary course in the workshops and the seamless transition from the preliminary course to the practical instruction was one of the problems of the founding phase of the Bauhaus that ultimately remained unsolved—in the spring of 1923 Gropius proposed introducing the work studies as an obligatory part of the preliminary course: "Albers, who has sufficient technical and pedagogical abilities, will oversee everything."44 It soon became evident, however, that this newly conceived preliminary instruction required adjustments to the organization: "There are so many sides to the preliminary course that for most of the participants half a year is too short. For that reason, starting in the fall, the preliminary instruction will last one year."45 The intended date for this change was 1 October 1924.

The first question is what this work study, which from 1923 on was an integral part of the preliminary course and required for all new students, looked like. The work study [Werkarbeit] should not be confused with the practical instruction [Werklehre] that followed the preliminary course. A text that Albers wrote from the great Bauhaus retrospective in New York in 1938 makes clear that this workshop course continued to be oriented around the particulars of the specific crafts and did not yet have the "abstract character"46 of the later basic course in Dessau (which is evident from the objects produced at the time: simple containers, children's toys, toy furniture, and so on):

The work with materials in this course was planned to prepare the first semester students for later craft-studies in the various Bauhaus workshops. The students were introduced to a simple and elementary, but appropriate use of the most important craft materials, such as wood, metal, glass, stone, textiles and paint, and to an understanding of their relationships as well as the differences between them. In this way we tried, without anticipating later workshop practice, and without workshop equipment, to develop an understanding of the fundamental principles of materials and the principles of construction.

To this end we analyzed typical treatments and combinations of materials, and worked them out with our hands. For instance, we visited the workshops of box, chair and basketmakers, of carpenters and cabinet-makers, of coopers and cartwrights, in order to learn the different uses of wood, the different characteristics of flat grain and quarter-sawing, split, bent and laminated wood, and to learn the various methods of joining: glueing, nailing, pegging and screwing.

We tried to apply our knowledge to the making of useful objects. . . . Thus, at first, we studied material more or less on a traditional handicraft basis.47

In Dessau the contours of our image of the course begin to be defined more clearly, aided by the large numbers of photographs by Erich Consemüller and other Bauhaus students that document the student works. The method of an eclectic mix of specific techniques taken from various crafts was abandoned by Albers as pedagogically inadequate (“finding snacks everywhere but never eating their fill"). This was not only because theemphasis was laidtoo much on imitative drilling of traditional techniques that hampered innovation and set strict limits on the creative explorationof new possibilities of workingwith materials and tools, but also because Albers recognized that this kind of use of specific crafts to prepare for the experimental work in the teaching workshops was still too dependent on specialization andthat theopportunities for transferring experiences from one area to another were too limited.

Consequently, Albers thoroughly eradicated the residuals of the specialized training in the crafts from his version of the preliminary course and built up his instruction upon two pillars: on the exercises with matière [Materie]andmaterials [Materia/]—two terms that, because of their similarity, have naturally been confusedagain and again.

The exercises with matière show that Albers was building on the work of Itten and Moholy-Nagy. With respect to terminology, he was closer to Moholy. The assemblage character of the individual works that resulted, however, is more reminiscent of Itten.

The object of the exercises with matière, as with Itten and Moholy, was the exploration of the external appearance of things—-the epidermis, to borrow a term from Moholy-Nagy. Albers also borrowed from Moholy the terminological distinction between "structure” [Struktur], “sur-

119 Matière exercises with paper, tracing paper, glass, wire, machine wire, sandpaper, gauze, circa1928. “Juxtaposition of matter" and“Finding relationships" Photo: Erich Consemüller

![](images/4e04960bb61948f0f1ed81919a24daeebab22340f248e9e23abb0a434c5358ee.jpg)

![](images/1651d5870f2615fb759a2db4ff9af3f53cc8f1e09921cf5753061029b35b1fdd.jpg)  
120 Ursula Schneider, Matière and gravity study, based on contrasts of materials, circa 1928; wire mesh, cellophane, rubber Photo: Erich Consemüller

face aspect" [Faktur], and "texture" [Textur], though Albers attributed to them different meanings:

“Structure" refers to those qualities of surface which reveal how the raw material grows or is formed, such as: the grain of wood or the composite structure of granite. "Facture" refers to those qualities of surface which reveal how the raw material has been treated technically, such as the hammered or polished surface of metal, or the wavy surface of corrugated paper. "Texture" is a general term which refers to both "structure" and "facture," but only if both are present. For instance, the “texture" of polished woodreveals both the “structure"(grain) and the "facture" (polishing).48

In Albers's preliminary course, too, the goal of the exercises with matière was a “feeling for materials"49 that was as highly differentiated as possible, first through “juxtaposing the matière" and "finding relationships":50 "Just as green and red complement each other, hence are both contrast and balance, so can, say, bricks and burlap, glass and stearin, fly screen and wool relate to each other."51 It is clear that Albers was here going back to Itten's general theory of contrasts, which is demonstrated, for example, by a "matière and focus study" by Ursula Schneider that is structured according to the following contrasts:"transparent-translucent, flat-linear, straight-bent, standing-hanging, colored-uncolored, light-heavy, shiny-matte, elastic-stiff, pliable-vibrating. Tiny stand. In wire mesh, cellophane, and rubber."52 What distinguished these kinds of matière studies from similar exercises in Itten's course was above all the choice of materials. The assemblages created in Itten's course were made from various discarded materials and had distinctly picturesque qualities. Those produced in Albers's course used industrial products andwere more sober andfunctional. Moving beyond this difference, which in itself is naturally significant in terms of the semantics of the materials, it is also clear that in Itten's course the matière studies often produced objective, Dada-inspired assemblages of figures, whereas in Albers's preliminary course a method that would attribute meanings to the objects beyond the simple demonstration of the effects of matière was, as a rule, not permitted. To put it in terms of art: “An art that presents rather than represents."53

![](images/93474276a17f0f3895788068dfecc8446819847eef5239c30f73e61b8e11c774.jpg)  
121 Pius Pahl, Sketched matière study, 1930, pencil

In his preliminary course Albers took from Moholy-Nagy the“systematic ordering" of materials “in increasing and decreasing steps between two polarities" that had admirably demonstrated its worth as a methodical instrument to develop the sensitivity for “the smallest changes in degree and the slightest transitions: “tactile scales from hard to soft, smooth to rough, warm to cold, angular to amorphous, polished smoothness to viscous suction. Optical matière scales, for example, tight-mesh-loose-mesh, transparent-translucent, clear–cloudy-opaque."54

122Material exercises with paper; Materialübungen in Papier; in der Mitte eine Übungsarbeit von Hin Bredendieck, 1928 Photo: Erich Consemüller

That is the second aspect of Albers's matière studies, which is supplemented by a third, which also points back to Moholy. In order to develop a sensitivity to materials, Albers not only had students arrange surface aspects and textures in free compositions or in systematic “matière scales," he also hadthem draw or paint them, translate them into other materials, colors, and light intensities, eveninvent newsurfaceaspects and textures,55 in order to stimulate their creative imaginations by sharpening their perceptions of objects.

In Moholy's course, the equilibrium studies were given more time than the exercises with tactile and surface aspects; in Albers's course, by contrast, the real emphasis was placed on the material exercises. Here we find what is clearly Albers's most originalandunmistakablecontribution to the pedagogy of the Bauhaus. As we have seen, we must distinguish strictly between matière and material exercises. In contrast to the matière exercises, which aimed to help develop the sensory recognition of the surfaces of materials, the ma-

![](images/c5acb43ca11bdc3f5889cb85b3467c96ca680b2afe5d8372643bacbcf3b2bb58.jpg)

![](images/4d49a6f67ad6ff41ed3373b44ad92908676b738f713ca7e25c709a2eaaf66e41.jpg)

terial exercises were concerned with exploring immanent features of the materials, such as stability, load-bearing capacity, strength, and so on, that is, to examine their “inner energies."56 In addition to itsspecifically pedagogical justification—namely, the introduction into the practical training of the various Bauhaus workshops on the broadest possible, unspecialized basis—the significanceof thematerial studies had a broader rationale that derived from the spirit of the time: “We findourselves in an economically oriented age. Formerly the bonds that came from worldviews were more important. Today no one can exist without considering economic aspects: we are concerned with economic form... Economic form results from the function and the material. The recognition of the function is, of course, preceded by the study of the material. Thus our considerations of form will begin with the study of the material."57

123 Walter Tralau and Arieh Sharon, "Material exercises in paper. Exercises in stability and construction without waste. Left: Standing sheet of paper, sculpturally folded and bent into positive and negative movement; Walter Tralau. Center: Arched, interlocking paper foldout. Semicircular rings at right angles with recessed clamping device; Arieh Sharon. Right: Variant of center exercise. Width of semicircular rings increase in geometrical proportion, overlap of 45°, produces active negative form and active resting form; Arieh Sharon."   
From: bauhaus, nos. 2-3 (1928)   
Photo: Erich Consemüller

This decision against a basic instruction in which students merely paint and draw in favor of a systematic study of materials, of their constructional, functional, and economic requirements and possibilities, was didactically significant in that it related to the Bauhaus founding manifesto of 1919, which read that the “mere drawing and painting world of the pattern designer and the applied artist must become a world that builds again." Thus building—admittedly in a comprehensive sense that does not relate exclusively to architecture but also as the development of the general ability of "constructional thinking"58—is the basic intention of these propaedeutic material exercises. We will examine this in greater detail in what follows.

## Educational Methodology

Hannes Beckmann, a student at the Bauhaus from 1928 to 1931, described his first impressions of Albers's preliminary course as follows:

Josef Albers entered the room, carrying with him a bunch of newspapers, which were distributed among the students. He then addressed us, saying something like this: "Ladies and gentlemen, we are poor, not rich. We can't afford to waste materials or time. We have to make the most out of the least. All art starts with a material, and therefore we have first to investigate what our material can do. So, at the beginning we will experiment without aiming at making a product. At the moment we prefer cleverness to beauty. Economy of form depends on the material we are working with. Notice that often you will have more by doing less.

Our studies shouldleadto constructive thinking. . . . I want you now to take the newspapers you got and try to make something out of them that is more than you have now. I want you to respect the material and use it in a way that makes sense—preserve its inherent characteristics. If you can do without tools like knives and scissors, and without glue, the better. . . .“ And with these words he left the room, leaving us quite flabbergasted. He returned hours later and asked us to put the result of our efforts on the floor.

There were masks, boats, castles, airplanes, animals. . . little figurines. He referred to all this as kindergarten products, which could have been made better in other materials. He pointed then at a study of extreme simplicity, made by a young Hungarian architect. He simply had taken the newspaper and folded it lengthwise so that it was standing up like a folding screen. Josef Albers explained to us how well the material was understood and utilized—how the folding process was natural to paper, because it resulted in making a pliable material stiff. . . . He further pointed out to us that a newspaper lying on the table would be hidden. Now that the paper was standing up, both sides had become visually active. The paper had lost its tired look— its lazy appearance. After a while we caught on to his way of seeing and thinking. Fascinating studies in all kinds of materials, like paper, corrugated cardboard, kitchen matches, wire, metal, were produced [my emphasis—-R.W.].59

This report from a former student is cited here at such length because it clearly reveals the working principles that characterizedAlbers's teaching. These working principles—which, as Albers emphasized, were basednot on a methodology of "fabrication" but of“education"60—are economy of materials and economy of labor.

124 Klaus Meumann, Hin Bredendieck, and Elisabeth   
Henneberger, "Construction studies. Wire and glass em  
phasizing edges. Left: with interlocking tensile clamp   
(contact along a line); Klaus Meumann. Center and   
right: with interlocking pressure clamp (contact at a   
point); Hin Bredendieck. Above left: Sculptural cutting   
and folding surface aspect in paper; Elisabeth Henne  
berger," circa 1928   
From: bauhaus, nos. 2–3 (1928)   
Photo: Erich Consemüller

![](images/d2e331084b42714144a599cbbff9202189339ba5ea6f8c8c5825d879425b1589.jpg)

![](images/9865339a564fe5029d40d33ac6248fca70d89d0036813fcbcc19acf5894056b7.jpg)  
125 Zigzag folding of paper and cellophane (lower center). Upper right: Bellows structure Photo: Erich Consemüller 126 Konrad Püschel, Hanging spatiai sphere construction, 1926–27; cardboard, metal Photo: Erich Consemüller

By economy of materials Albers meant an optimaluse“as muchas possiblewithout loss, without waste."61 Teaching economy of materials meant teaching rational,planned action. “Nothing unusedis permittedin any form, otherwise thecalculationswillnotwork out.Becausè. chance has playeda role. Chance has not been accounted for, and therefore it is unaccountable; moreover, it is thoughtless, because itderives from habit."62 Economy of materials implies discipline, and“cleanliness andexactness are the most important factors in discipline”;63in Albers's view, they are indispensable for aesthetic education, which for him in no way clashed with the goal of encouraging creativity. Economy in the use of materials“leads to an emphasis on lightness,"64 which was a widely accepted goal at the Bauhaus (in Moholy-Nagy's course, for example) andwas most impressively realizedin the realm of product design by Marcel Breuer and his steeltube chairs. The principle of economy of materials was made aesthetically relevant by

the activation of the negative elements (the remainder, intermediate, and subtractive)If the positive and negative elements are considered and valued equally, then nothing is “left over." We no longer essentially distinguish between load-bearing and load; we no longer separate things into serving and served, decorating and decorated. Every element or component of the structure must aid and be aided at the same time, be supporting and supported. Thus pedestal and frame disappear, and with them the monument that uses an excess of substructure to support a paucity of load.65

![](images/0a0253645f6ba38b95237384cf6cb4fa7276ce7345a05561733ac530db75865b.jpg)

![](images/0a9e09a3d7ae61a404d52669269b39455f8d74d2d1d31e105b2f7124baf9e677.jpg)  
127 Takehito Mizutani, Tripartite Sculpture, circa 1928; cut and twisted sheet of brass

(In the architecture of the twenties the principle of activating the negative elements can be studied in an especially distinctive form in the studio and living towers of the Dessau Bauhaus building, with their balconies that seem to have been folded out from the surface of the wall.)

In Albers's view the equal value of positive form and negative space that was characteristic of the material exercises of his students, which were usually based on folding and collapsing, was by no means merely a formal problem. It was a metaphor for an understanding of democracy

128 Alphons Frieling, "Sculptural material study with sheet of aluminum. Emphasis on economy of materials (made from a rectangle with no waste, maximum strength, maximum height), on economy of labor (one tool: metal shears—apart from the pedestal mount— one process: simply cutting with the shears, without turning back at all)," circa 1927   
From: bauhaus, nos. 2–3 (1928)   
Photo: Erich Consemüller

![](images/2ed818ee746633feb2e836bab51a8f4bfa6e507414ae5d9bcd69f1402a47b426.jpg)

that negated the difference between above and below, ruler and ruled. In this sense, aesthetic education was for Albers already an indirect form of political education, though, as the artist put it, "word has not yet gotten around, because the sociological parallels have not been noticed."66

Economy of materials and economy of labor are directly related in Albers's “methodology of education, In his teachings economy of labor was usually practiced by placing a limit of a single work process and, initially at least, by a radical limitation of the use of tools (only at a more advanced stage is the arsenal of tools expanded). A good example of economy of materials in conjunction with economy of means is a sculptural material study in sheet metal by Alphons Frieling, about whichAlbers commented:“Emphasis of material economy (created from a rectangle without any waste, tested as strong as possible, made as tall as possible) and of economy of means (one tool: metal shears, . .. one process: cutting with the shears, without turning back at all)."67

Restricting the students to simple materials and requiring them to use methods based on economy of materials andlabor could be interpretedas pedagogical rigor, which would seem to be incompatible with the goal of developing creativity. In fact, however, the works created in Albers's preliminary course reveal a variety that demonstrate the contrary, namely, that for creative people, despite external restrictions—which are, of course, primarily pedagogically motivated here, although we should not ignore the economic difficulties of the time as part of the general context—the number of possibie solutions is almost unlimited. Moreover, Albers, who on principle offered no model solutions and was wary of correcting his students' work (another ideal from reform pedagogy that Albers shared with Johannes Itten, who was born the same year), believed there was no ideal solution. Rather, in his view there were a number of different solutions, with different advantages, all of which were equally justified as long as the criteria he had set were strictly followed: "relation of effort to effect is the measure for judging the result. One element plus one element must at least exceed their sum by producing an interesting relationship."68

## .Criticism and Reception

Under the directorship of Hannes Meyer, the preliminary course was increasingly in the cross fire of criticism from the left. As was demonstrated in "The Bauhaus Directors as Educators" in chapter 4, the Bauhaus at the time was seeking to move away from being"the art school of modernism" and transform itself into a architectural university with associated design departments. The value placed on artistic training was rigorously limited, and as early as 1929 Ernst Kállai, the editor of bauhaus magazine, wrote in the catalog for a Bauhaus exhibition at the Arts and Crafts Museum in Basel that the preliminary course was an "opportunity to work off artists' complexes," and anyone entering the workshops at theBauhaus has "no more urgent task than to forget the preliminary course."69It was only logical that at time when political forces within the Bauhaus— as in society in general—were becoming more and more polarized between right and left, a group of Communist students would step in with a hectographed appeal that calied for the elimination of the preliminary course. The targets of the critique were Albers's "Werklicher Formunterricht" and Kandinsky's “Analytisches Zeichnen" (Analytical drawing) (for more on this, see "Critical Objections" in chapter 8). The critique of Albers's preliminary course read as follows: "With its practical instruction in form problems and with its experiments in materials, the preliminary course (allegedly) serves to develop a knowledge of these materials and an understanding of their uses. . . . We question the contention that this can be achieved by abstract, hence nonfunctional, use of corrugated cardboard, chicken wire, etc. Contrasting different kinds of material by no means demonstrates their practical value, but is instead a formal 'thing in itself.' . . ."70 Although this criticism was not entirely far-fetched, it was selective and tendentious. It was directed only at the matière exercises and did not address the material studies, which treated the constructional, functional, and economic possibilities of the materials. And though these material studies might seem at first glance to have been formalistic, this appearance is deceptive. They were—or at least were intended to be—exercises that served to train constructional thought and economic action. They were in no case purely formal exercises in which, as Heimo Kuchling asşerts, "the aesthetic refinement overshadows the practical tendencies."71

The desire on the part of several of the students to eliminate the preliminary course was accompanied by the odd notion of establishing a first semester workshop in its place. Workshop instruction in the very first semester would have meant a relapse to positions that Albers himself had overcome in a long clarification process in his pedagogy at the beginning of the Dessau period, namely, to positions that would necessarily have led to amateurish handicrafts (“’a bit of' cabinetmaker's work, ’a bit of' tailoring, ‘a bit of' bookbinding") and would have been incompatible with the need for an elementary education in creativity for future architects and designers (not fine artists, who did not officially exist at the Bauhaus).

Albers's influence on aesthetic education (meaning not his groundbreaking didactic work on color, The Interaction of Color, but rather the material exercises he introduced at the Bauhaus) has been insufficiently explored. Already during the Weimar Republic, Alfred Ehrhardt's Gestaltungslehre (Design theory) of 1932 tried to take Albers's approach and transplant it into arts education in the schools (see "Weimar Republic" in chapter 12), and several art schools of the time also took note of Albers's work.7² In the education of artists and designers after World War Il it was above all the former members of the Bauhaus themselves—Hin Bredendieck (Chicago), Hannes Neuner and Maximilian Debus (Stuttgart), Kurt Kranz (Hamburg), Hanns Hoffmann-Lederer (Darmstadt), and Iwao Yamawaki (Tokyo)7³who propagated the methods that Albers practiced (see "After 1945: The Federal Republic of Germany" in chapter 12). Nor should we overlook the fact that Albers's materialexercises found their way intoartseducation in schools after 1945, even though they were robbedof their genuine intentions, lockedinto formalism, and degraded into a “game with artistic means" (Röttger), serving more the “urge to entertain" than the "urge to design" (Albers). This would indeed be a place for a critique, a critique directed less at Albers than at the fellow travelers and followers who, for lack of ideas or simply to make things easy, 'chose to follow well-trodden paths.

# 8 Wassily Kandinsky (1866-1944)

## Between Theory and Practice

It is not easy to treat Kandinsky's contribution to pedagogy at the Bauhaus with the brevity required here. The artist was active as a teacher at the Bauhaus for eleven years, andthe variety of material that he taught during that time makes a comprehensivepresentation considerably difficult. (It is possible to get some notion of this diversity from a manuscript published in French in 1978, Cours du Bauhaus, for example.)

![](images/9b146e969bb80a58225c6153898fc21f3506e0ec1b7db4656c36efc0064b3cd6.jpg)  
129 Wassily Kandinsky, 1925 Photo: Hugo Erfurt

Overlooking these difficulties, however, it is at least possible to establish in principle that Kandinsky's teachings at the Bauhaus are more interesting in terms of method, as was the case for Itten, Albers, and to some extent Moholy-Nagy, than in terms of content. Kandinsky wrote his book Point and Line to Plane both as a balance sheet of his work at the Moscow Institute for Artistic Culture (Inkhuk) and his teaching activity at the Free State Art Workshops and Vkhutemas (see“Bauhaus andVkhutemas/ Vkhutein" in chapter 4) and as the summa of his first years as a teacher at the Bauhaus. As a theory of design, Point and Line to Plane is based on his theoretical reflections from the period around 1910 and builds on them in a systematic and didactic form. Although it is subject to criticism for its theoretical premises, its often seemingly subjective assertions, and its apodictic formulations, it belongs among the pioneering achievements in the field of rational exploration of the foundations of design. Evidence of the fascination that was evoked by Kandinsky's theory, its internal unity and coherence, can be seen in the numerous subsequent theories of design that were implicitly or explicitly built on Kandinsky's work and sometimes even took over specific formulations (e.g., Boris Kleint's Bildlehre [Pictorial theory]).

Only Paul Klee contributed to the elementary language of the pictorial with similar impact.

Kandinsky came to the Bauhaus in mid-1922, that is, more than a year after Klee. Nevertheless, we are treating him ahead of Klee, despite the chronology at the Bauhaus, because as an artist and theoretician he clearly has priority in the process of "questioning the means." His first theoretical book, On the Spiritual in Art, had already appeared in 1911 (though dated 1912), in which he took up the call,

which even predated Goethe, for the formulation of "hardand fast rules, principles resembling those of the figured bass in music."1 It should be noted here that Kandinsky had completed the manuscript the previous year, that is, 1910. This means that the writing of this theoretical work coincided with the incubation period of abstract or absolute painting (i.e., independent of the figure): Kandinsky createdhisfirst nonobjective painting in 1910. This context is by no means arbitrary, for it casts a spotlight on the dovetailing of theory and practice that is typical of Kandinsky. Art and science seek an inner harmony in the interplay of “hypothetical postulate andpractical experiment,"² as it were.

This special talent for theoretical reflection on his artistic practice and practical application of theoretical reflection is related, no doubt, to the specific circumstances of socialization having to do with the period preceding Kandinsky's career as an artist. Born in Moscow in 1866, he began to study law and political economy at the university in Moscow in 1886. In 1893 he passed his state exams in law, and in 1896 was offered a professorship on the law faculty of the University of Dorpat (Estonia), which he declined. The thirty-year-old went instead to Munich in order to study painting, first at the private art school of Anton Azbé and then at the academy with Franz von Stuck. A decade of intense study of the questions of law had trained his abstract thinking so that in the period that followed he approached problems in the fine arts with a distinctly analytic spirit, probing them rationally and giving them a new direction through thought--and of course artistic action. Eberhard Roters has remarked on this: “He possessed the ability to express his ideas and reflections in logically founded hypotheses and to formulate them clearly and coherently. .. . This ability was of great advantage to him in his teaching, particularly as he had to find entirely new explanations for creative experiments for which no words had previously existed."3

## The Concept of Synthesis

In addition to his ability to think analytically, Kandinsky was also eidetic and capable of synesthetic experiences. The latter in particular has a decidedly synthetic character and as such was significant for Kandinsky's artistic practice and theory of art in equal measure. As an eidetic Kandinsky was, especially as a youth, able to call up vividly events or images he had once seen, sometimes long before. This is an ability that is often found among primitive peoples but is very rare in advanced industrial societies. In his autobiographic "Reminiscences" (1913) Kandinsky spoke in this context of his "visual memory," and he reported that as a boy he was able to paint “from memory" at home the paintings that had particularly enthralled him at exhibitions and to "recite by heart the names of all the shops in a long street, without mistakes," because he saw them before his eyes.4 Later, Kandinsky apparently lost this ability but gained a corresponding increase in his “powers of concentration," which allowed him to penetrate the “inner life of art."5

Kandinsky's synesthesia was still more important for his artistic activity than his eidetic ability. Synesthesia, an intermodal quality, is generally defined as "responding to a stimulus from one sense modality with sensations which belong to another sense modality."6 That is, it refers to the phenomenon whereby automatic experiences appear in conjunction with particular sense impressions from other realms of the senses. Experimental psychology has determined that the percentage of synesthesics is significantly higher among people active in the arts than in the general population.7 Thus it would be superfluous to allude to Kandinsky's synesthetic abilities if they did not play such an important role in his artistic oeuvre and his theory of art. Psychology is aware of a number of synesthetic phenomena. A discussion of dual experiences of associated forms of sensory manifestations must distinguish between associations of perception and perception, of perception and idea, of idea and perception, and of idea and idea. In light of this complexity of the forms of manifestations that can be associated with one another, it is necessary to distinguish between "simple synesthesias" (usually sound-color sensations) and "complex synesthesias" in which “phenomena of varying complexity (e.g., letters, numbers, ... people, landscapes, etc.) are combined."8 Kandinsky seems to have had a form of simple synesthesia, namely, the ability to have a double artisticmusical experience. In this context, Kandinsky remarked, in the “Reminiscences" mentioned above, on a key experience in which visual perception combined with acoustic imagination: Rembrandt "moved [him] deeply" and the "great divisions of light and dark" appeared to him as “a mighty chord" and "reminded [him] immediately of Wagner's trumpets."9

This correspondence between music and visual art is also demonstrated, for example, by a series of woodcuts by Kandinsky, published by Piper in 1913 as K/änge (Sounds), and by the stage composition Der gelbe Klang (The yellow sound), published in 1912 in the almanac Der Blaue Reiter (whose very title suggests an artistic transformation of synesthetic experiences). Der gelbe Klang is also an interesting early document of Kandinsky's ideas on a synthesis of the arts aimed at the integration of various media: theater, music, painting, and dance (something of a common theme of twentieth-century art, extending to the happenings of the sixties).1º Even Kandinsky's later conception of synthesis remained, on principle, within the realm of aesthetics, distinguishing it fundamentally from Gropius's vision. This was true even if it appears that Kandinsky at least temporarily felt an obligation to the idea of an aesthetic and social synthesis under the primacy of architecture while directing the workshop for wall painting.

It wouldbe misleading to attributeKandinsky's concept of synthesis entirely to the author's ability to experience sensory perceptions in two or more ways at the same time; other factors must be considered, In this context, a biographical detail seems especially worthy of note: as a student on an ethnographic study trip in a remote Russian province, Kandinsky encountered houses whose interiors were completely painted with colorful ornaments and decorated with pictures, so that he was surrounded on all sides by painting and had the feeling he had entered into a painting, was moving around and living in it. This experience of a total pictorial space clearly influenced Kandinsky heavily and is presumably not without significance for the development of his concept of synthesis.

It was only a critical diagnosis of his time, however, that led Kandinsky to recognize the "inner necessity" of the aesthetic synthesis he sought. His theoretical writings are filled with an early and continuous critique of the intellectual legacy of the nineteenth century: a critique of the predominance of materialist and positivist thinking, a critique of the splintering of related things, and a critique of economically motivated specialization andof the increasing isolation in all spheres of life.11 In his opinion, the latter was especially evident in education, where colleges produce "highly trained specialists and completely uneducated people"12 and feature “endless varieties of 'specialist training,'which have nothing whatever to do either with education in general, or with each other."13 He felt that traces of a former unity had been retained in the arts only in the church, the theater, and in building but that in the architecture of the nineteenth century the connection between the arts was purely superficial and dispensed with any "internal necessity." For the theater, Kandinsky diagnosed, "in addition to external 'effects,"" a quest to "affect people inwardly, something made possible by Wagner's efforts in the realm of opera."14 For the church—for which all artistic genres served a single internal goal with equal prominence and to the same extent: prayer—Kandinsky noted a merely superficial retention of its old unity with no internal content.

Kandinsky saw tendencies of a new“synthetic art," in which the walls between the genres into which the arts had been divided appeared to be collapsing, in the interaction of music and painting (he mentioned Scriabin's Prometheus) and in dance, at least where it was not a goal in itself but rather an integral element of a work combining music, visual art, and dance. This synthetic art, in an overarching sense, was thus seen in the new unity of art, technology, and science at the Higher Artistic-Technical Workshops (Vkhutemas) in Moscow and the Bauhaus (see "The Bauhaus and Vkhutemas/Vkhutein" in chapter 4). At the Bauhaus, in Kandinsky's words, "the student should, apart from his specialist studies, receive as broad a synthetic education as possible." Ideally, they should be equipped not only as new specialists, but also as new people, for there are "no 'specialized' questions, to be identified or solved in isolation, since in the end everything is interconnected, interdependent."15

These brief notes on Kandinsky's ideas of synthesis seem necessary as background for an adequate assessment of Kandinsky's teaching at the Bauhaus, which at first glance seems extremely analytical and elemental but which ultimately aimed to establish a synthetic art. Therefore, in Point and Line to Plane Kandinsky entertained the expectation that through a "microscopic"16 analysis of form it must be possible to create a "dictionary of elements" which would lead to a "grammar" and finally to a "theory of composition" which "will overstep the boundaries of the individual arts and refer to 'Art' in general."17

## The Influence of Theosophy

In order to be more precise, it is useful to interject some remarks on Kandinsky's intellectual orientation and artistic development between 1908 and 1914. In his groundbreaking study The Sounding Cosmos18 Sixten Ringbom has demonstrated the extent to which—alongside romanticism—theosophical ideas (Madame Blavatsky, Annie Besant,

![](images/d414ce31412327a777a1ed4698e31c83237bd1df3f466acb792e561eecd6dd73.jpg)  
130 Wassily Kandinsky, Composition IV, 1911; oil on canvas

Charles W. Leadbeater, Rudolf Steiner) and other occult and esoteric theories contributed to the development of Kandinsky's ideas of synthesis and acted as a catalyst in the creation of nonobjective "painting as pure art" (to borrow the title of a 1918 essay by Kandinsky). Ringbom makes it clear that the elimination of the objective in Kandinsky's Improvisations andCompositions between 1910 and 1914 should not be understood as the result of a process of abstraction motivated by form but rather as the artist's search for an adequate form of expression for the “epoch of the Great Spiritual" that he evoked.19Objective art was, in Kandinsky's view, a correlate of the materialistic worldview of the nineteenth century. It exhausted itself in depicting external reality and was incapable of internal vision. Only an abstract design language freed of the objective and embracing all genres of art was suited to reviving the spiritual unity—of which an internal orientation formed an essential part—that had been destroyed by the materialist and positivist thinking of the nineteenth century. This internal orientation, which Kandinsky, following the theosophists, saw as the "true essence of man,"20 revealed itself to those sensitive enough to perceive it as an “inner sound."21 This concept, central to Kandinsky's theory of art, goes back directly to Rudolf Steiner, who commented on ur-images in his Theosophie: "As soon as the 'clairvoyant' ascends from the country of the soul into the country of the spirit, the archetypes will also be perceived as sound. . . . Ore only has to imagine that everything that is described as 'image,' as 'luminosity,' is at the same time something musical. Every color, every perception of light corresponds to a spiritual sound, and every interaction of color corresponds to a harmony, a melody, etc."22

It is evident that Kandinsky was especially receptive to such occult-sounding ideas because of his own synesthetic experiences. The subject of his painting between 1910 and 1914 was the exploration of the inner world of the spiritual, whose sounds manifested themselves to him in "pure" forms and colors—that is, those freed from the obligation of being defined as objects.
