In this context, it seems appropriate to mention what Steiner called“colors floating in space,"23 which appear in Kandinsky's painting of that period as a “running-over of color beyond the outlines"24 and which were clearly inspired in their form by several abstract representations that Besant and Leadbeater had illustrated in their theosophical book Thought-Forms (1905; German edition, Gedankenformen, 1908).25(Other possible influences that may have ledKandinsky to nonobjective art include the abstract forms of Jugendstil ornament and Islamic art, which the artist encountered on a trip to Tunisia in 1904–5).

Although we cannot elaborate here on the speculative theories of "thought-forms" and “soul vibrations" developed by Besant and Leadbeater, or on their significance for Kandinsky,²⁶ it should be said that, according to Kandinsky, colors and forms are the external manifestationof "inner content," and that harmonies of color ànd form must be based on "the principle of the purposeful touching of the human soul" (the "soul vibrations”), a principle he also called the principle of internal necessity.²7 Kandinsky did not precisely define this term, which he probably borrowed from Konrad Fiedler's philosophy of art. In light of the genesis of his theory of abstract form elements, however, the following paraphrase seems especially illuminating:

Internal necessity arises from three mystical sources. It is composed of three mystical necessities:

1. Every artist, as creator, must express what is peculiar to himself (element of personality).

2. Every artist, as child of his time, must express what is peculiar to his own time (element of style. . ).

3. Every artist, as servant of art, must express what is peculiar to art in general (element of the pure and eternally artistic . . .).28

In Kandinsky's view, only this element of the "pure and eternally artistic" transcends the individual and is invariable in space and time. The predominance of this third element in a work of art is "the sign of its greatness and of the greatness of the artist," so that the process of the development of art consists "to a certain extent in the ability of .the pure and eternally artistic to free itself from the elements of personality and temporal style."29 Or to put it another way: "this ineluctable will for expression of the objective is the force described here as internal necessity."30

Thus it is the artist's task to realize a maximum of the “pure and eternally artistic," of the "eternal-objective," in the "temporal-subjective,"31 that is, in an individual style that is codetermined by the epoch (the parallels to Blavatsky's phrase “eternal truth"32 are self-evident). The implication of this postulate of objectivity is that the artist must strive for artistic forms that are as free as possible of any subjective elements. It is thus logical that in his book On the Spiritual in Art (first published in 1911 but dated 1912) Kandinsky discussed the possibility of a "figured bass of painting" or a "grammar of painting"³3 on which he would elaborate in his magnum opus of the Bauhaus period, Point and Line to Plane, published in 1926. This is why Kandinsky saw the latter book as an "organic continuation"34 of the work from 1911, even though the ideas taken from the storehouse of theosophy retreated during the Bauhaus period to be replaced by an analytical approach: the goals remained the same.

This is not the place to explore the reasons that led to the decline in theosophy's influence on Kandinsky's thought. Kandinsky's conformity with the goals of theosophy seems in any case to have been limited to a compatibility with his own ideas of a new spiritual unity in the "epoch of the Great Spiritual."35 Even so, both during the period from 1914 to 1921 spent in Russia as a result of the outbreak of the war and in the Bauhaus period from 1922 to 1933, the artist occasionally referred to theosophy and especially to Besant and Leadbeater's Thought-Forms.36 (This book and Leadbeater's Man Visible and Invisible also brought Itten within theosophy's circle of influence in 1918; see "The Background of Itten's Pedagogy" in chapter 5).

## Kandinsky's Inkhuk Program

Although he accepted numerous official posts, Kandinsky can hardly have had a decisive influence on artistic developments in the new Russia after the October Revolution. He was, among other things, a member of the People's Commissariat for the Education of the People, which was ledbyAnatoly Lunacharsky; professor of the Vkhutemas; vice president of the Academy of Studies in the Arts; and commissar of the Russian museums. In 1920 he outlined a program for the Institute of Artistic Culture (Inkhuk) with the goal of combiningMalevitch's suprematism, Tatlin's concept of material culture, and his own theories into a pedagogical program that was as coherent as possible. Although this program was rejected by the majority of Inkhuk members, it is usefui to cite it at length because it already contained elementsof Kandinsky's later teachingat the Bauhaus. The program consistedof two parts, a“[t]heory of separate branches of art" and a “[c]ombination of separate arts to create a monumental art."37

The first part consists of an analysis of the specific qualities of each artistic medium. The point of departure is to be the psychological reaction of the artist to the quality. . . . On the results of such analyses an Art Dictionary of conscious line and forms is to be made coveringevery medium. . . .

Colour is to be examined in (a) its absolute, and (b) its relative values.

a. The colours are to be studied first individually and then in combinations. These studies are to beco-ordinatedwithmedical,physiological, chemical andoccu/t [my emphasis—R.W.] knowledge and experience of the subject, e.g. sensoryassociationandcolour;colour and sound, etc.

b. Colour in combination with drawn forms. This is to be approached by:

1. Combination of primary colours with simple geometric forms.

2. Combination of complementary colours in similar forms.

3. Crossed combinations of primary and complementary colours in simple geometric forms.

4. Primary colours combined with free, exceptional forms.

5. Crossing combinations of colour and forms.38

In the second section of the program Kandinsky called for the creation of a synthetic monumental art that could be most easily realized in the theater because he believed that the various genres of art would there achieve an optimal unity (this harmonizes with Oskar Schlemmer's concept for the Bauhaus stage, see“The Teacher" in chapter 10).

It is not surprising that Kandinsky didnot find acceptance from most of the other artists of the Inkhuk for this program, as it remained focused on the inherent aspects of the artwork and clearly insisted on a psychological, subjectivist position without taking into account the social developments of the early years following the October Revolution. The ideas founded on the artist's theosophicaltendencies wouldhave beensubjected to especially sharp criticism: in studying color he recommended considering insights from the world of magic in addition to knowledge from medicine, physiology, andchemistry. Clearly in response to such criticism, in his essay“Color Course and Seminar," which appeared in the 1923 book Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923³9 and whose content is closely related to the ideas of the Inkhuk program, Kandinsky deliberately avoided mentioning occult disciplines in his discussion of color research, although he did allude— if only in a footnote—to the significance of sociological (!) contexts: “Color, like all other phenomena, must be examined from different viewpoints, in different ways, and by appropriate methods. From a purely scientific point of view, these ways may be dividedinto three areas: that ofphysics and chemistry, that of physiology, and that of psychology."40

As early as On the Spiritual in Art there were frequent references to a figured bass of painting, to a grammar of painting—an idea that appears in the Inkhuk program in the form of a call for an "Art Dictionary," a glossary of artistic means and the possibilities for employing them, which Kandinsky later implemented in his book Point and Line to Plane. Even the Inkhuk program is permeatedwith the dualism, characteristic of Kandinsky's theory, between an analytical and a synthetic principle (individual explorations of elementary pictorial means and the formulation of an abstract language of signs unify the various genres of art, and follow the principle of “internal necessity"); this same dualism will constantly recur in the following treatment of Kandinsky's contribution to pedagogy at the Bauhaus

## Teacher at the Bauhaus

Kandinsky, who made no secret of his antimaterialist and anticommunist views even in socialist Russia, was invited to the Bauhaus in 1921. He arrived in Germany at the end of the year and, following an interim stop in Berlin, took up his activities in Weimar in mid-1922. His appointment by Gropius came at a time during which the Bauhaus—not least under the influence of Theo van Doesburg—was clearly beginning to free itself of its earlier expressionistic tendencies and working to base design more thoroughly in a system of basic elements. After Gropius's double-edged experiences with Johannes Itten, the expectation at the Bauhaus now was that Kandinsky would provide new stimuli with his development of a pictorial figured bass (his book On the Spiritual in Art was already known) and with his active support for a synthesis of all genres of art. Ré Soupault, a student at the Bauhaus between 1921 and 1925, later recalled: "Kandinsky was known to the students. Several of us had read his books, which were much discussed at the Bauhaus. And we knew Der Blaue Reiter. But above all the students were interested in the synthesis of the arts that Kandinsky was striving for. . . ."41

Gropius assigned Kandinsky as a master of form in the workshop for wall painting, which until 1922 had been led by Itten and Schlemmer in alternation. Kandinsky woulddirect it from 1922 until the Bauhaus moved to Dessau in 1925 (the directorship was then taken over by Hinnerk Scheper and later by Alfred Arndt in the interim between 1929 and1931). On taking over the workshop for wall painting, Kandinsky seems to have embarked on realizing his concept of a synthesis, which accorded well with Gropius's idea to unify all artistic genres under the primacy of architecture. Still, even though Kandinsky himself had been engaged as a painter several times for monumental paintings (the entry hall for the .Juryfreie in Berlin, 1922; the music room of the German architectural exhibition, Berlin, 1931), he clearly saw his ideal synthesis differently. Thus the works created in the workshop under his direction—-sketches and finished wall paintings—are few in number. It seems telling, moreover, that, although Kandinsky contributed an essay, "Abstract Synthesis on the Stage, "42 (a sequel to the Inkhuk program and the article "On Stage Composition"), to the great Bauhaus publication of 1923, he included not a single line on problems of wall painting. Only the following year, 1924, did he propose to the council of masters a "systematic program" for the workshop for wall painting:

The workshop for wall-painting differs from all the other workshops of the Bauhaus in that one cannot produce any articles with color alone.

Of the various forces that are inherent in color, the Bauhaus is primarily concerned with that force by which color is able to change a given form so that a new one evolves from the given form. In principle there are two possibilities for this:

1. The concurrence of color with the given form, whereby the effects of the form are increased and a new form is created.

2. The divergence of color, whereby the given form is transformed.43

For Kandinsky, dealing with the related problems results in

[t]wo separate investigations . . . which embrace the nature of color in the sense which is most essential for the workshop:

1. The chemical-physical characteristics of color—its material substance.

2. The psychological characteristics of color—its creative forces.

Associated with these two problems there are two methods of working:

1. Technicalwork—the utilization ofvarious characteristics, various pigments and bindingagents, application of the color.

2. Speculative experiments—analytical and compositional experiments—designs and decoration of surfaces and the treatment of space. . ..

![](images/10f474b9cb55ec23ca34cd77e2da344deadc40345076052b195e24eeaaef97f2.jpg)  
131 Wassily Kandinsky, Ceramic wall in music room of the German Architectural Exhibition, Berlin, 1931 132 Herbert Bayer (left wall), Wall design in sgraffito technique, circa 1923; Rudolf Paris (right wall), Wall painting in calcimine technique, circa 1923 From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

![](images/5832eecb053831d77ad2b7050e9cc10b704ef6ca4f2e3d22918020e4e78b30fb.jpg)

# RAUMGESTALTUNG EINER DURCHFAHRT Maßstab 1:66

![](images/19feacfface47fc74ff91bd93a7767420cec7cb138bca994175e02a538909e10.jpg)  
133 Peter Keler and Farkas Molnár, Color proposal for the design of a passageway using the primary colors red-yellow–blue (regulation), circa1923. From: Staatliches Bauhaus Weimar, 1919–1923

Even the practical outside commissions of the Bauhaus will have to be subordinate to this program, since all attempts to give them equal treatment have failed. Thus, the production work of the workshops should take second place.44

In light of the concrete problems of the relationships between architectonic form and its treatment incolor thisprogram remained strangely undefined and remote from the discussions of the day, for example, in the de Stijl movement or in the circle around Bruno Taut. Apart from a few exceptions, the works created in the workshop for wall painting are not conceived in terms of architecture with the result that they seem like free, abstract compositions applied to the building. It should be emphasized that in this program Kandinsky unambiguouslyadvocated free room for experimentation, which the pedagogical niche at the Bauhaus offered, and took a clear position against the reorganization then under consideration that would adapt the teaching workshops “to production exclusively."45

Kandinsky's real pedagogical effect at the Bauhaus was surely not through the workshop for wall painting but in the required course that was initially offered for the first semester and later in part for the second and then at the end of the twenties extended to the fourth semester.4⁶ The goals, content, andmethodology of this course are discussed in several written sources by Kandinsky himself, and these are the primary basis for the discussion that follows: in addition to the essays “The Basic Elements of Form,"47 "Color Course and Seminar,"48 “The Value of Theoretical Instruction in Painting,"49 “Art Pedagogy,"50 and "Analysis of the Primary Elements of Painting,"51 there is his book Point and Line to Plane (1926), and the manuscripts compiled from the course that were published in 1978 under the title Cours du Bauhaus.

The distiliation of Kandinsky's teaching at the Bauhausis, without a doubt, the compendium Point and Line to Plane (even if problems with color are treated only in passing), and an analysis of the notes andpractical exercises of the students found in the Bauhaus-Archiv in Berlin confirms the view of Max Bill that “the material laid out in Point and Line to Plane [formed] more or less the basis of his teaching, both in the basic course at the Bauhaus and in the 'free painting classes' he taught, a kind of master studio for free painting."52 (The establishment of such “free painting classes" by Kandinsky and Klee in the late twenties, which—institutionally isolated from the energetically encouragedproductive work of the Bauhaus—indulged in a remote ivory tower existence, is in Kröll's view one of the main indicators of the "phase of disintegration," as he characterized the late years of the Bauhaus.)53

Another important source that supplements Point and Line to Plane is Cours du Bauhaus, the work basedon handwritten and typednotes by Kandinsky and translated into French by former Bauhaus students Suzanne and Jean Leppien: its value, however, is diminishedbecause the frequently abbreviated notes are neither chronologically nor thematically arranged.54

## Instruction

Kandinsky's instruction for the preliminary course was dividedinto two main sections: an introduction to abstract elements of form and the course “analytic drawing."55 His teaching method—in keeping with the dualistic approach sketched above—was based on the inseparable relation of analysis and synthesis—"no'either-or,'but’and'"56 —whereby the analysis was understood not as a goal unto itself but as “a means to synthesis."57 We can illustrate this with a number of statements from various essays by the artist:

The method applied to these tasks must be both analytical and synthetic. These two methods are closely bound up with one another, and the second is unthinkable without the first.58

Thus, every individual form of study has before it two important tasks:

1. The analysis of given phenomena, which must as far as possible be regarded in isolation from other phenomena, and

2. The relation of those phenomena which have just been analyzed in isolation to one another— synthetic method.

The first should be as narrow and as limited as possible, the second as broad andas unfettered.59

The main aim of all teaching should be to develop the capacity for thought in two simultaneous directions:

1. the analytical and

2. the synthetic.60

Clearly drawn into the whirlpool of the increasingly rationalistic and scientistic basic orientation at the Bauhaus, Kandinsky spoke out in 1926—the year of his sixtieth birthday, of the publication of Point and Line to Plane, and of the completion of the new Bauhaus building—especially pointedly in favor of the analytic method:

Until now, no planned analytical thought has in fact existed in questions of art, and to be able to think analytically means to be able to think logically. . . . Even “pure" art needs today a more precise, consistent scientific basis. The one-sided emphasis upon the intuitive element, andthe "aimlessness" of the art associated with it have frequently led young artists . . . to ungainly conclusions that distract one from art. "Neue Sachlichkeit" [the new sobriety] would suffice as an example from the present day, trying to give art “political" aims. . . .

The young artist, especially when starting out, must from the outset accustom himself to objective, i.e., to scientific thought. . . . By absorbinghimself in those elements that are the buildingblocks ofart, the student acquires—apart from the capacity for logical thought—the necessary inner feeling for artistic resources. . . .61

In this text Kandinsky attributed an integral pedagogical function to the instruction in painting at the Bauhaus: painting shouldnot be understood as l’art pour l’art, asa specialized, marginalized subcategory (like the free painting class of 1928 on) but as a“co-organizatory force" that enables students to break the boundaries of genre and to create an aesthetic synthesis. The statement that "at the Bauhaus, painting should be the most suitable educatress" because it“has, for several decades, advanced at the forefront of all other art movements and has fertilizedthe other arts—especially architecture"62 where evidence that it was Kandinsky the painter speaking, offering an interpretation that consciouslycontradictedthe view of the primacy of architecture advocated by Gropius in keeping with his own call for universal validity.

It is interesting to see that Kandinsky's sympathy for the analytic principles of the Bauhaus period was accompanied neither by unqualified positivism nor by an elimination of the intuitive components. Although Kandinsky had stated in Point and Line to Plane (in contrast to his convictions during his early years in Munich) that composition is "nothing other than the logically precise organization of those living forces encapsulated within the elements in the guise of tensions"63 and the style of his paintings had clearly transformed from the dramatic and explosive paintings of the prewar years to the cool, precise, geometrically constructed compositions of the twenties, he nevertheless soon realizedthat a composition could never be purely rational, since it could never manage without the corrective of intuition.64 He drew the same conclusion for art pedagogy: "Great periods in art, however, had always had their 'doctrine' or 'theory' whose necessity is just as self-evident as was and is the case in science. These 'doctrines' could never oust the intuitive element, because knowledge is barren in itself. It must content itself with the task of passing on materials and methods. Fertile is intuition, which employs these materials and these methods as means to an end. The end cannot, however, be attained without the means, and in this sense intuition might likewise be considered infertile."65 The same issue of bauhaus in which that essay originally appeared also contained-—certainly not by coincidence but rather from a shared concern with the threat to the original spiritual quality at the Bauhaus that was posed by an increasing orientation toward rational, goal-oriented activity— Paul Klee's now famous remark “We construct and keep on constructing, yet intuition is still a good thing."66

In psychology intuition is a form of problem solving characterized by solutions that occur to someone suddenly and without special effort.67 This stands in marked contrast to an approach to solutions that proceeds "step by step ... without any noteworthy tinge of emotion."68In “Reminiscences" (1913) Kandinsky described in passing the intuitive creative act that all forms came "of their own accord": "presenting themselves fully fledged before my eyes, so that I had only to copy them, or else constituting themselves actually in the course of work, often to my own surprise."69 In Paul Plaut's book Die Psychologie der produktiven Persönlichkeit (1929), he spoke of "so-called 'spiritual' experiences" that poured out of him as ideas that "sometimes 'appear to one' so rapidly and continuously that one has no time to fasten on to them, even to a limited extent. Even this expresses itself in different ways: sometimes one sees only fragments, isolated juxtapositions, pictorial, linear 'sounds,'sometimes the whole picture, which only has to be held fast, i.e., must be given material form."70 This description is Kandinsky's response to his critics, who had accused him of "working too 'consciously,' too much, or too exclusively with [his] 'head'"71 in his boldly tectonic compositions of the twenties, that is, that they lacked intuition or that it played too small a role.

Summing up our discussions so far, we are led to the conclusion that the dualism of the analytic and the synthetic principles mentioned several times above can in some ways be depicted in terms of Kandinsky's view of design as a balance between calculation and inspiration, between rationality and intuition. Nevertheless, in teaching design—that is, when given the pedagogical goal of a propaedeutics of design's elementary means— Kandinsky tended unequivocally to the rational method (and in this respect differed especially from Itten of the teachers at the Bauhaus), as the following citation demonstrates:

![](images/9de65b6055228c5b1cbe55a450b4fa046c8aca328aee02b40c04197ec57366d9.jpg)  
134 Wassily Kandinsky, Yellow-Red-Blue, 1925; oil on canvas

I demand of my students that they think very precisely, that they execute purely cerebral exercises with exactitude; we even discuss the works they submit in purely theoretical terms. But I always emphasize with particular force that this theoretical path, this theoretical point of view is only a way of arriving at the “content," which is why I place particular value upon the living experience of"tensions."

Theory is (especially today) indispensable and fruitful. But woe betide him who would create a “work" in this way alone.72

## Connections to Gestalt Psychology

Before moving on to a more concrete discussion of Kandinsky's teaching and his theory of design, we should comment on the observation made above, which was perhaps not immediately comprehensible, that, despite the "positive"73 analysis of pictorial means that Kandinsky called for and to some extent also achieved at the Bauhaus, Kandinsky ultimately did not give in to pure positivism.

We will recall that the search for the "figured bass," a dictionary of elements, and a grammar of pictorial means (which had already been attempted by Adolf Hölzel, among others) was the result, in Kandinsky's case, not simply of interest in the perception of form but was based, as we have seen, on the intention to give adequate expression in pictorial language to the idea of the "pure and eternally artistic" that was metaphysically conceived but that nonetheless claimed to be objective. If the artist in his early years was not able to find his way to use forms that resulted logically, that is, not purely emotionally,74 by the time of

On the Spiritual in Art he had become aware of the need for rational, constructive compositions.75 A decisive step in this direction is marked by the analyses that appeared in 1919 in the Russian journal Iskusstvo, nos. 3–4, “Little Articles on Big Questions: On Point; On Line."76 These analyses clearly served as the basis for the first section of the later Bauhaus book Point and Line to Plane. If the first section of Point and Line to Plane is dominated by a more positivistic view of the problem along the lines of a detailed investigation of pictorial means, the second section (in which Kandinsky treats surface area)reveals a more holistic point of view that reveals remarkable parallels to the antipositivistic Gestalt psychology that was developing in Germany at the time. L. D. Ettlinger and later Paul Overy have devotedconsiderable energy to demonstrating such parallels or even direct correspondences between Kandinsky's paintings and writings of the Bauhaus period and the ideas of the Gestalt psychologists. Whether or not Kandinsky read the writings of the leading Gestalt psychologists in the twenties (i.e., Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Köhler, and Kurt Koffka), Overy proceeds from the hypotheses that these ideas were constant topics of discussion in the intellectual atmosphere of the Bauhaus and that they would have been especially attractive to Kandinsky."7 (It seems significant in this context to mention that under Hannes Meyer psychology became part of the Bauhaus curriculum and that the lectures on psychology given by Karfried Graf von Dürckheim were based on Gestalt and holistic approaches.)78

Gestalt psychology's approach to science may be broadly defined as antipositivistic because, in sharp contrast to the older elemental psychology, the process of sense perception is understood not as the combination of atomistic, individual sense units that can be counted and measured—that is to say, not as elementary and additive—but as complex and gestaltlike. "Instead of reacting to local stimuli by local and mutually independent events, the organism reacts to an actual constellation of stimuli by a total process which, as a functional whole, is its response to the whole situation."79

Thus, for example, according to Gestalt psychology, a triangle is not perceived as the addition of three lines and three corners but as a holistic gestalt that is more than the sum of its parts. It may be assumed with great certainty that this movement in psychology would have had strong appeal for Kandinsky given its basic antipositivistic stance, its synthetic approach to gestalt, and its assertion of intercultural validity of design principles. This is not the place to go into the details of the principles of design elaborated by Gestalt psychology—closureor completeness[Geschlossenheit], proximity or contiguity [Nähe], good form [Prägnanz], gestalt pressure [Gestaltdruck], figure [Figure], and ground [Grund]. It is enough to point out that Kandinsky's artistic oeuvre during the Bauhaus period and the text and illustrations in the book Point and Line to Plane frequently reveal a remarkable proximity to these gestalt principles. It is unimportant whether a direct influence of Gestalt psychology exists here or whether it might be an example of simultaneity of events; the fact remains that in his search for a figured bass of painting Kandinsky began to formulate at the Bauhaus a design language whose claim to validity could be supported and legitimated by the research of Gestalt psychology—more seriously and convincingly than would ever have been the case with theosophical theories.

## Theory of Design

The main weight of Kandinsky's instruction was placed on an introduction to abstract form elements, his true theory of design (in addition, Kandinsky gave a course on analytical drawing, which will be discussed later). The program of this introduction to abstract form elements retained the same basic structure over a period of years. It began with an introduction that critically analyzed the intellectual situation of the day and determined the standpoint and function of the new synthetic art. Then the following themes were discussed systematically:

1. Color theory (isolated color)

2. Form theory (isolated form)

3. Color and form theory (relationships of colors and forms)

4. Base.80

The course was highly theoretically oriented and—to use a current expression—teacher-oriented, in contrast to the much more task-oriented teaching that was otherwise typical for pedagogy at the Bauhaus. Nevertheless, the lectures were supplemented by small-scale, practical exercises for the students that were intended to aid in the practical acquisition of the theoretical material. The tasks that Kandinsky gave were as a rule so narrowly conceived that the range of possibilities was radically constrained; despite all that, a variety of individual solutions was possible (this was also the principle of Albers's preliminary course). Many of these assignments can be found in the Cours. We cite here just one representative example from the notes of a participant in Kandinsky's course:

In Kandinsky's class we were given a real vacation assignment, but it isn't bad. A square, 30 cm by 30 cm, is to be divided up into 5 by 10 cm rectangles. The following colors are to be used: 3 primary, 3 secondary, and 3 uncolored (black, white, gray). The arrangement of the colored rectangles is entirely up to you as long as they are horizontal and vertical, not diagonal. It's also up to you how often you use each color, so long as every color is used at least once. The task is as follows:

1. emphasize the center

2. balance top and bottom.

In the final session he did not say much new, just something about the tensions in the square; I myself did not understand it completely, andI think it's not that important anyway....81

The last sentence would seem to be not untypical, and it suggests that, despite all of Kandinsky's efforts to give a rational basis to design, it was clearly not always easy for the students to follow their teacher's thinking.

## Color Theory

As part of his introduction to abstract elements of form at the Bauhaus, Kandinsky regularly began with color theory. His pedagogical justification for this was that in an analytical study of elementary pictorial means the phenomenon of“color" would be more accessible at first for students of the first semester than problems of form (here "form" is taken in the strict sense).82 To clarify the terms here, Kandinsky used the word “form" in both a broad and a narrow sense. In the broad sense his concept of form includes color, because—"since neither surface nor volume can exist without color,"83 or in other words, color is always connected to form or itself establishes the form—color is, in Kandinsky's view, one of the abstract elements of form. Form in the narrow sense refers to the basic elements, like the point, the line, the plane, the body, and space, independent of their color.84

![](images/304c12050795cdcca86c37097bfb7ed7b5b4a54506d611ea69501717057e4aeb.jpg)  
135 Hans Thiemann, Student work Emphasis of the Center: Balance of Top and Bottom, 1930

As a painter, Kandinsky considered the problem of color not only in practice but also in theory in an extraordinarily intense way. This occupation found systematic form in his epochal theoretical magnum opus, On the Spiritual in Art (1912).

The theory of color Kandinsky explicatedat the Bauhaus, which was profoundly inspired by Goethe, underwent certain modifications that were, however, hardly reflected at all in Point and Line to Plane (1926). In his second major theoretical work Kandinsky made his central focus an analysis of form (in the narrow sense)—an analysis he had called for, but left unfulfilled, in On the Spiritual in Art—without fundamentally revisiting the theory of color. All that is new in Point and Line to Plane is the methodological explorations of the correspondences between forms and colors, which will be discussed in more detail below.

"When the spirit emerges from within it appears as color, as sound. Color and sound are nothing but pure spirit; exactly the same thing that we discover within us when we truly understand ourselves."85 This quotation from Rudolf Steiner may help to suggest the horizon against which Kandinsky developed his theory of color in On the Spiritual in Art. His interest was not to develop a theory of color that could be applied instrumentally. that would make it easier for thepractitioner to mix paints, or that would provide insights into the relationships among the colors, but rather to explore color as a means to have “a direct influence upon the soul."86In such an investigation, whose results—as Kandinsky emphasized—are based on "empirical-spiritual experience and... not upon any positive science,"87 colors are studied first individually, that is, in isolation, to discover their particular psychological effects.88 The parallels to Goethe's Farbenlehre (Theory of Colours), specifically to the didactic section (section 6, "Effect of Colour with Reference to Moral Associations"), are unmistakable, which has led some critics to claim that Kandinsky's color theory is merely a paraphrase of aspects of Goethe's theory.89 In the brief discussion of Kandinsky's color theory that follows, the relevant chapter from On the Spiritual in Art is supplemented by pertinent passages from the Cours du Bauhaus where the latter help to complete or clarify the former, especially when they provide new perspectives.

Whereas Goethe in his much more comprehensively conceived theory of color dealt in detail first with the origin of colors and especially the basic colors yellow, blue, and purple (polarity and intensification) before exploring the effects of colors individually and then the "harmonic," "characteristic," and"characterless combinations" of colors, Kandinsky began immediately by characterizing the effects of individual colors. He made the fundamental distinction between the "warmth and coldness of a color" and its "lightness or darkness," resulting in "four main sounds":90

1. warm-light

2. warm-dark

3. cold-light

4. cold-dark.

Kandinsky defined the warmth or coldness of a color very generally as its inclination toward yellow or blue and designated this color pair the "first great contrast." The warm (yellow) moves toward the observer; it is a movement toward the physical, because "yellow is the typical earthly color." The cold (blue) withdraws from the observer; it is a movement toward the spiritual, because "blue is the typical heavenly color."91 Although the proximity to the ideas of romanticism generally and Goethe's reflections in particular is striking, what is new with respect to Goethe is that yellow is also characterized by an eccentric (centrifugal) movement and blue by a concentric (centripetal) one. Sixten Ringbom has convincingly demonstrated that Kandinsky was inspired here by Arthur Osborne Eaves's book The Colour Cure (1901; translated into German as Die Kräfte der Farben in 1906), an occult book on chromotherapy, i.e., on the healing powers of colors, in which blue is described as, among other things, "astringent."92

At the Bauhaus Kandinsky characterized the contrasting pair of yellow and blue as follows:93

<table><tr><td>White</td><td>Black</td></tr><tr><td>1. wall of infinite</td><td>1. infinite, bottomless</td></tr><tr><td>strength</td><td>hole</td></tr><tr><td>2. all possibilities</td><td>2. no way out</td></tr><tr><td>3. maximum light: the sum of all rays</td><td>3. absolute darkness: no rays</td></tr><tr><td>4. highest sound,</td><td>4. deepest sound,</td></tr><tr><td>inaudible</td><td>inaudible</td></tr><tr><td>5. birth</td><td>5. death</td></tr><tr><td>6. silence</td><td>6. silence</td></tr></table>

136 Contrasting pair I: yellow-blue From: Wassily Kandinsky, On the Spiritual in Art

<table><tr><td>Yellow</td><td>Blue</td></tr><tr><td>1. eccentric</td><td>1. concentric</td></tr><tr><td>2. protruding</td><td>2. receding</td></tr><tr><td>3. upward; to the edges and beyond:</td><td>3. downward; introvert- ed; within the limits:</td></tr><tr><td>external tension explosive, aggressive,</td><td>inner tension evasive, secluded, with-</td></tr><tr><td>forceful, unashamed, insane</td><td>drawn (snail shell)</td></tr><tr><td>active: positive, materi- al, masculine?</td><td>passive: negative, ab- stract, feminine?</td></tr><tr><td>Sense of touch: hard, sharp</td><td>soft, without resistance</td></tr><tr><td>Sense of taste: sour</td><td>flavorless (fresh figs) aromatic; violet (rose)</td></tr><tr><td>Sense of smell: pungent (onion, vinegar, acids)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Sense of hearing: high, penetrating (canary)</td><td>deep, heavy (cuckoo)</td></tr><tr><td>Music: the fanfare is yellow; high pitch</td><td>the organ is blue; deep pitch</td></tr></table>

These sorts of "characteristics" of colors—in this case, of yellow and blue—document once again the synesthetic abilities or tendencies of Kandinsky the artist and theoretician. It should be immediately obvious that they remain largely subjective; this could be easily demonstrated by any psychological investigation, for example, using the methodology of the so-called semantic differential.

As part of his exploration of the "second great contrast," that of light and dark, Kandinsky arrived at the "noncolors" white and black. He localized these noncolors in the proximity of the colors yellow and blue, respectively (cf. Goethe, §§ 765, 766, 778); like yellow and blue, according to Kandinsky, white and black have “the same movement towards or away from the spectator, although not in dynamic, but in static, rigid form."94 In his Bauhaus manuscripts he spoke in this context of a movement in spe, a movement in embryonic state, and in terms of temperature he characterized white as internally warm (proximity to yellow) and black as internally cold (proximity to blue). In a table in the Cours he characterized this pair of contrasts as follows:95

![](images/074722c9e24d86952a648ff67ebe7acb58d2868dcdb19bd3872362409d8e7d26.jpg)

Whereas the silence of white, in Kandinsky's view, "is not dead but full of possibilities," black seems like "an inner sound of nothingness bereft of possibilities, a dead nothingness as if the sun had become extinct, an eternal silence without future, without hope. . . . Black is something extinguished, like a spent funeral-pyre, something motionless, like a corpse, which is dead to all sensations, which lets everything simply pass it by. It is like the silence of the body after death, the close of life. Black is externally the most toneless color, against which all other colors, even the weakest, sound stronger and more precise."96(lt should be noted here that metaphors of life and death also played a role in Klee's writings, namely, in the treatment of the spiral; see "Theory of Form" in chapter 9.)

Kandinsky described gray as a mixture of the two noncolors, as "toneless and immobile." This disconsolate aspect increases the darker the gray is, whereas a light gray retains a touch of hope.97 Both in On the Spiritua/ in Art and in the Bauhaus manuscripts there is, with reference to Delacroix, a remark that gray can also result from an“optical mixture of green and red."98

Green and red mark the "third great contrast." In “absolute green," understood as an “ideal balance" between thediametrically opposed colors yellow and blue, both the horizontal and the circular motions of these two original colors cancel each other out so that green seems motionless and the epitome of calm, passivity, and self-satisfaction: "Thus, pure green is to the realm of color what the so-called bourgeoisie is to human society: it is an immobile, complacent element, limited in every respect."99 Red, green's complement, is on the surface as motionless as green, that is, it has neither eccentric nor concentric, neither protruding nor receding movement. Internally, however, red seems to be “a highly lively, living, turbulent color . . . of immense, almost purposeful strength. In this burning, glowing character, which is principally within itself and very little directed toward the external, we find, so to speak, a kind of masculine maturity."100

Thepresentation of the effects of the red ranging from "ideal red" turning to "warm" and then "cold" red (an inclination to yellow or blue, respectively) will not be discussed here. If yellow is added to the warm red, blue to the cold red, the result is the "fourth great contrast," namely, that between orange and violet. In both cases, the same movements obtain as for yellow and blue but to a lesser extent. Orange "resembles a man sure of his powers, and for this reason evokes a particularly healthy feeling." Violet, by contrast, has "something sad, an air of something sickly, something extinguished about it (like a slag heap!). It is not for nothing that this color is considered suitable for the clothes of old women. The Chinese in fact use it as the color of mourning."101

![](images/bd00638e9097d1ec05211ff7df594e2197cb95690ef6ebdaad2f05bfab3e53b3.jpg)  
137 Contrasting pair il: Red-green; Contrasting pair IV: orange-violet From: Wassily Kandinsky, On the Spiritual in Art

Kandinsky's depiction of the four great contrasts in the form of a circle (Orobouros motif as the symbol of infinity and eternity) seems problematic for two reasons. First, because yellow and blue (l), red and green (Ill), orange and violet (IV) are opposed on the circle in such a way that the resulting sequence of colors defined by the mixture of pigments is wrong: yellow-greenviolet-blue-red-orange. Second, because the contrast of white and black (ll) remains outside the circle; thus, although the contrasts are visible, the complex relationships of the colors to one another are not—as they are for example in Runge's color sphere.102

Now is the time to briefly call attention to some conceptual differences between the version of Kandinsky's color theory in On the Spiritual in Art and in the Bauhaus manuscripts:

1. Kandinsky's epistemological position seems to have changed at the Bauhaus. As the author of On the Spiritual in Art, a work based exclusively on the results of "empirical-spiritual experience"—as he put it then—Kandinsky ignored both physics and the "material conditions" of pigments; now he more frequently introduced discoveries from physics into his course and, more importantly, started with pigments, that is, with the "material substance of colors."103

2. A second profound difference is that Kandinsky at the Bauhaus no longer exclusively focused on the contrasting pairs of colors in his teaching but rather started with the three primary colors—yellow, blue, and red—as had been the general practice since the eighteenth century. This was not only in accordance with Itten's theory of color, which was inspired by Hölzel, but also with the dogma of primary colors advocated by Mondrian (whose ideas, like Kandinsky's, were indebted to theosophy), Theo van Doesburg, and other members of de Stijl. This sort of correspondence should not immediately lead us to believe it was a case of influence; in the work program for Inkhuk of 1920 Kandinsky—surely independently of Itten, Mondrian, or van Doesburg at this point—called for the exploration of the "elementary color triad" yellow-blue-red.

At the Bauhaus, Kandinsky considered red not primarily in its opposition to green but in its relation to yellow and blue. Red is thus the color that "fills the gap between yellow and blue," that lies "in the middle between yellow and blue," and that results from intensifying yellow and blue (cf. Goethe §§ 699 ff.).104 Among the "great contrasts" that Kandinsky explored in On the Spiritual in Art only the green-red pair was truly complementary, that is, only these two colors become gray when mixed. Only by returning to the three primary colors at the Bauhaus and starting out from the material properties of pigments was Kandinsky able to overcome the inconsistencies that adhere to the theory of color formulated in On the Spiritual in Art. From that point on he was working with the secondary colors (mixtures of two primary colors) that are derived from the primary colors yellow, red, and blue—orange, green, violet—and the tertiary colors (mixtures of two secondary colors), citrine, russet, and olive. (This terminology is reminiscent of Hölzel's color theory, to which Kandinsky may have been introduced at the Bauhaus by Hölzel's students Johannes Itten and Oskar Schlemmer.) The complementary color pairs (pairs of the primary and secondary colors that produce gray when mixed) are yellow-violet, red-green, blue-orange. Kandinsky spoke in these cases of the "great contrasts." When the primary colors are replaced with tertiary colors, the following pairs result: citrine-violet, russet-green, olive-orange. In their greatly weakened form, these contrasts of secondary and tertiary colors result, according to Kandinsky, in a "small contrast."105

![](images/2f6b4c53af996d356317db3bad35fb895060cc55b10f1c0dadfaedd274e31174.jpg)  
Die Gegensätze als Ring zwischen zwei Polen = das Leben der einfachen Farben zwischen Geburt und Tod. (Die römischen Zahlen bedeuten die Paare der Gegensätze.)  
138 Color circle between the poles white and black From: Wassily Kandinsky, On the Spiritual in Art

3. These brief remarks on certain combinations of colors and their contrasts bring up a third point in which the earlier version of Kandinsky's color theory differs from the later one. Both Goethe and Runge concerned themselves more or less intensively with problems of a theory of harmony for colors. Both took it for granted that certain color combinations are harmonious while others are less so. Thus Goethe distinguished between "harmonious," "characteristic," and "characterless" combinations; and Runge distinguished between "harmonious,""disharmonious," and "monotone" ones. Goethe treated only dyadic color relationships, whereas Runge also had triadic ones in the case of disharmonious combinations.

Although in On the Spiritual in Art Kandinsky clearly considered it practical for methodological reasons to isolate the colors and examine them individually, he was of course very clearly aware of the fact that color is never an isolated stimulus but always occurs in context. "Form alone,"Kandinsky said, "can exist per se. Not so color."106Despite this recognition, however, he didnot attempt to go systematically through the relationships among colors and to examine their possibilities for harmony. In contrast to Goethe, Runge, or Hölzel, he was of the view that in a time of great spiritual antagonisms—he seems to have taken little notice of socialantagonisms—"harmonization on the basis of simple colors is precisely the least suitable."107 Consequently, he recognized that combinations of colors that, following traditional aesthetic rules, have generally been considered dissonant contain possibilities of new harmonies: "For example, the juxtaposition of red and blue, these physically unrelated colors, is today chosen as one of the most strongly effective and most suitably harmonious because of the great spiritual contrast between them."108Apart from this one concrete example, however, in On the Spiritual in Art Kandinsky retained his fundamental reserve with respect to a harmony theory of colors, tha is, he did not take the trouble to analyze the relationships of colors to one another based on a new, revised concept of harmony. More or less in passing, he merely remarked that colors seem dull, weak, and powerless against white, whereas most seem precise, strong, and powerful against black;109 and that red is especially "beautiful" as a complementary color to green in folk art and in folk costumes.110

In addition to the exploration of the corresponding "internal sound" of the individual colors on the basis of introspection and phenomenology, there was also a more in-depth study of the relationships of colors to one another at the Bauhaus, as is clear both from the Cours and the student works produced in the class. For example, Kandinsky had them systematically examine how the effects of colors change according to the size of the plane and the color of the surroundings.111 In relation to the three dimensions "brightness," "size," and "temperature" he selected several examples from the universe of possibilities to show how a small fleck of color (above the line) on col-.ored ground (below the line) changes its apparent color based on the surrounding color:112

<table><tr><td>Yellow Blue</td><td>yellow: brighter, bigger, colder</td></tr><tr><td>Yellow Black</td><td>yellow: brighter, bigger, colder</td></tr><tr><td>White</td><td rowspan="2">white: brighter, bigger, colder</td></tr><tr><td>Black</td></tr><tr><td>Blue Yellow</td><td>blue: darker, smaller, warmer</td></tr><tr><td>Blue White</td><td rowspan="2">blue: darker, smaller, warmer</td></tr><tr><td>Black black: darker, smaller, warmer</td></tr></table>

The goal of these sorts of experiments— which, under the rubric "the interaction of color," Albers would later make the theme of both his aesthetic practice and his theoretical investigations—was to make the students aware that color can never be an absolute given for the artist, as it may seem to be at times in On the Spiritual in Art, but is always relative and highly dependent on context (this, by the way, is also one of the central messages of Johannes Itten's The Art of Color).

We can only make guesses about Kandinsky's view of the color harmony problem during the Bauhaus period. It can be said, however, that the Cours has no hint of a skeptical attitude toward the question of harmony like that found in On the Spiritual in Art. On the contrary, in his course Kandinsky referred several times to Ostwald's "quantifying theory of color" and the latter's book Die Harmonie der Farben (The harmony of colors).113 According to Ostwald, the "main principle of color harmony" is that "Only those colors can be harmonious or compatible whose features stand in certain simple relationships to one another."114 Harmony represented for Ostwald a system of order based on measurement, and it is certainly plausible that in his search at the Bauhaus for the figured bass of painting Kandinsky might have agreed with this conception of harmony. This is supported by an idea already expressed in 1912 and reinforced in Point and Line to Plane of 1926, namely, that "the future theory of harmony for painting" was to be found where the relationships among the elements may be “expressed in a mathematical form," a thought culminating in the statement that "in every art, number remains the ultimate form of abstract expression."115

## Theory of Form

This presentation of Kandinsky's theory of form (like the ones below on the "Theory of Color and Form" and the "Picture Plane") follows in its main aspects the corresponding passages in Point and Line to Plane both in sequence and argument. Material from the Cours is introduced only in cases where amplification seems necessary.

Kandinsky's theory of form comprises the investigation of the elementary pictorial means of the point and the line and the three basic forms that result from these elements, namely, the circle, the triangle, and the square.

The geometrical point is, according to Kandinsky, invisible, nonmaterial. In language it indicates silence; it is the “symbol for interruption, for non-being . . ., and at the same time it is a bridge from one entity to the next."116 Pictorially, the point is the result of the tool's first contact with the material surface, with the picture plane. This contact of the tool with the surface implies that the point, which in mathematics remains invisible and nonmaterial and exists only as a thought construct, obtains a certain size and definite outline as part of its pictorial materialization so that it can be distinguished and defined against its surroundings. Although Kandinsky believed that the point can be defined as the smallest of theelementary forms, this “smallness" is relative. Its relativity results from the ratio of point to picture plane and from the relation of the point to other pictorial elements found on the picture plane.117 With respect to its outline (its form), the point can be thought of in the ideal as round, that is, as a circle. In practice, however, it takes infinitely many forms. Even so, the point was for Kandinsky “inwardly [the] most concise form. It is introverted. . . . Its tension is ultimately always concentric. ... The point burrows into the surface and establishes itself for all time." Because it has no inclination to move, the factor of time is almost completely excluded so that it may be considered the "temporally most concise form."118

The simplest composition for Kandinsky, then, is when a point is placed at the center of a quadratic picture plane. Here the tensions between point and plane are absolutely balanced, the dyad of point-plane takes on the character of a unison. Only in the case of a central placement of the point can a dyad be heard: the absolute sound of the point and the sound of the surrounding picture plane. Adding points would establish simple rhythms at first and then, as the points pile up and grow dense, surface aspects.119

One suggestion of the analytical and elementary approach that characterizes Point and Line to Plane, as discussed earlier, is Kandinsky's understanding of the line as the trace of a point's motion. As Clark V. Poling has shown, the artist is here following a thesis published by the physicist and physiologist Hermann von Helmholtz in 1876 that “a point in movement describes a line."120 This means that the line, though fundamentally different from the point in character, nevertheless owes its existence to it, and thus it is a secondary pictorial means. Under a self-imposed constraint to arrange the elementary pictorial means according to their degree of complexity into a logical; hierarchical system, it seems to have escaped Kandinsky that as a pictorial means the line, not the point, is both phylo- and ontogenetically primary. This can be seen equally well in the art of primitive peoples and the drawings of children (on this, see Klee's definition of the line in his "Contributions to a Theory of Pictorial Form," discussed in “Theory of Form" in chapter 9).

If the line is understood as a point in motion, it means that energies or forces have acted on it. Kandinsky distinguished between two fundamental possibilities: (1) the effect of a force that leads to straight lines; and (2) the effect of two forces that result in either zigzag or curved lines.

The three basic types of straight lines, of which all other straight lines are merely modifications, are the horizontal, the vertical, and the diagonal. The horizontal was, for Kandinsky, a "cold, basic support"; the vertical, by contrast, was "warm"; and the diagonal, because of its "equal tendency toward each of them," was described as "cold-warm."121 It is typical of the axiomatic form of Kandinsky's theory of design that he abjured from explaining or justifying the properties he assigned to the three types of lines.

“Axiom" is not understood here as a claim whose truth has been confirmed so frequently by experience that it can be considered absolutely certain but as a claim about whose truth nothing is known; thus it is placed at the beginning of a theory to be constructed in order to tèst the consequences that result. The consequenċes Kandinsky drew from his theory of design wìll be discussed below. Paul Overy believes that Kandinsky's

139 Relativity of the point; the point appears as a plane here

From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane .axioms correspond approximately to the findings of experimental psychology. He refers to "Gibson's description of how the horizon line or ground line seems to be one of the basic constants of visual perception" and comments, “[a]gainst the ground line vertical lines are usually seen as 'figure,' that is as objects, people etc.—things with which we are involved and interact in a more personal and intimate way than we do with 'ground.'122

![](images/dbd25140f53f871a0c1378f1e9f4c6cf35de73e5b7263f586654877beb840be8.jpg)

![](images/7bdc1d9bb6cce2c5c943bafdf9283379be80f23dbe1575a297b2c45b6a706773.jpg)

140 The ur-composition: the point in the center of a square

From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane 141A point made up of small points (spray technique) From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane

![](images/84196302fa8a0562c95abe55c8a63b1ae3b14fe85a4b35f80df2b28c86854879.jpg)

In his course Kandinsky always analyzed the relation between horizontal and vertical in depth, specifically by introducingexamples from the world of flora and fauna123 as well as from art history. With reference to his own time, the twenties, he characterized the architectonic principle of the Neues Bauen (New building) movement, with its limitation to a horizontal-vertical structural framework, as a “cold-warm or warm-cold—according to the emphasis upon horizontal or vertical."124

Following his treatment of the three basic types of straight lines—the horizontal, vertical, and diagonal——Kandinsky examined the other “diagonal-like lines," which he referred to as independent or without equilibrium, because they “can never attain equilibrium between warm and cold." He distinguished between central and acentral independent straight lines: the former run through the center of the picture plane, the latter do not. In contrast to horizontal, vertical, and diagonal lines, the independent lines, and particularly the acentral ones, have “a looser connection with the surface: they are less bound up with the surface and seem on occasion to pierce it. These lines are at the farthest remove from the point that burrows into the surface, since they in particular have abandoned the element of repose."125

After the discussion of straight lines, Kandinsky examined lines that result when two forces work on one point. If they work in alternation one or more times, the result is zigzag (or angular) lines; if they work simultaneously, the result is curved lines (also called wavy lines when multiple forces are at work). In both cases, in contrast to straight lines, “a far greater sympathy with the surface" arises, so that these types of lines “have, indeed, something surface-like about them."126 This characterization corresponds to what Klee called the “medial stage" of the line (see "Theory of Form" in chapter 9).

142 Basic types of geometric straight lines From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane  
![](images/0541d4c5bebce34340d9b974d1c481688ff5da18a40801f938374122c1d35c9f.jpg)

Corresponding to the division of straight lines into three basic types (horizontal, vertical, and diagonal), Kandinsky distinguished three basic types of zigzag lines according to the size of the angle: acute (45°), right (90°), or obtuse (135°). As an aside, in an exception analogous to that made for the independent lines, he noted those zigzag lines that do not conform to this scheme, which could be calledindependent zigzags because of this independence of angle. Just as cold and warm aspects were balanced in the diagonals, the right-angle zigzags were also “cold-warm" or "warm-cold"(dependingon thedirection) because they were composedof horizontal (cold) and vertical (warm). (A strange discrepancy is evident, however, when a bit later in Point and Line to Plane the right angle is called the “most objective" and“also the coldest.") Kandinsky believed the zigzag with acute angle has the most tension and is “therefore also warmest." By contrast, the tension of the zigzag with an obtuse angle, which he called cold, is negligible (the proximity to the tensionless, cold horizontal). Kandinsky associated the zigzag with an obtuse angle with the basic

143 Left: central free straight lines; right: acentral free straight lines

From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane geometric form of the circle and more specifically said that it gives an “intimation of the curved line" and thus of the circle. This seems to have been based on the idea that an angle that moves successively from $0 ^ { \circ }$ to 135° marks out a circular sector whose form and size is sufficient to complete the idea of a circle. Although this operation may seem somewhat forced, it is immediately clear that it requires only a short step to turn a .right-angle zigzag into a square and the acuteangle zigzag into a triangle.127

![](images/85e22adea6bd1162b949da2a45e0309d8f2a14acbf021f24781ec7ea36db8bf2.jpg)

Kandinsky saw the simple curve as representing another possible transition from the line to the circle. It has an inclination to complete itself or turn back into itself (Paul Overy has made reference to the Gestalt principle of closure in this context),128 so that the circle becomes both the most unstable and the most stable surface. In addition to the simple curve, Kandinsky analyzed the complex curves that can be assembled from parts of geometric circles, of independent parts, or from both geometric and independent parts.129

![](images/d000bf1f78b43771e0359b403cb6256a0b00a617e3017d36d0ce76c8e89e7077.jpg)

144 Left: free zigzag line (angular line); right: free curved line (wavy line) From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane 145 The effects of forces on the zigzag and curved lines From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane  
![](images/83427904d914a5d4a8180d288c6e667c40eda4359a65700d81d2e177a6da4089.jpg)

In connection with the question raised above about Kandinsky's relation to Gestalt psychology it is interesting to note that the zigzags and curves that Kandinsky divided strictly according to their contrasting sounds were used in a similar form in the psychology of the twenties and thirties. There are, for example, parallels to the test tables used by Edgar Rubin, a pioneer in research into figureground relationships, that had zigzag and curved outlines (1921) or to the figures that the Gestalt psychologist Wolfgang Köhler used in 1933 to examine correspondences between graphic and linguistic forms, which have since become famous under the names maluma and takete. Despite the similarities, however, no points of contact have been determined that would speak to a more direct dependence of Kandinsky on Gestalt psychology or of a dependence on the part of Köhler or other Gestalt psychologists on Kandinsky's theory of design.130 (We have, however, already alluded to the fact that the Graf von Dürckheim, a Gestalt psychologist from Leipzig, taught at the Bauhaus, though only after Hannes Meyer hadbecome director.)

## Theory of Color and Form

In this section of the course Wassily Kandinsky was not attempting to subject colors and form to an isolated analysis as elements but rather—from the point of view of an aesthetic synthesis—to discover correspondences between colors and forms. It is precisely from these synthetic perspectives, then, that his theory of color and form deserves special interest. We need not go into the conditions under which Kandinsky's ideas of synthesis were formed, as they were already discussed at the beginning of the chapter; thus we can proceed directly to the presentation of the relationships among forms and colors.

Kandinsky's central concept for working out these relationships was temperature. That colors are associated with temperature is a commonplace in the history of color theory since at least the time of Goethe. What is new, however, is that Kandinsky established axiomatic assignments of specific temperatures to specific formal elements. One significant step toward formulating a figured bass of painting was to bring these“form temperatures" into a system with the “color temperatures" in a way that was as accurate as possible and in this way to achieve form-color correspondences that would be valid at least within the system. To do this, Kandinsky began with the three basic types of lines (horizontal, vertical, and diagonal) and their characteristic temperatures (cold, warm, and coldwarm). Even in On the Spiritual in Art he had characterized black as internally cold(proximity to blue) and white as internally warm (proximity to yellow) so that now in his theory of color and form he concluded: “If the schematic straight lines——in the first instance, horizontals andverticals—are examined for their color characteristics, we are faced with an obvious comparison with black and white."131 Kandinsky assignedred to the coldwarm of the diagonals, since in his theory of color he had placed it between blue (cold) and yellow (warm). In contrast to the three schematic straight lines, which according to Kandinsky lie firmly on the plane, the independent straight lines are inclined to break free of the plane, and Kandinsky associated this with the protruding and receding of yellow and blue.

![](images/ca0cf152a3bac09d14f6952dee950c9bfa0147f292bbe6fb6b2debcf4e433eed.jpg)  
146 Wolfgang Köhler, Maluma and takete From: Psychologische Probleme, 1933

In the case of the zigzags, Kandinsky made similar assignments: the zigzag with the acute angle (warm) is “inwardly colored yellow," the zigzag with the obtuse angle is blue, and the zigzag with the right angle (cold-warm) is associated with red, "which represents a mid-point between yellow and blue and carries within itself cold-warm characteristics." From this results the general relationship: "the more acute the angle, the nearer it comes to acute warmth [yellow R. W.], while conversely this warmth decreases gradually beyond the red right angle, tending more and more toward cold, until the obtuse angle (150°) is reached, a typically blue angle."132 Expressed in terms of the degree of the angle, this means:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l } & { 3 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { Y e l l o w } \qquad } & & { 9 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { R e d } } & & { 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { B l u e } } \\ & { 6 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { O r a n g e } \qquad } & & { 1 2 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { V i o l e t } \qquad } & & { 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { - } \mathsf { B l a c k } } \end{array}
$$

For the three basic geometric forms—the triangle, the square, and the circle—this logically produces the order of the three primary colors: yellow, red, and blue.

The idea of such correspondences is not new to the Bauhaus period but can be traced back to On the Spiritual in Art:

This inevitable relationship between color and form brings us to a consideration of the effects of form upon color. . . . Here, it may be easily remarked that the value of many colors is reinforced by certain forms and weakened by others. At all events, sharp colors have a stronger sound in sharp forms (e.g., yellow in a triangle). The effect of deeper colors is emphasized by rounded forms (e.g., blue in a circle). Of course, it is clear on the one hand that the incompatibility of certain forms and certain colors should be regarded not as something "disharmonious," but conversely, as offering new possibilities— i.e., also [a form] of harmony.133

Although in his own paintings from the twenties Kandinsky frequently made use of the basic forms of the triangle, the square, and the circle, their form-color relationships were more likely to reveal these "new possibilities," so they do not reflect the scheme of triangle-yellow, square-red, and circle-blue. Nevertheless, this dogma seems to have been considered unassailable in his course. Oskar Schlemmer commented on this in 1923, not without an ironic undertone: "Kandinsky's classes: scientifically strict study of color and form. For example: the search for the three elementary colors that correspond to the three basic forms (triangle, square, circle). The decision was reached that yellow went with the triangie, blue with the circle, and red with the square—once and for all, so to speak."134And three years later he wrote: "All the experts agree on the yellow triangle, but not on the others. Instinctively I always make the circle red and the square blue."135In order to confront skeptics with data, Kandinsky conducted surveys not only among the students but in the general population that were intended to contribute to a statistical clarification of the relationships between basic forms and basic colors. Hirschfeld-Mack reported that an overwhelming majority decided in favor of yellow in the triangle, red in the square, and blue in the circle.136 It is obvious that this result is very much open to methodological criticism. For example, it must be considered rather naive to conduct such surveys at the very place where this form-color dogma was being taught, namely, at the Bauhaus. A lack of awareness of methodology is also suggested by the fact that the questionnaire gave the three basic forms in order (triangle, square, and circle), and it contained instructions to fill these three forms with the three colors yellow, red, and blue. It does not require any special insight into the theory behind constructing questionnaires to recognize that this wording and the three colors in the sequence that corresponded to Kandinsky's pedagogical view all but suggests the desired result. It is difficult to escape the conclusion that the goal of these surveys, for all their appearance of objectivity, was more to confirm Kandinsky's theories than to make an unprejudiced assessment of reality.

![](images/3c05b4775c1124bc3eb7b7f9e91ba804607aed9d44c80f1802ef2de2da2590f1.jpg)  
147 Fritz Tschaschnig, Student work Correspondence between Colors and Forms, 1931; Tschaschnig is basing himself here on Kandinsky's assignment of the angles 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, and 150° to the colors yellow, orange, red, violet, and blue

![](images/b3eff83bf0cb1677ee0fff673ee43695aca3da857f581080ab22da7de4bf6087.jpg)  
148 Reinhold Rossig, Study sheet The Color in the Angle, Seen in the Geometric Plane and the Line, 1929; Rossig is adopting here Kandinsky's assignments “acute angle-triangle-yellow," "right angle-square-red," and "obtuse angle-circle-blue"

As it happens, Johannes Itten came to the same color-form correspondences between 1916 and 1918 (see “The Course" in chapter 5), so that the works created in the workshops during the founding phase of the Bauhaus that follow this scheme, such as Peter Keler's Cradle (1922)or Hebert Bayer's wall decorations for the auxiliary staircase of the Bauhaus building of 1923, should not necessarily be attributed to the direct influence of Kandinsky. They may also have been inspired by Itten's teaching, even if it is, of course, also known that Itten ownedandhadread Kandinsky's book On the Spiritual in Art.

![](images/034d7043048bcbf068bc9a667bcbe82aeaa5b38b7ab10e888ca697d19d326fcf.jpg)  
149 Questionnaire on synesthetic relations between color and form, circa 1923

The drive to create a system, to differentiate and complete, can be seen in the student works from the Kandinsky course that displayed the fundamental correspondences not only between the primary colors and forms but between secondary colors and combined forms. For example, a student work by Eugen Batz from the winter semester 1929-30 displayedthe following correspondences: orange-pentagon, green-semicircle with semihexagon, violet-semicircle with semisquare. Another solution can be seen in a practice sheet by Petra Petitpierre from the same semester: orange-pentagon, green-octagon, violet-dodecagon.1 137

The main relationships between temperature, color, and form can be summarized in a table as follows:

<table><tr><td>Temperature</td><td>cold cold-warm</td></tr><tr><td>Color</td><td>warm black blue red yellow white</td></tr><tr><td>Form straight</td><td>horizontal free diagonal free vertical straight straight</td></tr><tr><td>zigzag</td><td>obtuse right acute angle angle angle</td></tr><tr><td>basic form</td><td>circle square triangle</td></tr></table>

## Picture Plane

After having examined the basic elements point and line in isolation in the theory of form—their relationship to each other and to the plane are only suggested occasionally—in the section of his course (and his book Point and Line to Plane) entitled "Picture Plane" Kandinsky treated the material bearer of the picture under that name (abbreviated "pP"). In doing so, he explored the picture plane both in terms of its internal tensions and in light of its relations to the elementary pictorial means.

From a variety of possible picture planes he chose three "schematic" cases: the oblong format (which, in keeping with his characterization of the horizontals, he called a "cold" format), the upright format (consequently a "warm" format), and the square (which he called "objective" because of its relative balance between cold and warm and therefore took as the starting point for his further discussions).

He divided the picture plane (square) into four force fields: above, below, right, left.

“Above” conjures up an image of greater rarification, a feeling of lightness, of emancipation, and ultimately, of freedom. . . . Constraint is reduced to a minimum.

"Below" has entirely the opposite effect: density, weight, bondage.. . . Constraint attains its maximum.138

Overy observed that this "double sound" of gravity and weightlessness corresponds to a primitive habit of vision. He refers to the following passage in James J. Gibson's standard work The Perception of the Visual World:

An out-of-doors world is one in which the lower portion of the visual field ... is invariably filled by a projection of the terrain. The upper portion of the visual field is usually filled with a projection of the sky. Between the upper and lower portions is the skyline, high or low as the observer looks down or up, but always cutting the normal visual field in a horizontal section. This is the kind of world in which our primitive ancestors lived. It was also the environment in which took place the evolution of visual perception in their ancestors. During the millions of years in which some unknown animal species evolved into our human species, land and sky were the constant visual stimuli to which the eyes and brain responded. In the typical indoors world of civilized man, a ceiling and walls take the place of the horizon and sky, but the floor is still an equivalent to the ground.139

This accords with Kandinsky's, as he put it, "literary" association of up with the sky and down with the earth.140

According to Kandinsky, the characteristics of the left side of the picture plane correspond to those of “above" (light, relaxed, free) but in altered form, that is, with a greater degree of density; by analogy, the right side is inherently close to "below" (heavy, dense, bound) but with a lesser degree of density: "movement toward the 'left'— toward freedom—is movement into the distance ... movement toward the 'right'—toward captivity—is a homeward movement."141 What Kandinsky described metaphorically seems to be connected with the way vision is conditioned in Western culture by reading from left to right. This would also explain why Kandinsky saw movement to the left as moving human beings away from their habitual environment, from their“almost

![](images/2286eeeb32e988e75898a0e13b458427091f1c9acd8dcf721cdef8221ae16d46.jpg)

150 Peter Keler, Crib, 1922; wood, painted rope web; the hoops are blue, the triangular forms yellow, and rectangles on the side red 151 Herbert Bayer, Sketch for the wall design of the three-level secondary stairwell of the main building of the Weimar Bauhaus, 1923, in the dominant colors blue (ground floor), red (second floor), and yellow (third floor)

![](images/d905c0cd23b3ea1e529146cee547bbcea13ae7de61bcacdcb4883c210dd7e4c7.jpg)

petrifying atmosphere," and attributed something “adventurous" to it.

The artist remarked on resistances at the four corners of the edges of the surface that correspond to the division of the picture plane into four force fields and that clearly vary in their efficacy (he depicted this graphically by drawing outlines of differing thicknesses).

Having divided the picture plane into “four primary areas," he then derived the following scheme for the distribution of “weights" in a square picture plane (see fig. 152). In this scheme, the areas a and d stand in the"greatest contrast" to one another. Their relationship is characterized by an “unharmonic" diagonal; Kandinsky called the resulting tension “dramatic." The areas b and c stand in “moderate contrast" to each other, so he considered this “harmonic" diagonal relationship a “lyrical tension." In the framework of his theory of color and form Kandinsky attributed to these four "primary areas" the following corresponding colors: a—yellow, d—blue (“greatest contrast"); b—warm red, c—cold red ("mild contrast").142

Kandinsky continued this examination by discussing the relationships between form and picture plane. In principle, the forms can be arranged parallel to the edges of the picture or parallel to the two diagonals. In the former case, an “external parallelism" exists, because the external borders of the picture plane are decisive; the latter case results in an “inner parallelism, since the forms travel in the same direction as the inner tensions of the PP."143

Kandinsky's remarks on the role of the distance of the form from the edges of the picture plane reveal a certain affinity to Gestalt psychology. In this view, it seems that

as a form approaches the edge of the PP, its tension increases until, at the moment of contact with the border, this tension suddenly ceases. And: the further a form is from the edge of the PP, the more its tension toward the border decreases. Or: those forms that lie in close proximity to the border of the PP heighten the "dramatic" sound of the construction; whereas those

![](images/d12e3f8ea20898f7c2885cbd2b7414a289cbc1d85b9842e2512cfc42ebc8128f.jpg)

152 The “picture plane" and its "four primary sections" From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane 153“Lyrical sound“ (left) and “dramatic sound" (right) achieved by varying the placement of a straight line on the "picture plane"

From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane

![](images/36746af4a8ff281989cdc8d9aefaccb12188910112f7688ff111ebdd8a2ca2ba.jpg)

that are far removed from the border, grouped more around the center, give the construction lyrical sound.144
