Kandinsky gave these general remarks more precise form in a series of graphic diagrams that use centrally and acentrally arranged lines to illustrate the possibilities of “lyrical" and "dramatic" compositions. They are reproduced here with Kandinsky's commentaries from Point and Line to Plane.145

In the manuscripts for the course, Kandinsky did not hesitate to state the following “absolute rule," which he also called a"psychological law" of the broadest validity (not just in“fine" art): “One must refrain from approaching the limits of the picture plane; one must preserve a certain 'blank space,' which in itself has the greatest tension.... The arts, handicrafts, architecture—all of them are subject to the same law."146

In the opinion of Paul Overy, Kandinsky's explorations of the picture plane are particularly close to central Gestalt psychological principles of proximity, closure or completeness, and good form [Prägnanz]. For example, Overy sees Kan-

![](images/3169458d982564c5fd60ebabbdcc6a8b337e73b1d2ed33da889a9dd8475f4d97.jpg)  
Fig. 95 Schweigende Lyrik der vier elementaren Linien erstarrter Ausdruck.

![](images/85a568f6b7e39945f3aabe86c7ee66fcf5df4c94cc4c9dd5077a8c8858912d85.jpg)  
Fig. 96 Dramatisierung derselben Elemente kompliziert pulsierender Ausdruck.

Verwendung der Exzentrik:

![](images/a14862380ebce939313e218f662fa1a45a0aea9c9f36d7619dd7cbf8432ce518.jpg)

Diagonale zentral.   
Horizontal-Vertikale azentral. Diagonale in höchster Spannung Abgewogene Spannungen der Horizontalenund Vertikalen. Fig. 98

![](images/8ac589e055b840f31ed04626c5552c6bfa5700fd26995fe01d921684eeada710.jpg)

## 154

Possibilities for "lyrical" and "dramatic" compositions From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane Fig 95: Silent lyricism of the four elementary lines— frozen expression.

Fig. 96: Dramatization of the same elements— expression of complex pulsation.

Use of the eccentric:

Fig. 97: Diagonal central. Horizontal-vertical acentral. Extreme tension of diagonal. Balanced tension of horizontals and verticals.

Fig. 98: All elements acentral. Diagonal reinforced by its repetition. Dramatic sound restrained at point of contact above.)

dinsky's remark that a line approaching the edge of the picture gives the picture plane in this zone a decided tension as related to the "principle of proximity," which states that two or more units next to one another will be seen as a whole; this in turn is in keeping with the "principle of completeness," which states that there is a tendency to see an open form as closed, that is, to complete it as part of the process of perception.147 Rather than discuss the relationships between the artist's theory of design and the findings of Gestalt psychology at greater depth, since they have been addressed above several times, we will instead briefly outline Kandinsky's course “Analytical Drawing."

## Analytical Drawing

Kandinsky—who expressly emphasized that his course on analytical drawing was based on the same principles and methods as his instruction on abstract elementsof form148conceivedthis course for the first semester not as an introduction to drawing from nature or things but as a propaedeutics of“precise observation" and of constructive pictorial organization. The goal of this drawing class was thus not to depict the external appearance of the world of things in a way as close to nature as possible but to provide a representation “of the laws that govern the forces (= tensions) that can be discovered in given objects and of their logical construction—an exercise in clearly observingandclearly reproducing relationships."149 This quotation demonstrates yet again that Kandinsky's concern in art was with an "inner perception," not with a mimesis of external reality of any particular concrete aesthetic form. (It is therefore hardly surprising that in his course Kandinsky frequently criticized realistic tendencies in the art of the twenties, particularly the painting of Neue Sachlichkeit.) This "inner perception" does not, however, preclude analytical drawing from treating objects, namely, in the form of still life. It is well known that still life—which had been common as an independent genre in painting since the early sixteenth century—was frequently used as an occasion for formal design in modern art ranging from impressionism through Cézanne to cubism. Similarly, still life in Kandinsky's course represented a means of formal analysis. This meant that his course on analytical drawing ig nored both the sensory stimulation of the various materials that coalesce in the still life——and whose depiction was of such extraordinary interest to Itten in his preliminary course—and the possibility of exploiting the still life as a vehicle for expressing certain kinds of subject matter (as was done, for example, in the vanitas still lifes of seventeenth-century Holland). The still life is well suited to formal analysis not only because of the lack of movement but also because it can be arranged at will, so that even the structure can conform with the artist's intentions for the design. As a rule, the students in Kandinsky's drawing course, too, constructed their still lifes themselves. In doing so, they consciously gave precedence to elements that easily lent themselves to reduction to·geometric forms and couldbe subsumed to a rigorous pictorial architecture. Kandinsky described the stages of the analytical process as follows:

![](images/e89e86029858a3ed67fbd6779e596883ea03d2a2c99a6594410c9d62f53b9242.jpg)  
155 Wassily Kandinsky, Graphic diagram of a jump by the dancer Palucca From: Wassily Kandinsky, Point and Line to Plane

The first tasks of analytical drawing are:

1. to subordinate the whole complex to one simple overall form, which, within the limits determined by the student himself, must be precisely drawn in.

2. to realize the formal characterization of individual parts of the still-life, regarded both in isolation and in relation to other parts.

3. to represent the whole construction by means of the most concise possible schema. . ..

![](images/4b9a4409684890bd465be08b40f37566f550924d1ccffeb864288de1ded9d29d.jpg)

156 Grete Reichardt, Linear and constructive analysis of a still life, circa 1928

157Analytical drawing. Above: general theme. Below: four different construction variants achieved by blocking out individual construction parts. Variations as stimulus for four fundamentally different tensile construction forms of the general theme,“ circa 1928

Gradual transition to second-level exercises, which, put briefly, consist in the following:

1. making clear the tensions discovered in the structure, which are to be represented by means of lineal forms.

2. emphasizing the principal tensions by means of broader lines, or, subsequently, colors.

3. indicating the structural grid by means of starting or focal points (dotted lines). .. .

## Third level:

1. objects are regarded exclusively in terms of tension between forces, and the construction limits itself to complexes of lines.

2. variety of structural possibilities: clear and concealed construction . . .

3. exercises in the utmost simplification of the overall complex and of the individual tensions—— concise, exact expression.150

On the basis of a substantial collection of student works held in the Bauhaus-Archiv in Berlin,

![](images/1942af44ccabecc89548dc53e1e7491844c64ba021d7c1007fcf0ab443e9e57e.jpg)

Clark V. Poling has been able to demonstrate that the analyses of this third level, which are characterized by highly rigorous formal reductions, were able to serve in turn as the basis for developing new pictorial compositions. He comments on a series of analytical drawings by Petra Petitpierre and the "free variations" derived from them: "In perceiving the set of linear relationships in the motif and determining the points of their intersections, the student revealed a structure. Then she used that geometrical order to suggest a composition. . .. A number of other studies by Kandinsky's students have the appearance of free compositions, but are ultimately based on analytical drawings."151 This confirms—very much in keeping with our earlier findings about the alternating relationship between analysis and synthesis in Kandinsky's work—that in the process of teaching and learning the analytical method is understood not as goal unto itself but as a necessary preliminary stage in the creative artistic act, an intermediate stage on the path to synthesis. (On the value of the analytical in Itten's course, see the remarks on his "analysis of old masters" in “The Course" in chapter 5.)

![](images/e00e9e0999a07d931fb52fc8b1c6bf5a805dab44ea42a435d23861a423036a97.jpg)  
158 Kurt Kranz, Analytical drawing; structure with inverted easef, whetstone, and tires; below right: quick diagram with main tensions, 1930

## Critical Objections

Gropius and Kandinsky were agreed on two points: (1) that art was “not teachable" (Gropius) or “could not be learned" (Kandinsky); (2) that a school for design like the Bauhaus had to have a propaedeutic course to ensure that students would acquire those very general insights into the basics of design that were equally important for fine and applied arts. In this sense, the elementary language that Kandinsky formulated as part of his teaching system at the Bauhaus was of central significance not least becausé his course was required for all first-semester students, and for a time even second- and fourth-semester students. Precisely because Kandinsky's design course ignored problems of practicaldesignwork and lacked any specific subject matter—apart from that of finding and interpreting the tensions inherent in a form or composition——it was clearly accepted for years as the general propaedeutic course in the sense of presenting the most elementary notions of form and color. Only under

Hannes Meyer did the relevance of this design course begin to be questioned, particularly by Communist students. A hectographed pamphlet published by the latter in July 1930 states, among other things: "What is the content and purpose of analytical drawing? Still-life, concise scheme, tensions, structural net, and as the ultimate objective—'free composition of three-dimensional, formal energy tensions.' This kind of instruction is bound to lead to individual, abstract, creative work, as is borne out by the various distinctly individual renderings of one and the same still-life. This kind of drawing is entirely unsuited for the work in the workshops, since it does not allow for an objective approach."152 If one tries to situate Kandinsky's design course within the overall context of pedagogy at the Bauhaus, it can be seen as the contribution of a “rational irrationalist," something that can also be said of Klee. For someone like Itten, by contrast, the irrational aspects dominate, as the rational ones do in the case of Moholy and Albers. Despite his metaphysical and transcendental inclinations, Kandinsky established a pedagogical edifice that is logically and deductively structured and clad with strict rationality, but whose fundamentalaxioms nevertheless elude a rational approach. In the same way, Kandinsky's true goal—the formulation of a design language that conforms to a principle of internal necessity—was founded on a stratum of irrationality. The strong emphasis on the analytical principle—the call for a rational penetration of the means of design, for their reduction to basic forms and colors, and for “microscopic" exploration of the inner tensions inherent in a form or a complex of forms—did not, as Kandinsky saw it, preclude communicating the spiritual and emotional qualities that are inherent in the forms and colors. It is obvious that the realization of this goal was often hampered by the “purely cerebral exercises," which necessarily harbored the risk of ossifying into a schematic formalism, resulting in a merely external, recipe-like set of rules acquired. There can be no doubt that there was at least the possibility of stunting students' sense perceptions through this fixation on a primarily cognitive approach to design, through this tendency to rigorous cerebralism. And this is confirmed by the dominance in numerous student works of a design language that places strict restraints on emotion.

Equally problematic is Kandinsky's insistence on design principles that he claimed were timeless and intercultural (as did Itten, following Hölzel)— a result of his search for a figured bass of painting as an instrument for the pictorial realization of the pure and eternally artistic. By postulating universal principles, Kandinsky, like the Gestalt psychologists, turned perception and design into categories that claimed to be independent of cultural, political, social, and other concrete conditions. It goes without saying that such a view has an inherently conservative aspect, and thus it is no surprise that leftist critics, both at the Bauhaus itself and from the so-called critical art pedagogy movement of the 1970s, have tried to disqualify Kandinsky's teachings as formalistic or hostile to emancipation. They did not, however, succeed in unsettling the outstanding historical importance demonstrated by his fundamental contribution to the pedagogy of design.

54 Also disturbing are the numerous typographical errors, especially with German proper names. In a letter of 14 November 1980 Jean Leppien described the principles of his edition to the present author as follows: "I left the sequence of the manuscript as it was numbered (probably by Kandinsky). In the manuscript there is no temporal or thematic order, all the more so as Kandinsky doubtless never intended to publish his lecture notes. No omissions or additions were made, and above all we avoided turning even clumsy or incomplete phrases or sentences into more readable language, as it would have been a misrepresentation." Suzanne Leppien wrote in Nina Kandinsky's Kandinsky und ich (note 41, p. 135): "The lecture manuscripts that my husband, Jean Leppien, and I recently translated into French show that Kandinskydesignedhis course according to very precise methods and rules. Over the years this schematism did not change.... What Kandinsky wrote in these lecture notes, which range from 1926 to 1931, is very often unclear, punctuated with many abbreviations, andnowhere nearly as diligently kept as, say, the home ledger. Anyone who did not attendKandinsky's class, does not know his handwriting, and does not speak very good German is in no position to understand these course texts. I am not certain that Kandinsky's course can still benefit young people today. We, however, grew into this course."

114 Wilhelm Ostwald, Die Harmonie der Farben, 2d ed. (Leipzig: Unesma, 1921), p. 1. In his Color Prímer (New York: Van Nostrand Reinhold, 1969; originally published in 1915 as Farbenfibel), p. 65, Ostwald writes, "such colors will have a pleasing effect between which there exists a lawful relationship, i.e., order. If this is absent, they will have an unpleasant or indifferent effect. We call color groups that have a pleasing effect harmonious; we can therefore establish this basic law: Harmony = Order." It is interesting to note that in his edition of Kandinsky's Point and Line to Plane (note 52) Max Bill makes explicit reference to Ostwald's book Die Harmonie der Farben (p. 210).

115 SA, p. 209. The following passage is found in PL, p. 611: “In the end, every force can be expressed in mathematical terms, something we call mathematical expression. In art today, this remains more a theoretical assertion, but one we should not leave out of account. Today we lack the ability to take measurements, although these will, leaving the utopian aside, be discovered sooner or later. From that moment on, every composition will be capable of being expressed in numbers. . .. Only the conquest of mathematical expression will enable us to realize fully a precise theory of composition, on the threshold of which we stand today." This is a clear foreshadowing of what will later become known as “numerical aesthetics." On the history of this concept, see also the essay by the Bauhaus student Max Bill, "Die mathematische Denkweise in der Kunst unserer Zeit," in Antoine Pevsner, Georges Vantongerloo, Max Bill, exh. cat.
