With this method Schlemmer won anti-academic ground for figure drawing too."119

![](images/574c75787b8fb86d15ac522e7f538320ec8727cb7057da91f7aacc97de4166d7.jpg)

It is interesting that in the formal section of the course“Der Mensch" Schlemmer was essentially building on the “anthropocentric constructivism" of his Stuttgart period, for example, where he developed the statics and dynamics of the human figure systematically from schematic structural lines (starting from vertical lines, the letter i, and vertical lines that have begun to vibrate),120 or where he, following Dürer and the Italian mannerists (e.g., Luca Cambiaso), constructed the figure as a “box person” made up of cubes (also of cylinders and spheres or circles) in order to demonstrate its physicality and tectonic structure using simple means.

Here again we see the tendency, so characteristic of the Bauhausas a whole, to reduce things to geometrical figures. It is related to a process that began at the latest with the beginning of the modern age, with the Renaissance, which Rudolf zur Lippe has convincingly analyzed as the “geometrization of the human being."121 Very briefly, this is the view that the human being, under the dictate of functional rationality, is subjectedto increasingrestrictions in hisoriginal physicality. The most enduring effects have been the result of industrialization, with its processes based on the division of labor (Taylorism), but the process existed even earlier, for example, in ceremonies at court, in the military, in dance, andin sports, where naturalmotions are divided into subprocesses and then reconstructed according to predetermined geometrical configurations. In art the effects of the geometrizationof the human being can be seen most clearly in the introduction

![](images/179aed315af8210e2affc58e124033e3ec586ceac2d58ee50e87c3ed591bc49c.jpg)

204 Oskar Schlemmer, The Human Being in the Circle of Ideas, display image for the course “The Human Being," 1928–29

205 Oskar Schlemmer, Proportion scheme Rectangle Division (Horizontal–Vertical); or, Box Person, 1928–29; pencil

O.S.:Viereck-Teilung (horizontal-vertikal) oder Schachtelmensch of the square grid and central perspective and in the reduction of the human figure to physical bodies—such as tubes, spheres, and boxes, as mentioned. Without explicitly raising the crucial anthropological question of the nature of the human being, zur Lippe illustrated this historical evolution with a series of terms from social and cultural criticism like uniformity, centralization, and coordination [Gleichschaltung]. If we were to apply this extraordinarily interesting interpretative approach to the evaluation of art or to Schlemmer's teachings, the result would have to be negative. The artist's figures would have to be interpretedas the corollary to the human being denatured by technology and industry, as a soulless robot--an interpretation that certainly accords with the tendencies to social criticism found in art of the period. With respect to Schlemmer's goals, however, the misunderstanding could hardly be more grave, and the artist himself always remarked on the danger of such a misinterpretation: "so now I try to construct the human body from the simple mathematical bodies and their combinations. The simple mathematical body is both the origin and the completion of every physical form. By doing this I am struggling to overcome the ever-threatening mechanism that would make the human being into a machine.... People often object that my 'human beings' have no 'faces,' at most 'puppet faces.' Unfortunately, they don't understand that that is the intention, that it cannot be otherwise. Before we can paint 'faces,' before we are allowed to do so, we must recognize the type, the impersonal. That is truly not a 'loss of individuality.'"122

![](images/6cc36a45faf9090b17112ad30529e9e40ccc880cdf4628ae069f4e7f5d5014cc.jpg)

![](images/49166f304becb525df8f08f8200cb7ed8ad1e8489899f88c70c2a8eccd796450.jpg)  
206 Oskar Schlemmer, Schematic diagrams of body movements, 1928–29; graphite and colored pencils

![](images/2fd9ff767b75855156cbf63ff6ede418a7d7c3f0d00b137fa25a6310ed8ee8fe.jpg)  
207 Oskar Schlemmer, Schematic diagrams of the relationship human being-space-piece of furniture, 1928–29; pencil

Schlemmer's humane aspiration is clearly manifested in the scientific (biological) section of his course as well, where he spoke on quéstions of the origin of life; of cell and seed theory; of anatomy, the mechanics of the body, and the function of organs; of problems of nutrition, hygiene, and clothing. This "theory of life activities," which points beyond the limited field of physiology, offered Schlemmer a transition to the philosophical section that he considered especially important. Here his intention was to lay out the major systems of thought from antiquity to the modern period, "focusing on discussions of the concept of substance, notions of space and time, and last but not least the essence of the soul," ending "with the metaphysical knowledge, to clarify questions .of aesthetics and ethics."123

With his comprehensive course “Der Mensch," which exploded the boundaries between fields, Schlemmer hoped to give his students what he felt to be most lacking at the Bauhaus, namely, a worldview; and hence in May 1928 he was "curious to see . . . what picture of the world will emerge by the end of the semester."124 It is clear that the course “Der Mensch" not only reflected very precisely Schlemmer's striving for totality that was nourished on the intellectual tradition of romanticism but also corresponded to the basic Bauhaus idea of reconstructing a totality of life by means of educating generalists rather than training specialists. That the goal of “totalization" (to use Klee's term) in an industrial society based on a high degree of division of labor and differentiation of function is, of course, extremely controversial, something that we can only mention but not discuss in detail. The only certain thing is that we are hardly more likely to reach this goal by overloading education with encyclopedic details of partial aspects from the most varied academic disciplines. Thus the critique of the course “Der Mensch" applies not to the formal (drawing) portion—here Schlemmer was sovereign as both artist and pedagogue—but to the biological and philosophical portions. There he not only clearly demanded too much of his students but seems to have taken on too much himself, despite his unusual knowledge of the literature and his intensive engagement with the material. The fact that this course“could not do justice to its universal proportions and high goals in all of its parts" can only be partially explained by the limitęd time available to prepare for it.125 Rather, it must be attributed above all to the fundamental impossibility that an individual in the twentieth century could make good on such a claim on totality.

![](images/b659d23486ee4e86f179af5c8657c0322b06acc807848a4903380d8dbe4c8dfb.jpg)

208 Oskar Schlemmer, Box Person, 1928–29; pencil 209 Oskar Schlemmer, Human figure constructed from circles and squares or rectangles, 1928–29

![](images/0216abf9af432df9d9ef2296fc17d1ae7eee8b4fc5672fc6bf3b63134bb0ff64.jpg)

Nevertheless, it is still Schlemmer's historical contribution to pedagogy at the Bauhaus to have been the only one to have placed the human being so completely at the center of his teachings and to have made it the theme for the design of the everyday world, both in terms of metaphysics and scale. The emphasis on the human being was an endeavor which also appears in the popular Bauentwurfslehre (first published in 1936) by the Bauhaus member Ernst Neufert and in The Modulor by Le Corbusier, who published his highly influential system of measure based on the proportions of the human body in 1948.

## The Possibilities and Limits of Artistic Teachings

Schlemmer was active as a teacher for just over twelve years: almost nine years at the Bauhaus, around three years at the Academy of Art in Breslau, and (until his dismissal by the National Socialists in 1933) half a year at the Vereinigte Staatschulen für Kunst und Kunstgewerbe (United state schools for art and arts and crafts)in Berlin. He was a teacher but no schoolmaster, and as a teacher he thought deeply about the possibilities and limits of teaching art: about what can be taught and what cannot. His thoughts on these issues will be outlined in conclusion in terms of a central point for pedagogy at the Bauhaus, namely, the problem of a theory of harmony in modern art and in the training of artists.

As a master student of Adolf Hölzel at the academy in Stuttgart, Schlemmer was familiar with his teacher's efforts to find a theory of harmony analogous to that of music, a counterpoint of painting. He played an instrument himself and loved Bach especially (like Itten and Klee), and his fascination with this idea of counterpoint or figured bass, which he shared with Kandinsky, is demonstrated by numerous passages in letters anddiaries, journal articles, interviews, and speeches. One is, however, immediately struck by an ambivalence in Schlemmer's attitude; on the one hand, as we have seen, he sought "strict regularity" (Runge), "number, scale, and law,"126and a seemingly supraindividual formal language; on the other, he approached the goal of counterpoint in the fine arts with critical distance: "Counterpoint in painting will never be discovered, or it will be, in contrast to that of music, dryly schematic. The creative processes in the two arts differ entirely."127 Further:

It is, however, still a strange fact that even today no universally valid canon for the laws of painting has been found—unlike in music, which is so bound to laws. Goethe certainly hoped that the contrabass could be found in paintingas well; certainly in Dürer's work and later in that of someone like Caspar David Friedrich a significant degree of law of color and form has survived; certainly Delaunay and Hoelzel of the ultimate academy of painters where the rules of art. finally discovered, would now besafequarded. But what is even the theory of form and color of someone like Klee or Kandinsky theones who have surely realized the dreams most completely, by creatinga well-ordered pedagogy—what is it other than the key to their own kingdom and—in the pedagogical sense— the ABC and the first aids for the composition of form, color, plane? For beyond the scaffold of form and the scale of color the imponderables begin: the immeasurable, the inconceivable, the mystical aspect of the work of art, which the more original it is raises the question of the law again. .. . Why does law in painting, if not practiced by a superior mind, so easily become a constricting dogma? Why is the result there so often the sterile stylization rather than the fulfilled style?128

Ofthe diverse reasons that makethe prospect of a figured bass of modern art seem dubious, Schlemmer recognized the central one with all possible clarity. It is a social question, and it pertains to the fact that the "basis ... for something shared, for an agreement"129no longer exists in a pluralistic society, and consequently the search for a binding theory of harmony is of necessity futile from the outset. So Schlemmer drew the only adequate conclusion for artistic theory—and here he agreed with his teacher Hölzel—that the knowledge of particular rules, of particular principles of design, may be helpful for the student, but these rules represent nothing more than a“skeleton of an artistic theory," and their schematic application necessarily leads to "sterile stylization," to “manner."

According to Schlemmer, no art can exist that is controlled entirely by a system of rules that can be rationally calculated, because “geometry, the Golden Section, the theory of proportions . . . are dead and fruitless if they are not experienced, felt, sensed. We must allow ourselves to be surprised by the miracle of proportion, by the magnificence of the relations of numbers and the correspondences and to form laws from these sorts of results."130 Art, then, not as a simple act of applying a law but as an innovative act of creating a law, is a process that in Schlemmer's view should progress from emotion to law, from intuition to construction, from the subjective to the objective. In this respect Schlemmer differed fundamentally from Hölzel, who in his own compositions started from pictorial laws, from a theoretically considered, rationally constructed pictorial framework, in order to free himself from it step by step and disperseinto unpredictable“accidental effects."131 By contrast, Schlemmer started out from the primary sensation and allowed "form . . . [to develop] out of the conditions set by the materials; the result is order"132 that is freer and newer than anything that had existed before. It is clear

![](images/27e7e8bddbc211bf43255264b36abbb6a7e3bbbcb20c39d0209b91e441333120.jpg)  
210 Oskar Schlemmer, Passerby, 1924–25; oil on canvas (CW G 136)

211 Oskar Schlemmer, Analyses of the painting Passerby: The verticals—The Horizontals—The Diagonals—The Curves, Arcs, and Circles—The Rectangular Masses and Centers—The Dynamics—The Rhythmic Relationships and Supplements—The Negative, circa 1929.

![](images/fb26f1a446393f38f8c395c39fd479783dae060a78a8b5bd37bd07f7980d6399.jpg)

that this dynamic and open conception of the creative process is incompatible with the rigid and static idea of establishing a universally valid theory of harmony in painting. It is equally clear that this open conception of the creative process—for all its emphasis on the individual's self-imposed commitment to law—leaves enough latitude to encourage what Schlemmer saw as the ultimate goal of all artistic training, namely, "the personal."133

Schlemmer distinguishes here between“two types of teachers: one kind, consciously or unconsciously, captivates the student, with the result that he becomes a reflection—and rarely a sparkling one—of his master. The other ones either have a sense for the student's natural talents and know how to awaken them, so that the student gradually becomes aware of them and concentrates all his intensity there, or they limit themselves to a minimum of direct help, produce their effects through their own work and existence, and leave the student to himself, prepared for questions and answers, if he needs them, The former educates students who will often remain students all their lives. The latter produce individuals."

## 11 Joost Schmidt (1893–1948)

## The Unknown Member of the Bauhaus

It may seem surprising that at the end of our series of the illustrious artists who gave Bauhaus pedagogy its direction and character a name should appear that has never been known beyond a small circle of those interested in the Bauhaus: Joost Schmidt. Obtaining coherent information about Schmidt, the un-

![](images/b2640d8c79243528b087412b284b278e42d5ba78006099f898863f64e4e6212f.jpg)  
212 Joost Schmidt, 1930s

known member of the Bauhaus, has:long been nearly impossible. Although Hans M. Wingler referred to Joost Schmidt's life and work in several places in his groundbreaking book on the Bauhaus, and the 1968 exhibition catalog 50 Jahre Bauhaus contained numerous illustrations of works by Schmidt and his students, the only book on Schmidt listed there—written by his wife, the Bauhaus student Helene Schmidt-Nonné—was never published. Not until 1981, when Eckhard Neumann—whose Bauhaus und Bauhäusler1 (Bauhaus and Bauhaus people), among other works, has shown him to be a connoisseur of the Bauhaus scene—wrote a small work entitled “Lernen und Lehren am Bauhaus" (Learning and teaching at the Bauhaus), was any attempt made to take the measure of Joost Schmidt's significance as artist and teacher. The present chapter is indebted to Neumann's study for a number of essential facts, particularly in its presentation of Schmidt's pedagogical ideas. We should also mention the documentation of the artist published in 1984 by EditionMarzona and the relevant chapter on Joost Schmidt in the 1995 catalog Das A und O des Bauhauses.² Giving Schmidt his own chapter in this book is not simply a function of our selection principle, whereby all teachers who were involved in implementing required courses at the Bauhaus are treated in depth, but above all a recognition of the need to win well-deserved repute for the most underestimated Bauhaus master.

## Biographical Notes

The artistic career of Joost Schmidt, born in Wunstorf near Hanover in 1893, initially followed the academic paths usual at the time. In 1910 he entered the Grand Ducal Saxon Academy of Arts in Weimar, where he was a student in Max Thedy's painting class, received his diploma in painting in 1913, and was named a master student in 1914. After

fighting in the war in France, where he was seriously wounded and taken prisoner by the Americans, he returned to Weimar in 1919, attracted by Gropius's Bauhaus manifesto, to learn at first hand about the palpable revolutionary mood and the innovations that were being discussed everywhere. According to his wife, Helene Schmidt-Nonné, however, “the modern trends and teaching methods were not to his liking, and so with some of the other former professors he joined the rival academic institution, which was re-established in the same building of the Bauhaus in the spring of 1921."3

When he finally did join the Bauhaus at the age of twenty-seven, having been accepted into an apprenticeship in sculpture, it seems to have been in connection with the work on the SommerfeldHouse, home toBerlinlumberman. Gropius, in collaboration with his colleague Adolf Meyer, had designed a wood house—stylistically related to the prairie houses of Frank Lloyd Wright—for which the client wished to have teakwood paneling and carvings made for the interior. It was Joost Schmidt who accepted the difficult task of executing the desired carvings for the doors, the stairwells, and the gallery on the upper floor. Schmidt was restricted in his themes by Sommerfeld's request for symbolic representations of his sawmills in Schneidemühl, Kolberg, Danzig (Gdansk), and other sites in eastern Germany. Only in the designs for the doors did Schmidt have a free hand. Here "decorative geometry was the formula for the design,"4 and it is obvious that he drew inspiration from Itten's theory of the socalled characters of forms (triangle, circle, square characters, see ,“The Course" in chapter 5). This is also confirmed by Itten's claim in his book Mein Vorkurs am Bauhaus (translatedas Design and Form) that these works resulted from the influence of his course. Even in these early reliefs it is possible to see the direction that Joost Schmidt would take, namely, “towardabstraction based on the spatial relationships of simple geometric elements."5

![](images/9b00e07f5298116ed7fa645f55924ef37f1fd9b54654afff231eaa940205bf9b.jpg)  
213 Walter Gropius and Adolf Meyer, Sommerfeld House, Berlin-Dahlem, 1920–21  
214 Joost Schmidt, Carvings in the stairwell of the Sommerfeld House, 1921–22

![](images/f990d0d95a7d8321cb97f131eba74d431c39288b78da9723b7c3d2550c78ce12.jpg)

Although Gropius was reproached by critics who considered both the exterior and interior of the Sommerfeld House to be a reversion to. expressionism (which was, after all, typical of the founding phase of the Bauhaus), it should be remembered that this house marked the first modest step toward realizing the Bauhaus idea of a workingcooperativebetween architects and artist-craftsmen. For example, Gropius employed Fred Forbat of the Bauhaus as the construction supervisor; along with Joost Schmidt, Josef Albers contributed colored leaded-glass windows as decorations; and the furniture was produced in the cabinetmaking workshop of the Bauhaus (including constructivist-inspired club chairs designed by Marcel Breuer).

215 Joost Schmidt, Wall relief in the vestibule of the main building of the Weimar Bauhaus, 1923  
![](images/012e1a1fe60d79a14dab87260e389d15cf870b9696fb0c8fd7dbac320c6798e4.jpg)

Schmidt's contribution to the Sommerfeld House gave him a status within the school that clearly distinguished him from the other students. Thus it was no coincidence that soon thereafter, on the occasion of the Bauhaus exhibition in 1923, he was awarded the task of decorating the walls of the vestibule in the main building of the Bauhaus, clear proof of the unusual trust that the council of masters had in the design abilities of this Bauhaus apprentice! It should be noted, however, that he, like Albers, could point to a completed training as an artist.

Whereas the language of the Sommerfeld House was limited by the hardness of the teakwood used to rectilinear and angular forms that could be easily carved, the plaster vestibule reliefs were highly rounded, which can be explained in part by the nature of the material (i.e., the fact that plaster is more easily shaped) and in part by the possible influence of Schlemmer, who was the master of form for both wood and stone sculpture for some time during the early years of the Bauhaus, and who, as an "undulist" as described in

![](images/80520d9fd49f07dd5610957990d36907d611effa11105b951eaef8e8e75e9e6b.jpg)

Goethe's theory of art, favored round forms (see "The Classicist" in chapter 10). The theme of the reliefs, inspired by Gropius, is a "didactic demonstration of the basic geometrical forms and their relations to one another. Elementary forms—spheres, cylinders, and cones—interpenetrate one another, break through the surface . . ., and produce a spatial modulation of extraordinary aesthetic appeal."6 Joost Schmidt later remarked:

This work was the fruition of my research with primary plastic forms. I employed both positive and negative forms and sought to incorporate them compositionally into architecture.. .. [I]t was through these experiments that the expressive possibilities of the primary forms became much clearer to me. No longer were geometrical figures inexpressive scientific configurations for me. Their beauty had revealed itself to me.7

Joost Schmidt also created for the 1923 Bauhaus exhibition a poster, using the Bauhaus logo designed by Schlemmer, whose elementary stock of forms (circles, rectangular planes, bars) reveals

216 Joost Schmidt, Poster for the Bauhaus exhibition,   
1923

217 Joost Schmidt, Printed advertising material for YKO Office Supplies (the firm of Paul Heuss, Weimar), 1924

![](images/4df031f12606bd0d21e2e7dad44fec29317f0f08ba53e73bdcbb6b52e64c655f.jpg)

a certain similarity to the vestibule reliefs and whose diagonal structure suggests the possible influence both of Russian constructivism and Moholy-Nagy, who had just been appointed to the Bauhaus.

Schmidt's reliefs for the Sommerfeld House and the Bauhaus building, together with his poster design, prefigured the two main directions of his work: first, investigations of the relationships between bodies and space, and second, concern with type, typography, and advertising. Both areas were to be emphasized in his later teaching activities at the Bauhaus, and both led to a syn-

218 Joost Schmidt, Title page of a prospectus for the Traffic Office in Dessau, 1931

thesis in his work designing industrial exhibitions in the late twenties and early thirties.

Three phases can be distinguished in Schmidt's typographical oeuvre:8

(1) an early phase, lasting until the midtwenties, of typographical experiments with the materials of typesetting, which included the printed materials and advertising leaflets for the products marketed under the label YKOmade by the Weimar office supply company Paul Heuss (1924), the title page of the special issue on the Bauhaus of the journal Junge Menschen 8 (1924), and the title page of the first issue of the Werkbund magazine Die Form (1925);

![](images/8a5cc9839bc78f37ea7e4da188542cf87253936b74a040d23d9d19a6949746eb.jpg)

![](images/7f196a37d0452e56231a85cb162142e34c220405cd1845564eacd3a81723aa6c.jpg)  
219 Joost Schmidt, Printed advertising material for the Bauhaus carpets produced by Rasch & Co., 1931 220 Joost Schmidt, Paraboloid Sculpture, 1928

(2) a second phase, in the second half of the twenties, of searching for principles of order in typography for tightening and regulating type and layout, which included the title page of the special issue on the Bauhaus of the magazine Offset 7 (1926) and the layout of type and images for the journal bauhaus, nos. 2–4 (1929);

(3) a final phase, in the late twenties and thirties, of expanding the communicative possibilities of type and typography by introducing graphic and/or photographic means andusing additional typefaces (such as ornamental and script types), which included the designs for printed materials for the “Bauhaus wallpaper"(around 1930), for the city of Dessau (1930), and for various industrial corporations.

Joost Schmidt's work as a fine artist has hardly been studied at all. Indeed, his intensive activity in applied graphic arts and his extensive teaching left very little time for his own art. Moreover, the established old masters at the Bauhaus (Klee, Kandinsky, Feininger, Schlemmer, Muche) claimed the field of fine art exclusively for themselves, and they saw Schmidt more as a teacher and theorist than as an artist,º an image that his relatively modest activity as a fine artist did not confute emphatically enough. Another factor that has obscured the reception of Schmidt's work as a fine artist is that almost all of his oeuvre was destroyed by fire bombings during World War II. What has survived, or at least has been documented in photographs, reveals, an original artist of unmistakable distinction. This quality can be seen, for example, in his watercolors from the Dessau period, which deal primarily with the interpenetration of geometric and stereometric elements, and in his abstract sculptural works, with names like “Hyperboloidal Sculpture" or “ParaboloidalSculpture," which consist of forms depicting torsional movement (that is, kinetic sculpture that have frozen in stasis) or, more precisely, of “illusionistic kinetic expression."10 The interplay between these constructions and Joost Schmidt's activity in the so-called plastic arts workshop is just as evident as that among several of the later, very subtle figural reliefs (e.g., The Runner, 1932; Female Figure, 1931–32)and the nude drawings done in Schmidt's course after Oskar Schlemmer had left the Bauhaus.

![](images/72eba2fd5250b8b226ba469bb3fc298f7ee027c32e000bff7bcd7cd9181da3d4.jpg)

Not until 1925, after the Bauhaus had moved from Weimar to Dessau, did Joost Schmidt pass his journeyman's exam as a sculptor before the chamber of crafts in Weimar.11 Shortly thereafter, he became a member of the teaching staff at the Dessau Bauhaus as a junior master in charge of the plastic arts workshop (formed from the workshop for wood and stone sculpture in Weimar). In parallelwith that task, he was teaching in the Bauhaus printing shop, officially run by Herbert Bayer, which at the time was developing into the advertising workshop, the directorship of which Schmidt would also take over in 1928, when Bayer left the Bauhaus. As part of the required courses at the Bauhaus for the first and second semesters, Schmidt was responsible from 1925 on for a two-semester course on typefaces and lettering, which he developedinto an elementary design course (see the teaching program in the following chapter). Even this list does not exhaust the spectrum of his teaching activities. Schlemmer went to Breslau in 1929, leaving behind the course “Der Mensch" (The human being), which he had conceived with an all-inclusive scope; Joost Schmidt jumped in to continue the course, or at least its drawingportion—figure and life drawing— though admittedly without the sovereign mastery that came naturally to Schlemmer. Schmidt insisted hisstudents adopt a“very idiosyncratic canon"12 of the human figure that he had developed himself: in addition to drawing from a live model he also had them draw from life-size puppets—a practice intended to make the function of the joints especially clear. Consequently, the nude studies produced in Schmidt's course often have a rather lifeless and mechanical character, and the Bauhaus student Kurt Kranz recalled that in re-

![](images/0e0110d13c61f8dabc9e49c88a5ab3c802051f79fa7eddd7bcc03723712db22b.jpg)  
221 Joost Schmidt, Female Figure, 1932; plaster on wood

![](images/3624c6a86bf78e12829def0428de7573b40b1cb7fdc434da257a86600d357490.jpg)  
222 Joost Schmidt, The Runner, 1932; plaster 223 Joost Schmidt, Figure Studies, 1932; graphite and colored pencils

![](images/094a0d51e097bb39cf90e7d540d328bc7522743556e07a6ec4cf34d6419d943d.jpg)

224 Kurt Kranz, Proportion Studies (Vertical and hori zontal sections of the torso), 1931–32

![](images/e500ac3aad56df524b07923c9b7d79e2dfeeecf97ef1e5aed37c6795e9f32048.jpg)

![](images/f5ff17477f86bd9e32b25c5aeff93cc58cd134adbe36f827fd3a0aedd20ac497.jpg)  
225 Kurt Kranz, Figure Studies, 1930–31

sponse the students in their nude studies soon "found their way to the sort of self-study that one finds in almost all art schools."13

In 1932 the Bauhaus in Dessau was closed. For Joost Schmidt, who had belonged to this school for more than a decade as student and teacher, this marked the end of his years at the Bauhaus, as he did not follow the institute in its relocation to Berlin. Difficult years followed: occasional work, prohibition from practicing his profession during the Nazi period, service in an antiaircraft brigade in 1939, the destruction of almost his entire oeuvre in 1943, and then another call to service in 1944. In 1945, after the war ended, Schmidt became a professor at the Hochschule für Bildende Kunst (School of fine arts) in Berlin. Three years later he died in Nuremberg.

## From the Lettering Course to the Elementary Design Course

As both teacher and person, Joost Schmidt was extraordinarily well liked at the Bauhaus. The students in particular liked him very much for his teaching and design abilities, his personal modesty, and his inexhaustible willingness to help. Especially in the years from 1928, which have been characterized as a phase of disintegration (see chapter 3), through his all but inexhaustible pedagogical engagement and the enormous range of courses he taught, Schmidt played a decisive role in keeping Bauhaus pedagogy from dissociating into the separate domains of learning and doing and did as much as possible to maintain the integration of the two as originally intended. This is evident in his efforts to combine the elementary design experience gained in the plastic arts workshop with the professional qualifications obtained in the advertising workshop into the special applied field of exhibition design while also establishing close connections between the advertising department and the photography department led by Walter Peterhans. The same intention can also be seen in his effort to expand the required course on type and lettering into an elementary design course that would complement the offerings of Albers, Klee, and Kandinsky.

In addition to teaching basic skills in lettering and the construction of type, Schmidt's course on type and lettering undertook explorations of the fundamental determinants of lettering and typefaces (consideredfrom the perspectives of language, the psychology of expression andoptics, technology, and economics).14 He was sharply critical of the bewildering variety of letter forms and especially ofGerman particularism in this area (the continued use of Fraktur, the German blackletter style): "Historical and national vanities and prejudices, along with all the corresponding forms of letters, belong in the junkyard of culture."15His goal was to simplify radically the forms of letters and to set international standards in order to establish the minimum conditions for a less problematic way to communicate among nations. The search for a systematic approach to the forms of letters was by no means rare during the twenties; for example, Kurt Schwitters worked to develop such a system as part of his interest in applied graphics.16

In contrast to several other Bauhaus masters who considered teaching a burden (this is true not only of Feininger and later Klee but also of junior masters such as Herbert Bayer), Schmidt showed a genuine interest in it. The background of Schmidt's concept of education was not so much his own aesthetic practice and theoretical reflections, as was generally thecase with the artistteachers already discussed,but rather the students, whom he sought“to develop" by working from their “abilities and skills."17 He did not merely trust in spontaneous self-realization but was convinced of the need for systematic instruction. Thus in 1928, as part of his expansion of the obligatory course on lettering and type, Schmidt developedan idea for teaching the basic instruction that was dividedaccording to “teaching program," "teaching method," and "teaching goals":

Outline of a teaching concept for the basic instruction at the Dessau Bauhaus, 19 January 1928

## I. Teaching program

Development (awakening) of creative energies from both the emotionally artistic and the rationally technological aspects.

226 Kurt Kranz, Lettering Studies, spatially illusionistic bends, 1931

![](images/ce33ad0c0288334fdd9ad5a96c81db05c1edf096a1d8994a2c364ad00c20f86a.jpg)

To cast doubt on the traditional, on prejudices, on education, on the milieu;

to become uninhibited again, open to experience and perception,

to engage with the means of design in a lively way.

Learning by doing (inventing, reinventing), comparing, founding,

training of creative abilities.

Starting from below with the simplest things.

## II. Teaching method

Stimulation and development of creative energies, starting with activity based on experience (interactions).

Planar and three-dimensional basic forms (elementary forms),

exercises in ordering, fixed order, free order. Seeking out connections that follow laws. Development of a feeling for space, form, and color.

Development of manual skills—-through relaxation exercises of a rhythmical nature, through freehand drawing and painting, through studying objects of three-dimensional or planar nature (in the latter case through structures of the surfaces as well).

Construction of spatial models, drawing of the connections that are found.

In every case, to orient in the plane, in color, and in space by means of seeking out, finding, and combining the regular relations of location, proportion, scale, weight, quantity, and quality

(quantity changes the quality with respect to color).

Dependence on rules, to experience through the act of depicting the relation of visual phenomena.

To start from the simplest thing.

## IlI. Teaching goals

Relaxation and liberation of the creative energies through interactions.

1. To develop a feeling for form and space, ideas of form and space.

2. To develop a feeling for construction, ideas of construction.

3. To develop a feeling for color,

ideas of color.

4. To develop a feeling for material, ideas of material.

5. To develop the ability to depict perceptions and ideas of the environment, of things.18

Though we may be justified in doubting whether the details of the division into three categories—"teaching program," "teaching method," "teaching goals"—are always correct, Schmidt's teaching concept certainly seems to be the summa of everything that constitutes Bauhaus pedagogy. It contains the idea——shared by all the Bauhaus masters—of the double determination of design by understanding and feeling, reason and intuition (although, as we have seen, the different masters certainly weighted the two differently). In addition, we see topoi of Bauhaus pedagogy in the wording of such goals as “liberation from dead conventions," "development of creative energies," and "development of manual skills," as well as in methodological principles such as "learning by doing," "from the simple to the difficult," and“from the elementary to the complex." Schmidt's teaching concept corresponds in general outline to the subject matter of Bauhaus pedagogy as well: design exercises with geometrical or stereometric basic forms; developing artistic principles of order and of the experience with design rules (scale, proportion, weight, quantity, quality, and so on); confronting problems of form, space, color, and materials; finally, very generally: increasing the sensitivity of our perception of our surroundings, intensifying our experience of things, and encouraging the ability to articulate individual artistic ideas.

What Schmidt's basic instruction consisted of in practice can be reconstructed, at least in part, from student works that have survived, from drawings by the artist's wife, and from the recollections of former students. However, it is often difficult to distinguish clearly between the products of this propaedeutic design course that developed from the obligatory courses on type and lettering and the practice works from the advertising workshop.

A characteristic feature of the design exercises from Joost Schmidt's courses is a standardized grid of three by three squares into which the students had to place nine variant solutions to the assigned task. This exercise was based on the idea of training creative thinking, thinking in alternatives, and also of making the students aware that there could be more than one solution to a design problem, a view that Schmidt shared with Josef Albers. The course began with exercises in the planar subdivision of assigned form elements like rectangles, circles, squares, points, lines, bars, and so on, in order to give the students experience in the variety of possible combinations even in a drastically reduced repertoire of drawing. These exercises took place against the background of a general theory of contrasts of the sort that Itten had taught during the founding phase of the Bauhaus (for example, ordered-disordered, big-small, round-angular, strong-weak, near-far, static-dynamic). Subsequent exercises in contrasts applied the same principles to individual letters or meaningless syllables, including studies in which nine contrasting variants of letters were to be found for a given, constant symbol. In accordance with the methodological principle of increasing difficulty in small, gradual steps, the level of difficulty of the assigned tasks increased from section to section as the course progressed. For example, using collage elements, the students were asked to design not only formal contrasts but also thematic ones; and, as Helene Schmidt-Nonné has shown, “a degree of social critique"19 can be seen in the selection of the materials used for the pictures and even more so in the combinations.

In addition to these exercises in contrasts and the straightforward instruction in type and lettering, the students in Joost Schmidt's course were given a solid introduction to the methods of perspectival representation (parallel and vanishingpoint perspective) that at the time—according to Kranz—had become an aesthetic goal unto themselves in all of the Bauhaus departments.2º This emphasis is also demonstrated in the studies of the geometry of the human figure (see above), which, like the exercises with type and lettering, were sometimes executed spatially (i.e., using isometrics or central perspective).

Finally, Schmidt also assigned exercises in color theory, though admittedly they were not nearly as intellectually in-depth as those of, say, Klee or Kandinsky. In contrast to the philosophical and metaphysical foundations of the latter, Schmidt's instruction on color was decidedly sober and practical. In the above-mentioned program of 19 January 1928 one of the goals was called the “development of a feeling for color, ideas of color." The way in which this goal was to be reached is clear from a passage in the catalog of the International Congress for Educators in the Arts in Prague in 1928, which remarks on Joost Schmidt's teachings on color at the Bauhaus: "Color—color system according to Ostwald; color studies."21 Though this program point may seem obscure, the mention of Ostwald speaks volumes. Whereas Klee and Itten—in the latter case, following Adolf Hölzel, his teacher in Stuttgart—vehemently rejected the scientifically conceived, quantifying color theory of the chemist and Nobel Prize winner Wilhelm Ostwald (1853–1932) and rejected it as useless for the arts, Gropius had begun as early as 1923, following the first far-reaching change of course at the institute, to display Ostwald's color circle in the rooms of the Bauhaus. In 1927 Ostwald was invited to the school for lectures, and in 1931 he was even accepted into the circle of “Friends of the Bauhaus." Kandinsky, who had led the workshop for wall painting in Weimar, had often referred his Dessau students to Ostwald (see "Theory of Design: Theory of Color" in chapter 8); and in the Dessau workshop for wall painting, led by Hinnerk Scheper, Ostwald's arrangement of colors was displayed for purposes of demonstration. This demonstrates yet again how determined were the efforts in Dessau to permeate design with science:

![](images/850160e352e0efa6a27f9e99e6325c8c92d026bf8e4d0eec2f80dba89e2c5cee.jpg)  
227 Kurt Kranz, Contrast study: variants of letters on a given black stencil D, 1931

nd noueWege gewiesen. In voll smmenster Weise entsprechen e den typo phischen Bodürt sson ury ind von ünstlor he aul dom m ebiete hist und der sklamo tender Stolle shen. Erfaht en fast hundert hriger Arbeit im Dienste dos Jchdrucks vereinigen sich mit en modernstentechnischen Ein

![](images/f35dd3f98f4532aa2262e29fc1832f0cb849f679f65460e25666ac154238a010.jpg)  
228 Hajo Rose, Contrast study: variants of letters on a given black stencil D, 1931

color, even more so than form, had traditionally been seen as a realm largely immune to a rational approach. The theory of color that Ostwald developed on a commission from the German Werkbund—with its determined effort to rationalize and quantify—cannot be discussed in detail here. However, the exercises completed by Bauhaus students in Joost Schmidt's class demonstrate that Schmidt had them conduct extensive studies based on Ostwald's arrangement of colors. As with the exercises in form, the students started with a standardized diagram—clearly borrowed from Paul Klee—of a five-by-five grid. The assigned tasks included systematic attempts to translate the "full colors" of Ostwald's normalized, twenty-four-part "color circle" into the "bright-clear" (i.e., lightened) or "dark-clear" (i.e. darkened) colors (using Ostwald's terminology) and to make "color translations from colored to uncolored." We cannot, of course, say whether such exercises did in fact serve the stated goal of training the "feeling for color" and the "ideas of color"—or even of cultivating them in the artistic sense. With respect to the training in the advertising department, however, they surely established a secure groundwork from which to obtain useful results for the use of color in designing posters and other printed materials.

## Advertising Workshop

In the advertising workshop, which Joost Schmidt took over in 1928, the exercises for beginners clearly overlapped to some extent with the assignment of tasks in the basic instruction.2² Very soon, however, particular fields of work were decided on, as is evident from the fragmentary notes that Schmidt made in 1931. It is interesting to note that-despite all the focus on practical training, even to the extent of accepting real commissions—the interest in basic methods of representation (drawing, say) did not fall victim to the dictate of specializations.

Practical typography   
Business papers   
Prospectuses, advertisements, posters, and so on   
Company logos   
Book and magazine design   
Product packaging   
Shop window displays   
Display mannequins   
Display models   
Advertising exhibitions   
Advertising photographs   
Montages of advertising photographs   
Supplemental instruction   
Masters' studio:   
Design of advertising materials in combination with actual practice   
(in addition)   
Life drawing, representative drawing   
Perspective: vanishing-point and parallel   
perspective.   
Photographic techniques.23

This teaching program shows Schmidt in the thick of contemporary trends. From about 1923 on, advertising in Germany enjoyed an unprecedented boom based on the arqument that it “improved quality and awakened egalitarian needs" and also functioned“in the serviceof democratization."24 To date just a few of the most important events: in 1923 the magazine Gebrauchsgrafik (Applied qraphics) was established; in 1925 the Union of German Window Dressers was founded and began to publish the magazine Schaufenster: Kunst undTechnik (Display windows: Art and technology); in 1926 Roy S. Durstine's book Making Advertisements and Making Them Pay (New York: C. Scribner's Sons, 1920)appeared in German translation; and in 1929 the International Poster Exhibition took place in Munich.

In Germany it was Moholy-Nagy, Herbert Bayer, and Joost Schmidt and, outside the Bauhaus, Max Burchartz, Jan Tschichold, and Paul Renner; in Russia, it was Rodchenko and El Lissitzky, among others, who made decisive contributions to the artistic distinction of the New Advertising that was flourishing at time, based on the principles of constructivism and the postulate of objectivity. In contrast to the raucous deportment of the usual rhetorical advertisements, the practice of these men was based to a large degree on ethics. Their motto was “Neither suggestion nor information"—that is to say, their goal was not to inveigle someone into something but to persuade“by means of a clear presentation of facts, of economic and scientific data."25 Along with students in the advertising department and in collaboration with the workshop for interior furnishings at the Bauhaus, Joost Schmidt put these principles into practice most convincingly in his avant-garde and groundbreakingexhibition buildings in Berlin (1929)andDresden (1930). These “three-dimensional, accessible diagrams or graphic demonstration sites"26were meant to form a synthesis ofart andcommerce—an idea that had already been propagated by the German Werkbund before World War I.

## The Plastic Arts Workshop

Thę students of the plastic arts workshop, which was also run by Joost Schmidt, took part in the works created for these exhibition stands as well. The information available about this workshop is sparse. Even the date of its creation has not yet been firmly established. Wingler dates it from the fall of 1925;²7 Eckhard Neumann comments that Schmidt led the plastic arts workshop “from the beginning in Dessau";28 Helene Schmidt-Nonné reports that the workshop began to be established in the spring of 1927, a date that fits with the details given by another member of the Bauhaus, Heinz Loew;29 in other relevant literature the date 1928 is found.30 The following remark from the notes of Helene Schmidt-Nonné might help to clear up these discrepancies: “The stage workshop and the plastics arts workshop were not separated initially; Joost Schmidt was master of form and work for the plastic arts workshop; for the stage, just technical master. Because his conception did not coincide with that of Schlemmer, he ceased work in the stage workshop and concentrated his work on the plastic arts workshop"; in addition, she notes that “at first this workshop did not have a budget and even later it had only a minimal, symbolic one."31 Thus, Schmidt apparently did lead the plastic arts workshop from 1925 on, but initially it existed only as an appendage to the stage workshop—it is no coincidence that Schmidt produced designs for a mechanical stage at that time—and only later regained the institutional autonomy it had had as the sculpture workshop before the move to Dessau. There seems to have been a gradual process of emancipation that found its culmination in 1928 with the formal declaration of its parity with the other Bauhaus workshops.

![](images/6e102ad648563a9efbb593bc861970ab9b4e56704ebb95721ed677c8087b74bf.jpg)  
229 Hajo Rose, Poster design for a music house, 1932 230 Eugen Batz, Student work Cellon, circa 1930

![](images/482c140effe7c7ff6bfc6297dcfe4bd5e030704c3eceecfc54b587c540ea8d9a.jpg)

The renaming of the Weimar workshops for wood and stone sculpture as the "plastic arts workshop" was more than just a rhetorical maneuver designed to shed terminology that was considered outdated and that evoked the fine arts. It corresponded precisely to the change of direction in the Bauhaus program from about 1923 that we have mentioned several times. In that context, it not only makes sense that no sculptures were produced in the plastic arts workshop during the first years in Dessau but also that this workshop would have been subsumed under the goals of the stage workshop (the production of threedimensional stage sets and props). It was Joost Schmidt's personal achievement to free the plastic arts workshop from this functional context and bring it the attention unquestionably merited by the design of three-dimensional objects in the teaching program of the Bauhaus. This was all the more necessary in 1928, because Moholy-Nagy, who had placed great emphasis on sculpture in his preliminary course, had left the Bauhaus and so—apart from Albers's preliminary course—there was hardly any opportunity left for a systematic focus on the phenomena of bodies and space.

Thus, in contrast to the work in other workshops at the Bauhaus, which focused on applications and consisted in part of commissions, the emphasis in the plastic arts workshop was on the "elementary study of three-dimensional, sculptural relationships."32 That is, it was not about “autonomous art" or about "art at the construction site" or about the production of three-dimensional utensils but instead provided a general education in the plastic arts. Its goal was “to awake, to develop, to intensify the ability to imagine space and the consciouș experience of spatial sense perceptions, and to realize spatial ideas."33

In keeping with the formal reductionism of basic geometrical forms that dominated the Bauhaus, and based on his previous experience with the design of the vestibule reliefs of 1923, Joost Schmidt rigorously limited the field of tasks to

systematic exploration of elementary spatial figures: the cube, the sphere, the cylinder, the cone, and so on. At first the figures are produced by the students, that is, they are modeled in clay, molded in plaster, and become objects of experiment. A period of almost scientific laboratory work begins: examination of scale relationships, proportional relations, strength, the possibility of changing positions. The students operate with figures that are placed in relation to one another; one body is allowed to penetrate another, the bodies are allowed to penetrate each other and then they see what the

231 Franz Ehrlich, Study of penetration (the individual parts and the whole), circa 1928

232Franz Ehrlich, "Study of penetration in two views. Tubular cylinder with curve of a spherical penetration and the penetration of a smaller, complete cylinder that is slanted on one side,“ circa 1928

233 Joost Schmidt, Virtual volumes: rotation of straight line and circle (rod and ring) to form hyperboloid and sphere

![](images/634b48ccd67ad79a2ed18694757bfc55827df38ea07b49942dd9d4042420dbaf.jpg)

![](images/d59e37c0c9218c0d132d2ab16969e742d902c95c1ea1fe9483bde1c2a43fc45c.jpg)

![](images/9ff6f2337146a4d7e381d21660f361268edd3f682f5b23a5059660b98f2360aa.jpg)

operations have produced: the penetrating figure leaves behind a hollow space, a negative volume, as a spatial curve of astonishingly high aesthetic quality—something that had been invisible is now visible.

The theme is further developed using several rotating bodies, until it becomes a kind of study of penetration created from two round bodies that have been penetrated twice: from a reclining cylinder that is penetrated by a sphere which is in turn penetrated by an upright cylinder with a significantly smaller diameter. The process creates a kind of sweeping spatial curve that is marked out on the surface of the sphere: a spatially sweeping lemniscate.

One of the exercises calls for the students to form series of analogies: "from plane by way of body to line"; that is, to experience how the changes that  form undergoes when its scale relationships are transformed can be shown using a body (in this case, a cylinder). A disk appears as a plane, a small cylinder as a body, and a raised cylinder with a very narrow diameter as a rod or linelike structure. Each is a cylinder, but they appear to be three different forms: a plane, a body, a line. It is an exercise for the eye and the hand, for seeing and feeling optic and haptic qualities—to sensitize spatial and sensory experience by means of one's own building activities.34

In the Hannes Meyer era these basic explorations were supplemented by concrete commissioned works (designing exhibition stands). This assured the practical context necessary to exorcise the danger of a self-satisfied aestheticism and an (unintended) glorification of formal analyses of art.

As we have seen, Joost Schmidt played a central role in integrating the Dessau Bauhaus and was at the same time—because of his modest and unassumingpersonality—one of its great unknowns. Ludwig Hirschfeld-Mack called him one of the“most richly endowed in plans and ideas,"35 and Kandinsky is said to have expressed his satisfaction at a meeting of Bauhaus colleagues: “In Schmidtchen's class [using the diminutive, as was common at the Bauhaus, to indicate sympathy and affection—R.W.] something very important is happening, and we didn't even know it."36

## 12 Preliminary Remarks on the Reception of the Bauhaus in Germany

A comprehensive history of the reception of the Bauhaus has yet to be written. Perhaps this is because it spread internationally, and the ramifications to which the ideas, principles, and methods of the Bauhaus were subject became too elaborate to summarize, so that such a gigantic task seemed all but impossible.1 This is true not only of any attempt to present the influences of the Bauhaus in the fields of architecture, design, and the fine arts—that is, at the level of material culture— but also in the field of education. A historical assessment of the reception of the pedagogical ideas for art and design at this art school of modernism, even if confined to Germany, would be a project that would exceed the framework of this book and would have to be the subject of its own study. In chapters 2 and 4 we saw it would be impossible to provide a prehistory of theBauhaus and its pedagogical premises that would be even approximately complete; a history of the reception of the Bauhaus that could claim to be thorough is equally impossible.² Consequently, the discussion that follows, which because of the need for stringent selection can do little more than cast spotlights and sidelights, must be accepted from the start as a mere fragment. This is true even though those who were involved in this history in some form but who are mentioned not at all or not adequately here might become sensitive or suffer wounded pride.

## The Weimar Republic

The historical effects of Bauhaus pedagogy which can be especially clearly recognized in the principle of workshop teaching, the value placed on architecture, the concept of the preliminary course, and the permeation of elementary design theory in arts education in the schools—did not begin, as is often assumed, with the closing of the Bauhaus in 1933 and the subsequent emigration of numerous Bauhaus masters and students to the United States in particular (see chapter 13) but is already evident during the Weimar Republic both in professional training for artists and in aesthetic education in schools. It is, however, important to distinguish precisely here. For it would be a mistake in a historical review to ascribe to the influence of the Bauhaus everything that at first glance might seem to be a concept or effect typical of this art school of modernism, even if it was the most important school of its kind. It is essential to recall that, like the Bauhaus, many art schools and applied art schools of the interwar period were indebted to the ideas of the debate on art school reform that were outlined in "Bauhaus Pedagogy in the Context of Art School Reform" in chapter 4; and that after World War I these schools deeply influenced by ideas that circulated first in the Arbeitsrat für Kunst (Work council for the arts) and the Novembergruppe (November group), for example, and later more broadly in the German Werkbund. For that reason, one cannot simply speak of a blanket dependence on the great model of the Bauhaus, even though it did, of course, have an influence. Such influence was most direct in cases where members of the Bauhaus themselves took with them to other schools the concepts of education and training they had developed as teachers or experienced as students.

This sort of complex set of problems can be seen, for example, in the case of the Academy of Art in Breslau, under its directors Hans Poelzig (1903–16), August Endell (1918–25), and Oskar Moll (1925-32). Hartmut Frank has shown, in an essay with the apt title "Ein Bauhaus vor dem Bauhaus" (A Bauhaus before the Bauhaus), that in Breslau under Poelzig "the concept of the reform of arts and crafts training that would later be considered the characteristic creation of the Bauhaus"3 was already in practice. For example, Poelzig considered the workshops—alongside the courses in particular subjects—as the most important places for learning at his school; and even before Johannes Itten introduced the preliminary course at the Bauhaus, the Breslau academy had a preliminary class. Moreover, Poelzig considered it essential to integrate all of the genres of the visual arts with all of the branches of the crafts under the primacy of architecture; this was, of course, an idea that Gropius later postulated as his central concern in the Bauhaus manifesto of 1919.

In contrast to Poelzig, who was a tireless force behind art school reform even before World War I, August Endell, one of the outstanding Jugendstil artists of Germany, supported from 1918 on "the transformation of the Breslau school from a reformed applied arts school to a pure art academy . . . that also had workshops."4 Although Endell was accused of weak leadership and a lack of vision, it is interesting to note with respect to support for the Modern Movement that the appointment of Adolf Rading, an active supporter of the Neues Bauen (New building) movement, as professor of architecture occurred during Endell's directorship. It should also be recalled that Rading in turn led to the appointment of another proponent of this movement, Hans Scharoun (who as an expressionist architect had been a member of the Arbeitsrat für Kunst), to the Breslau academy in 1925.

Under the direction of the painter Oskar Moll from 1925 on, the academy in Breslau became “a gathering place of the artistic avant-garde in Germany, . . . a place for an . . . undogmatic form of modernism."5 This meant that the school created an environment in which elements of the Bauhaus could flourish alongside expressionists like Otto Mueller andmasters of NeueSachlichkeit like Carlo Mense and Alexander Kanoldt; for example, Oskar Schlemmer was appointed director of the stage class in 1929 and continued to teach the project “Der Mensch" that he had begun at the Bauhaus, and Georg Muche was a member of the teaching faculty as professor of painting from 1931 to 1933. We should also mention Johannes Molzahn, who was not a member of the Bauhaus in the strict sense, but who was closely associated with the early Bauhaus and was active in Breslau between 1928 and 1932. Rather than go into detail about the subjectsandmethods of these artists as teachers, it must suffice to cite a statement Schlemmer made that casts a spotlight on the pedagogical principles at the academy in

Breslau during the late twenties and early thirties: "Bauhaus teaching (Klee, Kandinsky): making the personal universal. Applied to Breslau, extremist: creating, participation of the students, involvement in their own larger projects (the old system of the 'master studio'). Thus an egoistic principle: work as the best means to develop oneself and others. Basically the principle that goes without saying: the best things happen when someone enjoys something, i.e., is totally engaged in it. That can only happen with one's own work. For the academy, then, the point is to harness this egoistic element for the universal. The whole teaching plan must be built on that."6 This “individualistic" conception was also reflected in the fact that Breslau had two preliminary classes side by side, one for fundamentals of drawing and another for painting, which in their increased specialization and the closer connection of the teachers to artistic practice were conceived as far less general than was the case for the preliminary course at the Bauhaus. The material studies in drawings from the preliminary class of Paul Dobers, which aimed at knowledge of the “secret life, [the] uniqueness" of things,7 reveal certain parallels to student works from the preliminary course as taught by Itten and Albers, but the academy at Breslau lacked the comprehensive goal of a general introduction to elementary principles of design that was sought at the Bauhaus.

![](images/4e721f90efb3736910f16e75317d75a98c06e33b6536cd5998f0a60bc3f2fb09.jpg)  
234 Johannes Rickert, Diagram of lengths of heads, after Leonardo da Vinci, circa 1930; Oskar Schlemmer's class at the academy in Breslau 235 Victor Wendel, Sketched material study, circa 1930; Paul Dober's preliminary class, at the academy in Breslau

![](images/f023403c35ebbfdc8ab0ca4c11fc79c90eba74505baf63d75bd298d9a57cb7a0.jpg)

If at the academy in Breslau under Endell and even more so Moll the accent was clearly on the fine arts, the trend at the Kunstgewerbeschule (arts and crafts school) in Halle, better known as Burg Giebichenstein (Giebichenstein castle), under its director, the architect Paul Thiersch, was emphatically on applied arts in the period between 1915 and 1928. Very much as Gropius did initially, Thiersch strongly emphasized the crafts, though, it seems, not so much with a utopian romanticism of the medievalBauhütte (masons' lodge) but with a more realistic andpragmatic orientation; this was almost a given, as the school had evolved from the artisans' school of the city of Halle.8 In keeping with this inclination, the workshops were systematically built up beginning in 1919, as teaching workshops at first, but already in 1920— much earlier than at the Bauhaus—as productive or production workshops. In Halle, however, the industrial orientation that was typical of the late Weimar Bauhaus and especially of the Dessau Bauhaus did not play a significant role. The “Burg," as it was called for short, "remained faithful even after 1925 to the workshop program and myth of the handicrafts that it had developed in the early twenties."9 The small craft-production series, not industrial mass production, was considered the standard, and the products designed and produced in Halle were distinct from the design's of the Dessau Bauhaus in their “moderate modernity," so that, despite all the similarities, "the Burg could be called an anti-intellectual pendant to the Bauhaus."10

Several members of the Bauhaus who came to the Burg beginning in the midtwenties were also representatives of this kind of moderate modernism. Gerhard Marcks did not make the move from Weimar, or rather Dornburg an der Saale (where the Bauhaus ceramics workshop was a kind of annex), to Dessau and instead accepted an appointment as director of the sculpture workshop (from 1928 to 1933 also interim director) at Halle. Benita Otte, who was at the Bauhaus in Weimar from 1920 to 1925, became artistic director of the workshop for hand weaving in 1925. Marguerite Friedlaender also made the move from Weimar to Halle in 1925, becoming director of the ceramics workshop (and later the porcelain workshop as well). Erich Dieckmann—alongside Marcel Breuer one of the outstanding furniture designers at the Bauhaus—took over the cabinetmakers' workshop at the Burg Giebichenstein in 1931, following an intermezzo at the Bauhochschule (School of Architecture) in Weimar. Hannes Wittwer, who was for a time Hannes Meyer's partner in his Swiss architectural office and a teacher at the Bauhaus from 1927 to 1929, became Paul Thiersch's successor as director of the architecture class in Halle in 1929. Erich Consemüller, who was at the Bauhaus from 1922 to 1929, taught at the Burg from 1931 to 1933 in the architecture class and in the advertising department; and Walter Herzger, a student at the Weimar Bauhaus from 1921 to 1924, led the graphic workshop from 1930 to 1933. Despite this brief sketch of the personal interconnections between the Bauhaus and the Burg,11 and despite certain stylistic influences, the school in Halle should in no way be seen as an offshoot of the Bauhaus. Rather, the Burg had its own version of the reform ideas typical of the time, even if it may not have been as radical as the Bauhaus in artistic matters or as intellectually sharp in conceptual ones, and perhaps even lacked its rigor and structural logic in pedagogical aspects as well.

236 Marguerite Friedlaender, Teapot, 1926; stoneware; Burg Giebichenstein Halle  
![](images/1d6b8ff30182217e6d17ddaa27661c0e502b21a749e91253f22fb617ee297f04.jpg)

![](images/531b59c0e7c01920cd506288b8ddcd115af42cdb2f54e7176304bc7ce2728fec.jpg)  
237 Werner Stachelroth, Exercise with elements, 1932; watercolor; Erich Dieckmann's course, Burg Giebichenstein Halle

This is true in broad outlines of the School of Architecture in Weimar as well, which after the move of the Bauhaus to Dessau was led by the architect Otto Bartning from 1926 to 1930. With this school Bartning, who had already worked with Gropius at the Arbeitsrat für Kunst to plan a new art school based on the ideal of the Bauhütte,12 continued the Bauhaus tradition in Weimar. Although, like the Burg in Halle, stylistically more moderate and, because of the lack of prominent artistic personalities, less innovative and of lesser spiritual quality, the School of Architecture can "certainly be seen as a subsidiary institute of the Bauhaus,"13 especially given that the faculty was to a large extent recruited from former students of the Bauhaus: Ernst Neufert, Heinz Nösselt, Erich Dieckmann, Otto Lindig, Reinhold Weidensee, Richard Winkelmayer, Wilhelm Wagenfeld, Ludwig Hirschfeld-Mack, and Otto Dorfner. Much as was the case in Halle, the workshops focusing on productive work formed the pedagogical core of the school, and—similar to the Bauhaus program—Bartning's conception for the school saw architecture as the focus: "We want to give the artisan a sense of the machine as a creator of standards, and the technician a sense of the hand as touching, grasping, and receiving inspiration from the material; in both the artisan and the technician we wish to awaken the sense for the context of their work within the architectural project and to enable the master builder to achieve a great task: to combine handwork and machine work, individual pieces and the flow of series into the most human work of all: architecture."14

238 Wilhelm Wagenfeld, Table lamp M 25, 1930; nickel-plated brass, painted black, mirror glass, opai glass; Bauhochschule Weimar  
![](images/cc0fa3ae9f8eb963a755770cc155dae4e8eb1587e1984ad32d54312a3b5aefff.jpg)

The Frankfurt Kunstschule (Art school), founded through a merger of the Städelschule (Städel school) with the local arts and crafts school, was, according to Hans M. Wingler, "the most important original derivation of the Bauhaus"15 of the Weimar Republic. Under its director, the art historian Fritz Wichert, it was reorganized beqinning in 1924 on the model of the Weimar Bauhaus and divided into three main sections: preliminary classes, designclasses, and workshops.16 Whereas the preliminary class for the first semester, under the direction of Peter Rasmussen, a student of Orlik, remained rather traditional with its instruction in drawing from nature (especially animals and plants), the works from the preliminary class for the second semester, under former Bauhaus member Peter Röhl, clearly reveal “the relationship to the teachings in the preliminary course at the Bauhaus and the Dutch de Stijl group. Structural analyses of drawing compositions and work with the complementary and contrasting effects of color occupied the foreground."17 The stimuli from the Bauhaus can also be seen in the series of other Bauhaus members who were called to the school from 1925 on: Adolf Meyer, Gropius's closest collaborator in his private architectural practice and an auxiliary instructor at the Bauhaus, took over the class for high-rises; Christian Dell, who had been the master of crafts in the metal workshop at Weimar, now helped buildthe metal workshop under Wichert; Josef Hartwig, who collaboratedasa technician with Endell in the design of the Jugendstil facade of the Elvira House in Munich and was master of crafts in the Weimar workshop for stone and wood sculpture (where he designed the Bauhaus chessboard), was thedirector of the sculpture workshop in Frankfurt from 1925; and Ferdinand Kramer, who was a lecturer at the Frankfurt Art School from 1926 to 1928. Kramer had been at the Bauhaus only briefly, in 1919, but his understanding of functional architecture and standardized methods for production furniture were compatible with the ideas that then dominated at the Dessau Bauhaus. Paul Renner, who was then developing the now-famous typeface Futura and who led the class for advertising graphics and typography, and Willi Baumeister, a student of Hölzel who was Renner's successor from 1928 until he was dismissed by the Nazis in 1933, were both close to the spirit of the Bauhaus. As at the Dessau Bauhaus, the orientation around the needs of industry and collaboration with commerce played a decisive role-except in the classes for painting (Max Beckmann), sculpture (Richard Scheibe), and fine art graphics. The activities that took place on the threshold between teaching and actual practice were often connected to what is perhaps the most spectacular cultural renewal project of the Weimar Republic, which has become known and influential under the name “Das Neue Frankfurt" (The new Frankfurt).18

![](images/c458cae803bfe8f53c178d09b4e4de09a05bde284436bd5186de1254cf20cebb.jpg)  
239 Otto Dragässer, Nature studies of stones and shells, 1927; Peter Röhl's preliminary class at the Kunstschule in Frankfurt

It is not possible to discuss all the public art schools during the Weimar Republic that adopted elements of Bauhaus pedagogy. We will mention only the preliminary instruction that Max Peiffer Watenphul gave at the Folkwang-Schule in Essen from 1927 to 1931,19 the preliminary classes at the Landeskunstschule (Regional art school) in Hamburg given by Fritz Schleifer (a member of the Weimar Bauhaus) and Alfred Ehrhardt (a member of the Dessau Bauhaus; on whom more below) from 1930 to 1933,2º and the course given at the Kunstgewerbeschule in Stettin by Kurt Schwerdtfeger, Vincent Weber, and Else Mögelin.21 A quick look at several private art schools of the period that are of interest in the context of Bauhaus pedagogy would have to include Édmund Kesting's school Der Weg (The path) in Dresden (in Berlin as well from 1926 on) and the Itten-Schule in Berlin. The basis of both schools was not so much the idea of workshops that had been propagated with such vehemence by art school reformers and members of the Werkbund but rather the idea that the point was to use the medium of art to educate people to become creative (not necessarily to become artists or designers). This is understandable when we recall that Kesting, like Itten, was close to the expressionistic tendencies of the Sturm circle; thus, both started out with a very specific image of the human being and understanding of the world that, in contrast to the aesthetic ideology of the Werkbund, aimed more at developing and nurturing the individual.

The Dresden school Der Weg, which had the programmatic subtitle Neue Schule für Kunst (New school for art) and thus signaled its antiacademic aims even in its name, was founded in 1919, at almost exactly the same time as the opening of the Staatliches Bauhaus. As with the early Bauhaus, Kesting's school was dominated by expressionistic tendencies and the search for a “new human being" on a spiritual basis. A 1926 pamphlet reads:

Every human being travels a different path

But all of them should find a single goal.

We try to give all of the power to enable the path to be completed.

We summon all who want to know the reality of the spirit.

We summon all who want to explore the experience of creative laws.

We summon all who want to find themselves.

We summon all who want to find the other human being.

We summon all for whom art is life.

We summon all who seek life.

Because we walk the path to the human being.

Because we walk the path to art.22

Kesting found a companion to walk this path in the Sturm artist Lothar Schreyer, who had led the expressionistic Bauhaus stage from 1921 to 1923 and who not only taught at the Weg-Schule in Dresden from 1924 to 1927 but also led it together with Kesting, just as Hans Haffenrichter, a student at the Bauhaus from 1921 to 1924, took over the directorship of the Weg-Schule annex in Berlin in 1926. In a prospectus for the school from the midtwenties, the following global pedagogical goal was expressed: "The goal is to allow the students to develop individually and to place before them the rich variety of means of artistic expression."23 In addition to artistic subjects like drawing, painting, graphic arts, modeling, composition, and design, there were also courses in diverse branches of the applied arts (for example, textile design, metalworking, bookbinding, advertising, construction for trade fairs, interior design, and stage design), areas in which the abovementioned goals, somewhat effusive in their formulation and certainly no longer up-to-date in 1926, were given a realistic interpretation.

The situation was similar with the private art school run by Johannes Itten, which opened in 1926 as the Moderne Kunstschule Berlin, but was renamed the /tten Schoo/ in 1928 and was closed in 1934.24 Following his departure from the Bauhaus in the spring of 1923, Itten first spent several years at the Swiss Mazdaism center in Herrliberg, but he returned to Germany in 1926 to establish his own art school and realize his vision of another Bauhaus as a "school of the fine arts"25 (seę “Between Art and Design" in chapter 5). The teaching was on the basis of the concept of a holistic art training that Itten had developed during his years in Vienna and continued at the Bauhaus in Weimar. Consequently, the goals, topics, and methodological approaches in Berlin did not differ fundamentally from what Itten had practiced earlier. Thus a 1926 prospectus for the school read: "The goal of the instruction is to help develop the creative forces in the human being and to teach the means of the visual arts. To reduce . . . inhibitions and to develop physical abilities; to train the ability to think artistically and the powers of the soul, to teach the means of the visual arts."26 The artist-teacher Itten did not, of course, give up his spiritual and mystical orientation²7 in Berlin.

![](images/2988742f8d68e249513ac5800f585afd4d9c6fa3a4cd319d75812e3bcb3fec99.jpg)  
240 Maximilian Debus, Composition of graphic elements, 1930 Itten School, Berlin

Nevertheless, the student works from his school in Berlin often display a more strict, constructive pictorial language than most of the studies from the preliminary course at the Bauhaus, which owe more to the spirit of expressionism. More so than in Weimar, where Itten emphasized the emotional understanding of the essence of things and the transformation of that understanding into art, his course on art was now permeated by rational elements. This can be seen most clearly in the textbook on design the Itten School published in 1930 under the title Tagebuch: Beiträge zu einem Kontrapunkt der bildenden Kunst (Diary: Contributions to a counterpoint of the visual arts). The subtitle clearly signaled a very specific ambition, namely, that of establishing a set of rules for art analogous to those of music that could provide the student of design with orientation and structure. It is equally obvious that Itten was building on the work of his Stuttgart teacher Hölzel, who spent his life searching for the "figured bass of painting."

Itten's private school in Berlin can be thought of as a very specific variant of the Bauhaus; this is clear not only from the founder's views on art pedagogy but also from the fact that practical subjects like architecture, photography, advertising, and graphic arts were taught alongside the fine arts. Unlike the Bauhaus and the reformed arts and applied arts schools of the period, Itten's school did not have workshops itself but rather supported the idea that "the students should, on completing the basic training, work as interns in the field."28 Elements of Bauhaus pedagogy flowed into the Itten School, in part because of the courses taught by the teachers who came from the Bauhaus; thus it acted both to preserve and multiply certain Bauhaus ideas. We should mention above all Georg Muche, who came from the Sturm circle, was appointed to the Bauhaus in 1920 on Itten's initiative, and joined the Itten School in 1927, before accepting an appointment to the Breslau Academy in 1931 (see above). Former Bauhaus students like Gyula Pap, Max Bronstein, Fred Forbat, and Ernst Neufert belonged to the teaching staff in Berlin; in addtion, Otto Umbehr (better known as Umbo) taught the photography class from 1928 to 1930, followed by Lucia Moholy, László Moholy-Nagy's first wife, who taught the class until her emigration in 1933.

In addition to the appropriation of elements of Bauhaus pedagogy in public and private art schools in the interwar period, the permeation of Bauhaus pedagogical conceptions into art and handicraft courses in the primary and secondary schools of the Weimar Republic represents a second strand relevant to the history of the reception of the Bauhaus. In handicraft courses in particular, according to Antje Middeldorf, the influence of the Bauhaus was “broad and anonymous,"29 though she qualified this with the remark that such crafts training was oriented more around the goals of the German Werkbund and not so much around the principles of the Bauhaus, which would have seemed too intellectual and formalistic to a typical schoolteacher of the time like Georg Stiehler. In 1925 Stiehler characterized a Bauhaus chair as "properly built—'like 2 x 2 = 4'— but also 'horribly impoverished, lacking all feeling.'"30 In this context it should, of course, be emphasized again that the Bauhaus, in turn, at least from 1923 on, was deeply permeated by Werkbund ideas.
