The Bauhaus had become an international model for training in modern industrial and graphic design. Accordingly, the call for a Bauhaus in Chicago primarily reflected a desire for a training institute for modern aesthetics and design. Little or nothing, however, was known in Chicago about the significance of architecture at the Bauhaus in Germany, as the discipline uniting all branches of art, about any metaphysical dimension or about a Bauhaus philosophy.122 Neither was any consideration given to the status of the degrees awarded by the New Bauhaus in the sense of a government-approved final qualification.

![](images/40be5f750264e9627a8926332d6ed7370ed7905afb1d8f583e003880b5ae8913.jpg)  
267 László Moholy-Nagy and Man Ray at the opening of the exhibition Art in Advertising of the Container Corporation of America, Chicago, April 1945 268 New Bauhaus, Chicago, circa 1938

![](images/bab161f1b51e103c20427ca8053a088ab4fa2aabde1c91e9a6d821847daa384b.jpg)

Furthermore, hardly any funds were available to the Chicago Institute, and the teachers who had been employed by the new school in the early part of 1939 were teaching there without receiving any payment. In 1940 Walter Paepcke was the only board member of the former Association of Arts and Industries who began to support the school with cash resources. This financial assistance provided by Paepcke represented a valuable contribution toward the Institute's ability to make a name for itself as a place of training for product design. Paepcke was the owner of the Container Corporation of America (CCA). This company, which he established in 1926, manufactured all types of packaging for consumer goods, from shipping containers down to cardboard boxes. Elizabeth Paepcke's knowledge about the developments in modern art and the influence she had on her husband in this respect, ensured that, particularly after 1935, when the designer Egbert Jacobsen became art director there, the company was identified with commercial modernism. Jacobsen created the corporate identity of the Container Corporation, which subsequently became the first North American company to systematically and continuously commission modern, contemporary artists for advertising campaigns. Artists such as Herbert Bayer, Fernand Léger, Man Ray, Jean Hélion, Henry Moore, Willem de Kooning, and Xanti Schawinsky designed the CCA's advertising material. In April 1945 the company made available its collection of more than one hundred works for an exhibition at the Art Institute in Chicago. The show was entitled "Modern Art in Advertising" and, together with the annual international design conference in Aspen, Colorado, held for the first time in 1951 by Paepcke and Jacobsen under the title "Design as a Function of Management," underpinned the company's image as a link between designers and industrial managers.123 Paepcke's early public support, therefore, had a noteworthy influence on Moholy-Nagy's school. The fact that “Moholy virtually banished the term art from the school, supplanting it with Mies's detested design,"124 bears witness to this fact.

By setting up vocational training in the disciplines of graphic design and photography, the Institute adapted itself to the demands of North American consumer culture. This development had already been heralded before Moholy-Nagy's death, when the New Bauhaus became the Institute of Design (ID) and, as a result, the curriculum was restructured: it was intensified by the takeover of ID by IIT in 1949 and the appointment of Jay Doblin to the position of Director at the Institute in 1954. On the one hand, this development led to a situation in which supporters of Moholy's pedagogical principles—first and foremost Sibyl Moholy-Nagy in her book Moholy-Nagy: Experiment in Totality (1950)—felt dutybound to once again draw attention to Moholy's passionate experimental spirit and cúltural visions. On the other hand, given the growing acceptance of modern design by the world of commerce and business, together with factors suchas market economy profit-seeking, advertising, and consumer taste, as well as the demands for a more vocational type of design training, it is easy to see why Gropius held so relentlessly to the original Bauhaus idea. The deveiopment associated with mass technology and consumer culture was nat only inconsistent with the utopian character of the Bauhaus and Moholy's subversive activism, but it also cast doubt on the topical relevance of the Bauhaus principles. "Having embraced the aesthetic and economic benefits of modern design," Allen concludes, "what need had consumer commerce of the modernists' metaphysics and cultural anthropology? What need had it even of the Bauhaus's elementary art education for professional designers? 'Who needs to go to a Bauhaus any more,' asked prominent Chicago architect Harry Weese some years later, 'to learn about space and volume by waving your hand around in a paper bag? Kids do that in first grade.'"125

The problems that appeared between the thirties and the fifties in attempting to define the term "design" were caused by the fact that it was no longer used only by artists to describe an impulse, based in theory, to improve all areas of life. A variety of other sectors of business, and consequently consumers, also pursued an interest in design. Museum administrators in North America, in particular at the Museum of Modern Art in New York, promotedthis development. That representatives from other fields, for instance businesspeople with no interest in the arts, shared in shaping the notion of what design should be and participated in the formulation of divergent definitions thereof, represented a conflict of interests.

The Chicago Institute found itself caught in the crossfire of these divergent interests. Moholy-Nagy himself held on to his claim in favor of universal design and, in contrast to the Bauhaus curriculum, wanted additionally to assure the completion of the Institute's curriculum with subjects such as history, sociology, literature, mathematics, and physics. At the same time, in order to do justice to the expectation of his financial backers and patrons, he had to attend to the provision of professional and practical study. Although he had no influence on the organization of “Industrial Design as a New Profession"—a conference held at the New York Museum of Modern Art in November 1946—he did make his position clear that this new profession could only be learned by

269 Robert Brownjohn, Tactile table of bent plastic, Institute of Design, Chicago, 1944 From: László Moholy-Nagy, Vision in Motion

![](images/8a3f17e68a5d992e4f228a8ca9b037b8622df90ce5d0e9df566e1d23c3873615.jpg)

“vision in relationships,"126 by "seeing, feeling and thinking in relationship and not as a series of isolated phenomena."127 Moholy-Nagy was aware that in the relationship between design and industry, there was aconflict between“guidance and being quided."128Even if he was forced to betake himself to the“fruitless level of an unconscious sociobiological harmony,"129 it must be noted that the harmony among human being, nature, and technology that he strove for could only have been created within a dynamic process. In this sense, he never doubted in the permanent necessity for a corresponding need for social action. Whatever the content of his conception of Utopia may have been, for Moholy-Nagy it demanded vigorous action, "Utopia into action."130

## Conclusion

All the former Bauhaus members pursued their teaching activities in their respective highly specialized fields. Here are some examples: Gropius, initially together with Marcel Breuer, taught architecture at the Graduate School of Design at Harvard University in Cambridge, and Mies and Hilberseimer at the architecture department of the Armour Institute and the Illinois Institute of Technology in Chicago, respectively. Albers taught painting, drawing, and graphic arts at the Yale University Art School in New Haven, among other places; Werner Drewes at Brooklyn College in New York City, at the Chicago Institute of Design in Chicago, and at the School of Fine Arts, Washington University in St. Louis, and elsewhere; Hannes Beckmann worked as a professor for color and design at the Cooper Union Art School in New York City, and so on. Following the period he spent working as an assistant teacher for painting at Black Mountain College, Schawinsky lectured at New York University and at City College in New York City (later CUNY). The departments of design and commercial art were shared between Moholy-Nagy in Chicago and Hin Bredendieck, who was, among other things, a professor and head of the Department of Industrial Design at the Georgia Institute of Technology in Atlanta, and so on. Anni Albers taught weaving and textile design at Black Mountain College, which Marli Ehrmann taught in Chicago.

What is striking in this respect is that those art courses teaching fundamentals or preparing for a particular discipline, that is, color theory or figure drawing, were often taken on by former Bauhaus students, particularly from the thirties to the fifties. Thése teaching activities of the second generation of Bauhaus members mainly took place at colleges, institutes such as night schools, or in the context of workshops. This can be documented in an exemplary manner in the cases of Beckmann, Bredendieck, Dearstyne, Drewes, and Lux Feininger, who initially worked at Sarah Lawrence College, in Bronxville, outside of New York City. Around 1935 Lila Koppelman held a position as professor at Bennington College in Vermont. Paul Wieghardt began his career in North America in charge of the painting department at the summer school, Cummington Playhouse in the Hills, in Cummington, Massachusetts. And after her emigration, Marguerite Friedlaender-Wildenhain was in charge of the pottery/ceramics workshop at the College of Arts and Crafts in Oakland, California. These preparatory or basic courses provide evidence for the fact that in part, the educational ideals of the Bauhaus were carried on.

The essential aspects relating to the reception of Bauhaus pedagogy in North America can be recognized by examining the examples of Gropius, Albers, and Moholy in their roles as teachers in the United States. Notably, the principle of interdisciplinary design only gained general acceptance at North American educational institutions as classes in basic or general courses of study. These courses, on the level of a general education, were completely in keeping with the Bauhaus's preliminary course idea. Nevertheless, whether they were, andare still today, called "preliminary course," "introductory design," or "basic design," they merely formed the basis for a course of study within a particular field. Furtherreaching courses, which wouldhave taken on a Bauhaus tradition combining all the artistic disciplines, would have been an almost impossible notion within the very strict and goal directed curricula at American colleges. Consequently, even Gropius, who had wholly identified the principle of his teaching method with the marriage of subjective training and objective observation, contented himself with the inclusion of his idea of the preliminary course. Later, Albers and Moholy satisfied themselves with the same; Albers owing to his professional reorientation at Yale University, Moholy due to the financial difficulties at the Chicago Institute. Their personal evaluation of the significance that should be awarded to the preliminary course varied nevertheless. Albers's fouryear plan at Yale appeared convincing by virtue of the equal relationship between “elementary general art training" and "specialized professional study." Under Moholy's direction, the curriculum in Chicago maintained the preliminary course at the beginning of study; the subsequent course of training, however, was organized into four specializeddepartments. The intentionalmodification that the idea of the preliminary course underwent can be summarized as follows: In North America, it no longer served as a preparation to an interdisciplinary form of design, but rather as the preparation for a specific discipline. With this shift, the educational goal of the former Bauhaus members doubtlessly altered, for now, it finally became fixed, that is, aimed toward aparticular ultimate purpose.

![](images/57b22b27d597bbcab0e29239f3b17913ee7b6a7cb36d7f6bf9495845ebb3181b.jpg)  
270 Sculptural paper exercises, Institute of Design, Chicago, 1943  
From: László Moholy-Nagy, Vision in Motion

Thischapter was translated by Simon Lèbe.

2 See, among others, Peter Hahn, “Wege der Bauhäusler in Reich und Exil," in Winfried Nerdinger, ed., Bauhaus-Moderne im Nationalsozialismus: Zwischen AnbiederungundVerfolgung(Munich:Prestel, 1993), p. 208.

3 Most recently at the symposium Architektur und Exil: Die deutschsprachige Emigration und die Transformation der Moderne, 1933–1945 at the Technische Universität Berlin, Art History Department, 30 October 1998-1November 1998.

4The fifty Bauhaus members discussed here are teachers as well as students who actually taught or were registered as students at the Bauhausin Weimar, Dessau, or Berlin for any period of time. Thus, artists who belonged to the Bauhaus circle and emigrated to North America but never in fact studiedat the Bauhaus or taught there for any considerable time suchas Ellen Auerbach, György Kepes, or Hans Richter, have not been taken into account.

5On this, see Exiles + Emigrés: The Flight of European Artists from Hitler, ed. Stephanie Barron with Sabine Eckmann, exh. cat. (Los Angeles: Los Angeles County Museum of Art; New York: Abrams, 1997), especially the introduction by Stephanie Barron, "European Artists in Exile: A Reading between the Lines," pp. 11–29.

(New York: Museum of Modern Art, 1938), p. 6; reprinted in Irving Sandler and Amy Newman, eds. Defining Modern Art: Selected Writings of Alfred H. Barr, Jr. (New York: Abrams, 1986), p. 99.

48 Ibid.

vaguely sensed that my enthusiasm was reaching eager ears-especially my characterization of Gropius as a genius at fusing other geniuses." 58 Dorner even used the speech "In Memoriam Moholy-Nagy," which he made on 17 September 1947 at the Art Institute Chicago, to clear Gropius of the charge that he relied on stylistic clichés. "Nothing therefore could infuriate the founder of the Bauhaus—Walter Gropius—more than to hear that the Institute of Design tried to introduce the 'Bauhausstyle' in design and nothing could miss the core of Modern architecture so widely as the unfortunate term: 'International style.'" see Alexander Dorner, "In Memoriam Moholy-Nagy: Speech on 17 September 1947," typewritten manuscript at the Bauhaus-Archiv Berlin, p. 2.

The correspondence between Moholy-Nagy and Gropius from 1937 to 1946 in the Houghton Library, Harvard University consists of, among others, 49 letters from Moholy-Nagy to Gropius and 13 letters from Gropius to Moholy-Nagy. Moholy's letters include the structure of the New Bauhaus, lesson plans, and curricula. On the correspondence between Moholy and Gropius with Walter Paepcke, see esp. 'Our curriculum doesn't fit into the competitive mood of an approachingpostwar boom, because we refuse to promise a two-semester training for a breadwinning job. . . . I shall keep on considering the process of education more important than the finished result.'"

## Bibliography

The literature on the Bauhaus has grown immensely in recent years, and it is scarcely possible to get a handle on it anylonger. The Bauhaus bibliography by Christina Biundo et al., Bauhaus-Ideen, 1919–1994: Bibliografie undBeiträge zur Rezeption des Bauhausgedankens, ed. Andreas Haus (Berlin: Reimer, 1994), alone includes 4104 titles. The present bibliography includes all of the titles that were cited in the present work, those to which the author is indebted for particular ideas, and those that couldbe especially useful for further exploration into specific aspects.

Where more than one title by the same author or editor is listed, they are arranged chronologically by date of first publication.

## The Authors

## Rainer K. Wick

Born 1944; studies in pedagogy, sociology, art history, and art education in Bonn and Cologne, 1st and 2d state exams; 1969–78: schoolteacher; 1975: Ph.D. under René König with a dissertation on happenings and Fluxus; 1979–85: professor in the department of design and art education (Folkwang) at the Universität Gesamthochschhule in Essen; 1985: tenure in art pedagogy; since 1986: professorship for pedagogy in arts and culture at the Bergische Universität Gesamthochschule in Wuppertal; in addition to numerous essays in journals and catalogs, book publications include: Zur Soziologie intermediärer Kunstpraxis: Happening, Fluxus, Aktionen (Cologne, 1975); Kunstsoziologie, ed. with Astrid Wick-Kmoch (Cologne, 1979); Bauhaus-Pädagogik (Cologne, 1982); Ist die Bauhaus-Pädagogik aktuell? ed. (Cologne, 1985); Johannes Itten: Bildanalysen, ed. (Ravensburg, 1988); Das Neue Sehen: Von der Fotografie am Bauhaus zur Subjektiven Fotografie, ed. (Munich, 1991); Fotografie und Ästhetische Erziehung, ed. (Munich, 1992); Bauhaus: Die frühen Jahre, ed. (Wuppertal, 1996); Bonato: Kunst im öffentlichen Raum (Cologne, 1996); Johannes Itten: Kunstpä- dagogik als Erlebnispädagogik? (Lüneburg, 1997); editor of the series Kontext: Schriftenreihe für Kunst, Kunsterziehung und Kulturpädagogik, published by the Bergische Universität-Gesamthochschule Wuppertal.

## Gabriele Diana Grawe

Born 1966; studies in art history and German in Trier; 1994: M.A.; 1998: dissertation on members of the Bauhaus in North America, under Andreas Haus at the Hochschule der Künste in Berlin; 1999-2000: intern at the Staatliche Museen in Kassel; coauthor of Bauhaus-Ideen, 1919–1994, ed. Andreas Haus (Berlin, 1994); publications on Kurt Schwitters and contemporary art.

In comprehensive individual analyses, the author Rainer K Wick presents the varied pedagogical approaches of the Bauhaus teachers Walter Gropius, Hannes Meyer, Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, Johannes Itten, László Moholy-Nagy, Josef Albers, Wassily Kandinsky, Paul Klee, Oskar Schlemmer, and Joost Schmidt, who effected a profound revolution in art education. The author also sheds light on the historical circumstances behind the development of the Bauhaus, identifies its important precursors, and provides a brief sketch of how Bauhaus pedagogy continued to have an effect long after the end of World War II. This sketch is supplemented by an essay by Gabriele Diana Grawe on the reception of Bauhaus ideas in North America, where many of the former Bauhaus teachers and students emigrated.

Rainer K. Wick is professor of art and cultural pedagogy at the Universität-Gesamthochschule in Wuppertal.
